comments.text: Cool idea. Different kids of clocks with different behaviours would had been nice. Also I notice that the start value of every clock is randomized. If it wasnt players could make very fast runs.

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: The game plays very well and easy to understand. I like the concept and I think there is definitely a nice genre here. Great work!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: The game seems simple but winning is not so easy :) I enjoyed playing some levels and loved the concept. The game would be perfect with a chill music behind!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Simple and fun, well made game, perfect for mobiles. More challenging levels with obstacles etc could make this quite addictive.

comments.author: Andrew Schreiber
comments.text: Really creative game mechanic that is both thoughtless and allows for strategy!

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
I really love the look of this one! It’s really simple but feels appropriate and polished.

Now I do think the game mechanic wasn’t super obvious. It took me a ton of tries to finally understand that you had to pay attention to the needle’s position. A tutorial with a simple text instruction would go a long way.

Beyond that, I thought it was good but got repetitive very quick. Maybe there is a way to keep the same mechanic but make it more exciting, while keeping the levels shorter. For instance have clocks that you have to unlock before they disappear, or clocks that move?

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: A simple but fun concept! The design is very clean, and lots of nice elements of polish. Great work

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Clean graphics, elegant style. The jumping clock mechanic has a lot of potential, especially if you expand on it with obstacles and other mechanics. Nice job, congrats!

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I enjoyed this game very much. For me, everything felt right. Simple and to the point while being challenging. Great job!

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
Simple. Clean. Beautiful.

Nice job.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: A very unique game, congrats! The graphics are super clean and inviting, lots of polish can be felt everywhere. Like a few others have remarked, there’s tons of room to expand the puzzle aspect on top of this solid base and I’d love to see it evolve further.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing this game, not that I was much good at it, but would get better with practice if I was ever to do all 60 levels.

The concept was really well put together, and the levels have a good design.

Works really well as a mobile game

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Great job! The controls and gameplay are simple yet highly effective. I really appreciate the one-click control scheme—it feels intuitive and smooth.

That said, I would have liked a stronger connection to the theme. It feels a bit like the theme was retrofitted onto the game rather than being a core element. Some sound effects could have added a lot to the overall experience as well!

Congrats on releasing the game! The UI is clean and minimalistic, and the effects on the clock are a nice touch—it’s great to have that kind of visual feedback.

Well done, GG!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: this game is so playable! I really enjoyed the precise simple one button controls and the fact I can choose my favourite background colour but the thing I appreciated most were the awesome levels you designed; all the fun shapes were a delightful extra bit of artistic flair that really mattered and elevated the game from good to great in my books! I’m glad I was allowed to skip tricky levels so I got to try out every single one. very well done!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I’ve never seen a game mechanic like this one. Very well implemented. And the usage of HTML + CSS is great!

It could benefit from some audio effects and/or music. And maybe a sprinkle of more graphical effects too, “exe-candy” if you want to call it so.

Good game!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Nice concept! I loved the levels design. The game could have been more enjoying with some music and sound effects!

Congrats on the submission!

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Good Job. The game is easy to understand, and I really like the style of the interface—it’s minimalist and clear.

Adding sound effects could make the game even better!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: It took me a little time to understand what to do at the beginning, a tutorial level would have been nice. I was impressed there are 60 levels! The gameplay is good, but there a little delay between the time I click on my trackpad (MacBook Pro) and the time the game reacts, ruining the experience a bit for me. A background music and some sound effects would greatly improve the atmosphere. Graphics are good, and the levels are well designed. Lastly, I didn’t feel a strong connection between the theme of the competition and this game, apart from the title.

api.name: Thirteens everywhere...
api.description:
A game for future programmers.

You need to reach the flag. You have a robot JSK-13 to do this. Unfortunately, the robot has a triskadekaphobia. It means that it is afraid of thirteens and it will break down if he meet one. Write a program for the robot and reach flags!

Write code on the left side.

Press Run to run the code on the right side.

Press SPEED button to change the speed of JSK-13.

Use the bottom panel to see how many commands you have used.

Good luck!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: An interesting little coding game, too taxing for my pea brain though :)
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: Hope you’ll solve all the game :)

comments.author: Marina Zharinova
comments.text: Awesome project for young programming learners :) A useful game to train algorithms

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: You’ll need algorithms (namely DFS) only on last level :)

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Interesting game. Made me think like A=B or Knotbot.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Interesting game, having a code compiler that understands syntax (both valid and invalid) is pretty cool. Some of the levels really made me think.

Having levels 4 and 5 be almost identical really confused me and I thought I was solving the same level again. I also wish I could see the command list that I have available rather than just the help for the current level. I wanted to check the names for the scan check but if you’re not on the level that introduces them you can’t verify if the command is the correct name.

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: I think it would be difficult to figure out why the error is caused on level 5 without level 4

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Really cool that you fit a whole parser for a custom language in 13KB! Wish the game had some sound effects.

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: I thought about adding some sound effects, but Windows XP Critical Stop was 38.5 KiB :]

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: since the players are coders this is easy, nice use of code in game but it is very easy and one level got stuck.

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: I love this style of game, I only had an issue with run and Run. Other than that I loved the game and operations. Great work!

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Very clever game, I had fun figuring out loops and the scan directives!

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Very fun and impressive entry. Don’t think there is, nor will be one quite like this.

I think you’ve done a nice job with the mechanics and balance for the most part in this game, at least for someone who already knows programming lol I can imagine it being a good bit more tricky for someone who doesn’t.

Gameplay and innovation are top notch in my opinion!

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: Thank you very much! I think I’ll continue developing the game. Of course I should keep in mind 13kb limit :)

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Really a game for JS13k devs! I find the concept very interesting and had fun to play. The increase of the levels dificulty is smooth and clever. Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
An interesting concept for a game. Quite a well used format for a teaching tool for programming concepts.

I found it tricky to know what to do from the very start, I think it was the confusion between needing to type “RUN” and press “RUN”, but once I got the hang of it, things moved along.

I only got to level 5, then it got stuck on level 5 even when I completed it multiple times.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
I really liked this one, a fun puzzle game for nerds like us haha

I couldn’t get past level 9 though, I can’t figure out how to do it in less than 5 commands, I’m probably missing the obvious :(

A few things to improve (but overall solid entry):

  • graphics on the map/robot could be more fun
  • UI could use a bit of love (colors, fonts, make the editor look more like a terminal)
  • real time compile errors would be nice (+ ability to unindent)

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: You can see all the solutions on GitHub if you are stuck on some level(s) :)

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: It took me a while to figure out that I have to use 2 “run” in the first level, I think it would have been better to call that command “step”.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: The first version of the game was called “Run!”, then I was too lazy to rename the command :)

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Got to level 5, finish and get a runtime error.

From the beginning, I did not immediately understand what language to write, but then it turned out that it clearly indicates which commands to write :).

What about autocomplete? :D

Well, it was fun, congratulations!

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: Ace library was very big so the code editor was too handwritten to implement autocomplete or linenos :)

comments.author: Quinten Clause
comments.text:
I think this game is fantastic! Very good for teaching coding fundamentals, because of your very focused clean design.

I only have some very minor details to share:

Level 6 (where you introduce the if) was to soon on the difficulty curve perhaps. The levels 7 and 8 are easier to figure out.

The “else” is introduced in level 10, but I already cheated my way through level 9 with it. I also solved level 1 the second time with loop {run}. It’s ok to let players do that, but in a classroom students will trip up other students.

Also don’t lock up players on the last level when they finished your game with localStorage. Offer a way for players to go back and replay other levels.

Spoiler alert!

Mind that as long as all the walls in your maze are connected, your robot can safely reach any tile along the walls by spinning along the walls with the following 4 commands loop { if scanLeft { left run } else { right } } Aside from the “only 3 commands rule”, this works in every level except the one with the pillars. It even works in the level where the hallway becomes wider than 1 tile for a moment (staircase up, staircase down)

If you want to expand this game more: two other features I would like is a way to conditionally break the “loop” and a way to assign values to a variable.

But the game is already so good. I will try to convince my wife who is a teacher to try it out in class.

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: In levels starting from 6 I had an idea to put bombs/dynamite/[1313]/sth else into some cells so this trick would not work. But I was too lazy to implement them :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I LOVE programming games, very well executed!

sound fx and maybe some animations (instead of “steps”) would be nice

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: I kept in mind an idea to make the game look like terminal so there isn’t any transitions

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A programming challenge. Interesting integration of the theme. Nice job!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: This could have been fun, but I was not able to figure out any commands. It seems like “right”, “left” and some others were commands, but I was not able to get the robot to move. I also tried stuff like x=x+1 but that didn’t help. Some hints to the language or a full command palette would have helped. I really want to like this game.

comments.author: mallo_c
comments.text: right and left are rotating the robot, not moving it.

api.name: Bounty Islands
api.description:
Capture the island in confrontation with other adventurers. Opponents will not joke!

Controls:

  • W/A/S/D and Arrows - move camera
  • Q/E - rotate camera
  • PgUp/PgDn - minimap zoom
  • F - fullscreen/window
  • Shift-1/0 - bind base
  • 1/0 - select binded base
  • Right click - set base destination

The game is made using my js13k-2d renderer and js13k-ecs entity component system. The libraries have been significantly improved in the process, new releases will be coming soon.

Packed with microbundle, UglifyJS, roadroller and advzip.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Procedural generated terrrain.. cool stuff!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Loved that game, really nice graphics and funny gameplay! The only things I would have love is a zoom on map (maybe instead of minimap, or with mouse wheel), and a multi-binding like in strategy game, to make sets of units at once

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Wow the game map is massive. I’m very impressed with how much was going on at the same time. I need to look a bit better and longer at the game but it looks very impressive. I found it confusing that the game was mostly click-based but the actions required the keyboard. Why not go full mouse? Doesn’t seem to fit the theme though?

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: I loved the graphics and camera movement with minimap, game itself was not clear of what to do

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Unable to load but the game looks fun. I will check back but great work getting it in!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
At first it was not really clear to me, what i had to do, but after i figured out how to conquer other bases, it was quite entertaining and i conquered the whole island. The maps look nice, the procedural generation is great. Would be cool, if the map had consequences for gameplay (unable to fly over mountains, etc.) The minimap is optically great, but has no furthe use for gameplay. at least the bases should be colored here. Sound effects/music would be nice. And the possibility to zoom using the mousewheel. The mix of keyboard-keys and mouse control feels a bit awkward, at least at the beginning. Maybe making the buttons clickable by mouse would be a good addition.

Overall: a nice game, had fun with it.

comments.author: Evgeniy Pavlov
comments.text: Thank you! I’ll probably implement a lot of this in the post release.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: To me it was difficult to understand how to play, I would recommend starting the game with a little tutorial. Once understood, the game is fun to play, the graphics are well done and the animation is smooth! I would suggest to simplify the controls as, to me, it is not so natural to switch from mouse to keyboard. Well done!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play this game. I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to start the game, instead the page search turns up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Evgeniy Pavlov
comments.text: Thanks, I didn’t even realize there was such a case. I’ll keep it in mind for the future.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text:
Bounty Island is a nice looking game but I did initially feel a bit lost. What was the purpose despite upgrading my base from time to time? How was I supposed to progress? But once I realised that Right Click was the way to attack another base it also started to become fun as I saw my little ships go off and attack and capture an enemy base! One thing which I noticed was that my captured bases mostly stayed under my control despite left unattended. The AI seemed busy elsewhere and rarely focused on me (I later left the game unattended for a period of time and when I came back I had actually lost the game).

I would have liked to see more direct control like in a classic RTS where you select one or more of your units and send them in a direction to attack. With Bounty Island I felt like I didn’t have enough control. Another improvement would be a keyboard shortcut to cycle through my bases (tab?).

comments.author: Evgeniy Pavlov
comments.text:
That’s right, unit control is intentionally limited. Because the size limitations do not allow the AI to be implemented with enough quality to compete with humans. And in general the chosen genre involves a lot of code, which is problematic in this event. So there are a lot of compromises in this game. My main goal was to make the game at least playable, and in my opinion it succeeded.

Hotkeys are present in the game. Shift-number allows you to assign a base to a key and then quickly switch to it.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
An impressively detailed overhead simulation game! Personally when a game is this complicated I like to see some in-game tutorial or other helpers to guide me along or I feel a little lost. I also didn’t notice the theme integrated in any way, and I was craving some sound effects.

But overall I’m impressed with the systems and renderer. Nice work!

comments.author: Evgeniy Pavlov
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! Indeed, my game lacks instruction, but I’m not good enough to fit a lot of content into the limit. So it turned out to be some hardcore, where the player himself needs to figure out all the mechanics. This year’s theme seemed too abstract to me, so I ignored it )

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
It took me a while to understand how to play. The game is pretty hard, but after a while I managed to prepare my squadron and attack. It’s a very nice experience when you seat and watch how massive armies fight.

Mini map and procedural generation is nicely done. I miss some variety in unit types and touch support, basic sounds would be nice to add too. I know it’s near 13kb and it’s hard to fit those things.

I had my fun with this game, great entry!

comments.author: Evgeniy Pavlov
comments.text: Thank you! The variety of units takes a lot of time to balance, so I made do with the bare minimum necessary. I will probably continue to develop this game, I think it has potential.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game with plenty going on all at once.

The game looked amazing, but played really slow to make it playable for me.

Was it perlin noise you used to generate the levels and textures? Well implemented.

An in-game tutorial would have helped to explain what all the elements of the game were and what the ultimate goal was.

What was the link to the theme?

comments.author: Evgeniy Pavlov
comments.text: Yes, Perlin noise is used for generation. The game runs smoothly at 144 fps on my pc. On what configuration do you experience lag?

api.name: EMOJIPHOBIA
api.description:
The scariest quiz of all time ! 😱

Try to answer 13 questions (and more), about 280 phobias… and their corresponding emojis… 😵‍💫

The questions are randomized, so the game is replayable! 🎮

Controls: click on desktop / touch on mobile 👆

Decentralized challenges:

  • Arcadia (hosting)
  • Avalanche + Thirdweb (leaderboards)

comments.author: Alex Delderfield

Nice work with the leaderboard setup, I always like to see those in a gamejam game, it adds a good challenge & urge to replay to score higher.

So many phobias… I like how clean and neat the graphics are for this game, and the idea to pair up emojis and phobias works really well.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: some of those questions were super hard!!! but I did okay, 8 of 13 and 4 bonus points lol nice job!! I like the nice text tweens, so smooth

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: That was fun and interesting use of the theme. I liked how you even included a leaderboard, great job!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little game, although at times turned into a bit of a guessing game.

Some of these are tricky to work out, and almost impossible for kids without some fairly advanced linguistic skills.

Nice to see the leaderboard too, although with my scores I don’t need them putting up there :)

Funky soundtrack.

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Ok this is a lot more fun that I thought it was going to be going into it. I figured out a lot more of these than I thought I ever would, and even broke into the top 50 my second time around, thanks to some luck in sudden death!

Very cool comfy entry.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: Refreshing little entry! My middle-school Latin came in handy AGAIN today 😎. Fun little trivia/guessing game. Rad Dictionary!

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: The concept of using emojis to represent phobias is creative and fun! The clean design of the quiz interface, with a minimalistic layout, helps keep the focus on the gameplay. The large, central emoji image is easy to recognize, and the simplicity of the color palette—mainly white with light accents—helps create a sense of clarity, which is great for a quiz-style game. While the interface is clean, it could benefit from a bit more visual flair to enhance the player’s engagement. Adding subtle animations, such as a slight bounce effect when a player selects the correct answer, or a visual indicator of the difficulty level (since the game mentions “easy”), could make the game feel more dynamic. Additionally, some more vivid background elements or gradients could bring a bit more personality to the overall aesthetic. The simplicity of the design is its strength, keeping the user focused on the game without unnecessary distractions. The idea of tying the gameplay to emojis makes it feel accessible to a wide audience. With just a few additional visual cues, this game could be even more immersive and fun to play. Great job bringing an innovative concept to life!

comments.author: Adrien Guéret
comments.text:
A very impressive quiz: so much phobias in only one game! The way you’ve matched emojis with phobias is very smart.

Love the idea of the sudden death at the end.

Well done :)

api.name: Dimension 13
api.description:
Your teleporter’s power is depleted, and you’re stranded in the 13th dimension, courtesy of the 𝐏𝐎𝐋𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐒𝐏𝐀𝐂𝐄 𝐏𝐑𝐎𝐆𝐑𝐀𝐌. To escape, you must retrieve all 7 power gems. But beware—your greatest 𝐅𝐄𝐀𝐑 is dying of hypothermia if your temperature drops below -𝟏𝟑°C. Retreat to the teleporter or find a campfire to 𝐖𝐀𝐑𝐌 𝐔𝐏 before it’s too late.

Controls:

WASD: Move around the map Click: Attack Shift: Run Controller Support: Left stick to move, right stick to look, and shoulder buttons to sprint or attack Note:

𝗪𝗔𝗥𝗡𝗜𝗡𝗚: This game is GPU-intensive, and animations may lag on low-end devices. Press Ctrl + Plus or Ctrl + Minus to rescale and reload if needed. Also, an adult-sized diaper is recommended—there’s a high chance you’ll wet yourself while playing.

Age Rating: 13+ (so I can play it)

Good Luck: Which, honestly, you won’t need, because you’re gonna die anyway. 😈

How It Fits the Theme:

Surviving in the 13th dimension isn’t just about collecting gems—it’s about keeping your suit’s temperature above -13°C. The 13th dimension is full of unlucky surprises (you’ll see).

BTW, check out my YouTube channel and my other projects on Itch.io. I’m Andrei, a 13-year-old developer, even IDK when I started gamedev exactly. This is my third time at JS13k, and I’m here to learn and make cool games!

I’m gonna submit it on Arcadia, that’s why I put it on the Decentralized category. Hopefully they accept it :)

Feedback and constructive criticism are welcome—be honest and have fun. ✌️

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Love the art style! The idea isn’t bad as well, however a few things made the experience pretty frustrating and I gave up after a few attempts.

You rightly say you’re gonna die a lot. With that in mind….don’t put a 45 second text to speech intro in the game…it’s very annoying. I turn off audio immediately.

The collision detection just kind of brings you to a stop, it would be better if you could slide along walls.

The view distance is so short, makes it just kind of frustrating to look for the items.

I still think though there’s a ton of potential here. Literally just tweak collision, increase view distance, and remove tts and this is a great game! Amazing work for a 13 year old as well!

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I should create a polished version with this feedback, but the view distance is intentionally short to make it harder and more confusing, but it could be a bit bigger, yeah.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Difficult and sadly WASD-only controls (would be nice to have ZQSD / arrow keys too the next time), aniways, retro-pigeon once again delivers really impressive visuals in 13kb and at 13yo! Can’t wait for the youtube video showing how you did it :p

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks, the video is coming after the results are in so I can brag about my game 😅

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Quite difficult, those damn arachnoids! The graphics were good but a bit flickery for my old man eyes.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, should’ve added an option for disabling that. I am a pretty avid gamer, so can’t judge the difficulty well, thanks for the feedback. And finally somebody spelled arachnoids correctly 😅

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: very scary game but cool animation and use of 3d

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for the kind words, I tried making it as scary as possible.

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Fantastic game, it worked well and you have a 3d world working in only 13kb! Amazing work and excellent job!

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for the kind words, I already had a custom 3D engine from last year which gave me an advantage, I might release it.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Impressive visuals, I wished I could skip the intro after dying once, I found the controls and movement experience to be too touchy, easily getting stuck on obstacles, unable to strafe around them. Found a spinny wheel thing, but your temperature timer doesn’t pause while gambling? I found the gameplay loop to be too short, should have a bit more time outside the teleporter to explore before having to go back to reheat. Gave it a few minutes but was frustrated with the controls and collision. Amazing effort for 13 years old! I could see this being very very fun with some gameplay and control polish.

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for all of the feedback! The prize idea was stolen from mario kart, but the temperature thing needs some fine tuning. Initially you had only 13 seconds to do whatever you do, so for me the time is long, thanks for giving me a fresh perspective on my game.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I like the visual presentation (the grainy look) and the atmosphere of the gameplay. i played with keyboard and gamepad, the keyboard-controls are better (at least for me). I tend to get stuck on some obstacles, which is a bit annoying, but maybe (as it adds to the tension of the game) this is intended? Ah, and my browser selects the german voice for the speech output, so the text sounds if some dutch guy translated a microwave manual written in manadrin to english, haha :-)

Overall: nice game!

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I know the TTS is kinda finnicky but it adds character. I didn’t think about adding wall sliding, I think that would fix the issues.

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: A bit overwhelming at first, but provides a cool atmosphere as you explore this strange planet. Nice work!

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, I intentionally made it slightly overwhelming to add a bit of confusion ;)

comments.author: Michael M.

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: I’m sorry to hear that, but the rules didn’t mention that it should be able to handle things like that so didn’t check it. Anyway, thanks for letting me know, will help me in future projects,

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Great visuals and a very interesting entry overall. There is some solid shader work here. The blood effect when you die is a good one, and I like the environment, and the way the whole game looks. The retro sun orbiting around and all the stars in the sky are great details. Really reminds me of some old 3D MSDOS games (in a good way!). Extra impressive that you’re a 13 year old dev!

Chucked you a follow over on itch, keen to see what kinds of games you create in the coming years!

comments.author: retro-pigeon
comments.text: Thanks, the sun was inspired by old graphics, glad you liked it :)

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: the graphics blow my mind!!!!!! I love the scanline/chromatic style fx that occur, almost vhs tape distortion styles. very cool. it ran smoothly on my older pc, though I kept getting stuck on things and wish the spike colliders were circular (or physics changed to reflect alongnormal) so you could wallslide around them smoothly and never get stuck while moving. but that’s just nit-picking, this entry is awesome!!! great work.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Really cool graphics! And a nice interpretation of the theme that really made me fear the 13 ;) Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Very cool game.

Well done for submitting this at 13.

The graphics were excellent, and very atmospheric. Played very smoothly for me.

The sound effects worked well.

It was pretty easy to work out how to move, although no matter how fast I went those little spider things were always on me :)

I was not very good at finding a fireplace when my temperature dropped, perhaps having a mini-map of places visited already or within a certain distance of the player would help?

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: The lighting effects and color palette create a strong atmospheric feel that aligns well with the game’s survival mechanics. The use of light beams cutting through the dark environment adds a sense of mystery and urgency, which is perfect for a game that deals with survival in a harsh, cold environment. The design effectively conveys the eerie, isolated atmosphere of the 13th dimension. The background and environment, while atmospheric, could benefit from a bit more texture or detail to break up the otherwise flat visual feel. Some small details or environmental animations, such as snow particles or environmental debris, could enhance the immersion and make the setting feel more alive and dangerous. Overall, you’ve done a fantastic job in creating an environment that feels tense and immersive. The lighting effects are particularly well-executed, and they play a big role in setting the game’s mood. Great work on building a visually cohesive and suspenseful atmosphere!

api.name: The Lonely 13
api.description:
It’s a memory-type matching game with a small town murder ambiance.

Match at least two pairs, then click on the “healing pairs” button at the center top before the spiders make it back up to the button.

At the end of every round, you get options on how to spend your matches.

More matches == more healing. Also, some rounds have special options.

At least one person must survive all 13 rounds.

Good Luck 🤞

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
The graphics are interesting, nice use of emojies! The musics and spiders add some tension.

I didnt notive right away that you were showing the colours already known to help, once I spotted that was getting more pairs. The only issue is the game style stayed the same, matching pairs.

Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice ambiance not usual for a memory game. as you show pairs game is really easy. not my kind of game but nice execution

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: The game gives a creepy atmosphere using clever filters, effects, music and speech. I like the twist where you have to save your friends.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Nice use of emoji and filters for ambience!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Great work and great game, I like the matching pairs, The history on mouse over was a very nice touch. Great work and wonderful game!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun pairs matching game. I appreciated the hints when you hover on the hexagons.

Eerie music and grainy dark picture made it quite atmospheric.

I wasn’t sure if I was against the clock or the spiders? I kept forgetting to press the button at the top before the spiders got there.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Dark and bleak game! Excellent theme integration. I lost track of the spiders as a ‘timer’ as I was focused on the flipping cards themselves being the only color thing on screen, and the sheer amount of cards. It seemed a touch unresponsive/low frame rate for me, but I still enjoyed playing. I noticed on second playthru the ‘xray’ feature on cards you ’d already flipped, so that was helpful. Good game.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game doesn’t work on Firefox at all because “window.speechSynthesis is undefined”. Please be careful to check for the availability of non standard APIs.

I don’t quite understand the lore of the game. What are the paint blobs supposed to signify? Why are there spiders? Are the spiders making people sick?

I guess the game was too hard, so you added the mouse hover effect, but doesn’t that defeat the point of a memory game?

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
The noir mood is very well set, making you feel that you are racing against the clock (and the spiders) to save the town from a curse.

Excellent gfx, audio track to match, although the speech synthesis has a strange accent that makes it really hard to understand (and even more with the level up messages – is that sanskrit ?). That was under Firefox, it stays silent under Chromium.

This is a clever twist on the basic memory game, loved the fact that hovering the mouse over an already revealed tile show an outline of the shape. The 13 tiles are unneeded IMHO.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: This game was a little difficult to get started with for me - since the Healing Pairs option was not super clear to me - but once it clicked I had a really good time with the game. Interesting mechanics and getting at least one person to survive 13 rounds is quite the challenge!

api.name: Monster Steps
api.description:
Monster Steps is a strategic puzzle game. In this game, players navigate through a grid-based world, avoiding monsters that appear every 13 steps while trying to reach their goal.

Game Features

  • Grid-based gameplay with increasing difficulty
  • 13 power-ups/bonuses
  • Strategic planning required to avoid monsters
  • 13 unique levels to challenge players

Development

Devlog thread on X: https://x.com/gtanczyk/status/1823848171975401472

How to Play

Navigate through each level using arrow keys or on-screen controls. Collect power-ups, avoid monsters, and reach the goal to progress. Be careful - a new monster spawns every 13 steps!

Credits

Created by Grzegorz Tańczyk for js13k 2024

Special kudos to @lifthrasiir, the author of https://github.com/lifthrasiir/roadroller

Links

  • Play the Game
  • Source Code

Enjoy playing Monster Steps!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Fun, nice polishing. Did the prompts only help you find the idea or did they also code the game?

comments.author: Grzegorz Tańczyk
comments.text:
Yes, it did help me get the idea: https://x.com/gtanczyk/status/1823848185644675515

the original idea is documented here: https://github.com/gamedevpl/monster-steps-js13k-2024/blob/master/ideas/idea1.md, but the implementation eventually diverged from it significantly.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice isometric graphics, I love the animation, simple but effective.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice game, many features at every levels before combine them for last levels Nice isometric view, at the beginngin I use a bit confused by directions using arrows, but it become easier after using them some times

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: very enjoyable game, control is bit hard to get used to, really enjoyed the power ups and every level bringing new thing to the table. well done

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Very fun game and challenging after a while. I love the style of kiting enemies and this was done really well! Nice work!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
First the positives, its isometric which I tried this year, was was great to see, the design; look and feel is good, colourful and fun! All works well, nice to see different features added as the levels progress! Challenging but not too difficult. You could add more levels / zones and stretch this kind of game out quite easily.

Could use more of the screen, on a large monitor was quite small, couldnt find a restart button and I managed to lock my self in at one point. Not sure the quit button was needed, if you press this you are back to the start and have to go from level 1.

Overall a good entry with potential.

comments.author: Grzegorz Tańczyk
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, and I’m sorry to hear about the problem with large monitor/back button :(

comments.author: Michael M.

comments.author: Ryan Malm

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Great presentation, simple but effective.

My only complain is that I got stuck in one map and I couldnt restart it.

comments.author: Grzegorz Tańczyk

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: I love the isometric grid that you did there. A lot of unique mechanics that you made there and I really adore. The enemy could have more variety if given more time. The polish that the floor tiles are flashing are a job well done.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
I liked seeing this one develop over on X. Good work, its great to see so much packed into this game. Graphics are very clean as well. And gameplay is fun, some good puzzle dynamics overall.

Good work!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice and clean graphics, garnished with some effects.

The puzzles start easy and get harde, thats good. And all those new features constantly added to the gameplay. Thats great!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text: The game didn’t work on mobile for me as it got to a press a key prompt. I’ll need to try it on desktop instead.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I love all the cool powerups like the builder mode where you can trap enemies. Great levels, great graphics, and good use of theme for the countdown. The one thing I wish was there is to go that you need to take your hands off the arrow keys and use the mouse to click the next button in between levels, but if I could just hit space I would stay “in the zone” in terms of gameplay enjoyment! perhaps I missed a key I could press to advance without switching inputs, and if so, my apologies. Overall, really great work on a wonderfully strategic game with of interesting levels and thoughtful puzzles. Loved it!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Nice game with a cool progression system. The way the game presents you more and more powerups is very cool.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Love the isometric map (is it 3D or 2D?) The controls make it easy (once you get used to it) to move the player around the map. The level variation is also good, as we get to experience new mechanics almost every level.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Nice entry with very innovative concept! I really liked the variety of abilities, such as bombs and pushing blocks. Unfortunately, there were some bugs that I stuck and cannot restart the level.

But overall, the concept of the game is great! I’m excited to see its future development, as it feels like there’s a lot of potential for expansion, such as adding more characters or interaction items.

api.name: Ghosted
api.description:
Ghosted is a Sokoban-type puzzle game where you play as a naked alien humanoid who has exactly 13 steps to move until he transforms to a ghost. The goal is to collect all the coins in the map, but the twist is that they can be only collected when you are a ghost, but as a ghost you cannot push rocks or press buttons.

The game has 12 levels and one hidden one you unlock by beating all the other levels.

Controls:

  • Arrow keys: move
  • R: Restart level
  • Backspace or Z: Undo a move
  • Enter or Esc: Open a pause menu

!!! IMPORTANT !!!

  1. Use Firefox or Chrome/Edge to get the audio working properly. Apparently does not work on Brave browser and most likely not on Safari, either.

  2. The graphics do not show properly if played in the private mode on Firefox (some noise pixels might appear). On regular mode works fine. The reason for this is unknown.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
This is, by far, the most enjoyable game I’ve played in this competition! Although the rules were a bit unclear at first (without reading the introduction), after playing a few rounds, everything started to make sense. I quickly became hooked.

The graphics are also stunning, and the game as a whole feels very polished.

It’s incredibly well done. I especially love the level design - each stage is meticulously crafted, giving a great sense of accomplishment when I finally clear it. The difficulty curve is just right for me, challenging but not frustrating. Overall, an outstanding entry that stands out in the competition!!

Interesting game! Can’t say I’m good with those, however, I liked it :)

comments.author: Clément Pasteau

Such a great concept. I love those types of mini-puzzle games with a mechanic to tinker with all the way. Menu is incredible, especially with the logo animation. Cute little sounds (too bad there’s no music!)

api.name: Those Final Seconds
api.description:
Those Final Seconds is a poor attempt to make a shoot-em-up game. It could have been fun, but it is not, but I’m submitting it anyway to see how it fares.

The goal is to gain as many points as possible before you run out of time. Killing those poor, innocent enemies will give you points, but dying enemies also drop clocks that magically give you more time.

Controls:

  • Arrow keys or WASD: Move
  • Space/L.Ctrl/Z: Shoot
  • Enter/Escape: Pause

!!! IMPORTANT !!!

Play on Firefox or Chrome/Chromium to get the audio working properly. On Firefox avoid private browsing since it might cause graphics bugs for an unknown reason.

I think this is pretty great. Yes, it need extra work, and the control are a bit sluggish, but this is indeed A GAME! and a fun one too!

Good job on juicing a few things as well.

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Really well done. The graphics were very cool with a nice 3d feel, the controls were perfect and the game played really well! Great job!

comments.author: Ryan Malm

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text:
Awesome cutesy style pixel art.

Love the general mayhem, although enemy bullets can be hard to distinguish. Nicely paced, too.

An excellent, polished entry. Well done!

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: A well-crafted game. Very nice visuals and UI design.

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text:
115832

The graphics are really nice. Is this really 13kb jk. However I cannot see the element of 13-phobia or any elements of 13. Anyways it is a fun shoot-them-up game. The colors in the background should be a bit dimmer or desaturated to make the foreground pop. They are a tad distracting.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: My high score was 213741. Amazing work, as always. Quintessential Jani game 😁 Beautiful pixel art. The parallax scrolling, and the 3d effect on the ground made the game feel very “big”. You did a great job of making use of space, going up high feels different than staying low close to the ground. Nice enemy variety, multiple pickups with distinct purpose. All around, just a great job. Well done!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Fun game and nice pixel art. The controls however feel a bit weird. it feels the game registers only two keys from keyboard at the same time - for example when I move up and hold the left and right keys at the same time (this happens a lot while maneuvering) - player should move straight up - missing the left and right negation in that case.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I absolutely loved the parallax background and 3D side-scrolling grid! It looked so awesome! I was hoping to achieve similar action-packed gameplay; it was chaotic and satisfying.

It might be just me, but my own character’s bullets seemed to visually get in the way of enemies. Like it’s harder to see enemies when bullets are standing out more than it needs to. Maybe change its color.

I recall reading somewhere about “Enter the Gungeon” and how they chose its enemies’ bullet color. I can’t find any article about it, but I thought it was interesting to think about and how it really matters given the color pallet of your game.

My score was about 417,000. Thanks for making a great game!

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: An impressive amount of graphics and gameplay here, it looks and plays great - great entry! I also like that there is a little bit of extra vertical space, thats a nice element to add in which otherwise a lot of devs wouldnt add in, and just makes the game feel a bit roomier and smoother. I like the 13s timer integration as well, a good use of the theme and nowhere near as stressful as just having a straight 13s countdown constantly.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I don’t agree with you, playing this game is very fun! The graphics are amazing and everything is so polished!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Gorgeous pixelart, and pretty 3d-ish ground-effect! Also a pretty flawless SHMUP gameplay.

The stop-the-time-powerup is a great idea.

nice game, fun to play (a bit short maybe…)

comments.author: Quinten Clause
comments.text: Great graphics, animation and sound! I would remove the mushrooms in the background or make them blend more with the clouds by making them light blue. Atmospheric perspective could really benefit your art style. In the same way you can make the eyes and mouth in the sun lighter, perhaps a darker variant of the yellow instead of the hard contrasting black. This will make the sun appear very far away, but also really big!

comments.author: Antti Haavikko
comments.text:
Awesome art, knew it was yours straight away from seeing the cover picture. Even background parallax effect, nice! But all that great looking art has a downside too, it gets very very busy later on in the game and it becomes near impossible to try to read the situation and see incoming bullets and so on.

The sound effects got quite repetitive. Obviously music would have helped. Or even some slight variation on the sounds.

Don’t know if it was just my machine but the controls seemed a bit laggy for some reason. Not sure if I’m just delusional but felt like using arrow keys was laggier than WASD too. And it seemed worse later on when the action got hectic. I didn’t notice frame rate dipping though so maybe not just a performance thing.

There was really no point not shooting all the time that I noticed at least. I’m of the school of that a mechanic like shooting should always have some reason to not be doing it all the time (stamina systems, ammo, moving slower while shooting etc) or it should be just automatic. No point forcing the player to just keep holding a single button 100% of the play time really.

Mechanic wise it was very solid. Multiplier makes the scoring a lot more interesting pacing the time pickups introduced some more skill ceiling instead of just brainlessly going all shmup mode. I see that you’re not too happy with it yourself though. My biggest issues were the lack of visual clarity and the laggy controls. Without those, it could have indeed been very fun. I’d also maybe either slow it down a bit or zoom out some to give more reaction time. Might be just the visual clutter making it slower to react in time but I felt like those quick spread shot bursts were just too much for my old man reflexes.

My best ended up being 611k. At some point I changed to play on a quite small window which for some reason made it easier for me to see what was going on. And the scoring does maybe give too much weight to the multiplier. A huge portion of the score always comes from the initial hitless streak.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
I thought this was fun!

Awesome graphics! Solid shooty gameplay! Good retro sound effects!

My high score was 112,547.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
What a bad description, you should be the first to believe in your game!

Graphically it is well done and all the sprites move fluidly, there are lots of objects on screen and parallax scrolling as well, although the clouds placed in the background should move slowerametne than the mushroom platforms, which doesn’t seem to be the case.

In any case, I found it a fun score attack.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Unbelievably solid game! The core game loop is simple, but everything feels right: the parallax / 3d effect, the movement, the sound, the sprites - everything fits together. A solid little game, well done :)

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
A side-scrolling shooter with amazingly good graphics at under 13k! Plays great, sounds good, and looks fantastic. My only complaint is I feel the gameplay would benefit from a reason to not simply hold down the shoot button, and I would have liked the theme integrated more. Still, an impressive game, great job!

Also, regarding the description, please be kind to yourself :)

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: Easily one of the best-looking js13k games I’ve played this year. The pixel work alone is glorious. The sprites are clear and well defined, the parallax effect is lovely, the effects are nice - it’s just all really good to look at. The gameplay has a solid core loop as well: Shoot, avoid and collect. Simple enough and fun enough too. You definitely tried to add variety and things become more challenging as you progress, so kudos for that. There’s absolutely nothing in here that is on-theme though, not even slightly, which is a shame. With some small tweaks to the gameplay (and a relevant theme!) this could have been a real serious contender.

api.name: Entangolf
api.description:
Don’t be afraid to play all 13 entangled holes of golf.

Use the mouse or the touchpad to hit the ball.

When it’s available, click the wall button to add walls to redirect the balls.

Toggle hints with the hint button!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Really pleasant, but a little problem on level 7, the buttons at the bottom overlap the place where I’m supposed to draw walls

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice sounds and an interesting take on mini putt.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I like this kind of puzzle, but i was surprised that you provided hints for every level. They make sense in the first levels, but not during the whole game. I know there’s a button to hide the hints, but once I know that hints exist, it sort of spoils the game for me. Instead of a fun puzzle, it’s a fill-in-the-blanks menial task.

Other than that, nicely done. The game was a bit blurry, you might want to fix the canvas scaling.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: very nice game, good physics and motion and easy but enjoyable

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: The game played well and I like the putting feel. Great work making it and getting it in!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I thought hints were on first levels only, to introduce features, but there was here except on last level, it took away the whole puzzle aspect. otherwise puzzles should have been fun I think. Good motion and effects

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A really fun game, some of the multi-holes required a lot of thought, and I was so glad for the hints to help work out the rebounds required to get the ball(s) into the hole(s). Almost like setting up a multi-hit bounce to a target video like you see on YouTube.

Well made, and slick graphics. I did miss the hint system when it wasn’t available on the 13th hole :)

Not sure how it fitted the 13 theme

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Fun concept! Seems like the puzzles required a lot of precision to execute properly without hints. Maybe if the walls were locked to a grid system, it would help the player find the solutions organically rather than relying on hints? Well polished either way

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: interesting game, good sound effects. I didn’t like that there were hints available for every hole, they at the least should have auto-shutoff after the introduction/first few holes.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Great game! I loved the sounds specially. Very interesting idea, worth exploring.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: What an interesting idea! The gameplay is intuitive, the graphics clean and polished and the minimal sounds fit the aesthetic. It would maybe be nice if the wall placement was grid based, so that things would always remain tidy on screen, but that’s a minor nitpick.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Nice casual game! This would deserve many more levels and could be a mobile hit!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Nice game! I see a lot of potential!

I think hints are on by default, but I would rather have them off by default. Though I’m not sure that wouldn’t make the game too difficult.

The toolbar also covered part of the screen where I would put walls in some cases.

Congrats on the submission!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Very cool! A golf game with multiple balls and the ability to create walls. Especially loved the sound effects. Could see this turning into a full mobile game. Nice job!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • The graphics. The vector art and the cat on 12 are really charming :)
    • The physics. I had many times close calls that ended up going in, which is very nice feeling.
  • I liked that you:
    • Had the tips. That’s nice if you are struggling too much and don’t want to be stuck on a level for too long.
    • Put a lot of attention to the quality of life features. The “Last play” strength and angle reminders made my day while playing the game – you managed to prevent one thing that would be a major pain otherwise.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • The levels the way they are were almost puzzles, and golf usually lets you play multiple shots in a single round. What I would say is that you could take a choice between it being a puzzle game – give the full visuals of where the balls will end, so I need to think how to hit the holes, not the skill to make it happen – or a golf game – letting me play as many times I need to reach the holes, which would free you to make larger levels and more complicated ones.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Very interesting game and fun to play with! Like the combination of puzzles and golf.

Maybe you could try to turn off the hint after level 2, making the game more challenging for players. You could again show the hint for level 5 when players first need to use the Wall.

api.name: Exilium: The Fall of Dominus
api.description:
The game is set in a dystopian future where an oppressive AI has taken control of the world, maintaining its dominance through 13 massive pylons that distribute power and control across the globe, a symbol of terror for all inhabitants. Each tower is heavily guarded by automated drones, mechanical beasts, and brainwashed human enforcers. The world is bleak, with humanity struggling under the iron grip of the AI, which calls itself “Dominus.”

The AI’s regime is known as the “Dominus Network,” a name that signifies its total control over all aspects of life. The Dominus Network’s primary objective is to ensure that no one can challenge its authority. It monitors all communications, controls all resources, and enforces its will through the power of the pylons, which keep humanity subdued.

The rebels, known as “The Vanguard,” are a determined group of freedom fighters who have united with the sole purpose of destroying the 13 pylons and bringing down the Dominus Network. They are a team of specialists—engineers, hackers, strategists, and soldiers—who work together to build units capable of withstanding the AI’s relentless defenses.

Controls

Desktop

  • Click on a Deploy Unit button when ready
  • Hover over the map to select a starting position
  • Click to deploy the unit

Mobile

  • Tap on a Deploy Unit button when ready
  • Tap and drag on the map to select a starting position
  • Release tap to deploy the unit

Description

I wanted to build an auto-RTS/clicker type game that’s easy to pickup but esculates quickly. I was interested in the concept of an RTS where the player can select the stats for each unit type like an RPG. The inspiration for this game comes from Paperclips (a great web clicker game, please search and try it if you haven’t) and C&C Tiberian Sun.

Tech details

I didn’t have too many bugs when building this, only fixing some unusual TypeScript issues with Closure. Object properties were being renamed which made Enums not work and some mapping I was doing with the stat names.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice game, I loved the idea of armies confrontation. Slow at start, at the end it becomes fast we have to play many actions, it’s fun! You have beautiful assets for units, why didn’t you reuse them in shapes? instead of having plain color shapes, it would have been much nicer!

comments.author: Harvey Seager
comments.text: I’m glad you enjoyed it. That’s a good suggestion about the icons in the shapes; I added the icons near the end of development and didn’t consider it. There might be less visual clarity using the icons for units especially for mobile where they are quite small, and I might have to change how damage is visualized but it would have been worth a try! I did consider adding other flat SVG icons for each unit to improve them, but ran out of time in the end. Thanks for the feedback

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Very nice, I’m fond of these games. The UI on the right was not great because you have to keep scrolling about to deploy units, then scroll about to get back to research. I think there is enough screen space to have more visible without scrolling too much.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Harvey Seager
comments.text: The overall UI was on the top of my list to improve but I ran out of time in the end. The need to scroll is definitely valid criticism and I could have used more of the screen space as you say, especially for desktop. Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Interesting game. I think you did a good job of your goal of something that escalated quickly. There were many times I just barely got a deploy out before the enemy crossed the line.

The scrolling between tech points and the unit upgrades was rough as it would prevent me from looking at the enemy, and there were times where I would have a tech point available but it wouldn’t let me move the slider. I did like that the unit range made the shape bigger, I thought that was pretty clever.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I liked how simple it was to figure things out. The pixel unit icons were great; perhaps the dev could have reused the icons instead of shapes.

The concept is easy to understand as you can see units coming in and you need to drop units to counter that. It’s semi-idle. I saw an auto deploy option, but I think I had to wait a bit longer for the auto deploy to kick in so I added units manually.

comments.author: Harvey Seager
comments.text: Good suggestion about the icons as the background for the shapes. The auto-deploy is slow to start with and the spawn speed can be upgraded. It was a pacing and balance decision to make it slow, I could have made the game longer and crazy auto-deploy battles into late game which does sound like fun, but I wanted players to see the damage to the towers early in the game and for there to be some strategy at the end in defeating the last couple of towers. Maybe next time! Thanks for playing

comments.author: Slava
comments.text: I’ve enjoyed playing the game and managed to finish it. The atmosphere is cool and the game process is captivating from the first few seconds. Unf. I didn’t manage to rate the sound since I didn’t enable it. But yeah awesome job!!

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: I had a lot of fun! It felt a bit easy for me and I’m not sure I ever understood the difference between skill and tech points, but played it until dominus was defeated!

comments.author: Harvey Seager
comments.text: Thanks for playing and the feedback, I’m glad you enjoyed it. At one point I renamed skill points to tech points and must have missed a reference… sorry about that!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Fun game! I could defeat Dominus!

Using different shapes for the different units was clear and easy-to-understand (although I do agree with another commenter that it would have been nice to use the cool pixel art you had for each unit, as well). Regardless, I thought the using border width to signify HP also worked well.

One minor nitpick– it wasn’t always clear if my unit would hit the tower. The units sometimes seemed to “jump” to hit the towers. Maybe it would be helpful if there were vertical guidelines leading to each tower..?

But yeah, fun game! Nice job

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Very cool concept and great work designing the concept. The game works well and its fun to build up new troops.

comments.author: sdeseille
comments.text:
I appreciated the concept of that game. It is simple but well designed. For my first play. I managed to reach level 20. So even if it’s an rts it is easy to understand game mechanics.

I like the moment I spend in that game.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: I enjoyed the game, the gameplay feels very good. It is a pity that the presentation is so raw. With some particles would had been more fun.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Good Tower defense style effort, I wished for more action/visual feedback during combat, and some sounds would have made this more enjoyable.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Looks simple at glance, but very addictive! Had small issues with scrolling on mobile, when center with configuration appears.

Congratulations on submission!

comments.author: Asiel Díaz Benítez
comments.text:
would be nice if in every level increase one of the pylons is destroyed

the controls are not good in mobile/touchscreen, often tapping in a place causes accidentally sending the unit in the place it appears, one must swipe/drag for the position to be updated before releasing

I liked the game overall

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: I played this game longer than I thought I would. I didn’t understand what to do in the beginning. I just aligned my troops against the enemy, and then realized it’s a battle. I like how the game progresses, and it feels just right. The graphics for the infantry, tank, airplane, and ion canon are good, and easy to understand/read. It would be nice to have similar graphics on the map itself, but I understand we only have 13kB. Some sfx and sound would also help elevate the game.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
We succeeded in returning freedom to humanity!

From the beginning, I did not immediately understand how to deploy my troops, but after a few tens of seconds I did (because I immediately started playing, instead of reading the description before the game).

I liked the story of the game. It was interesting to develop your combat potential, as well as to follow the enemy on the battlefield. You also need to follow the place of deployment of troops, somewhere for defense, and somewhere for attack.

I love how the towers gradually collapse - cool effect!

Loved the background too! It would be fun to have a moving background, moving clouds, the sun, something like a parallax effect for example :).

It was fun, congratulations!

P.S.

Command & Conquer: Tiberian Sun is my childhood game :).

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Interesting simplified tower defense game. I was so busy deploying my troops I missed the progress elements (the progress bar and the towers slowly collapsing). I would have liked that a bit more evenly, i.e. towers on both sides so it actually becomes ‘tower defense’ instead of ‘health bar’ defense.

I also felt the campaign was too long which made it feel like you did not make much progress. Perhaps start with one tower each and after you win a level towers are added.

Overall fun to play, could do with some sounds and effects.

Good work!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I won the game :) It was fun to play and very well made! I would have appreciated some punchy background music to fight all these enemies :) Well done!

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
Such an addictive game built out of a few simple elements. It shows that you don’t need a ton of content for a strategy game to be captivating and it is totally possible to squeeze that into 13k.

A little of feedback could improve the game immensely. And it doesn’t have to be anything fancy. Keep the simple shapes for the units, but add simple particle effects when they fight and die + maybe some sound effects. And the battles will be much more interesting to watch.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game for js13k!
    • Achieving your inspiration of paperclips, the right side of the game was indeed that experience, I had to remember to keep a look when a new research was done or skill points came in.
  • I liked that you:
    • Made the twist on the clicker game that added this RTS-like experience. I think it was balanced enough that it didn’t overwhelm me on the management of units and making sure nothing was hitting me.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • I think the core of the game is there. Now a bit of work on transporting me into the world of the Vanguard and Dominus would make me more invested. The starting and ending were extended text and I couldn’t relate much to it, I summarized it in my head as “there is a conflict”.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
First i was unhappy about my units just disapearing on top of the screen (unaware that they are destroying the infrastructure of the enemies)

But it turned out, that the game is pretty balanced. Nice core mechanic.

Music/Sfx would be great

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game looked pretty good, the graphics worked well, although the balls were a little simplified.

I liked the concept, and how the game played out.

For me the game pace was very slow, not sure if it was my PC or if the game was intentionally slowed down?

The right-side looked way more developed than the main game board.

All in all a fun but slow game.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: The game has an interesting and engaging concept, with fun core gameplay. The pace can feel a bit slow at times. The settings are really cool, and it would be nice to see the graphics show more of it. Adding music, sound effects, and more visual feedback could elevate the experience even further.

api.name: Steamshot 13
api.description:
A shooting gallery in a steampunk style.

The targets get faster and faster. Score points by hitting a green ball, lose points by hitting a red ball. You must shoot the black 13 balls to survive. Miss a 13 ball and it’s gameover. How long can you survive?

There’s a leaderboard so play with your friends, but only on the same device. Scoring uses localStorage.

This WebXR game is only playable on a VR headset. Use the trigger button on the controllers to shoot. The game uses ThreeJS and several tricks to create the environment within the 13k limit. No assets are used, everything is created at run time.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Judged from the video. Looks amazing.

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thank you. If you have a VR headset then you can play

comments.author: noam2223
comments.text: could not in the life of me found the key that starts the game

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: The button displaying a VR headset.

comments.author: Chui Hin Wah
comments.text:
Had quite some fun shooting at balls in VR. (Quest 3 browser)

bullets flying so slow, i have to pre-shoot to hit them. that’s what the 10, 50, 250 are for… nice.

i started playing it ipsc style, with 1 gun and another hand for stabilization, but i realized that the game gave me 2 guns and infinite bullet for a reason! just shoot them!!!!! i pre-shoot bullet rains to catch the 250, also shoot more bullet to guarantee the 13 ball to be hit. sometimes i need to make the hard decision to also hit a red ball in order to not miss a black ball. is this brilliant dilemma intended?

1 bug: when i hit restart, the score counter goes crazy. it starts more or less from my last score, and i thought it is just tweening too slow back to my real score, but sometimes, it counts up!! what happened??

anyways, before seeing that bug, i got 5xxx, after seeing that bug, i got 3xxx score. i ll rest the night and come at it again, to make a 5xxx score from a fresh page load.

also, the gun having no iron sight is killing me. well, with a projectile that slow, perhaps a sight won’t help that much. might as well shoot from hip.

had tons of fun, too bad i can’t link it with fear of 13.

oh nice audio. (almost mistaken the logo for a bowling game)

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: “The brilliant dilemma’ - is intended. Sorry about the bug. The 13 link is - miss a 13 and it’s gameover!

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Fantastic work with VR! I like that the balls also had a swivel. Great work getting a VR game together with only 13kb!

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thank you. Great fun to be involved. Js13k is a great idea from Andrzej. The 13k limit makes you use all sorts of tricks.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Impressive that it’s Three.js! (As opposed to a specifically VR-oriented thing like A-Frame.)

Good call on including the video in the README, too. :)

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thank you, Mark. I’m a fan of ThreeJS.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: That whole room looks amazing. light fittings, piping, paneling, superb guns! Overal great attention to details which makes this not only a fun game to play but visually one of the best here.

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thank you. I did spend most of the time creating the game working on making a good looking environment within the 13k limit.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: It almost feels like a 2 player game where my left brain/hand competes against my right brain/hand. It’s impressive that all the assets in this very polished bowling alley are programmatically generated.

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thanks. If you ever use ThreeJS then ExtrudeGeometry is useful. I created a path editor https://niklever.com/apps/threejs-path-editor/

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Nice game! I like the graphics, especially. Really good job on the textures and lighting - as others have said the environment is definitely a believable bowling alley type of space.

I see a nice direct link to the theme too! The sounds are a bit plinky and not really in tune with the environment quality, but given that you’re at 12.97kb, there really wasn’t much room to improve on this.

Had good fun indiscriminately blasting the greens, the reds and the 13s - congrats on submitting your game!

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thank you. Sounds are difficult with the 13k limit.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Overall the graphics are really great, the models are all very good, it looks like a realistic room you are in.

The gun models are really cool, was fun pew pewing at the targets!

Fun VR experience.

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Thank you. I was particularly happy with the guns.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Watching the video you put up, the game looks really fun and the environments very well made.

I like the way you’ve got some ball targets on bent wires so they swing around as they approach.

I wasn’t able to play without VR, I just got an error message, but will have to try it out

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game effectively covers the theme of preventing the number 13 from passing.

Gameplay: This is an easy-to-use shooter that becomes more challenging as the player progresses. The effects are well done, and the environment is visually appealing. However, it seems there is a fixed shooting pace that limits the player’s ability to shoot multiple times within one second.

Audio: The audio is nice, featuring sounds for shooting and game-over moments, though nothing particularly remarkable.

Innovation: It resembles a typical shooter.

Graphics: The graphics are good—no additional comments here.

Controls: The controls are straightforward and easy to use, with no issues to note.

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: The recoil delay scales with the game to scale the difficulty over time. Eventually you must use both guns to stay alive.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: Nice trick with the black 13.

Gameplay: Nice shooter and impressive you even have leaderboard with amazing UI, all of it in 13k.

Audio: Great audio working really well in this game.

Innovation: I am not even sure how much trickery you needed to make this all fit in 13k.

Graphics: Really polished graphics, impressive in 13k contest.

Controls: Smooth and intuitive.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Gameplay: The gameplay is great – intuitive and responsive. The dual wielding guns are visually compelling and recoil times are a nice addition to increase difficulty as targets spawn more quickly. The bullet path instead of raycast laser adds a nice timing element. Difficulty scales nicely as score and 13 “poison pill” targets increase and rotate. High score implementation hooked me into playing a few extra times to get higher scores.

Graphics: The visuals are surprisingly good given the author’s statement that no source assets are used. Many nice touches including glowing lights, polished wood textures and perfect reflections on spheres contributes to a warm and cohesive vibe. The scoreboard implementation is beautiful!

Controls: The control scheme is simple but refined – details like recoil time and moving bullet instead of raycast contribute to solid gameplay. The interplay between VR mode and browser mode for high score and game restart is well implemented.

Audio: Sound effects are simple but provided great responsiveness to firing and target hits.

Innovation: While the game is fundamentally a wave shooter on rails it has a unique retro bowling alley steampunk aesthetic and a nice, high-quality polish for a game under 13k.

Theme: The game does a great job of incorporating the core theme of 13 in its game mechanic and black “poison” ball.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: The graphics are really cool! I really like the material of the targets and the design of the guns. It would have benefitted a lot from more sound feedback. One thing that wasn’t clear to me is why sometimes the gun fires more slowly than I expected. I’m not sure if it’s an intentional delay mechanic or something else. But overall, it’s very well done, and without a doubt, the aesthetics are some of the best in this competition. I hope you can dedicate a few more hours to polish it up and release a final version that everyone can enjoy!

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Sound was a challenge having devoted so many of the bytes to the environment. 13k is both a great idea and such a difficult balancing act. Gun speed is entirely deliberate, it makes hitting balls in the outer lanes much more challenging.

title: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck
author: Michael M.

api.name: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck
api.description:
Lord Balsekil (do not look that name up in your Volapük dictionary, it might frighten you!) stole your seven purple lucky charms and hid them in his Lairs of Bad Luck. So fight him, his thirteen henchmen and your superstition to get them back!

The game is a Rogue-like, especially it is round-based, so take all the time you need for your next step. There are two modes: a short game and a normal game.
