comments.text: Very impressive graphics! I think a single lap would do given the lack of competitors, but was still a nice zen experience racing around each planet. Nice job!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Fun game, playerd it to end. Maybe could be made more fun if levels would be more complex. Maybe, kind of “open world” with obstacles and checkpoints, or scavenger race. Or there were some technical limitations for that?

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice game. It’s cool to drive on sphere! even if it doesn’t fit the theme. It was frustrating to reach the last level and immediatly lose and have to restart everything without retry option. Races should have been more variety, most of them are a bit simple and just circles

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: Great graphics, well done with the track on the sphere. I’m not sure if it just didn’t run well on my machine, but the controls were really hard for me - difficult to control speed/direction even with the easy rider.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Cool animations and love the intro screen. Should have a “press any key/space to start”. Controls are intuitive, but still good to have them on screen such as (left/right to select etc.)

Music is fun, great concept, as a car racing gamer however, I find this super trippy - playing racing game from top down on a sphere! I managed to get around on my second try. Love that each level is dynamic (ie. the tracks are randomly generated so you can’t just “learn” a track like normal racing).

Be cool to enable controller support for this one. Cool that you can choose different planets to go to and they each have separate environmental factors. Would also be good if you were able to change cars between planets instead of having to go back to the start.

Great work, fun game. Nice GitHub README.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
I remember seeing this one right at the start of the gamejam, glad to see the end result! The 3D sphere must have been a painful and complicated element, but it was well worth it and really makes the game stand out.

Also great work having such responsive and polished controls, the drift version of the car is certainly more challenging, but not too hard - and the most fun to drive! Great submission :D

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Letting the races happen on the spheres of “whole planets” is a genius idea, and because of the resulting rotation of the tracks the laps keep challenging.

Nice gameplay and graphis (and music)

SFX would be nice.

Btw.: i had much more fun, when i skipped some panets and the random difficulty-elements were added. Those races were really cool!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Love the mini planet and little car! The game is very forgiving for bad driving :D

Really enjoyed following the process of the game being built, enjoyed playing. Really nice concept, deserves a larger version!

comments.author: Fábio Santos
comments.text:
“Tofu Drifter”? I think I experienced deja vu!

I like the graphics and gameplay a lot. Driving the Tofu Drifter felt really good, even though I was only pressing A/D keys, and it was reasonably challenging to make sure I never hit a wall. I don’t think I succeeded.

The top-down, not-rotating style is not really for me, because I get confused with directions. But I recognize it’s a personal preference so I’m not taking away any points for it.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: racing on a sphere track is new well done

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
This was a very enjoyable game to play. Very polished 3D graphics with smooth rotation of the planet, and the controls felt very responsive, and the physics of hitting the fences were not annoying. The music was simple but very effective, only missing some sound effects.

The only things i can point out at this game is that the theme fit is a stretch, and the gameplay could use a bit more variety than changes in camera (although i loved how it looked in the night levels (although it doesn’t really make sense that a whole planet is in day time or night time 😅)).

I think you did really well overcomplicating yourself because the fact that the map is a sphere really gives game that extra wow effect.

edit: after playing some more, i got to see some extra level variations which does add a bit more to the gameplay.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Great game. It has so much potential. I love the way the globe is moving, and the steering feels good. I wish there were some more drift to the red car, because the tofu car had too much. But it wasn’t a problem. It’s always fascinating to me when I see 3D games executed so well, especially under 13kb

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Beautiful graphics! Love the spherical rendering, very pro level stuff. Would love to see this built out even more! Congrats

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: Nice graphics for sure! The game is impressive. I hope you continue working on it further, things like engine sound effect, making the car slow down on grass or when hitting the walls could be the icing on the cake!

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Really cool gameplay, it got me hooked and I wanted to beat the whole game! Nice graphics as well, would love a version with more content :)

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Great visuals, audio fits well, fun to play, what more could you want in 13Kb?

Very well done! Congratulations on one of the best entries of the year!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! It is always impressive to see complete games under 13kb, but the 3D ones are always a show off on how far we can go :)

Great work on graphics, sounds and the generated tracks. I tripped with the manner that the camera seems to slightly rotate on the view axis, making the tracks feel like they are morphing as you ride.

I liked that you created a context on where the races are happening, we need to fight against the time to escape. And heck, the escape is hard. I tried three times with the easy car and didn’t even make the first turn with the tofu rider on the rainbow road :) Also, shouldn’t half of the planet be night anyway? How there was planets fully on dark?? hehe. Great work on the night levels, they look amazing with the lights.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Thanks for the review! For the record the decision to fully light the planet was one of the hardest things to do! Showing both a night and sun scene at the same time on screen and make it look good without a more sophisticated render setup is incredibly hard. So between a more realistic setup and a nicer looking one I choose the second. And for the fully dark planets the sun was changed with a tiny black hole. And for everything else, a wizard did it.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: What a great looking game! Easy to play, clear goals, beautiful graphics. I do wish the theme were integrated into the gameplay somehow, but this is an impressive game for under 13k. Nice work!

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: It’s impressive to see such 3D visuals in just 13k, especially the night lighting and car lights. I really love the visual presentation! It would be even better if the car had manual control for drifting.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
2 times reached the last race and 2 times fell :).

From the beginning, I thought that it would be good to be able to continue the game from the last completed race, but then I read the description of the game and realized that instead there is a mechanic of jumping between races due to certain inconveniences, which is quite interesting and innovative! No less innovative - racing on a spinning planet, does that mean you think the planets are round? :D

I also liked the graphics, especially the night races and how you drive under the lights, you shine your headlights - quite fun!

I drove in an ordinary car, for drifting as you said - you have to try ;).

Of what I would like to have in addition is the ability to turn on/off the music, although the music is fun, it gets too noisy if you listen to it for too long with the headphones. It would also be nice to have a description of the controls in the game.

I love racing :). It was fun, congratulations!

api.name: Fresh Fruit Feeder
api.description:
🍎🍊🍌🍇🍈🍅🍆🥑

Welcome to..

Fresh Fruit Feeder!

🍎🍊🍌🍇🍈🍅🍆🥑

  • Drag fruit to face to feed! 😐
  • Bigger fruit #s = more points 🍉🍍🍓
  • Fill the happiness meter to go into a feeding frenzy! 🤪
  • Beware of 13s! 😖🤢🥴

comments.author: m-nakasato
comments.text: Moderate difficulty and comical theme! The BGM and “ugh” voice were great too!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Not bad, but I never gone to “red” side of bar. You should have improved difficulty with levels maybe (level 19 was same as 1)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Fun use of emoji. Always good to see the fresh fruit message. Nice and easy to work out how to play.

Hard to play on touchpad, I will have to try again with a mouse or touchscreen. But I did get to the frenzy with the character bouncing about going mad, but it didn’t last long before the game restarted.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Clever use of emojis, fun and comical

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Clever interpretation of the theme. Funny to watch the poop emoji pile up. After the 3rd round of “frenzy”, I was ready for the difficulty level to start increasing, maybe tetris style? Still, a very polished experience. Nice job!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
It took me a while to understand I should let fruits “ripen” by bouncing a few times. That wasn’t immediately obvious. After i got that, the pressure was on to not let any fruit spoil!

It’s a simple game, I would not say it’s super fun after the first frenzy. I missed something to keep me engaged. But I could imagine this to be a successful children’s game for sure.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Too addictive and Ive no Idea why! Made it to level 100!

Simple and fun, you nearly died so many times, just managed to stay alive and get the frenzy though. Could have kept going but felt 100 was far enough.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice use of emojis, was more fun on mobile than on the desktop.

Could be a good casual game app :-)

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Cute little game that leverages emojis for its interface. Very playable, with lots of fruit bouncing around the screen. I wasn’t sure what my overall goal or strategy should be, but the physics worked well and I enjoyed the music. Nice job!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: I played this game with a trackpad so it was quite hard to control. But other than that, it is a cute little game. I guess it was meant to be played on mobile, right? Thanks for adding some sounds and music. And thank you for submitting :-)

api.name: Cool dolphin
api.description:
Help the dolphin find the sea where there is no number 13.

Press UP and DOWN to swing the dolphin Press the SPACE bar to use the dash Press E to continue the dialogue

The game uses Crossing the Seas music from the Playstation The game uses the Seafoam color palette created by Jimison3

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Love the look and feel of the game, sounds really nice too. I found it way too hard though, never made it past 60%. Still enjoyed playing it for quite a while though just because the mechanics of controlling the dolphin felt really nice. Maybe if the dash made you invincible as well it would be a bit easier. Still had fun with it, good work!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: I liked the graphics and controls

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: I like the movement mechanics and with a little tweaking this could be a really fun game. But its just too difficult as it is now. You dont have enough time to adjust to incoming rocks and bubble vents. It took me many tries to even break 50% and I felt there was a lot of luck involved.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Fun game, nice graphics, a little difficult, hard to avoid the obstacles if your timing is not quite right.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A cute little game, fun to play and nice graphics.

Some of the obstacles were super tricky like a geyser appearing right before a mine, making it hard to jump.

The SPACE didn’t always dash, sometimes it just put a white border around the dolphin but didn’t make it any faster

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
What a cute little game. The swinging movement feels very smooth and natural, but it requires quite a lot of practice to get right. Because of the bubble which surrounds the dolphin while dashing, I first thought it would make me temporarily immortal—consequently I died a lot :)

The story is abstract in the best possible meaning of the word. Cool dolphins count to 20? Sure, why not! There’s a see without 13? Of course!

Nice work transforming “13” into a sad face. I also liked how you avoided having a separate tutorial or how-to-play level, and instead incorporated it into the main game.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text:
how to mute this ? no you cannot mute

the idea is innovative, the cutscene is showing effort spent on it , the randomness of the mines and towers are not too friendly tried dozzen times but mostly got impossible situations right away it kind of like fly dragon fly without the swoosh aint no realy controlling it as u wish.

the js13k cloud in main scene is kind of cool, drawing of main dolphin is very graphical idk if its a spritesheet or code generated but i liked it.

i checked the code, interesting idea yet I see your overall size is less than 7k witout any roadrolling or gc consider making things interesting more you have the space for it its not js7k

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Nice little game!

A bit hard in my opinion, I didn’t really manage to master the jumps. Maybe a little tutorial or slow motion start would have helped to completely grasp the controls. It goes really fast and I wasn’t sure what to do in the game :) (apart from dying lol)

Pretty cool submission though, the mechanics are somewhat addictive and you want to try again

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Difficult game, as the obstacles are generated at random, you may get a tricky situation anytime, including at the beginning.

The colors between obstacles and the sea are too close. Controls are very responsive though, and the music is nice.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not properly play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I press “E” the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A very cute infinite-runner style dolphin game. I found it to be tweaked too hard, and the constant up/down swim of the dolphin was difficult to deal with. I also wish the theme were integrated into the gameplay. But overall a lovely looking game with clear goals that even has music! Great job.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: The music is so lovely! I dig the vibe of this game. Finally a “flappy bird” that tries something different :)The game is quite hard but fun. I couldn’t reach the end after playing for quite some time, so I’m curious what the end looks like.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: Just like Flappy Bird, you’d need several tries to get a grip on the game. Easy to play, hard to master.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Arrrg, pretty difficult to play haha. I will keep trying! Great music!

api.name: Niwatori
api.description:
So you’ve found yourself lost in a maze with chickens running rampant?! Find your way to the exit and see what challenges await you in the next room.

Use your computer arrow keys (or WASD) to navigate. Push objects around to make a path for yourself. If you have ended up in a position that is impossible to get out of, hit R to reset the level.

And oh, be afraid of the small room with the 13 chickens! It looks impossible to escape, but you can get out unscathed if you are quick enough.

Naming

Niwatori (鶏) is the Japanese word for “chicken” (the animal, not poultry). I named my game using a Japanese word to pay homage to a game called Sokoban (倉庫番), which originates from Japan and plays similarly to Niwatori.

Inspiration

A game I made in college for a game development project course inspired Niwatori. I was unfamiliar with Sokoban until I started building Niwatori for js13k 2024. Perhaps I played a game as a child inspired by Sokoban, which inspired my college game and now Niwatori.

Special thanks

I want to shout out the following artists:

  • PetricakeGames and their Mystic Chroma! Dungeon art, which is where most of the sprites came from
  • Bagong Games and their Hana Caraka Animals Pack art, which is where the chicken came from
  • Kenney and their Tiny Dungeon art, which is where the mine cart came from

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Cute presentation.

I found annoying that I couldnt distinguish the pushable objects from the non-pushables. Having different icons would have helped.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Brilliant puzzle mechanic! Some levels are positively devious, I love it. Great art choices. Chickens!

This is the puzzle I wish I had invented.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Solid entry, quite challenging puzzles, got close but stumped on the first large level.

With more time could likely get through the bigger level, was fun to play. Nice graphics, cute chickens!

good job

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Very clever game. I need to come back to this one. The chicken/slime interaction is a super clever twist on the classic formula. Well done

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Fantastic game design and very difficult to master. It is a lovely puzzle game that stumped me a few times! Great work!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little game of pushing blocks. Having blocks that could only be pushed horizontally or vertically was an interesting twist on sokoban. Not sure what the chickens were doing, as they seemed like blocks, but could have moved more to change the dynamic.

The gameplay area was quite small on the screen, perhaps you could scale it to fit?

I got stuck on the level with the slime, but it was fun trying to get out.

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Fun sokoban game, I liked the interaction between different types of enemies + the unidirectional blocks. Definitely made for some puzzles that I haven’t seen before. Nice job!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Got stuck on a level with 7 chickens in the corner :) Nice puzzle game with cool graphics!

Missing some zoom on desktop and music (perhaps kontra size is limiting a lot). Congratulations on the submission, good job!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Intresting game, good art style it would be better to have it bigger at least for full hd screens

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Great twist on the sokoban theme. Interesting levels. Only nitpick is that pixels have variable width on my screen, making graphics a bit “cheap”. devicePixelRatio is usually to blame.

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text: A really solid puzzle entry, I liked the idea of the carts being only pushable on one axis though it took a while to figure that out. Adding a 2-3 tutorial levels with one sentence of hint below it would be quite useful I think, or on an intro screen, showing and explaining the elements. But overall I really enjoyed it, great work!

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text:
This looks so lovely! But the puzzles get really hard quickly! ;) Well done!

PS: I remember the original Sokoban the 80s! This is a really worthy homage!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice puzzle game and chicken art. But sometime it was a pain to know what I could push or not. In first levels I was able to push everything, not after, and sometime it’s frustrating when I try a path and it’s locked by design.

comments.author: Ben Gloger
comments.text: So much fun and quirky! Love using this as a break when working.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game is really cute. I wish it was a bit bigger though? I know some times pixel art looks weird in some sizes, but i personally prefer when games use the whole screen.

While the art is quite lovely, some animations need a little polish in the transitions. The game also looks pretty static. I was expecting chickens to move around a bit to give the game a bit of life. Also, if the player is most of the time facing forward, why is the sprite always looking back?

The puzzles are interesting and get pretty challenging (although i didn’t finish them all yet), and I found the difficulty progression to be very good. The first level is dead simple, and you introduce the elements one by one.

I missed some music and sound effects.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Solid Sokoban/Rush Hour mashup with chicken sprinkled on top!

Nice puzzles! Music/SFX and some graphical effects would be a nice addition

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
This is a nice looking and easy to play variation on the classic Sokoban theme. Some things can be pushed, some only in one specific direction, and of course there are chickens and slimes in the way. But hey, all is good, as you can push them too! The objective? Reach the exit.

Graphically it’s super cute with a good use of sprites, but would benefit from scaling to the browser window size IMHO, because at the size it’s rendered at, it’s quite hard to see which items slide in which direction, and a mis-slide can cause a carefully planned level to need restarting.

The addition of some music or fx would really elevate it, too. I’m not entirely sure this is on-theme, but hey, it has 13 chickens in it. So for that alone, I’ll give it a (small) pass :)

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
I love Sokoban games and all push & move puzzles, and Niwatory perfectly gives the feeling of this kind of games.

The thing I liked best is the different behavior of the various elements, which include carts moving horizontally and vertically, baby chicks and amoebas.

Good game, and I really appreciated you listed the artists who provided you the graphics.

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This is a fun little twist on standard Sokoban, good work. I love the colour palette too.

api.name: Superstitious Story
api.description:
Balancing the aspects of life is not easy. You need luck, wisdom, money and love. Knowing superstitions well can help to plan your actions correctly.

Gameplay

  • Read the card and chose an action from the top of the card.
  • Swipe the cards left or right to make your decision.
  • If you swipe the card slowly the changing aspects getting bigger on the top bar.
  • Plan your actions and don’t let the aspects fade or shine.
  • Try to survive more days and unlock all 8 endings.

Good luck!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Interesting game. The music was nice and didn’t feel repetitive. Graphic style of the cards was a nice touch.

I didn’t understand what determined if the card faded or shone. I understood which of the 4 icons would be affected, but just not which direction the change would apply. A lot of the actions also affected the same icon, so swiping left or right didn’t matter a lot of times.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Quirky little experience. I wasn’t really sure I was in control of my destiny, but i guess i still got married and rich so I must have done something right! Nice graphics and lovely music and sound effects.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Interesting idea, but the rules are confusing.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I think I understand the rules; I’m making decisions based on if I believe the superstitions or not and finding out if they impact me. I imagine there are scores for each card and the decision I make.

I managed to get rid of “13” on day 5; played through several times; 14 days was my longest run.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text:
I got married on day 20! :D Must have been my lucky day ;)

Quite a creative interpretation of the theme! I really liked it! Well done!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: 37 days at maximum :) Huge inspiration of “Reigns” I supposed (my game “Tindeath” 2 years ago was half inspired by this game too) Cool music and game feel!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Decisions, decisions. Luck seems a bit random.

More of a curiosity than a game. Gfx could be improved, audio is nice.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: After playing it a couple times, I think I finally “get” it (albeit I don’t fully understand all the logic underneath), and I have to say that I quite like it. It’s a very creative approach to the theme and it feels interesting to “play”. I wish to see more games like this! :)

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Well done!

I think you’ve mastered very well the introduction to the game! Music coming as soon as you swipe is great. The art is simple yet effective.

I’m not sure I really understood how the game works, but ended up winning the lottery :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Sweet little game

Easy to pick up, but I was confused as to which way to swipe indicated which choice?

The music was fun and graphics were well done

Got married on day 18

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: the different choices form a lovely poem and took me on a mental adventure that I really appreciated! it made me feel thoughtful and wistful. afer a while i started to feel like I was swiping opposite to my intention, since the touch starts on the thing you do not want, and it raises upward and almost seems to overtake the yes option, which folds away downwards. I could see this being intentional, a way for us to suddenly question our entire life decisions, or a clarity issue that could be removed with more visual feedback confirming which option you just chose. by the end, I was completely unsure about everything!!!! which is a good thing. wonderful work.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Fantastic moody music, perfect graphics, fun interactions, this game is brilliant!

I did several runs on pure role-playing (without looking at the aspects) and it’s just so satisfying to lead the character somewhere.

Great work!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
For the first time, I think I received some kind of curse :).

Married on the 9th day.

Won the lottery on the 24th and 20th day.

Retired on day 16.

I liked that the game has a help where you can familiarize yourself with the main points.

I also like the idea itself, as well as its implementation. Everything is quite atmospheric. Good job!

I also like music, but would like to have an option to turn it on/off.

Perhaps certain animations that would be played upon receiving the result would add more coolness to the visual part, although here you have to think about how you can beat it while respecting the existing style :). Maybe a black cat that walks all over the screen - it would be very cool in my opinion :).

Nice game, it was fun, congratulations!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This was really fun, I love that you took the Reigns-style swiping and factors system and pivoted it to the theme so well. It would have been nice to have keyboard inputs for the swiping, but not necessary.

api.name: Flee
api.description: Use the mouse to control the character’s movement. Avoid monsters marked with a red “13.” The character’s face will show if monsters are nearby. Find the treasure and flee.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Lovely start screen and introduction. Wasn’t clear at the start if it was mouse or keyboard controls (adding support for both would be great).

Should have the first level as smaller, and easier to get the hang on the game. I played five times and still couldn’t find the treasure that I was supposed to find. Some looked like they should be treasure, but nothing happened. Good music and sound effects.

Nice work on your GitHub README. Love all the emojis.

comments.author: Sonny Qin
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. I may make some misleading information about the game rules. The treasure is only located at the end position of the level 3. (End pos is always at the bottom-right corner) And the background of the game shows some objects are Epiphysis by the 13 god, some are not. That’s why some monsters seem not moving. This may be a misleading setting. I have noticed about the difficulty, I played several times and finally passed level 2. (Level one is about 2020 in size, level 2 3030 in size) It seems need to have some skills of control and tactic of solving maze to better pass the level. I may reduce the difficulty of the beginning and may shrink the size of fog in level 2 and 3.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The emoji’s were quirky and the music was good.

Maybe a life-bar or power ups of some sort, could all to the maze exploration.

What was the ultimate goal of the game? I went around the maze as my character followed my mouse cursor, but didn’t know what or where I should have been aiming for other than avoiding the dangers.

comments.author: Sonny Qin
comments.text: The aim of this game is to reach the yellow sqaure which always at the bottom right corner.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: After several attemps, I can’t find the treasure room. Level 1 seems really huge, I don’t know how to beat it. Are there other levels bigger than that? Level generation is nice (is it fixed or precedural?) Reminds me a part of my 2018 entry “Alone in darkness” with a small maze and creatures attracted by player light once revealed :)

comments.author: Sonny Qin
comments.text: There are three levels with increasing size. I am quite regretful to set up maze so big like this. The maze and the monsters are generated randomly. The end of each level is always at the bottom right corner. I will post easier and newer version after the competition.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Fun use of emojis. I liked that character and music changed based on the players state, though I couldn’t figure out what being cold or hot meant or how I become such.

I couldn’t find my way out of the maze. Level 1 seems huge. Maybe some sort of clue or minimap would have helped navigate it. Also the number 13 that shows up on the enemies was very hard to see so made it hard to distinguish who was the enemy between a group of emojis.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I wasn’t able to find the treasure. I tried twice, and spent a long time moving around, but eventually got chased by several monsters.

The game has some potential. It was causing me some legit distress and fear because of the random non-demonic emoji scattered around (which is good!). The different music changes were well executed.

I would make the movement speed much faster, as it’s frustratingly slow as it stands.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
A twist on a classic maze, but at that zoom level, your only option to find the exit requires testing most branches (or following a wall).

Background music is a good indicator of the looming dangers, switching to a gloomy melody when they are too close. Unfortunately, I could not find a way through when they are between my character and the exit. And still didn’t figure out what the cold and hot symbol mean.

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
I love the way how the music transitions to indicate the upcoming danger. Changing emojis also provides good feedback.

I would reduce the camera zoom to increase situational awareness - right now it is nearly impossible to plan your route. Also, some additional feedback like camera shakes or particles could spice it up a little.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Cool music transition!

I my Linux box, some emojis didn’t work. By reading the README, it seem they are the tired, relief and “player win the game” emojis.

Seems also that with a little more work, you could also support mobile ;)

Congrats on the submission!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: It’s nice to see an entry with an interesting story to tell, and this one certainly has that. In playing it, the smiley emoji will seek to the mouse pointer and that’s your cue to navigate around the seemingly massive maze in order to find the exit. As you traverse you’ll encounter other emoji, some nicely animated (I especially liked the moon transition), some static and some downright scary. The way the music (and your face) changes when those are close is simply great. In short I feel like the zoom level made this more of a challenge than the challenge of the game itself. It’s so close you can’t really plan to solve the maze, you just have to wall-follow. As such, I sadly never made it to level 3 and the treasure.

api.name: Guess My Triski
api.description:
Guess My Triski, “Triski” inspired by this year’s game jam’s theme, is an engaging and challenging guessing game where players must solve puzzles by uncovering hidden numbers on a game board. The ultimate goal is to find the elusive Number 13 among a series of randomly generated numbers. Each level presents a grid of circles, each hiding a unique number. Players click on these circles to reveal the numbers, hoping to uncover the Number 13 before running out of guesses or, in competitive mode, before the AI does.

The game features multiple modes:

  • Fun Mode: Play at your own pace with no time or guess limits.
  • Challenge Mode: Race against the clock and a limited number of guesses to find the Number 13.
  • Competitive Mode: Test your skills against an AI opponent, with both the player and the AI taking turns to guess the hidden numbers.

This is a point-and-click game, however, when in “Competitive” mode, this becomes a turn-based game.

NOTE FROM THE DEVELOPER: PLEASE Refresh your browser after playing in “Challenge” mode. There is an annoying bug that seems to creep up and I’m yet to fix it.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Somehow found the number 13 on my first go, then on my second it took 13 guesses - both seemed quite coincidental :)

The turn based version of the game adds another element which is fun.

Not sure what the 3 different levels related to, as they just appeared to be different sized diamonds?

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: A game of chance, I suppose. Yes, the levels were meant to be as you’d expect from Easy to Difficult. I just wanted another name for it instead of the obvious. Unfortunately it was all I had time for. Wanted to implement the Star puzzle as well, but I decided to do that post game jam.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: I like that you’ve provide different modes for players! Currently, it seems like the number 13 appears quite randomly, which can make it possible to find it quickly. Adding some puzzle elements, like having the number 13 change positions or other dynamic features, could make the gameplay more engaging and enjoyable!

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: Thank you for your feedback. It was the quickest idea I could come up with. I do intend to improve on this game.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Ah, this is very deceiving! You should move the “Story” button up to the top. On my first playthrough, I assumed that the numbers were random. After learning about “figurate numbers”, I realized there is a pattern and you can use strategy. I love a game that teaches me something! Nice job!

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text:
Oh, sorry, was not my intention to deceive. The story was added on after the game’s implementation.
Thanks, yes, I do intend on making more educational games.

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text:
A really nice number theory based game! I would have loved to see all the remaining dots revealed once I find 13, just so I could see how correct my guesses were.

I think there’s quite a lot of potential with this mechanic, like making the player try to find multiple numbers that follow a pattern, while avoiding others, kind of like minesweeper.

Nice game!

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: Noted! Something I’ll be considering in my later versions. Yes, a friend of mine did say it looks like a minesweeper game, I hadn’t thought of that, which gives me ideas for more variations.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Congrats on the submission and creating an innovative puzzle. However, the game is purely luck based, so there’s not much of a game element to it. Unless there was some secret clue that i missed.

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: Just a game of luck, for this jam.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Good job on the game submission. The different modes were interesting.

I was hoping the game would have some sort of logic or puzzle behind finding the number 13, but it seems that the number is just randomly hidden and the numbers around it are also random. Without any sort of clue or puzzle the game just boils down to randomly clicking until you find it.

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: What can I say, it’s my first try after 6 years of my last game submission. I wanted to do puzzle games but obviously I have much more to learn.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Is it only a guessing game – or a clickfest in challenge mode ?

At some point I noticed a knight-move pattern between successive numbers and other reviewers suggested a hidden pattern to guess. Then I checked the code to discover it is truly random.

The background story is interesting, could have led to a richer game. For further developments maybe ? Also, the difficulty menu is buggy, the options are displayed on the bottom left – offscreen.

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: Yes, that was not the only bug. So, this game is meant to be an add-on to my maze game of which I hadn’t had time to complete. However, that is something I do intend on improving and finalizing.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Nice game!

The graphics are simple yet good enough for a static game like this. The concept is very interesting, never saw it before! It does feel a bit random, but I guess some people will like it more than others!

A little tutorial in-game would be cool I think, just a small reminder “here’s what you need to do” for those who jump directly into the game.

Well done!

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: Thank you. Will definitely consider these suggestions.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
The clean UI looks fine.

I would have loved to get hints to the hidden 13, not only luck based clicking. But good job on completing your game and keeping it below 13 kb (7 kb, in fact! )

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: The game itself is very interesting, it reminds me of the Mines game computers use to have. Neat project!

api.name: Treasure-Thirteen
api.description:
Treasure -Thirteen : The Hidden Treasure Game ainspired by Minesweeper. Players navigate a grid to uncover treasures while avoiding obstacles. Each treasure found grants extra lives, and obstacles reduce lives if clicked.

Gameplay:

Click Tiles: Click on tiles to uncover either treasures or obstacles. Safe tiles may reveal empty spaces. Flag Tiles: Use the “Enable/Disable Flag” button to mark suspected obstacles with flags. Find Treasures: Locate hidden treasures on the grid. Each treasure you find grants you an extra life. Avoid Obstacles: Avoid clicking on tiles with obstacles. 1> Samll red bomb reduce your life 2> Bigger mines result in Game over Game Over: The game ends when you run out of lives or if you clear all non-obstacle tiles and find all treasures.

Restart the Game: Click the “Restart Game” button to start a new game.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: thanks for joining js13k! great work finishing a game in such a small size. it worked perfectly, and I was able to explore the map and find some treasure 💰 before getting blown to smithereens! 💣💥 who went and buried treasure in a minefield anyways, I need to find a safer job

comments.author: RabdeepKaur
comments.text: Thanks for trying out my game. I am glad you liked it. It was fun trying to fit a game in such a small size; by fun, I mean scraching my brain and trying to figure out how to make it 13 KB, but at last I did it, not perfectly, but I still did. And if you find a safe job do let me in your secert organization

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Not sure if the game was working properly for me or not. I expected there to be numbers on the squares that are next to bombs to represent how many bombs the square touches. No numbers were shown on squares so the game mostly played as a hope my random click didn’t reveal a bomb, but no way to know ahead of time. It made it so the flags didn’t really do anything either as I had no way to know if a square would be a bomb or not. Tried in Firefox and Chrome on a mac.

comments.author: RabdeepKaur
comments.text: Thanks so much for pointing that out! It sounds like there may be an issue with the logic. I’ll definitely work on improving that part of the game so it’s more intuitive and fun.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Interesting puzzle. However, unlike minesweeper, there doesn’t seem to be a skill involved, as all the tiles appear to be random. As far as I could tell, there’s no way to predict where the bombs would be, so I don’t know how to avoid them and use the flags. It’s pure chance.

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Congrats on releasing the game. I didn’t quite understand the purpose of the flags, since it seems like the tiles are placed randomly and there’s no indication of potential bombs nearby. Or maybe am I missing something! Adding a few sounds could have been nice when triggering a bomb or losing a life.

GG for the work you I have put into the game!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I used to love playing minesweeper so was keen to try your game.

I like that you’ve got a 13 mine target and a concept of lives so that the game doesn’t end immediately when you uncover a mine.

In traditional minesweeper clicking a blank square would reveal all neighbouring blank squares giving some hope of flagging possible mines and avoiding them.

So in this game I didn’t see the point in marking something with a flag that you had no idea what was contained underneath as there were no cues.

Perhaps having right click to toggle flags would be better than having a button you need to keep going back to for toggling.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: The game is really random, when discovering an empty space I don’t know if there are treasures of mines nearby having that indication should be a great improvement Flags could be a right click (and disable “contextmenu”) instead of toggle a button You have a lot of space left, I’m sure you can make an director’s cut version still in 13kb constraint, maybe even add a bit of CSS too for responsive grid and colors :)

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Minesweeper but without the numbers helping for proximity detection ?

To me, this is nothing more than clicking at random on the grid, trying to reveal yellow squares before red ones. Or did I misunderstand something ?

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
The game is a bit too simple and based on luck, a small strategic component should be added and it should be made so that you cannot keep losing lives - or gaining lives - by clicking on bombs or treasure bags that have already been discovered.

I increased my lives to 100 by always clicking on the same treasure bag, and finishing the game became super easy.

There is some work to be done.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month :)

Great work on having a complete working game for js13k! Even more in your case with 3kb :)

I noticed that you tried to make a twist on the minesweeper game but I think it didn’t work very well. It can help sometimes to use a game mechanic and remove things from it, but here I think it took too much. The issues is that there is no way to learn how to play it. Basing your gameplay exclusively on luck won’t cut for a small game.

The added lives idea did add something to the minesweeper game, so what if you kept it, and make it such that you had to treasure hunt in a minefield? Keeping the numbers of minesweeper to indicate where mines are, and then adding a second row of numbers where you indicate where treasures are – now there are two things to look out for, and one of them you need to risk uncovering.

api.name: Wands of Triskaidekai
api.description:
🪄 😊

Once upon a time, there is a wizard, Trika, who is a freshman in the Deka Academy.

He does not perform well in his class and all his classmates, even his teachers, are all teasing him about how bad and unfit he is.

One day, he wandered into the Lost Woods and found a mysterious wand.

He picked that up, wishing to find its original owner.

However unfortunate starts to befall him…..

Game Play

Use ⬅️ ➡️ to move sideways. Use “C” to shoot fireballs to move spikeballs. Spikeballs can hurt Trika now. Use ⬆️ to jump

comments.author: Aleksander Szczepura
comments.text: I reached the door at the bottom and nothing seems to happen for me (I have the key). Spawning spikes by walking is an interesting idea, but IMO it hurts the gameplay the way it’s working now, I found myself just walking left and right for the most of the game. Graphics are looking good tho.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
Looks like a cute little game with a few wee bugs to be ironed out.

Don’t get the key until you have the door cleared. If you die with the key, the game forgets you had the key and you can’t open the door. So clear the door, get the key, and run like heck!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: And also try not to fall into 1 column of pits as it would soft-lock the game as well.

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: I like the graphics, and the basic mechanics are pretty solid. I kept until up falling down a narrow hole, and then I can’t get out, can’t even take steps to summon the spike.

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
The particle systems for me felt a bit overkill

I’m unsure how this game matches the theme

Physics engine is pretty cool, and so is the graphics

Nice job

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: It’s a nice game. The first time I played it, I remember that it was not so zoomed out, and I think it was better before. I like very much the particle effect. What I have noticed is that when I shoot, it happens from multiple positions, not just the player’s.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to shoot the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: Thanks. I will keep this in mind.

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: I am not sure what the intention of this game is. I got the key and dug to the door, but nothing happened. The bouncing spike sphere is fun when it destroys blocks but impossible it is impossible to avoid.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: This is an interesting one! On my first 2 playthroughs, I kept getting stuck in a single width well, with nowhere to walk. On my 3rd and final playthrough, I tried a breadth-first-search approach, and tried to stay on top as long as I could. Finally, after about 40 spawned spikes, I hit bottom. After 50 spawned spikes, I got to the door, but it didn’t open. After 60 spikes, I had full cleared all bricks around the door. I admire the exploration of new ideas, and new mechanics. I would love to watch a developer playthrough of this, to see how the experts do it. Nice pixel art, nice sound effects. Congrats on submitting!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I like the artstyle.

It might be jsut a bug, but my deceased wizard-incarnations all are shooting fireballs, which makes a wonderful particle-effect-firework :-)

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: fortunately, I could play the game: as I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser but disabled it so i could play and I also dont want to search as I type whilst playing a game. Got to the door but it wouldnt open then i read the comments on how to do it but got stuck. Really want to see whats behind it so i will persevere! 👨‍🚒

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A neat little platformer.

Cute graphics, the background stars and the fireballs look really good.

I so wanted to break blocks with my wand to dig lower, but alas, only exploding spike ball did it.

What was the ultimate prize, what was at the bottom of the tower than I wanted to much?

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text: Love the art style and the particle effects, nice unique mechanics too! I especially like the mechanic where old Trikas stick around and shoot fireballs, adds room for lots of planning on where to spawn the spikes.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Okay so it turns out I never left an actual review for the game, despite discussing it at length on Slack, so here goes.

I think there’s a lot of potential in the “platformer but the world is entirely destructible” formula. It’s just so satisfying. Many of the games which have this mechanic focus on resource extraction a lot (e.g. SteamWorld Dig), so making the very act of vertical movement the goal is a nice differentiator.

How you control the spike bombs by nudging them in a direction is also interesting, it’s a bit of a Breakout vibe in that you’re never fully in control, making it all the more exciting when a good hit clears a large chunk of the level.

Another mechanic I’d just love to see explored more in general is how your corpses still have wands, which all fire in unison. This strangely makes the game world more cohesive to me, more believable. Corpses aren’t just some abstraction, they’re still instances of the player character. Such an interesting touch, if maybe accidental, and very much underrepresented in games.

All in all, I really want to see more games from you in the future. :)

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: My intention is to make it bounce forever but in LittleJS it seems to always damp for some reason. The firing bodies is beautiful accident that I forgot to switch off all the input checking when the player is dead. As too many bodies will impact the game performances, in the post compo, I would like make use of them and then despawn them after a certain number of bodies are on the overworld.

api.name: SUP3RFR1GHT VR
api.description:
Confront some of the 13 meanest geometric shapes you’ve ever met in this SUPERHOT VR clone made with AFRAME.

Controls

  • Time moves only when you move your hands, head or body.
  • Press the Grip button to grab objects.
  • Press the Trigger button to fire guns or punch with your fists.

Only playable with 6DoF VR headsets (Quest 1/2/3, Vive, Rift?)

NOTE: When hit by an enemy, the RETRY portal will reload the page. Unfortunately, since the game is embedded into an iframe of the js13k site, this will exit the VR session and force you to re-enter VR mode.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Such a fantastic game! The demake is very well done. Would love to play a (much) longer version. 😍

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Couldn’t play it as it’s VR but seems amazing

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Best gameplay of the competition if it wasn’t so short. It feels like tutorial instead of the game. This deserves a much longer game!

Great aesthetics, not by accident reminiscent of Super Hot, which fit wel with the game play.

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
Any chance us actual* reality plebs could get a gameplay video so that we can ponder the advantages of getting non-actual reality gear?

* Presumably, but the debate is open.

Edit: Awesome! Thank you. Now I can see why others would love to see a longer version.

Obviously I can’t speak about the gameplay, but it looks smooth and fun. I imagine smoothness as crucial to VR experiences, more so than “fidelity” since immersion is key. Love the minimalistic aesthetics as well. I’m sure it all blends neatly together in VR.

If you do decide to expand on it, my suggestion would be to continue building on that Superhot inspiration where gameplay is concerned (it’s a fun game after all), but go into a slightly more… supernatural direction. with slightly less gunplay/weapon/object use/cover, but a bit more active movement, dodging and “bullet time” powers instead.

That said, I am not sure how easy/fluid it is to perform several things at once in VR.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: It is done! The video was uploaded on YouTube and added to the game page.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Love the style of the game, you really need VR to appreciate the environment!

Its fun to play; you could keep adding more levels!

Enjoyed watching your progress through out the jam, the retry did confuse me but then I didnt read the intro text!

Clever how you have to move for time to progress, good job overall!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Looks really amazing

I tried playing without VR and didn’t get very far.

But watching the video you posted it does look really well polished and fun to play.

I just need to get a VR setup so I can try it out

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game effectively highlights its main theme by introducing 13 different geometric shapes that the player must destroy.

Gameplay: The gameplay is enjoyable, making great use of hand controls and allowing for varied interactions with different objects through punching and shooting. I believe adding new levels could further enhance the game, making it a worthy showcase.

Audio: The audio is well-executed and complements the experience nicely.

Innovation: It’s impressive to see such a polished game created with just 13KB of code.

Graphics: The graphics are simple yet appealing—no complaints here.

Controls: The controls are excellent, providing a smooth and responsive experience.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: Great fit to the theme.

Gameplay: All smooth and polished. I particularly enjoyed the variety of moves available throughout the game.

Audio: The audio felt just about right for the game.

Innovation: Most of the mechanics are copied from “superhot vr” as you mentioned but on the bright side, you did an amazing job in just 13k !!!

Graphics: Simple yet efficient and greatly reminding superhot !!!

Controls: Enjoyable, smooth and intuitive.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Gameplay: The core gameplay loop is both immersive and enjoyable, fully leveraging the capabilities of VR and stretching expectations for WebXR. The game is immediately successful in getting the player physically moving around their space and feeling inside the game. Left me wanting more levels and thinking about the use of mixed reality in future level design.

Graphics: While minimalistic, the visuals are nicely designed. The game shows impressive technical achievement hidden behind a minimalistic interface with clean, functional aesthetic that serves the gameplay well. The simplicity but effectiveness of bullets and trails combined with the unique geometry “boss” is nicely done.

Controls: The controls are crisp and use grab and trigger actions in intuitive manners with high responsiveness and accuracy. Instructions for control use is clear and concise.

Theme: A crisp implementation of “Super Hot” style gameplay on WebXR in <13kb which is a blast to play, but may not have the most direct connection to the proposed contest theme.

Audio: The sound design complements the gameplay effectively without being distracting, fitting well with the minimalist visual style of a Super Hot-inspired game.

Innovation: Adapting this style of gameplay to WebXR within tight size constraints is impressive. However the game style itself is of course heavily inspired by existing games and may not the most innovative.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: My favorite game of the competition, a very good adaptation of the wonderful SUPERHOT. The mechanics are really well done, the game feels very fun, and the Matrix-like slow-motion feeling when the bullets come at you is fantastic. Great job!

api.name: SECTOR 13
api.description:
Commander, it is all up to us!

Thirteen sectors and untold legions of enemy ships…

All that stands between us and our final victory…

We are scared, but our fear makes us strong!

We are scared, but we have faith in your abilities!

We are scared, but we will emerge victorious!

About

SECTOR 13 is a hectic space shooter built for Mobile and Desktop devices.

Touch/Click and drag to move your ship.

Technical Details / Spoilers

Expand The Section

Fear Meter

  • The more fear, the faster you shoot, the more powerups you find
  • Meter grows when enemies escape, when you progress sectors, when you die
  • Meter shrinks when enemies die and you get powerups

Death

  • All powerups lost
  • Score multiplier reset
  • Invulnerability for ~2 seconds

Score

  • Different enemies worth different point values
  • Collecting powerups you already have adds .5x to score multiplier

Powerups

  • Rate of Fire Increase - faster firing main gun
  • Extra Guns - 2 on the wing tips
  • Diagonal Guns - 2 from the ships nose
  • Bomb - seeks enemies, detonates if target destroyed before impact
  • Extra Bomb - 2 more enemy seekers
  • Shield - Absorbs 4 projectiles/collisions, will not spawn if player has shield
  • Extra Life - Max 3

Enemies

  • Blue #1 - 20pts - shoots lasers
  • Blue #2 -40pts - shoots bullets at player
  • Green - 60pts - chonky shield guy
  • Pink - 80pts - fast moving diver
  • Yellow #1 - 100pts - hides in corners, shoots diagonally
  • Yellow #2 - 10pts - not very exciting

Strategy

  • Don’t die
  • If you die, use invulnerability to pick up any powerups on screen
  • If shield is low, take the final hit in a lull to find a new shield

Shoutouts

  • js13k starter / codyebberson
  • Kontra.js / straker
  • zzfx/M / keithclark

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: A very well executed classic game with good sounds and music, although there are perhaps too many explosions that obscure the music. I am jealous that I was not able to implement these movement patterns in my airplane game.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Simple shooting fun, great effort!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Luke Nickerson
comments.text: This is a solid shmup: lots of things to dodge, lots of enemies, power-ups, and steady ramp-up of the difficulty. I made it to level 11 on my first go. I can’t give this high marks for innovation, but definitely high marks for gameplay and sound.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Woah, this is so fun. I got to level 12. I’ll come back and try to beat it later. Really nicely done!

Just for some nitpicky suggestions, I think the “explosion” sound when an enemy is destroyed is a bit loud? It makes the music and other sound effects a bit hard to hear. Also, I found the font a bit hard to read (even though it is cool and stylized in its own way).

But yeah, really fun game. Awesome job

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
This game is an absolute banger! I want to play it all day! I loved everything about it.

In the spirit of improvement, having to keep holding the left move button to move is really tiring. I can see how it makes sense in mobile, but on desktop not so much. Either WASD or just listen to mousemove without the click.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I really like this game - it is quite fun. I cleared the game in my first try, but there were a few critical moments, where I was left without shield and probably one hit away - a powerup was always on hand. The thing I’m most impressed is the pixel art - more precisely the colors, in such bullet hell everything is clear and that’s very hard to achieve. Well done!

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: Had a blast, literally! Good game!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Love it! Nice sense of progression, a bit to easy but enjoyable!

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: Great sounds and gameplay! It feels like something I would definitely play on a retro console!

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Great use of music and sound effects. Fit your game nicely, loving the 8-bit graphics. The text is a little hard to read in the smaller font sizes (for example on the home page, I had to read it three times before I read the word “Hints”).

Really fun game! I found myself playing a few different rounds. Nice take on the Galactica game. Would be cool to include a few more elements around the theme; ie. you could have special ships that have the number 13 on them, maybe these are special power ups, or maybe a difficult enemy. The use of click and drag was interesting; I really enjoyed it. Maybe add some keyboard capabilities for further accessibility.

Nice work putting instructions into your GitHub README. Also like how you built on a starter package and mentioned that in your README.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thats a solid shmup!

Not much to complaint about, i like the pixel-art, and that the bullet-hell is always clear to see and dodge.

Great entry!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the game and graphics.

It was great to have the movement tied to the pointer, so it was snappy and quick to avoid the enemy fire.

I got as far as sector 11 where things got a lot trickier.

More of a mobile game that works on desktop than a desktop game, due to portrait screen setup.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Funny game, I could reach the end (level 12 was the hardest I think). Nice music. It was unusual to use mouse to play such a game instead of keyboard. Dragging made it a bit easier, and we should teleport while clicking too.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Nice little game, though I think it is too easy. I won on first try, and with all those power-ups I had the feeling that levels became rather easier than harder. Also, the pixel font was more or less unreadable for me.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Nice and simple controls, and some challenging gameplay. This really feels like space invaders, but if smart phones were a thing in the 80s.

It was satisfying gathering so many powerups and cutting through so many enemies, but at a certain point if you die, it seems to reset your powerups back to zero. And coming back into the level at that point I was just getting stomped XD Perhaps a dynamic that could be a focus of a game like this, where you can get super powerful but are in big trouble if you die and restart your ship.

Great work overall

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Nice old school shoot’em up like 1980’s Galaga coin-op.

I liked the game, but mouse control can be difficult if played on a desktop, when the pointer moves away from the play area, the spaceship remains immobile on the edge even if we move to the other side, until the mouse returns inside the play area.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: galaxiaaaan, well done game its very nice and brings memories, fits for mobile but can utilize wide screen and make more room, falling enemies hard but not very much to make it too hard. well done on the game and it may fit to be in arcade

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The graphics and the sounds capture the retro “home arcade” vibe perfectly. Great to have the story mode, too, good job on that!

Made me want to make a shmup.

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text: I really liked the style and the feeling of the game, also loved that it was easy to continue on a sector after game over. Great Tyrian vibes, too :)

api.name: Cursed Elevator
api.description:
An interactive story about getting on a cursed elevator.
But it seems you are not the first this happened to.
Get to floor 13 … as often as you have to.

Controls

  • [W][A][S][D] / Arrow keys to look around
  • [R] to reset the camera
  • [P] to toggle Pointer Lock
  • [E][Enter] / Mouse click to interact
  • [Escape][1] to pause/unpause

Resources

  • ptex – Mini randomized pixel-art textures
  • W – A micro WebGL2 framework (with a lot of modifications)
  • ZzFX – Zuper Zmall Zound Zynth

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Cool idea, the glowing eyes and text game me an Inscryption vibe. The eyes gave great effects and it was cool. I got stuck after a while though and didn’t know what to do next though. Enjoyed it along the way though, I thought the story telling worked nicely.

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text: Now that you mention it, I did recently play Inscryption, maybe that influenced me subconsciously. : ) If you also got stuck in loop 5: The button can be found on floor 8, lying on the ground close to the doors. But you saw most of the game at this point.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice 3d model, felt like an elevator, sounded like an elevator! Like the story unfolding, sounds were good.

Quite spooky when I had no idea what was coming, like the eyes in the dark. I got through most of the door numbers but got stuck after 13 was removed, had a 5 tallyed on the door.

I tried every door multiple times, I even managed to move floors with the doors open at one point! I eventually let the monster get me to see what would happen :D

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
Thanks! : ) In loop 5 the button for 13 can be found on floor 8. It is lying on the ground close to the door. Also a general rule is that “story” floors (usually 3 and 9) have to be visited before button 13 becomes enabled.

But after loop 5 is only a very short final loop with the ending, so you saw most of the game

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Out! Very clever game, and smart use of glowing eyes to create a monster with limited code/assets. Excellent story telling. Just a very well crafted experience. Great job!

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
The glowing eyes really adds a nice vibe.

The plot sounded cool, but I quickly lost interest after I got stuck.

Nice job.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Looks like walking simulator in a horror genre. Very well done! Not to long to get bored and keeps tension. Sounds fit well, especially when the red eyes starting to chase you :)

Congratulations on submission!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Very cool atmospheric game. Loved the colors of the eyes to indicate the bad one (which was funny for floor 9 over the course of the story). Liked the blinking elevator light that is very typical of scary places.

Because the mouse wasn’t locked to the game I kept leaving the game window as I would look around (I play in a browser that is about 3/4 width of my screen).

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This was a cool, creepy story! I agree with other commenters that the different colored eyes were a simple but effective way to differentiate between potential allies and foes

The interior of the elevator also looks and sounds nice. Very well-done and polished.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I was pretty confused with the game. I clicked around and I think I killed the red-eyed monster by closing the door in its face. Then I was able to leave. The mechanic of repeatedly visiting the 13th floor was confusing to me.

I like the vibe a lot and the 3D graphics.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: That was really cool and scary! I was impressed with the overall presentation including 3d graphics and sound effects, great use of those libraries! The story had me engaged and I would have kept playing if there was more content.

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
You’ve managed to squeeze a lot of horror into 13k! Looks very good, but also terrifying!

That is the second game with elevators I’ve played in this jam - both pretty scary :) People are afraid of elevators for some reason. Maybe because you can get stuck in them. A perfect match with the theme, considering people are also afraid of the 13th floor.

I love how simple and yet immersive it is. You are just stuck inside the elevator and can’t move yet fully engaged in the story, and accept the gravity of the situation.

It was a bit confusing in the middle of the story though. At some point, I had that feeling that I was stuck not only in the elevator but also in the story as well - moving up and down and not really understanding what to do next. So maybe it makes sense to provide some hint or feedback when the player is stuck for a while moving up and down with no story progression. Maybe another note or a hint from the green-eyed character.

Overall, very solid and polished entry.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text:
Bravo! This was an excellent little horror story. I thoroughly enjoyed it. My only wish is that the story would have been longer.

If I had to complain about something it’s the pointer lock wasn’t on by default.

comments.author: sj-mediola
comments.text: Ok, that was quite amazing. It was frightening and thrilling. Love it

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
nice horror-ish game / experience.

Good 3d model of the elevator, and the glowing eyes are looking spooky :-)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Interesting concept for an interactive puzzle.

I got a bit confused as to how to progress. At each stage I was able to only really do one thing - which didn’t always feel like progress?

Arrow keys and wasd just ended up doing the same thing as the mouse but much much slower.

I couldn’t move around, only look around and click a few things to interact, which felt a bit disconnected and linear.

Still the illusion of being in a lift worked well, and the “eyes” out of the door were spooky.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: Very creepy feeling puzzle game. I am not really sure if I was actually getting further in the game, but I did feel creepy vibes.

api.name: The Curse of 13 Cats
api.description:
Somewhere in an old alley - the Catcher is lurking for the homeless cats. Fortunately, the Catcher has Triskaidekaphobia and is afraid of only one thing in the world: the number 13… so, the only way to save the GANG is to use the ancient CURSE OF 13 CATS!

Rules:

  1. Click on the blocks with numbers.
  2. Click on the white stacks to put blocks in place.
  3. Collect exact 13 points in ONE COLUMN and ONE COLOR using no more than 4 blocks to prepare a CURSE!
  4. Reduce the Catcher health points to ZERO to win a game. Watch out for time! After each 4 moves the block pool is reseted.
  5. WHITE blocks are JOKERS - they can be used with any previously assigned color in the stack!

Tips and Tricks: Even if you don’t need to match a certain color to get a point - you’ll gain bonus time that will help you survive :)

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice loading and rule screen, cats are beautiful for the few place we have! Game is funny, I beat the extreme mode :) sound is a bit repetitive on every action, and maybe adding a drag and drop instead of 2 clicks should be an idea to be faster and better control

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: The Impact font and the pixelated effect on top makes the text really hard to read. The constant trumpet sound effects are quite annoying for the intense time constrained gameplay. But the game is fun! And I like the 8-bit graphics - the cats are cute :)

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Intense game, and gorgeous pixelart!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I defeated the catcher! Fantastic pixel art. The rules are actually pretty simple, but it wasn’t obvious to me when I first started playing. After I cleared the first stack of 13 points, then it all made sense. Nice job!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice pixelart and gorgeous CRT effect.

Interesting game mechanic, feels a bit like a boardgame with dice.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Nice gprahics and simple but fun gameplay, took a while to understand the rules, sometimes the text / hints did not show up long enough for me to see what I did wrong but I got the basics and managed to win!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Loved the pixel art and look of the game. Being able to delete all the blocks in a column is super helpful and needed in order to progress sometimes. Mostly meant I would keep one column free as a dump column I could constantly delete and add things to when I needed to reset the pool.

comments.author: Kamil Żurek
comments.text: Hello Steven, thank Your for Your feedback :) There is a ‘X’ sign under columns - to dump all unused blocks :) Best regards, Kamil :)

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Loved the oldschool pixel art gfx, suits the theme very well.

In spite of the instructions, it takes a few seconds to understand how the game works before being able to enjoy it (and beat it).

Extreme level is really challenging, more so for a nasty bug that sometimes prevents your combo from being accepted, I reckon it mostly happens when you have 5+4+3+1 or 4+4+4+1.

Showing when the block pool will be replenished would also help, a simple marker will to the trick. Audio is a bit repetitive.

comments.author: Kamil Żurek
comments.text: Hello, thank You for Your feedback - I just tested the indicated number combinations (solid colors + colors mixed with jokers) and did not experience any bugs. Best regards, Kamil :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun looking game and an interesting idea.

Lots of quick maths involved here, and then having to get the blocks of the right colour. Quite a challenge against the clock.

The graphics and sound effects are good.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: the cat portraits are truly masterful pixel art wizardry. 😻I absolutely love them. in fact, the entire presentation is so cool! the mental math was a bit tedious for me, lol, but maybe that’s because I count using my fingers and can’t handle numbers above ten!!! teeheeheehee I kept wishing there was a nice big bright gui display that did the math for me but then again that would have made it way too easy and I am just being silly. This game is so cool. wonderful work.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: I liked it. Fun with a math challenge and some thinking. I would like some progression and a score so that I have a reason to come back to the game and try to beat my previous result.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: That’s quite a unique entry. The art design is great, and the controls are good. It took me some time to figure out what to do, a tutorial would be welcome. Also, thank you for adding sound, it added to the atmosphere. Good job, and thank you for submitting your game!

api.name: Trading Buses
api.description:
There used to be a similarly named game here - it has been disqualified and delisted due to infringing on the trademark held by a large aircraft manufacturer.

Update by @alkor: If you’re curious what exactly happened here, storytime is freely available on Slack, just poke me.

A fork is available i.a. at https://github.com/invanity/trading-buses

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
First of all, well done on reaching this milestone, it’s no small achievement. As for the game itself, while I didn’t quite see the connection to the theme, I think there’s a lot of potential here. The strategic aspect is solid and engaging, but the UI could definitely use some refinement. It’s a bit hard to understand what needs to be done at times, especially with the alert messages being too large and covering much of the screen. Additionally, the font size is a bit small, which makes it harder to read.

Keep up the great work! You’ve built something promising, and with a few tweaks, it could be even better!

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: Thanks man, I appreciate your comments. Definitely I’ll add improvements!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: This is a very unique and interesting js13k entry! So cool! I can see the beginnings of a fantastic logistics and transportation sim here, and wish there was more, that it was bigger, and had industries that each produced things another required, perhaps trucks and trains would be added using the existing code for planes? that would be awesome. in any case, I am very impressed with this entry and you should feel very proud of the result given such a tight constraint! now see what this could be if there were no limits! I could see this evolving into a huge transport tycoon style game! good luck! ✈

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: Thank you, I’m glad that it could be unique game because was the main idea I got after all. You are on track! The main idea it’s expand with other transport station mix per level progression. 3 days to make it wasn’t enough time for me, but I’m still working on it 🙂 looking for support with some companies to make it bigger

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game looks good, the concept is good

For me the game as not intuitive, I didn’t know why I would want to fly to any of the cities, or what or how I was trading?

The graphics were a little glitchy for me and at times text was printed over other text making it hard to read.

What did all the acronyms mean, “P:300”, “B:13”, e.t.c. ?

When I ran out of fuel, there was no way to start again without a refresh.

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: Definitely is a plan produce the first steps-leaning playing. People, Box and fuel are the main topics with the different airports and tradings they made.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Interesting concept, but hard to managed due to UI I think. It’s not easy to know how to reach the objectives

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
