comments.text: Nice to have use 3D for the game. Maybe move should have been “cell by cell”. Meny times after rotation I could go stright, then rotate back, move a bit, rotate again… and too far for move again.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I like that you’ve been able to reuse logic across your games. It’s almost like a signature.

3D is impressive, but it was difficult to move around and orient myself in first person, especially when I got too close to a wall.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text:
The instructions weren’t entirely correct.

WS - move forward/back QE - rotate left/right AD - strafe left/right

Bird view is a bit of a cheat isn’t it? Perhaps you could use the bird view as a hint feature where the user can trigger a zoomed out bird view a few times (or once) per maze to get an overview and then go back to first person view?

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Upon discovering “bird’s eye view” I was able to beat the first level in less than a minute. In FP view, it took me 10 minutes!

I recommend the first few levels be dead simple so I can feel progression immediately. In FP view I felt constantly lost and unsure of whether I visited that spot already. Maybe on the 5-10th levels should I experience such a difficulty.

Otherwise, I love the 3D graphics for a simple maze game like I experienced in Castle of Dr. Brain (a very old game).

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
You move around a 3D maze collecting 13 cookies. Use WSAD but also use Q and E to turn! Those keys are even more important I think, so should be included in the game description on the site. They are, however, in the ‘help’ section. There is music you can turn on, too.

I’m not entirely sure what to say. I mean, it’s a maze game with 13 spinning cookie sprites in it. There are no ‘baddies’ or special effects. Nothing really to excite you beyond the challenge of solving the maze - but for some, that will be enough. And honestly, even just getting something entered in JS13k is an achievement on its own.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Thanks for submitting this game. I am not much into maze games, but this one was well executed. The controls and graphics do the job. And thank you for adding a background music, it adds to the atmosphere! Good job!

api.name: Aargh! Triskaideka attacks!
api.description:
13 days ago, a fleet of aliens - the Trisdaideka - started an invasion of earth. As everyone else on the planet is paralyzed by the resulting Triskaidekaphobia, it’s up to you and your experimental rocket to defend the planet!

This game is controlled by keyboard or gamepad, not the mouse! (details, see below)

Story-Mode

Read the messages to unveil, what the game is about ;-)

Horde-Mode

You want to start into the full action? You can “pause” the storymode (using the button at the bottom) and jump to a endless level, including a displayed score. You can return to the story (where you left it) anytime

Controls

Gamepad

a standard gamepad is supported, the left analog stick has a different gameplay-feeling than the arrow-buttons. The arrow-buttons have the same feeling as the keyboard.

Using the analog stick:

  • the heading of your rocket is controlled by the stick-direction.
  • accelerate using the right trigger-button (index finger)

Using the arrow buttons:

  • the rocket is rotated by ← and →
  • the rocket is accelerated by ↑ or the right trigger

In both cases the A button fires your lasers

Keyboard

  • the rocket is rotated by ← and → or A and D
  • the rocket is accelerated by ↑ or W
  • the lasers are fired by SPACE, CTRL or ENTER

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Super fun, my score was 1967. Love the effects, game feels great. Awesome work.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: 1967! Thats very good. At least better than my attempts :D Thx for the feedback!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is really cool! Awesome retro vibe. The physics feel really nice. The serpent monster things look great, too.

I also like the narrative of starting on the 13th day and then flashing back to the beginning. That’s pretty clever

My minor nitpick is that I wanted to click the menu buttons, haha. Took me a second to figure out that I was only supposed to use the keyboard.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks! It’s the first time, I added some kind of story to an action-game, and I thought that “flashback” would fit here. ( I love films that do this … )

That mouse-click thing… hehe, yes. I did think a lot about it, but as the whole game is controlled by keyboard (or gamepad), I didnt want to accept a mouseclick in the menus, so a player is not forced to switch controls, and has the hands already on the right device, when the action starts. Maybe that restriction is too strict ;-)

comments.author: xem
comments.text: I enjoyed the creature’s design. The music sounds familiar, isn’t it from an old tv series ?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thx! The creatures are drawn using forward kinematics and some circle-point-calculations for the outline-points.

Yep, the music-theme is from the 80s series “Airwolf” :-)

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Fantastic game! I love the “freeze frame - yep, this is me, 13 days into an alien invasion” intro, and then the gradual buildup through the levels. I did find a way to really cheese it, but won’t spoil for others. I loved playing this! The alien attackers are very varied visually, though there’s not that much difference in the way they attack. The difficulty buildup through the levels is leisurely, so you really get the hang of it by level 6 or 7. The connection with the theme is there, if a bit tenuous. But generally this is just a great game and I can totally see it expanded into an epic journey of alien types and upgrades. If I would have one minor thing to criticize, is that it would be good to check that new enemies don’t randomly spawn directly where the player is.

Congrats Christoph!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for the kind review, i’m glad you liked it!

New aliens should spawn about 1,5 screenwidths left or right from the place of the destruction of the last one, but i obviously did not take into account, that the players lasers reach much farther than that…

The spawning itself is just a simple counter that defines (per level) how many aliens are to be respawned. So the gameplay is a bit extended, and not all enemies from one level are present at the same time.

I also had envisioned much more (and even larger) enemy-types, like ground based units (walkers in some sort), carriers, composed enemies ( you have to destroy their shield-generators first) and stuff like that.

Maybe I’ll extend the game in the future (said that also about the witch-racing from last year, the extended game is still in the project-phase… haha…)

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: You had me at the airwolf theme song! Nice spin on Luftrausers, with lots of details up to the starfield that elongates with the ship’s speed to give an impression of faster motion.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thx! I initially wanted to add some heavy music (like Doom), but on the search for inspriration i stumbled upon Airwolf. I simply had to recreate it in soundbox. (Links to the song and the youtube tutorial for piano are in the github repository - songs.txt)

The starfield initially only where some tiny dust-particles (pixels), but as they pretty much disappeared when the camera moved I had to do something about it. So they “evolved” into the trails that always have the size of the current speed and the roation of the current movement-vector. The effect of emphasised speed was a side-effect, but i loved it on first sight ;-)

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: 5/5 across the board! Awesome graphics and sound, great pacing. And I love that it works with gamepads. The moment-to-moment interactions, just flying around and blasting monsters, it’s just perfect.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Oh, thank you very much! I’m delighted, that you like my game :-)

It was the first time for me to add gamepad-support, and I will never again make an action game without that feature. Its just too nice to have different input-choices.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Some really nice features in the game, the monsters are very cool, move their design and movement.

Liked how you can go straight into chaos to see what a full screen of enemies is like!

I wished it was a little easer to get low down and have the water trail behind you are that looked awesome. Maybe the ship should explose when you hit the water, was a bit odd to just stop when you hit.

The reflection in the water is nice and also the sky gradient was simple but effective!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you Ryan!

I initially had the water exploding the ship, but found that this makes the game rather unplayable at low altitudes, so i changed it to the current ship-floating. Maybe this would be a good option for a hardcore mode in the director’s cut :-)

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text:
galaxian pew pew, would have wished for some aimer using mouse or something instead of sruggling to get aim. I know some say that sound and music should be enabled by default and I strongly disagree and would rather have the option to enable, and what about adding a pause button!

all in all its a great game and I know for fact the effort spent on it from the discord anouncements lol good luck

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for the constructive feedback!

Indeed, that could be another control scheme: Keyboard for thrust, Mouse for aiming and direction of movement… Maybe I’ll add that in the director’s cut (with credits for your idea, of course)

Also a good point regarding sound. This should be in an options-menu.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Final score 2363, 102 scouts, 211 jabberwockies, 25 squids, and 15 starfishes. This was awesome. I loved the overwhelming intro teaser, then the story mode, then the slow build up - that was masterfully executed. Great gameplay, great music. Fun arcade-y physics. Fantastic procedurally generated enemy design 🧑‍🍳 All around amazing entry, great job!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for the kind review!

I’m very glad to read, that you like the story and the gameplay.

2363 is astonishing! A far better score than I was ever able to achieve :-)

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I had a lot of fun to play your game! It could have been a simple remake of “asteroids”, but it is much more with interesting features like adding gravity. I also like the begining of the game starting with the end :). About controls it seemed to me that the angluar step was a little too big making difficult to precisely aim at the enemies. Great game !

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for your kind feedback! Makes me happy, that you had fun playing :-)

The first iteration of the gameplay indeed started like asteroids with gravity, but it never featured the asteroid rocks. The first enemy where the snake-like “jabberwockies”. But i had it on the list of maybe-features: add falling asteroids that cant be destroyed…

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My result is 2380. Score Details: 188 scouts, 201 jabberwockies, 16 squids and 11 starfishs.

Well… well, have you seen it? It’s something from another world… from aliens… or from BIKINI BOTTOM residents? BIKINI BOTTOM? Really? I laughed! It’s a lot of fun!

The only thing I would like to see more of here is more relevance to the theme and really, really that’s all, because everything else is perfect! Oh and maybe also the ability to turn on/off the music, although this is just as an option, because the music here is perfect!

Have you seen how many mechanics are here?

  • mirror reflection from below
  • dangerous zone from above
  • infinite space (actually, I was wondering if this is true, it turns out that at some point we make a circle, does this mean that the world is really round? :D)
  • navigation system
  • a trace from the plane
  • laser improvement system (favorite food of octopuses)
  • loss of control (from a dangerous area or a crashed plane)
  • different types of enemies (octopuses even have eyes that move, Karl!)
  • story mode
  • endless battle mode
  • notes (very creative and funny!)
  • a system of particles

Graphics in general need a separate Oscar. As well as plot twists ;).

All this together only complements the overall picture and does not conflict in any way, which creates a flawless gameplay.

There is potential to advance the game. I think an additional multiplayer mode would be very good here.

I was also personally pleased that I was able to see a place for laughter in this game, I also tried to add it to my game, laughter is good :).

Congratulations!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
WOW This is not a simple feedback, its a full essay about my game! Thank you very much! :-)

Yeah, the connection to the theme is not the best, haha. I’ve bent it pretty much ;-)

I’m very glad, you liked it that much, I’ll try to create an enhanced version on itch.io later this year (without 13k limit, but still in the spirit of the minimizing with as less size as possible)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
That was an amazing game! Just all around fun blasting cool-looking vector style squid/fish/ship enemies and dodging bullets. I had an amazing time flying through the air being effected by gravity.

It’s a fun version of LUFTRAUSERS because I suck at that game and I survived for quite a long time in your game.

I liked how the game was very simple and the game starting with tons of enemies and full-powers was a great twist because it immediately let me know what to expect if I do well in this game: fun-filled chaos and the destructive power of my weapon maxed out.

Overall this is an amazing game!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for your kind feedback!

Yes, it evolved into a Luftrausers-like, in the first iterations of development the player controlled one of the snakes/Jabberwockies. Later i changed them into enemies since then it started too feel like the game from Vlambeer ;-)

I agree with you: Luftrausers is a quite hard and unforgiving game.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Shocking start, but a great introduction. Feels like I was facing the final boss in the beginning.

The animations sound effects, and the HUD helps create a good atmosphere for the player. The controls are also tight, and satisfying being able to master them good enough to dodge bullets. The monsters and the particle effects are superb, a lot of polish in this game. The eyes also help add some character to the game, well done.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for the nice feedback.

I added the “shocking start” pretty much at the end of development, because I wanted to provide the player a preview of what to expect later in the game. And the first stage with no powerups might be a bit boring and might let the player/vote exit early and judge only the incomplete experience :-)

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
This is nothing short of stunning. If it doesn’t win (or rank very highly in this contest) then something is very wrong with the world :)

I love how you’re thrown into the final level first - and have next to no chance to survive, because you’ve yet to learn what is going on. Once you start the first level for real, it eases you in nice and gently. If anything, the first level is kind of boring after the thrill of the opening :) But you quickly learn the mechanics and then it’s just glorious shooting heaven until the sweet, bitter end.

Graphically, it’s outstanding. I mean, your ship looks nothing like Airwof like the great music would have you believe :D but everything is sharp and clear and well animated. The later squid like baddies, the worms, the bullets, the way mirror effect on the ground, the perspective shift in deep space, the gradients, even the logo on the title screen. Hell, the only thing that looks bad about this game is the thumbnail used on the JS13K site! :D

The playability is top-notch. The physics are solid. It’s addictive. It sounds great and it fits into 13KB. Even after years of witnessing and then judging this content, it still never ceases to amaze me what is possible. Astonishing work.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Don’t make me blush :-)

Thank you for this very kind feedback!

Ha, the thumbnail… I had a very fancy one (AI generated, showing an epic space-battle, with outstanding title-text) but did not manage to get it below the 50kB (the upper limit for the thumbnail).

Turned out i am much better at minimizing javascript than PNG images, haha…

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Oh my !!! 5/5.

Incredible game. I was not ready for a game of this level! Congratulations.

I’m really impress by the visuals, the UI and the design creatures!! Making 13 levels, with a story and the audio with it, you managed to do everything.

I’ve only tested with the keyboard but it works well. One thing that “annoyed” me a little is that I had 1 point to improve my skills but the two was already at MAX, so my point was useless. But that’s all!

I think I’ll check the code because I really want to know how you made this creature!

Congrats for the release.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you very much! Great that you liked it that much!

The “Jabberwockies” are created in src/snake.js which heavily depends on src/point.js as the snakes are “only” linked “points” (with an improved rendering…) The code is quite strange, as i only used functions this time (for improved minimizing)

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Super cool!

Not easy to master at all, but really interesting mechanics. The art is top notch, you have your own unique style. Great music as well for the atmosphere.

The controls are easy to grasp, it’s great that you thought of handling all different types of controls.

Overall, not much to say, really cool!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you!

It was one of my main goals this year to add gamepad-support into my game. Mission accomlished, I’d say, and it was quite easy to implement. ;-)

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
So many good elements here. I like it how theres a story weaved in - and how you start off with a more maxed-out game, but are completely overwhelmed due to lack of experience!

The gameplay is a lot of fun too. And it was a really fun moment discovering how you put a limit on the max altitude in the game. I sense an Ironman reference XD

Great work overall, I really enjoyed this one!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks! Glad you enjoyed it!

Yes, the frozen engines are quite like that Ironman moments :-)

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Stylish graphics, awesome music, fun gameplay. The game has everything!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thank you for your kind words, Miguel!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Excellent game, I followed development on discord. Smooth animations and cool gameplay. The enemy direction indicator is smart, and even if altitud/life bar can be diegetic to a cockpit, it would have been greater if there was “clouds” at high altitud to set the max; and color change, more smoke, blink effect… on the airship to indicate remaining shield instead of the bar (but all this are details)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thank you. I even had (half transparent, procedurally generated) clouds in mind, but could not fit it in the 13 kb in the end ;-)

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text:
What a great entry! While I must admit the theme connection is a bit creative, to say the least, the gameplay hooked me quite fast and I wanted to get more! More upgrades, more enemies, more everything!

Great work as well on the audio and the graphics, the latter being really cool with all the organic moves from the creatures :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you :-)

Yes, the theme is only weaved into the story of the game, so: maybe I’ve bent it too far ;-P

Maybe (when i have time for it) I’ll release a “MoarOfEverything”-version on itch in a few months….

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Super cool! Love the way the story is told and the horde mode. Great game! :D

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you, good sir! Especially for your valuable feedback during development and the idea of the “horde-mode”

Hope you saw the in-game-credits ;-)

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: This game is ADDICTIVE! The old school vibes of Raptor: The Call of Shadows & Tyrian were felt in the game. Good call on the Upgrades, being important elements of progression and replayability.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thank you! The “retro-vibes” were one of the things i wanted to have in the game :-)

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
I love the unique and stylish artwork, especially the effect when the aircraft gets close to the ground.

The game is easy to understand and quite addictive. I can definitely see this game having great potential for expansion!

Great job!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thanks a lot! Maybe i’ll create a directors-cut version with more levels/enemies and so on in the next months…

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
Yeah, what a cool game! Asteroids on steroids! There is a LOT of detail! it seems you loved making the game … which is what this whole thing is about, i think. So many small details: reflections, sky color, falling particles on-hit, smoke on death, engine smoke, landing, dust cloud if near the ground, etc. Never mind the animation of the Triskaideka!

I think the controls need to be a little tighter. Perhaps a max forward speed, and a bit more push when accelerating in an opposite direction (or from standstill).

Another thing that feels strange is, when the lasers are fully upgraded, they feel VERY overpowered and at the same time I feel like I want more… more power, more laser, more damage. I guess you could say they are overpowered but they don’t satisfy.

Awesome game! good job!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks! Yes, i have to admit, that with every effect i added to the game, it rose to be almost a child of mine ;-)

And i see, the end is reached to fast. Maybe i’ll create a directors-cut version with more levels, more enemies, more weapons, more powerups, moar of everything :-)

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text:
Great visuals and a banging soundtrack!

I may have died but went down fighting! Fun times!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thanks, I appreciate that you liked it!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game was really well made.

I thought the initial logo style reminded me of Airwolf with the helicopter, but then even more nostalgia when I started playing the game and the theme tune started playing - neat.

Your ship is pretty hard to control with keyboard

I found it a bit odd that you can fly faster than your bullets!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback!

The Logo had to be changed to its curent look, after i finished the cover of the theme-song :-)

Yes, i should have added a max speed for the ship. The bullets already get the inertia of the ship upun creation, but if you accelerate, then you can overtake the previously fired laser-bullets.

comments.author: Burian Sándor
comments.text: I love the UI of the fighter plane!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: I’m glad you liked the game, at least the head-up-display :-D

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Bullet hell is a real place!

The graphics are fantastic, so much attention to detail. Very satisfying large aliens, breaking into smaller aliens when destroyed. The reflection in the water is beautiful.

The story mode is brilliant!

Great job 🤩

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The opening intro was hilarious.

But I just can’t seem to play this game. The enemies are sooo tiny?? It’s really fricking hard to hit them.

The music is really nice, as are the visuals.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback.

Yeah, the little ships in the first real levels are pretty hard to hit with the single-shot lasers. but they train you for the events to come ;-)

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text:
Very interesting graphics, so many details! And the monsters are cute. Explosions could be made juicier, though.

Also, at first I thought that some levels are above water and some are underwater. Figured out it was not the case, but would be an interesting twist, imho.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thanks! Underwater is really a nice idea. On my (not completed) feature list were walking enemies for the sea-level. Diving below sea-level indeed would open up an ocean of gameplay possibilities!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Cool graphics and I really like the UI and monsters design!

Also I like the idea to show a challenging level initially with the upgrades, reminds me souls games approach when you face a boss with level 1 character :D

Good job and congratulations on the submission!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks!

The first sneak-peek of the fully upgraded ship was mainly because of the limited time many people have to test and vote for the game. When they only would have played the first normal level (single shot, small enemies), they would have received a complete wrong experience ;-)

But I also liked this “flashback” as story-telling trick.

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text: Every little detail is so well fleshed out in this game. Responsive controls, satisfying explosions, music, art style, lovely game!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thank you very much for the kind feedback!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: I gave you some feedback on discord already. But really nice math and physics! Very like your very technical entries! The game feels pretty smooth and I imagine it was not easy to implement those rotation, gravity, friction, etc. I would have really liked that the rotation was less sensitive, aka more resolution in the rotation; it felt a bit frustrating to not be able to hit the targets. Congrats on your entry!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks for your (very pleasing) feedback!

Good point regarding the rotation! I tried many different rotationspeeds and settled on “180 degrees per second”. see player.js on github As i am using a 144 Hz display this results in degree-steps of 1,25 per update frame. On a 60 Hz display it would be 3 degrees per frame. pretty much…

I did not think about that… hm. Maybe a little ramp-up in rotationspeed would fix that.

I’ll try it in the directors cut, and keep that in mind for the next years. THANK YOU!

api.name: 13th Restaurant Rush
api.description:
In 13th Restaurant Rush, speed is key, but tradition is just as important! According to an old saying, there should never be 13 people seated at a table. This restaurant takes that very seriously, and it’s up to you to maintain the balance while typing orders as fast as possible!

The challenge? Keep the line moving and don’t let more than 12 people wait for a seat, or face the consequences. Every second counts!

Here are the golden rules to remember:

  • No more than 12 customers can be in line at once! Serve them quickly or lose the game.
  • Never serve an order if the total of its items equals thirteen!

How to Play

  • Type each element order in the input field and hit Enter. Example: “2 carrots”
  • If the order total adds up to 13, type refuse order instead.

Keep the flow moving, and never let 13 customers wait! It’s a fast-paced, number-crunching challenge that tests both your typing skills and your mental math. How many orders can you serve before the line grows too long?

It’s my first time participating, and I hope you’ll like it! Don’t hesitate to follow me on X if you like the game and want to keep in touch.

This won’t be the proposal with the most technical challenge inside, but my challenge was to find enough time to finish my project with two young daughters!

Thanks, everyone, for the challenge. It was nice to be part of it!

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Such a cute looking game! Nice GitHub README. And great work providing the instructions on game start.

Great concept for including the number 13, and makes you think on your feet. Great for typing skills too! Really fun game.

Did find a couple of loopholes/bugs. Can copy and paste - so can copy “refuse order” and paste it in whenever items total 13. One of the orders if it has the same item as the previous order, it strikes out the item on the following order, meaning I had to type it out again even with the strike through. Would be also great to have a life counter score somewhere at the top; could make it something fun like three strawberries or something like that.

A score counter in the top would have been awesome, and difficulty levels would also have been a nice touch. Great work on finishing your game whilst looking after 2 kids. Nice work!

Would love to see some sound effects/music to add the cherry on top. Needs a pop up or similar to restart the game it ends. Loved the cute and simple artwork.

Def one of my favourites!

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Thank you for your feedback! Haha, I definitely need to implement some sort of anti-paste protection—I hadn’t thought about that during development. And yeah, I didn’t manage to include the sound in time.

There’s a score counter in the top left, but I didn’t notice it on your stream, so maybe there’s a responsiveness issue. As for the difficulty, it currently increases with the number of lines/items per order and the decreasing time between orders as the game progresses.

I’m really glad you enjoyed playing it!

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: Cute and silly, I really like this. Typing from sight as an input method, it’s like, it’s so obvious but one hardly ever sees this. It really works. There were a few errors in the console (listOrder is not defined), and at one point I got stuck because all the items were crossed out but the customer wouldn’t go away.

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Thank you for your feedback!

Damn, I think the last refactoring caused me some troubles, thank for noticing it. I’ll try to fix it for the director’s cut version!

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Love the concept! Fun way to control a game.

Also great fit for theme.

Having different difficulty levels could give the gameplay some depth, e.g. adding more complex orders, like “3 pairs of sausages” or a person suddenly deciding to order more

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text: Thank you for your feedback! You’re right—currently, the game starts with a random selection from a small set of lines, and the number of options gradually increases over time. I agree that introducing elements to break the rhythm could make things more interesting and add depth to the gameplay. It’s definitely something worth exploring!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little typing game with a maths element.

I enjoyed playing it.

For me it can be tricky to keep up with the orders. As not only do you need to read them quickly, but type them quickly and without spelling mistakes, but then also make sure you’ve added them all up and it doesn’t equal 13 - wow :)

There was one instance when 2 people in a row ordered water and when I typed it as the last item of the first person, the second person had it already crossed off when their list came up.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My highscore is 250 - that’s if you don’t cheat :).

If you like to count and also want to improve your typing skills, then this is the place for you!

I’ve often seen programs to improve typing skills, but counting while doing so is something new, quite innovative! The game perfectly reveals the theme of the competition. I like the minimalistic style, and it’s quite nice. I also love SVG graphics!

Of the possible improvements in my opinion, I think it would be even more fun if there was a small animation of pushing a crowd of customers if there are several in a queue, maybe along with their parallel orders, where the player could choose which ones they can close the fastest. The movement of the seller’s eyes imitating the inspection of the order would probably deepen the immersion in the atmosphere. In my opinion, it would be even more fun :D! But of course it should be tested :).

Congrats on your hard work - really nice work!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: 330 highscore xD A cool typing game, with a few maths. It could be a cool game for kids (removing or reducing the timer) to count and type :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
As the text was on the screen I could copy and paste :D

Clever idea, typing games are not for me but can see you have put some work into this so well done.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: I’m pretty quick on the keyboard but it turns out that things can go south quickly if you misspell parts of an order. I thought I was going to be clever and have “refuse order” in the clipboard and paste it to save time, but most of the time I failed on mistyping a word and letting the queue grow to maximum length. I could definitely see that the use of audible queues could help. A “ding” when a new order has arrived. A “booo” or other negative signal when I misspell and another sound when I typed a correct part of an order.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game vibe is really cute. It really needed appropriately cute music to go along. Also sound effects (like a chime when a new customer arrives).

I’m not a big fan of fast typing as a game control, but it’s well implemented. I think it would make more sense to just listen to key presses on the document instead of having an input field, as it was possible to copy-paste “refuse order”.

The game also lacks animations to make it look a bit more alive. For example, the people arriving in the shop and leaving.

Cute idea, but wish it was a bit fleshened out.

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Thank you!

Yeah I totally agree with you. At first my idea was to not have a input bar and directly typing on the screen like you say, but with the time that I had I thought it would be to complicated to handle all the UI and animation that I had in mind for this behavior so I choose a simpler version.

Same for the audio and the animation, I wanted to do more but slight wasn’t possible, I’ll try to focus more on this aspect next year!

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: Really nice work!, nice logic and probably you could improve the audio and graphics, the theme is something different which make a plus! 😊

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text: Thank you! Yeah I lacked of time for the audio and animation, I’ll try to do better next year!

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
There was a bug on this order - can’t skip! “10 raspberries 1 carrots 1 pork 1 plums” page

I like how to combined typing tests, dinner dash, and math all in one game! The game starts on a gentle learning curve, and gets steep really fast. I also like the idea that this game has a edutainment aspect that would be fun for kids to play, and at the same time, learn

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice artstyle! Some animations/effects would be nice. On the other hand, i would not see them, while looking at my keyboard… ;-)

It would be nice if “1 carrot” would be a valid entry, not only “1 carrots”.. And additionally if “5 pineapples 2 mushrooms” would also count as single input, not only each separately.

Some soundeffects would also add much to the game. Positive feedback for correct orders, an “aaawww” for refused orders, a horn for wrong orders… something like that.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: A very stylish typing exercise game with a solid link to the theme, GJ!

api.name: Deep13
api.description:
You are in a submarine in the depths of the Artic circle.

Your vessel is investigating a strange signal emanating from deep the water surface.

We have launch a flare to mark the entry spot.

Submerge in the frozen waters to find the source of the signal, but remember, your submarine cannot stand more than 13 atmospheres of pressure. Also, something is moving down there, be careful.

Keys:

WSAD to control the vessel

QE to control the up and down tank

F toggle light

1: toggle sonar

2: toggle scanner

G: fix leaks

Space: brake

Disclaimer: The collision engine is a little simple so sometimes you could get stuck in the map without any way to escape. If that happends, sorry :/

comments.author: Mezriss
comments.text: Incredible visuals and great sound design. Unfortunately it’s hampered by overly sensitive controls - even the shortest presses sets speed to full and sends sub into a wall.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Hey, I think it is a bug. My physics are very tied to the framerate, if the framerate is very different to mine probably the speed parameters would not work. :( sorry, and thanks for your reply.

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Awesome visuals! Didn’t manage to find the source of the signal but was fun exploring the depths and really triggered my claustrophobia. Great job :D

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks! I hope you enjoyed. To find the signal just go to the flare and start diving until almost 13 atmospheres, you turn around and it should be there.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: wow, amazing visuals and atmosphere

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: thanks a lot! the atmosphere was my main focus.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Awesome. A lot of good atmospheric touches in graphics and sound. Could use the list of controls on the screen, and a bit more forgiving levels (i.e. wider passways) at start.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks! I wanted to add controls somewhere but it was hard. They appear briefly on startup but yeah, I should add them.

About the map, I cant, is procedural generated :( that was one of my biggest problems.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Impressive graphics. The pixelated HUD brings me back to Subwar 2050 by Microprose. This game gives great Iron Lung vibes.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Nice references! I love pixelated interfaces, I am old-schooler, hehe. I remember watching Iron Lungs and thinking “this game needs a sonar!!”

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to move the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Hey Michael, sorry and thanks for the tip. I fixed the bug in here: https://tamats.com/games/js13k2024/compo/

It is exactly the same game. So at least you can check it.

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
Really atmospheric visuals and sound design! Unfortunately I could not find the source of the signal in 4 attempts before dying, but I saw something worm-like moving on the sonar.

Did not work in Firefox (some WebGL issues), so I used Chromium.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks! It is quite easy, just go to the flare and dive there as much as you can, then turn 180 degrees and you will see the source of the signal. And yes! there is a worm creature lurking at the bottom, sadly the AI is quite basic and just moves around, it doesnt attack, but you can get damaged if it passes too close.

About firefox, it is strange, it works in my machine in firefox, but thanks for telling me, I guess something related to WebGL2 in Firefox.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text:
Really nice idea! What a cool atmosphere! Impressive!

Is there something to do when you find… the thing? ;) Gave me chills when I first saw it :8

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Yeah!! if you mean the glowing thingy, you must carry it to the surface (it is written on the top of the screen but I understand it is hard to see). Thats the end game.

If you mean the snaky thing… just run away!

Thanks a lot for your reply.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Wow, fantastic atmosphere. I was able to grab the object. I ran out of battery before I could get back to the surface. Part of me wishes the controls were a little more forgiving, but I imagine that would also take away from the atmosphere, which is the strongest point of the entry. Beautiful graphics, excellent use of sound, brilliant interpretation of theme. Amazing entry, great job!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks a lot Cody, it encourages me to see people who enjoyed my proposal. As you said, the controls are hard so you are more likely to fail, otherwise the game is too easy (also to showoff my glass-breaking shader)

Probably you runned out of battery because you hit the snake (it drains battery!!).

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Great visuals, really clever 3d map and navigation. Takes a lot of time to move around, as I imagine it would in real life!

Have to really take take to control the sub, at first thought I could zoom about, more of a simulation!

I liked how the screen could break and later can leak in. Also when you are deep the water FX with no light on are very cool!

Just a great tech demo and a reminder of what can be done in JS, HTML etc!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks for your comments! I wanted to see how much FX I could cramp in 13KBs.

But it is not only a tech demo! there is gameplay too! did you see the snake? did you find the artifact? Did you surfaced?

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I am incredibly impressed with the beautiful rendering you coded here! wow! is the terrain some sort of perlin-esque noise, rendered with clusters of spheres?? so cool. the distance fog darkening, the sonar adding to the tension, the awesome control panel cockpit, and the incredible dust mote style particles in the water helping convey movement… all these touches add to the great atmosphere. and the radar looks so cool! what an amazing technical accomplishment. fantastic work.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks a lot Christer!

You are right on spot, I used perlin noise with some atractors to construct the map, which looks nice but was hard to control to make the map more interesting. I wanted to render using SDFs or Marching Cubes but I knew that would take lots of time so I ended up rendering perturbed spheres in every cell (removing the ones that cannot be visible), and it worked fine.

For the sonar I rendered the same map but playing with the frustum and it worked very well!.

The rest was adding tiny fx to hide how simple was the map, like the dust or playing with the phong equation to make it less blocky.

Anyway, thanks a lot for your reply, it encourages me to see other people value the same things as I do.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Impressive game! 3d is really cool, UI too, controls are quite responsive, sound design is nice. Not an easy game but eveything is done to help player. Don’t know if the snake would attack me but he was really big! I didn’t find the signal (I broke the submarine before)

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks Jonathan! Yeah, the idea was that when you stumble uppon the snake it should feel very menacing, but in reality it just follows its way (although it can damage your sub). To find the signal dive to the bottom in the flare area, and turn 180 degrees, it should be easy to find from there.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Very atmospheric and impressive game!

I caught me multiple times holding my breath while manouvering through the caves… and had more than one jumpscare, when i again hit something hard enough to cause a leak!

Very, very good entry!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks Christoph!! Did you finish the game? Did you find the snake?

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I like your pressure idea for the theme (I promise it’s not because it’s the same idea I had.)

The atmosphere is amazing, and truly scary. I’m a scaredy-cat, and this game makes me freak out (but that’s a good thing.) The controls takes some time to get used to. I quickly realize that I had to adjust “q” and “e” to set a fixed speed. I kept going down or up even if I stopped pressing the button. After learning it, I think it’s actually better. The meter in the HUD helped me understand it, so it’s not an issue, but a good thing.

At first I thought I was supposed to investigate the flare itself, but I’m happy you added some text to the game to convince me otherwise, or else I would be stuck.

The graphics are truly amazing. I don’t know how it’s possible. The music and SFX also help set the vibe. Great experience.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks John! Some people had told me about the Q-E problem, I though about making them like WSAD so only when pressed affect, but submarines usually require to fill or empty a tank to control flotability and I though that could be an interesting mechanic.

About investigating the flare, I was aware that could be a common mistake, thats why I added the message to encourage people to dive deeper, Im glad it worked.

About the GFX, the trick is to render the same thing again and again all around the 3D world. In this case the mesh of the rocks, artifact and flare are the same!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
The graphics are ridiculously good! The general concept and tone you created is also great. It felt so claustrophobic and weirdly hopeless the whole time (in a good way). Especially when my battery ran out, haha (I think my sub started filling with water, too..?)

I know I’m driving a submarine around, which can’t be easy, but I wish the controls were a tiny bit easier; I ultimately couldn’t finish the game, because I couldn’t maneuver my ship around well enough.

But yeah, this was really impressive, and it was definitely a memorable experience! I’ll try to play again later to beat the game!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks Benjamin. I expended most of my time in the graphics / sound atmosphere of the game, and the gameplay is not as strong (I wanted the controls to be hard by fair).

Hope in your next run you can beat the game.

Cheers!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Great game: perfect atmosphere, I love the feeling of diving deep in the caves - the tension, the lighting, the sound - it’s all perfect. I have a pretty beefy machine but it still struggled a lot - I think this affected negatively the physics / updates of everything. Even turning off the sonar and scanner, it was barely playable for me. The input felt super sensitive and laggy, it was really really difficult maneuvering around (I think more than was intended to). Despite this, the game is amazing!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Hey Almar. Thanks for your comments.

And sorry, but I abused a little bit the GPU to save size and I can see how in some hardware the game could struggle (which would make the game unplayable). Im trying to make a version where the physics are not associated with the framerate so at least people like you can enjoy it.

Cheers!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Well, I was able to dive at least to some depth with 4-5 attempts. :). The steering is quite sensitive, not like racing strangely, maybe it has something to do with the submarine, but I’m not sure :).

Once patched a hole, went down to a certain depth, although I’m not sure if it went there at all.

Quite innovative and suitable sounds, as well as full compliance with the theme.

Interesting game, congratulations!

UPD:

Well, after reading about the adventure of others, I decided to play again and explore more of the world. Well, I wandered for clearly more than an hour :). It’s really amazing and very playable! I’m glad I decided to try again.

From my research I have learned the following.

I don’t know much about radars, but I was able to intuitively understand them as much as possible. If I understood correctly, the top right is a map, and the bottom right is a signal by which you can search for a transmitter?

I have been to quite a lot of places, at least I want to think so :). I sailed through several caves, went deeper than 13-14 km, repaired potholes on the way, used up all the fuel several times, crashed the ship into a walls even more, by the way, I was the most successful at crashing, because when I wanted to make a U-turn or quickly adjust the altitude, I usually got very it was drifting and due to panic I simply fell into two extremes, then forward but back, here and there I got a new collision :)).

I also managed to meet a snake - great!

I thought I could see a pendulum, but most likely it was a snake on the map :D.

Well, really cool! But I want the control to be easier, it is possible to make difficulty levels - easy/medium/hard core ;).

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Hey Roman, you got it right. Glad to see that you enjoyed all the aspects of the game.

The artifact to find will appear in the map as a blue dot (small though) if that helps you to find it.

Having dificulty levels may be an option, but instead of the controls, more for the stats of the submarine. But great sugestion.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Wow the game looks amazing. The terrain, the mini-map, the dials, e.t.c.

The sounds are atmospheric, the feeling of submersion was really well imparted.

I didn’t find the source of the signal and crashed a few times, but I enjoyed playing and wanted to keep playing again and again

A well executed idea and a very impressive entry.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks Jasper! To find the origin of the signal go to the flare and dive to the bottom from there, if you turn 180 it should be very close.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: An underwater exploration game that looks amazing and has well thought out sounds. I honestly couldn’t really get into the gameplay because the controls and goals weren’t clear to me. But I’m in awe of the atmosphere and impressive depth with just under 13k! Excellent work.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks Matt! I expended all the bytes in the atmosphere so I didnt have much left to polish the gameplay.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
I really enjoy exploration games, and this one creates a great atmosphere that kept me wanting to find the source of the signal. I only find the creature’s tentacles and wasn’t quite sure what the final goal of the game.

It’s impressive that you created a 3D map within 13K! And I really linked the scene design. It truly captures the feeling of the deep sea and conveys a sense of helplessness in the depths. Great job!!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks Leo! Creating the map is easy (it is just a mathematical function, check perlin noise), but making it interesting was a problem as I couldnt control much of it (like passages or areas). But anyway. thanks again!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Fortunately, I could play the game not having “search as you type” enabled in my browser, as its pointless. So, I enjoyed this, the underwater effect is perfect. My dog didnt like the background ‘pinging’ noise for some reason and got up and left.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: hahahaha, I will make a “dog’s friendly version” with ping sound being less dominant :D

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Spent a good deal of time playing this one. I really like the feeling of the game, and the 3d aspect of it all. Visually a very strong entry, 3D games are always extra impressive in js13k.

I did struggle a bit with the difficulty though, its a bit tricky to know exactly what to do, and as others have mentioned the controls can get you into trouble pretty quick (but I mean to be fair, it does handle like a submarine XD). But thankfully the game itself is interesting and fun enough that some control issues dont matter!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: Wow, this was really really impressive. Amazing job on the visuals, atmosphere and controls. Incredibly impressive. I wasn’t expecting the glass viewport to work as a visual representation of damage either.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks Dann! I appreciate it. Lots of effort to cram as much cool features as possible is such a small space. Glad that others value it.

api.name: Final Lift
api.description:
You’ve awoken in a strange elevator that doesn’t seem to indicate what floor it is on. You know you’ve started on floor 1. Each floor makes different demands of your mental facilities. After you’ve completed the task, you must select an amount of floors to go up or down. You’ll need to keep track of your current floor yourself.

Don’t let the time run out, and whatever you do, make sure you stay away from floor 13!

Tools used: Kontra and ZZFX

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I liked the eerie look of the game, and the concept of having to do tasks whilst remembering where you were.

The shudder mechanic was good, as were the door animations and zooming in/out.

For me it was more of a mobile game than a desktop one.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I got 9 floors :) The concept is very interesting and the math exercices are quite varied. I had fun to play several times and I loved your interpretation of the theme !

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice use of elevator. I did the game multiple times, I loved the variety of questions and sometime “inverted” questions between two attemps.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Super clever, I like the fact that you don’t need a tutorial to understand the game. You really get into it right away. The sounds are very smart and well done. The animations simple & effective. I ended up on level 13 and didn’t really understand why I lost!

Really well done

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Fun game! On the first play through I ran out of time, so second time I really wanted to stay ahead and well away from 13, but it turned out that the timer and the floors were not related. That made the 13 come out of the blue, I assumed your sums would have to keep you away from 13, that would have made the theme more a part of the game as opposed of a random ending.

Still, engaging, not too difficult, but the clock ticking away especially with the sound does force you to think quicly.

Great work!

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I mention this in the instructions, You start on floor one, and after each level, you select the number of floors to go up/down. You’re supposed to keep track of what floor you’re on in your head and make sure not to pick the one that sends you to 13, so it isn’t random. If you forget what floor you are on, you can figure it out again when you get the puzzle that asks you your current floor, since you can’t move on until you get it right!

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Nice game, it suites the theme.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: An interesting quiz game that takes place in an elevator, with an elevator interface! Clever. I knew exactly what my goal was on each level, but I wasn’t sure what my overall goal was. Especially appreciated the animations between scenes. Well made game, nice work!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This is really, really clever. I love the fact that you’re challenging different parts of the brain here (with recollection, the math and the trivia-ish nature of the questions). Audio is clear and crisp too. My mark on controls here is how it is because of how great the buttons feel to interact with due to the sound and general design combined.

api.name: Unblock
api.description:
Destroy the blocks as they appear on the screen.

Desktop Controls

  • Space – shoot
  • Left Click – powerful bullet

Mobile Controls

  • Touch – move and shoot
  • Swipe Up – Powerful bullet

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
AH! The spacebar is the main-fire trigger….. ha! I was only clicking and waiting for the sparse violett powerups …

The gameplay turns out to be pretty hectic soon, and the fear of the 13 is omnipresent :-)

I like the clean style and the music. Maybe a pure keyboard control ([left] [right] to move, [space] to shoot, [up] for power-shoot) would be nice?

Solid casual game entry!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Thank you Christoph!

I’m glad you liked the music. It is always fun to compose music for the games. I was considering that for the controls, but did not have time to implenent it. I do agree it would be nice to have that option.

Thank you!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: A nice chill arcade shooter. Powerups only working once is an interesting take.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thank you for the feedback! :) Glad you find the powerups interesting!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My record 192800, stage 10/10 - half the level was not enough, 13 was hooked :)

Very polished game! It can be seen that the balance was chosen, during the whole game you can’t relax and the closer to the end - the harder it is. I also liked the music!

However, pressing the spacebar endlessly - the hand is tired, as an alternative, you can consider a continuous shot while holding the spacebar, but with a certain delay (I see that it is on the phone, so it would be good to have it on the desktop as well).

Good job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback, Roman! I’m happy you liked the music! Good to know about the spacebar. Yeah, for some reason I thought that it would be more fun to have to press it to shoot, whereas for mobile it was the right thing. Lots of learnings here.

Thank you!

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Good use of 13 as a game mechanic. The audio matches the vector graphic style really well.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, Jerome! :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Looks nice, I like the graphics style, I have a mechanical keyboard and pressing space over and over is not fun.

If you are giving away free bullets, let the user turn shooting on / off, hold to shoot or just have continous shooting.

Music is nice, and its all works well.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Hi Ryan,

Thanks for the feedback! The issue with the spacebar was a common thing! Some good learnings here. Happy you liked the music! Thanks!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: having space down should enable shooting and not on every single keydown its tiring especially when game is about spamming the blocks, which I think should be spread evenly and not only on the first column since each row add number you have it impossible to win when it keep going on a single file even when you spam the space bar.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, Mohammad!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Excellent classic arcade shooter + clever interpretation of the theme. My high score was 61,800 on stage 6. The purple power shot was a nice touch, and a fun minigame to manage them carefully. I found the cyan spread shot was typically not worth the effort, so I tended to ignore them, but maybe there’s some strategy there that I didn’t appreciate? Great music, sound effects, and artwork. Great job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thank you, Cody, Interesting feedback on the cyan spread shot. Thanks for the comments on the music!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Good idea, nice game and cool music too! Maybe shoot on space could have been kept enabled as I was spamming it everytime. Interesting micro-management to keep column shot for extreme case. Cool start screen allowing to test features and how shoot works

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, Jonathan! I was wondering if the “tutorial” in the start screen would be useful. :P Thanks for the comments on the music. I enjoyed making it!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun space shooter.

The powerful bullet was handy, shame there was so few of them.

Reminded me of a defence game with a marching horde where you need to decide which best to attack first based on their points.

The music was catchy and the sound effects were good.

Overall a fun game

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thank you for the feedback, Jasper!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Really nice take on this years theme! Keeping all the numbers low can become quite challenging pretty quickly. Personally, I would’ve liked to be able to click and hold to shoot in addition to the spacebar, so I wouldn’t have to spam the spacebar as much - but that’s up to taste. The graphics for the game were also a little minimalistic - but that also adds to the feel of the game. Overall - nice job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thanks, Joep! Yeah, several people mentioned the spacebar issue. I put it in the mobile version, but thought it would be more fun to have to hit the spacebar for the desktop version. Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A top-down space shooter in an oldschool Atari style. I played on desktop and would have liked to be able to hold down the shooter button instead of pressing it repeatedly. Also would have liked to see the theme integrated more clearly. Very playable with nice music and sound effects. Great job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thank you, Matt! That was a common feedback. I implemented that in the mobile version, but somehow I thought it would be boring for the desktop version. Thanks!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Nice looking visuals and fun gameplay! I would have liked a bit more “juice” to the effect of the powerful bullet and when clearing the stage. Perhaps some screenshake?

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Thank you for the feedback! Totally agree with the screenshake :)

api.name: 13% LOW
api.description:
This game illustrates the chaos of charging cables when your phone’s battery is running low. Players are challenged to find the correct path through tangled wires to help charge the phone. It’s a puzzle-solving experience where you need to think quickly and carefully to connect the right cable and keep your phone powered up.

How to play? Type: Desktop/ Phone Input: Click/Drag Rect View: Perfer Phone

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I don’t understand how to play this game. The instructions in the description don’t help. I couldn’t pass the second level. Too bad because the game looks fun and has nice music.

Edit: ok, i got the drag and drop now, but the game suffers from several bugs where I can’t click on anything, or drag blocks to their right location. It’s a shame because the idea seems to be really fun, but it could use with some amount of testing. I have the graphics and the music.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Fun little puzzle game. I like the music and sound effects. It’s nice an chill and works really well for the game.

However, for a long time I was unable to get past level 2. I can’t seem to drag charger at all (per the instructions). The only thing I’m able to do is click the arrows which move the charger non-stop in that direction. This causes it to run into a wall or obstacle on level 2 and lose. It took me asking about the game in Slack for others to try for someone to figure out how to play the game. The instructions were quite unclear.

Also, sometimes the game gets stuck on the loading screen. I’ve found I’ve had to close the game and open it back up in order to get past the screen.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: You found an excellent set of mechanics that work together for puzzles. I imagine with more time and without the 13kb constraints, you could add more help text and tutorial levels to explain the basics. Congrats on submitting, nice job

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
For anyone coming here and getting lose:

Drag the shapes onto the playable area, they control direction of the cable, bit tricky but you can use the shapes to route the cable to the end point.

Was able to select the correct shapes and build a path “UP > RIGHT > DOWN” etc to get to the end. Was not sure at first but worked it out.

Simple fun game. Consider a tutorial level that highlights to the player what to do.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Clean idea. It took me a while to understand the controls. Great work.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thanks to Ryan’s instructions i was able to play the game beyond level 2 Nice concept!

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Very unintuitive, but a fun puzzle concept once you understand the controls. Cool minimal atmosphere!

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: The premise is hilarious, but there is something about the graphic style that makes it unclear what can be interacted with and what cannot. I didn’t realize the shapes above could be dragged in the play area. It would also be useful to be able to remove a shape, as they have a tendency to snap on the wrong tile. These 2 things get in the way of what is otherwise a cool game.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: A tricky little puzzle game. Could be a bit more guidance to get started, but once you know what youre doing the game makes a lot of sense. Some tricky puzzles!

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: It takes a while to get used to the overall function of the game. However, it’s very fun! For a game with this much function I wish there was some kind of solid tutorial or practice area that the player can use to get used to things.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Nice puzzle game. Several types of blocks adds diversity to gameplay. Some of the levels can be solved in several ways. Good music.

Dragging elements would need some refining.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hi there! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • A puzzle maze! I will drag the cable around like that parking lot game with cars.
  • Great work on:
    • Visuals! There is a bunch of visual work here and it’s interesting.
    • Levels. Puzzles are tricky to get right and balance without lots of playtesting and simply time. You have some good levels here, so good work.
  • I liked that you:
    • Had a progression over the levels and added bits of misdirection.
    • Created this difference between the strategies of placing blocks vs moving the cable. It was a different game thinking about each part and it demanded some thinking sometimes.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Again, puzzles == lots of playtesting and work. I think you have the core mechanics here and the new blocks are interesting enough of a variation, that the later levels feel different. For now though some levels are a bit confusing on what they are trying to ask you. I completed some with seemly the “wrong” answer given the amount of leftovers and I discovered multiple solutions for some of the puzzles. I think it could be ok if there would be some points for the smallest cable or less movements. I have screenshots with the “wrong” solutions if you’re interested in them. Let me know!
    • I personally liked the “snake game” more than the tile mapping one. It made me think more and reflect on a plan. I liked the levels where this thinking was needed. The tiles were ok, but I felt they were too obvious and the not so great handling of them made the experience a bit worse.

I took some screenshots with some minor feedback https://x.com/javascripl/status/1838331441354281122

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: With just little improvments this game should have been nicer. It took me time to realize I should drag items to solve puzzles. Moreover, drag placement is a bit hazardous due to mouse position detection I think. It’s not easy to place an item, and not able to move it after, unless restart whole level. I did’nt understand all features, like some brown block that killed me at first level, then later allows me to stop movement then continue. Levels are not bad after all this steps passed, music and sounds are ok too

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: Good concept and design, but the controls weren’t intuitive for the player. Tried on both desktop and mobile and was struggling with the 2nd and 3rd stages.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: I’ll be honest - I had NO idea you were meant to drag the very chunky pixel icons from the top strip down onto the gameboard. So on level 2 I kept dying over and over and was just about to give-up, when I accidentally dragged an icon and then it all made sense! You essentially plot a path for the cable to wind to the battery, by dropping down guide arrows and avoiding danger zones. On later levels you can rotate the guides, and you always have a limited number of them, plus the cable can’t intersect itself! So planning is required. Graphically it’s quite nice, super chunky pixels and bleepy synth sounds. I’m not entirely convinced this is very on-theme, but that doesn’t stop it being a thoughtful puzzle game.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Took me a while to figure out that I have to drag the direction arrows ;) Solid puzzle game! Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I’m afraid the game didn’t work at all on desktop, it just got stuck on a flashing battery and went no further. There wasn’t even anything showing in the error console.

Then when I changed the shape of the screen I could see something hidden underneath.

When I finally got something working, the game was pretty neat.

Very much a monochrome game, and the swipe on desktop wasn’t as intuitive as mobile.

The music sounded really good.

api.name: Sharks and Trains
api.description:
Sharks or Trains, your choice.

You are the leader. Your followers follow you and each other until they are split apart by the Evil rotating 13 that drops pinball bumper 13s on the screen.

You have the power to erase the 13s and try to get your followers back together.

Want to WIN? Press Bounce Followers button. Check the follower count in the top left screen. When your total followers reaches the total number of followers , then You WIN! Yea! But the struggle continues. You get your followers back by touching them.

Buttons: Bounce followers - makes your followers non followers and sends them to random locations. Turn them back into followers by touching them

Clear Trails - Clears the screen of 13s that were laid down by the Big 13 Audio button - toggles the sound effects on and off. Train button - toggles between Trains and Sharks

Sliders: Follower Speed - the speed that followers try to get back together Big 13 Speed - the speed of the Big rotating 13 that drops little 13s on the screen Trail Distance - changes the distance between the dropped little 13s

Information: Total followers Total Following (currently) Total Collisions (collisions per second) // used to change sound effects

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Interesting concept, I like the sharks, was not expecting trains even though the game is called Shark or Trains.

My browser struggled a little with the game, a lot of rendering going on, some interesting patterns and shapes going on, kind of an art piece?

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
The visual effects in the background were pretty cool, but a bit distracting at times, especially when trying to see the 13s left behind. Being able to play with the settings of the simulation was also interesting.

Lots going on, lagged my computer at times.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Very novel idea for a game. The concept was very interesting.

I wasn’t sure that the blue blobs on the sharks meant, or why they went away when I moved the “sticky” shark over the blobs. Was it something to do with the followers?

Was there a benefit to being a shark or train? Perhaps add a character select screen at the start and make it clear what the difference is.

Good job

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I’ve reached the total number of followers after bouncing, but didn’t get an ending screen. The animation effect in background was laggy :/

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I liked the sharks graphics and the “bubble” effects in the background that are very cute! To me this game sounds more like a demo and it was not clear how to reach the end after having the 25 followers. I don’t know if it is a specific problem on my computer but I was not able to hear any sound. The concepts is interesting and I never played this kind of game.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A trippy game about sharks and trains. I honestly couldn’t figure out what the goal was, but I always love seeing sharks and it looks like the theme is integrated well. Nice job.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: what a unique game! original and strange in a good way. a piece of art that I didn’t fully understand but I did appreciate. thanks for joining js13k!

api.name: 13 Seconds To Midnight
api.description:
Escape the dark forest before time runs out!

Collect green cubes to add time, red spheres for speed boosts. Avoid the red enemies hunting you.

Controls

  • WASD / Arrow Keys: Move
  • Mouse: Look around
  • ESC: Pause

13x Green cubes, 13x Red Spheres, 13x Enemies!

You have 13 seconds. Can you survive until midnight?

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
I get a very choppy 13 fps. I have a Ryzen 9 5900x 12 core processor and a RTX3090. Something is extremely wrong. The time countdown still runs fast even though the rendering is really slow, so pretty unplayable.

Edit: I actually did check out your code to see if I could figure out what was wrong, but I’m not sure. I think perhaps if you merged your trees into one geometry that would help? It would also help to fix the framerate, as my 120hz monitor tries to run the render more often of course, which makes the performance even worse.

Also, if this is your first ever game, that is awesome! A little more testing and performance improvement next time and I think you’ll have a great entry!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Hey, thanks for the feedback! This was my first time developing a game, and participating in js13kGames was a real challenge. I developed the game on a MacBook Pro M3 and didn’t encounter any lag or performance issues. My mistake was not testing it on other machines, so I assumed there were no performance problems. Unfortunately, I noticed the issues after I had already submitted the game, and it was too late to fix them. I’ve learned a lot from this experience, but there’s still much more to learn. I’ll be better prepared next year!

comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text:
I love horror games and your game is the first game i wanted to try just because of the graphic design. Unfortunately, i couldn’t progress much. The fps drop is kinda brutal and you end up teleported against the trees without really a way to avoid it.

It could have been a great competitor without this problem.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Ouch I am very sorry you couldn’t play it my mistake I didn’t check how it’s working on other devies. I have a pull request with a minor fix that will help a bit with the performance if it works, please give it another chance! ty

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
The atmosphere of the game is good. Really gives off a creepy dark forest vibe. I only ever came across a single enemy though, so it didn’t feel too difficult. The cubes seem random though which makes the game difficult to know how to escape. I could see a cube or sphere through the trees and head to it, but a lot of times I wouldn’t have enough time to make it between cubes. Hard to know if I picked the wrong direction or maybe had missed some along the way I should have picked up.

The game would lag a lot, typically before I was able to pick up a cube or sphere. Also the colors on the cubes didn’t come out very green on my machine so I didn’t know if it was a red enemy or not at first.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! Yes you re right, and the thing with red enemy is just to trick the players, there are no red enemies just black spiders :)) this way is more surprizing for a horror theme. I did a PR to fix the performance it may not lag now but still is not a 100% fix.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
The forest and fog look really creepy, great atmosphere, sadly I only ever saw a single red collectable after 5 attempts. I saw a few spiders and they look cool also.

The positives are; great potential, graphics look great, enemies look good. With some performance tweaks and more balanced gameplay would be onto something.

When you limit a game to 13 seconds you need fast paced gameplay, your game looks creepy and would be better slower paced with enemies coming out to scare you / fight.

Hope you come back next year or join the JS gamdev gamejam if they add a 13k challenge again!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback really appreciate it. Definitely I’ll keep making games and you’ll see me next year, this is just the beginning and already love it!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Has that instant hook to it wanting you to keep playing. Couldnt work it out at first but realised you could pick up the powerups to give extra time etc. On my last attempt I found a route that where the pick ups where quite linear so you could follow the path where I eventually escaped. Didnt notice the lag until I saw it mentioned. Love the blair witch style ending ;)

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thank you for your feedback! I am glad you didn’t encounter any lag.
