comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game was really atmospheric and spooky - just right with Halloween around the corner.

I had several fun tries with the game but ended up going round in circles or getting lost.

It played really slowly for me, the movements were glacial.

I found one sphere on my 4th attempt at the game, but it wasn’t green or red, and I couldn’t work out how to collect it, walking over it didn’t do anything. Perhaps make them glow or something would make them easier to find.

The little spider things are brutal.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: I am glad you had fun :D Yes, and I was afraid the spiders were not big enough :))

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: The game has a beautiful look to it. My machine also struggled to keep up with a playable frame rate, which is unfortunate, because it looks like it would be fun. Congrats on submitting for the first time, that is an achievement all by itself!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thank you! Next year I will do better :D

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Wow, this was hard! I struggled to make any decent progress and usually ran out of time quickly. The dark and eerie forest is really nicely made and there’s a real feeling of impending doom. Modest use of sounds, but what’s there really works!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thank you! Yes, it is supposed to be hard, and never exit the forest but in the same time doable :)) About the sounds I choose not to complicate things as I am not good at it.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: The forest looks and feels really scary! And the spiders make good on this promise! Maybe it’s a bit hard, but maybe I’m just not good enough to escape ;) Well done!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thank you! yes it’s a bit hard but after a few tries it’s doable

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: It looks awesome, very atmospheric! Unfortunately, on my machine it freezes every few seconds (not just a performance drop, but it just stops completely). Despite this, what I managed to play felt really good :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
The trees and the fog look great. Very spooky atmosphere!

I didn’t make it to midnight. Found some cubes, but never enough to outrun the clock.

The framerate is on the lower end of “ok” ( i7-12700H with an Nvidia RTX4060, so… a not too slow machine)

But it’s playable. And i tried to beat it quite often. :-)

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Really hard game, but a lot of kudos for building a FPS engine in less than 13KB.

13 seconds maybe are too few to start with, but this is the theme of the competition, so there’s nothing I can say but “good job”

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thank you I am glad you like it!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Very good sound effects and visual atmosphere which makes for a good horror-type of game. Unfortunately, the game performed very poorly on my PC with a 1070, which isn’t the greatest setup, but it was generally unplayable on Firefox and Chrome.

I hope you could work on some optimizations so that I could really enjoy the game. I did grab red spheres and green cubes, but before I could take advantage of it, the game would freeze up and time would run out.

A great game with a lot of potential!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Impressive visuals for just 13k! Enjoyed the text animations on the title screen (and the in-game instructions are appreciated).

Theme is integrated perfectly, and it’s extremely clear what you should be doing in the game. Great job.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I focused a lot on visuals for this one.

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: I like the wavery font and the visuals of the game. However, the game is running poorly in performance and the input lag is too bad…

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Yes I know next time I will take my time for proper testing, thank you!

comments.author: Tom Copeland
comments.text: Looks very cool! Performance is low, even on my pretty high powered desktop, but playable.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: I am glad to hear that you managed to play it, about the performance indeed I couldn’t made it better in the given time a lot to learn here!

api.name: DECIDOPHOBIA
api.description:
A betting game. Choose a scenery and bet for the winner. The winner will be chosen right before the timer hit 13 seconds (we do not want to scare anyone.)

Three won for a scenery unlocks endless/relaxing mode.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: It’s fun to see the “bet” and animations, expect who will win depending of their start position and a bit strategic :). but the 13 seconds waiting before start each bet is really annoying after some games.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Interesting concept, and fun to watch the simulations. I’d recommend removing the 13 second timer from the main menu, so you can jump right into the games. Nice variety of minigames, impressive!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Interesting submission with several mini games.

I don’t understand why we have to wait for 13 seconds in the main screen?

The game is missing, well, “game” elements. It’s essentially a pure luck game that you watch unfold.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Good job on the submission. Lots of minigames in such a small package.

As others have stated, removing the 13s wait before playing a game would be great. At first I thought the game was broken because nothing was happening and wasn’t showing the game.

The games have fun animations to watch, but other than that there isn’t much to do. Betting mostly comes down to luck, especially when you’re not sure what the rules of the game will be before you play so you don’t know what you’re betting on.

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
That’s interesting! I’ve the feeling that you added the theme after having your initial idea, and as others said the 13seconds delay before each bet feels too long.

The simulation are great to watch. And you’ve created many bets on a small game, that’s impressive. Nice work!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
An interesting game concept.

More chance than anything, so feels more like a simulation than a game.

The graphics were good and the movements smooth.

When two different items collided I wasn’t sure which they would both turn into, it sometimes seemed random, was there a chance element, or was it based on angles or relative speeds or something?

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Funny pastime, not exactly a game. Good use of emojis. But why do you have to wait 13s before the sequence starts ?

Also, the graphics are hardwired for a HD (1920x1024) display, and is difficult to use on lower resolutions (for instance, I could not bet on the last lines)

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: In this game you select which type of mini-game you want to bet on and then have to wait 13 seconds for it to start. To kick things off, I picked the first one - yin and yang. When it starts you pick the color to bet on. Because there are only two, it’s a 50/50 start. I picked white. Then the ‘simulation’ runs for 13 seconds. In this case, the white and black balls bounce around, changing the color of the grid as they go. The one with the highest % at the end wins. And that’s it. You go back to the menu, pick a different type of mini-betting game, wait another 13 seconds and ‘bet’ on another random outcome. There was no sound that I could hear and the game got stuck in some kind of loop of death a few times and I had to kill my tab. Aside from this and the painful forced 13 second wait before each mini-game starts, it was definitely an interesting take on the theme.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Thanks for submitting this game! I feel it is mostly for gamblers. As a player, you bet on the outcome of a mini-game that plays itself. It is a nice and unique concept, but it is mostly about…. waiting. And there is no sound or music to help you with that. I guess if you are a gambler, maybe you’ll enjoy it! I did for the first few rounds. Note: the back button when finishing a game is unresponsive. I thought it was broken.

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: It’s fun, I like the systems at play here. I get that the timer at the start was to match the theme, but it feels like it was at a detriment to the experience.

api.name: 13legions
api.description:
You’ve been captured by the enemy and thrown into a prison deep in the heart of an island. To reach the sea and escape, you must face 13 legions, each composed of 13 enemies. Enemies come in colors of red, yellow, and blue. In order to defeat them, you will need to use a sword with a matching color. Also, be mindful of the rocks falling from above. Choose one of the four paths you want to take, your choice will determine the difficulty of your journey.

Controls

  • W, ArrowUp, Space - Jump
  • A, ArrowLeft - Move left
  • D, ArrowRight - Move right
  • J - Attack (Red Sword)
  • K - Attack (Yellow Sword)
  • L - Attack (Blue Sword)

If needed, you can adjust the game’s window size using browser zoom. Also, the game has some sounds, so make sure to adjust the volume.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Such a cute little game! Nice attention to detail: the biomes are cool, main menu has the key bindings, I like how everything has names. The falling rocks is the right idea, forcing the player to move around.

Using WebGL for this game is an interesting choice, didn’t expect that. Had a good chuckle when I saw the ThirteenElements type. :)

Two things that I think can be improved:

  • The levels have high ground, but it’s not very useful. Maybe the enemies could jump to the platforms and then divebomb the player. Or the player character could have a ground slam attack, something like that. This would make each level’s layout have more impact on the gameplay.

  • Tangentially related to the previous point: in a game about fighting, I feel like the fighting could be more varied, for both the player and the enemies. For example, let’s say the blue sword isn’t a sword anymore, but a blaster. And blue enemies don’t come close to you, but instead fire arrows from the corners, something like that.

comments.author: Aleksander Szczepura
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! There could definitely be another enemy in the game, and I could probably squeeze out like 2 kilobytes for it (I had a lot of space left in the end and filled it with stuff like the logo in the main menu), but I ran out of time. In general, you are right in that the fighting could be more complex, the game can get boring quickly. When it comes to weapons, it was on purpose that there is only a sword (and the same enemy in three colors), I tried to save some space by having only one sprite instead of three, and applying the colors in the shader.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Fun beat’em up. At first I though I would suck with these three different sword attacks, but no, I even did a couple of perfectly cleared stages (would be nice to have some kind of bonus for that). I found that jumping on platforms don’t help if you are getting overwhelmed already.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Interesting concept.

Very colourful! Sound effects are good - even made it left and right audio (directional audio) (but probably a bit too far). The game stalled at the end after I died, and the sound continued.

I like the idea of the different coloured swords, but the controls are super intuitive. Once I got the hang of using both hands on the keyboard was a lot more fun. Wasn’t clear that I had all three swords from the start - but this is awesome.

The platforms didn’t really do much, I fact I could just stay on a platform whilst the enemies gathered below.

Some ideas to make this even cooler:

  • make the enemies able to jump on the platforms too
  • have a level indicator at the top so you know which level you’re up to
  • have a way to pause the game (I “paused” it by standing on a ledge)
  • have each sword make a different sound effect
  • press down to get off platform
  • when die have a pop up screen to either play again, restart that level etc.

I liked that the rocks falling got faster for some levels - made it fun and challenging. The sword sounded more like a whip and really reminded me of Indiana Jones; which is def not a bad thing!

Background colours might be a bit too much for some players; especially those with colour sensitivity. Overall, great game though. Found myself playing till the end. Add a little bit of game music and this could be the final touch (I know it might not have fit in the <13kB though!)

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
Interesting concept with the different-colored enemies.

I found the platforms quite useless since enemies can’t jump.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: interesting game, good idea to have weapon useful for every kind of enemy. platforms seems useless as enemies can’t jump

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to attack the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Interesting game mechanic with the matching colors for swords and foes.

What a pitty, that the enemies can not jump on the platforms, and that they are not affected by the fallig rocks. Would be cool, if the player could use the pushback to use the rocks for additional damage.

Different behaviours of the enemies also would add much to the gameplay. Currenty they are all the same: just moving towards the player and eventually hit with their sword.

If the player would start with only one sword and the different colors would be added in later levels, that would automagically create a story in the game. :-)

However: solid game entry!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: I have to admit, I ended up playing this for far longer than I expected :) In this game you control a little sword wielding player who can dash left and right and swipe his sword. 13 baddies rush at you, in one of 3 different colors. Hit them a few times with the right colored sword (use JKL to change your sword color) and they’ll die. Survive 13 levels of this and I assume you win the game (I only made it to level 5). I liked that the levels had variety - they changed color, the attack waves felt different, the hits required to kill them felt different. Some levels have rocks falling from above that you should avoid, although for some reason the baddies are impervious to them. The graphics and sound are basic, but work and are kind of charming in a retro way. The only thing I couldn’t quite understand was why the levels had platforms you could jump onto. The baddies don’t follow you up there, so all you do is run around above them while they carry on spawning in until all 13 of them are going crazy down below. Perhaps you need to use it on later levels? But I didn’t in the first 5 I played. Overall, I enjoyed my time with this little swash-buckler.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Clever idea, if the knockback was more would create some room to hit one and then the other.

Some powerups would be great, maybe a rainbow sword.

Nice graphics, simple and fun!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on finishing a game. I tried but I didn’t manage to arrive to the end of the game after the 4th level in 3 attempts.
  • I like that you gave lore to the game and you have these different levels with different names. The levels really did feel different.
  • A big issue I had from the 4th level on, I think due the health amount of the legion, they started to pile up and I didn’t have any means of separating them. I also couldn’t hit multiple colours at the same time. So effectively I would take a hit from one of the colors and just finally die. And I was on the easy mode :D

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text:
Simple katana-switching game that with a bit of effects and juice it can be pretty fun.

I managed to the get to 8th I think and the enemy just stack on top of each other, making it hard to know which sword to use and being hit becomes unavoidable. Having the enemies collide with each other would be a good choice.

Also the jump is too sharp. I would like some on-air time so that the character is really jumping.

Looking forward to more gimmicks in the post-compo

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
A cool little slasher that reminds me of classic Atari games.

I get that the theme was integrated with the counts of legions and enemies, but I wish the theme was involved with the core mechanic of the game (the color-switching/attacking) in some way.

A very playable game with lots of charm. Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game was fun and played well. Needing to remember which colour sword was which button was fun.

I liked how the attack sound effect moved from left to right depending on which way you were facing.

Luckily I was able to avoid the falling rocks, so never found out what happened. Although the enemies seemed immune ?

The animation of fighting was good, maybe it would be good if the enemies also had an animation when the swung their swords?

Also it would be good if the enemies chased you onto the platforms.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: good work on submitting the game in the challenge keep it up. scroll screen distrubt seeing everything in canvas

api.name: plusminus13
api.description:
13 is bad, but can be good too. Just like this game. Probably needs a non-potato computer and mobile is not supported.

WASD or arrow keys for movement, spacebar for shield.

Music theme by Zarja. Play testing: Aleks and Zarja. Thank you!

This is a tech demo of sorts, of a global illumination approach developed by Alexander Sannikov, called radiance cascades. This particular game’s engine is a raw WebGL port of Jason McGhee’s Three.js based blog post. I’ll be releasing this mini engine as standalone soon.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
This one looks fantastic! These radiance cascades is my favorite thing now.

The music speeding up is a great touch and contributes beautifully to the tension.

This is my favorite “need to take it apart and learn how it’s done” game this year. I think you’ve mentioned releasing the renderer as a standalone library, please ping me when you do! :)

comments.author: xem
comments.text: I see someone had fun with lighting :p

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Amazing lighting tech! The optical presentation and the music add a lot to the quite simple gameplay. And the highscores at the end motivate to give it another try. And another,… :-)

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: the lighting is so cool! the soft shadows and glows and shines on the floor really knocked my socks off. this would be amazing at any size, and is doubly so for how small the code is for such a cool entry. it ran nice and smoothly on my old pc! awesome work. I’d love to see this engine used for a cyberpunk style game that is mostly dark except for long lines of glowy neon!! I bet it would look so cool using your tech. =) keep up the great work.

comments.author: WHITE6982
comments.text: The neon lighting is looks Good and the music is also Great.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Looks great, the best graphics of all the game so far! Nice mucic also. There must be so many games this type of look and feel could be used for!

Deserves a greater than 13k version!

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Fun music and sound effects. Loving the graphics.

I liked that the levels started easier and got harder and faster; I feel like the music got faster with the faster animations which was really cool. Nice work on this game, it was super fun. Lovely lighting effects, good smooth animations, intuitive controls, and overall great job.

Game over screen is good, maybe add a 60% background so can easily read the text over the rest of the game. If you wanted to make it even better (although you’re right on the 13kB limit), add an intro screen and it’d add that extra cherry on top.

Good GitHub README, add a few extra pieces of info and I’d be perfect.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The lighting was impressive.

Unfortunately, the framerate on my PC was too low on this game to be able to manoeuvre the character towards the other game pieces.

Still it did look great

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Sorry about that - it’s quite resource intensive, unfortunately. I hope you can return some time in the future with a beefier GPU and, after you’re done winning in a fully path-traced Doom or something, play a round of plusminus13 :). Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice looking game. My final score was 384 (669). It was really fun to watch your progress on Slack and see this come together. Also educational, as I had to read about radiance cascades after seeing the screenshots 😀 Great job, congrats!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: impressive lightning effects! Gameplay is simple but funny. Having a music is cool too!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Radiance cascades FTW ! :)

Small feedback: I have a somewhat beefy computer and it still run below 30 fps - I suggest downsampling it a bit (it will still look awesome setting probes each 2 or 4 pixels apart). I know it might not be trivial but having minimal settings would help a lot too.

And I do not have color blindness but it was hard to deciphering the glyphs anyways - using a different color palette might be helpful.

Having said that, I like a lot the way you managed to fit the theme in the game and the game’s engine looks very cool. Well done! :)

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Nice one!

Unfortunately, my laptop couldn’t run it smooth enough to enjoy it haha I have an integrated GPU card, yet it’s a good laptop. But I think you can definitely optimize the performance of the game, maybe removing some effects? Otherwise you’re not gonna have a lot of players :)

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: It looks really cool, but i’m not able to play either on my integrated GPU, and thus can’t vote. I literally get 1FPS on FIrefox :’) Maybe you could add a “low graphics” button for us common folk?

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Yeah, apologies about the performance requirements! Radiance cascades are more performant than alternatives, or, in other words, they provide much higher quality at a given performance cost, but they are still an active area of research and there’s not that much that can be done here without severely degrading the quality of the image.

In a perfect world, had I not been pressed for time, I would have implemented proper scaling, so that low perf setups could at least play at a low resolution in a small window. Currently the size of all elements is absolute, making smaller window sizes unplayable.

However, since JS13K is, like diamonds, forever :), I hope you’ll be able to revisit this game at a later point and play it in all its beauty.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Very cool visual effect. I remember from last year, you games a very technical, which is interesting. Would like to have a look to the postmortem, if you write one.

Congrats!

api.name: rollermaze
api.description:
You play as a cuboctahedron on the run. You have only 13 seconds to get from the drop point to the safety of the woods before you’re caught.

Some paths are blocked and will need to be opened up using the nearest button.

For longer distances you’ll need to find a hidden safe spot which will reset the timer.

Be careful of the grey sentinel cubes, if you touch them they become active and will send you back to your drop point, then chase you.

Controls are :

  • Keyboard - Movement … Cursors / WASD (or equivalently positioned keys, ZQSD etc) / ZX;.
  • Mouse - Movement … Click then drag in a cardinal direction to roll that way.
  • Touchscreen - Movement … Touch then drag to roll that way.

Decentralized challenge : OP Guild (Sentinel NPC)

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Good use of the theme and nice 3D

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thanks for playing, hope you liked it and got all the way to the end.

Did you notice that I used your microW engine for a WebGL starting point?

comments.author: Asiel Díaz Benítez
comments.text: in a mobile/touchscreen it is a bit hard to move fast so it is hard to make it in time

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
There are hidden time resets dotted around the bigger levels to make it easier (possible), but there are also dead-ends and buttons you don’t need to press.

All 25 of the levels are completable on mobile and desktop, you might just need to plan your route and be ready holding a diagonal direction in advance of a corner.

Thanks for the feedback and for playing

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Very good 3D! The game is fun to play (not too difficult, not too easy), the controls are intuitive and I love the airplane moving around :) Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you. I appreciate that you found the difficulty about right, as that’s always something tricky to get right - keeping the player interested by it being fair whilst giving them something to work out.

I was thinking originally of having the airplane circle the playing area from above, but I prefer the back and forth patrolling.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice 3D graphics, propper retro 3D! Some challenging levels, but all fun. Those cubes are quite tricky when they catch you out!

One of them caught me at the begining on a later level and I had to start over as It cornered me and the time stayed on 1s.

Over all a well made game, fun, challenging but also sort of relazing with the music!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you. I’ve always liked the Virtua Racing style of 3D graphics, but that period in gaming didn’t last long before texture mapping came along.

Yeah, some of the levels were impossible due to their size and the speed of rolling so I added a few hidden timer resets just as you were about to run out of time. So larger levels may require some planning on which route you take, and some have red herrings, like buttons you don’t need to press :)

Music is something I want to get better at, I struggled with the user-action permissions on touch devices, and I’d like to make it sound more like an instrument than a sin wave, haha. Perhaps I’ll take notes from other entries.

Thanks for playing

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Nice entry, and it fits the theme well! The game has a good variety of levels.

I really like the combination of time, maze, safe spots and the concept of grey cubes. Especially how sometimes you can use the grey cubes to teleport back to the starting point, which can be helpful but also increase the risk of being caught. I really enjoyed that mechanic!

The music is a bit repetitive at the moment. And at the beginning, players might not fully understand the concept of the safe area. Perhaps providing an sfx to guide players could make it clearer.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you so much for your kind words and valuable feedback.

I’m glad you found it fun to play and that the balance and variety was about right.

So the grey cubes were the sentinels, silently guarding the maze tracks, when you roll into them they activate and seek you out. As part of this activation they push you back to the place you drop into the level with a fresh 13 seconds.

Some of the levels may be easier if you deliberately activate a sentinel to place you back at the start and then you can go a different way…

Yes the music was quite short on a loop, I need to get better at doing music and so I’ll have to look into how other entries do it. I like the thought of doing a music tracker type instrument.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Well, I have no idea how many levels I played, but I saw a level that said “JS13KB” which is pretty cool! And after that I played a few more rounds and I think I would have played like that all evening, because it is very fun, simple (in an emotional sense, which is good for me, because I already have enough :)), but unfortunately at some point I just got combined with the green cube and there was 1 second left on the timer, then another 1 cube approached me and now we have a triangle-party :D.

Well, a little feedback. I thought in advance that I could fall off the tracks, but it turned out that I could not fall, so at some point I started cheating, I saw on the horizon cubes that were always located in the center of the road, so I flew along the edge of the road holding two keys, forward and to the side, thereby guaranteeing that I will almost never collide with a cube :). I think as a possible improvement, the ability to fall off the track could be added, but this can play both ways, so it remains to be seen.

It would be nice to also be able to turn the music on/off, which is actually quite pleasure, but-but :). And to have a menu with a description of the controls and a description of the game, although in reality this does not change the fact that it is a very good game! (just don’t always read game comments).

I also liked the airplane and the animation of hitting the forest, the airplane was especially pleasing to the eye :).

I also noticed that colliding with cubes gives you back time, so sometimes I specifically collided with them when I didn’t have time, until I realized that there are special timer recovery zones, and actually even then :D.

It seems on a few levels that were quite large and had a lot of cubes, the initialization of the level seemed a bit laggy, but during the actual game everything was fine (chrome browser).

I also read your postmortem where I liked the idea of resting before continuing the game (the timer starts when the movement starts).

Very good, I had fun, congratulations!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you for playing my game and thanks for such a comprehensive review with some fantastic feedback.

I made 25 levels in total, they do get a bit harder as you progress.

I was going for a vibe like super monkey ball but one of the things I wanted to change about that was falling off the track, hence my desire to keep your character on it.

I agree the music can get a bit repetitive after a while. So being able to turn it off would be good.

I also take onboard the comment about menu or instructions, if it was standalone that would be essential. Being able to see what level you are on and how many are left would help. All the levels have names after all, I just didn’t show them.

The level switching shouldn’t have been too slow, all I do is copy a tiny 2D map and generate the models. All the triangles data is sent via WebGL to render 60 times a second.

But thank you so much for all the feedback.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice job with webgl graphics. TIL “cuboctahedron” 😂 Some pretty tough levels, even with the hidden resets. I think it might have been nice for the checkpoints to be visible, so that the player can plan on how to use them. I particularly enjoyed the jet model and the level complete celebration animation. Great job!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you for playing and providing feedback.

I think my theory was with the 13 seconds countdown, that you would try to race for the finish, so this was the thing you were always working against. But knowing it could be reset by another 13 seconds by knowing where the reset points were might take away from the whole fear of 13 :)

Thanks

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
I appreciate that the countdown didn’t start until after I started moving. Controls were smooth, and the grey sentinel cubes provided interesting challenges at times.

The game did lag for me a lot. Was using Firefox on Mac. I also felt the transitions between levels or the game over were held for a bit too long. I thought the game had frozen at first.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thanks for your comments and feedback.

I wasn’t able to test on a Mac as I don’t have one.

Perhaps I could have included a little countdown timer between levels or game over. I think it was 13 seconds when you complete the whole game before you can continue, 6 seconds when you fail a level for you restart it and 5 seconds when you complete each level.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Cool 3D. Camera follow well and as we can’t fall from borders it make it safe to move. Levels are mostly the same in the end I think, but quite a lot for the size limit

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thanks for your feedback, and thanks for playing.

I think if I were to do more on the game, then yeah more complicated levels with more elements would be something I’d look at.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: nice puzzle game but very easy, the plane is a nice touch and animation of goal is also very cool, trees also give a nice addition. well done on 3d game

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you for your feedback.

I hand made the 3D models myself. The plane was created from a concept sketch that I measured pixel by pixel in 3 axis using orthographic projection.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Impressive 3D graphics. Especially the airplane. I don’t understand how it’s possible to make a 3D game working in less than 13kB. I thought webGL code takes up too much space. Guess I have to take a look in the repo to learn.

I didn’t understand what the stationary boxes did. I think they makes me respawn, and reset the timer, and the box itself becomes an enemy, but I’m, still not sure.

It would be cool to see some more variations, but the levels were increasing in difficulty which is nice, and the controls felt good.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Hi, thanks so much for the feedback.

I used Xem’s micro 3D library which uses some browser built-in transformation functions intended for CSS.

I had loads of space left as it only came in at 8.4kb so I intend to make a much bigger 3D game next time.

The stationary box is a sentinel NPC which stands guard trying to block your path, when you touch them you get flung back to your start point and they become activated - so they glow, chase you and the background darkens. They make it harder for you to get around.

Since the levels are just 2D courses I can’t really do height, but that’s something that would be fun for future dev.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice game, reminds my of “Marble Madness”, from the good old days of Amiga computers :)

The 3d-graphics look nice and clean, is the pixelated style intentional? Or just because of the smaller canvas? Anyways: the pixels add something good to the nice visuals.

The classical music of Schubert is an interesting choice, and matches nicely :-)

Good job!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback. I’ve only played marble madness briefly but I can see the similarity. Glad it brought back memories for you.

Yes the pixelated style is intentional to make it feel old-school. It’s a combination of restricted canvas size and crisp non-aliased edges.

My grandfather was a big fan of Schubert, plus the trout is the same tune my washing machine plays when it’s done :)

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Good use of theme, nice graphics and interesting gameplay. I sort of wish it would be possible to roll off into the abyss for some extra difficulty, but it might also become too difficult then.

And: oh, those are secret resets, I was certain it was a bug! :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you for playing and for your feedback.

My idea with the secret time resets was to get you to rush and feel a sense of fear for the 13 seconds to drain away making you concentrate harder on getting to your goal.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I really dig the art style. The fighter jet going around is so pointlessly cool, the animations are so smooth, the lighting is also nice. You clearly put attention to detail even though the game is quite simple. Besides the jet, the game could use with more animated parts, be it the buttons, the gray cubes, the trees..

Gameplay wise, I think the game feels too rigid. I can only move in a grid and only in 90 degree angles. I cannot fall and the only danger are the sentinels, which are very rarely a cause for concern since they are easily avoided because I move perfectly along the grid.

It wasn’t obvious what the pink squares did, since they don’t really look like buttons, and for give any feedback that they have been pressed. Most of the times, the button is right on the path to the blocked path, so the button is pointless.

The music is cute, but a bit flat and repetitive.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thanks for reviewing my game and providing feedback.

Yes I need to work on my music skills in games, I would like to make more of a synth feel with more depth.

I did originally think a jump action on the buttons would be good, but it took up too much time and the 13 seconds restriction on the levels (or level segments) was harder to reach than just rolling over them.

I agree it’s a bit rigid, this is partly due to my limited 3D maths skills because the model rotations required were a little complex.

I hoped that the tiny early tutorial levels would make players explore a bit as they had enough time to try out what would happen when they went over the button tiles, and got blocked by the dark barrier tiles.

Yes the buttons were often on the paths, but as the levels progressed towards #25 there are decoy buttons, or ones where you have to go a long way off the path, so need to plan your route and hunt hidden reset tiles.

Thanks

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: rollermaze immediately catches the eye with its bold and clean visual design. The vibrant purple background, combined with the simple, geometric trees and checkerboard path, gives it a retro 3D look that feels playful and engaging. The bright, contrasting colors make the scene pop, and the use of basic shapes keeps the game visually straightforward, allowing the player to focus on the gameplay. While the graphics are charming in their simplicity, adding a bit more variation to the environment, such as different textures or more complex shapes, could enhance the player’s immersion. The aesthetic can still maintain its minimalist appeal while introducing a bit more variety in the obstacles and background elements. Additionally, more dynamic lighting or shading effects could give the game a more polished look, making the world feel more three-dimensional and alive. The visual style is refreshingly simple, and this approach works well for a game that aims to keep the player focused on the mechanics. The nostalgic, low-poly style evokes the charm of early 3D games, and with some added visual depth or complexity, rollermaze could stand out even more. Keep up the great work, and it’s clear you have a good sense of design that complements the gameplay!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thank you so much for your review and feedback.

I was definitely going for the simple flat shaded, old-school 3D from the Virtua-Racing type era, with bright low poly graphics, in a pixelated and retro way.

I also had the idea in the back of my mind of SuperMonkeyBall where you roll around a course - but are able to fall off the edges.

I hope that by keeping the player on the course, focused the mind on getting to the destination within the time limit.

I will take on board your feedback about having more texture, effects and variation. I was running out of ideas for how to use the same gameplay elements but in different ways with the 25 levels I made.

Thank you

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Simple and interesting. I was expecting the timer to reset when touching the buttons and I got confused when it didnt happend.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Thanks for playing and for giving feedback.

So the levels were all meant to only have a 13 second timer, but as they grew it became impossible to complete them in the time limit, so I added some invisible timers which gave another 13 seconds just as your time was about to expire if you followed an optimal route.

Since the buttons were part of the challenge of getting to the end they didn’t need to reset the timer.

But I get your point about having more obvious feedback that the time has changed and what it was that changed it.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Congrats! This is a fun game. The controls feel pretty good and the game is a solid entry! At the beginning I thought I was gonna fall, but then I realized that doesn’t happen, which is good. Good luck!

api.name: Blind Fear
api.description:
Captain Valen Aster is a skilled pilot, but he hides a crippling fear: triskaidekaphobia, an irrational terror of the number 13. This fear doesn’t just haunt his mind—it warps his vision. When triggered, Valen’s field of view narrows, making it harder to see and react to threats.

Years ago, Valen barely escaped an attack in the 13th Sector, where his crew was lost. Ever since, his phobia has grown worse. Now, he’s drawn back into that cursed region, where enemy waves attack relentlessly and asteroids tumble through space. The number 13 appears everywhere, threatening to trigger his vision loss at the worst moments.

To survive, Valen must control his fear and stay sharp as his vision narrows. Will he escape the sector’s curse, or will his phobia blind him to his doom?

Use the arrow or WASD keys to control your ship and space to shoot. Get the power-ups to increase your fire power or reload your shield.

This game is a tribute to those classic space shooters available on 8 bit consoles. Have fun!

Credits:

  • Game and code: Marco Fernandes
  • Graphics: Gustavo Vituri https://gvituri.itch.io/space-shooter
  • Music: Unknown author (taken from https://keithclark.github.io/ZzFXM)
  • A shout out to Steven Lambert, creator of KontraJS, a game engine where I took a lot of inspiration and some code 🤭.

comments.author: sdeseille
comments.text:
There are so many good contributions. This one is in the top 3 for me. Good music, good sound effects. The ship is fun to control. Action is intense but not unplayable. Clean execution. I like this game too.

The concept of reduced field of vision linked to the theme is really brilliant.

comments.author: Marco Fernandes
comments.text: Thank you for your feedback. I’m glad that you liked it! 😉

I also feel like I’ve heard this exact song in a lot of games. Still liked the pixel art though and played for a bit. I just think some different music and different ship behavior would make this a really fun game.

I personally find it more challenging and fun but I do understand that others aren’t really fond of it 😅 I just hadn’t the space and time to include an option for the player to choose the ship behaviour, oh well, next time 😉 About the music, yeah, no original there but I think it fits well in the game 😎

I like the graphics and the enemy patterns are really nice.

This could be a classic. Love the visuals, the music, the sound effects, the power ups! It all fits well together for a really smooth gameplay.

With that said, there’s nothing truly innovative about the game, even the music is repurposed.

7704 I think I scored, had a few goes! Really enjoyed the game, Any reason you did not use the full screen size? Could have used more of the window space.

comments.text: Wow, awesome. High score 10812. A good reminder that the classics had rock solid gameplay mechanics, and still hold up. You triggered my fear of 13, the limited visibility mechanic is tough. This is a super polished experience. Excellent combination of pixel art, sound effects, and music. Nice job!

The control of the ship would feel better if it was tightened up, increased drag. For the music, since you are re-using example tune, it would be good to remix either the sequence or just changing up the zzfx instruments to better fit the gameplay. Overall, great work, I totally could see this being in arcade cabinet.

I loved the pixel graphics.

The parallax stars worked really well.

It felt like an old-school arcade game being played in an emulator

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Great twist with the “tunnel vision”, adds a different element to the flow of the game.

The game was really well polished in terms of visuals as well. The graphics and the FX look great.

Overall, quite fun to play. The game rewards the player when they perform well, since if they collect enough power-ups they feel unstoppable.

comments.text:
Absolutely amazing! Wow this brings me back :-)

Love the beat, and the whole gameplay is seriously addictive.

Great entry!

api.name: Thirteen Truco
api.description:
Clone of the classic card game Truco! One major difference: a card numbered 13!

This card loses to any other card. However, it mimics any card in the deck until you play it. Only when you drop it does it reveal it as a 13.

You might think you have a strong hand, only to discover that you were better off not raising the stakes with such a weak card.

But beware - don’t let triskaidekaphobia get the best of you!

How to Play

The game is played with four players: two opponents and one teammate, whom you can distinguish by their colors.

At the start of each round, you receive three cards. To play a card, drag it forward to drop it into the current round. The other players will do the same.

The highest card wins that step of the round. Next steps starts by the player who won the last step. Repeat this process until all cards are played. You win the round if:

  • You win two steps.
  • You win the 1st step and draw in either the 2nd or 3rd step.
  • You draw the 1st step and win the next step.

You can also drag a card backward to discard it as a hidden card and hide your strategy.

Card Hierarchy

The cards are ranked as follows, from lowest to highest: 13, 4 to 7, Q, J, K, 1, 2, and 3.

Trump Cards

In each round, a “turned card” determines the trump cards - cards that beat all others. The trump card is the card immediately following the turned card in rank.

Unlike regular cards, trump cards don’t tie. They override all other cards and each other, depending on suit. The suit order is as follows: diamonds, spades, hearts, and clubs.

  • Turned card: 4 → Trump cards: 5♦, 5♠, 5♥, 5♣
  • Turned card: 7 → Trump cards: Q♦, Q♠, Q♥, Q♣
  • Turned card: 3 → Trump cards: 4♦, 4♠, 4♥, 4♣ (instead of 13 - remember, triskaidekaphobia)

Raising the Stakes

If you think you have strong cards, you can raise the stakes! Each round typically awards one point, but raising the stakes increases the value to 3, 6, 9, or even 12 points.

The 1st making 12 points wins!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text:
Oo, I finally won! I’ve never played Truco before, so there are a bunch of things to learn. First, the card hierarchy is quite different from any card game I’ve played before, including subtle changes such as Jack being higher than Queen. Second, the trump card is decided by flipping one extra card up, while most games it is determined by the first card played. Third, I didn’t really understand that the “13” card only becomes a 13 after being played, which is quite sneaky. At first I didn’t like the 13 mechanic, but then it started working in my favor, so I decided that I liked it in the end, haha. After getting through the learning curve, this game was quite fun. I imagine this could be a really fun fast paced drinking game. Nice job!

For anyone else reading this, the Wikipedia page on Truco is quite interesting, and helped me understand the game: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Truco

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Yeah, I explored some of these challenges in the postmortem (https://github.com/michelts/thirteen-truco/blob/main/docs/POSTMORTEM.md). It contains video-demonstrations that could be useful for those who are not familiar with the rules.

This game is a great choice to break the ice on parties!

Thanks for the review and also for the vite plugin you wrote, that was really helpful! :)

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I like the feel and look of the game. The sounds are great too! I have it admit that I didn’t“t know if I would like the surprise element of the cards at first, but after fully understanding everything it ended up being okay!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Yeah, the surprise element makes it a little trickier :)

Thanks for the reply!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun card game.

I’ve not heard of truco before, it’s an interesting combination of luck and skill. The first round I played I won without realising why. I got better as I reviewed the scoring system.

The graphics were good, and felt like playing a real game of cards.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: I’m happy you liked the graphics! Thanks for the feedback Jasper!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice graphics and 3d simulation. I’d never played Truco before, it took some time to understand a bit. 13 can arrive anytime I think, nice suspens addition to fit the theme

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Fitting the theme was a bit challenging, I hope a 13 coming at any time could cause some fear :D

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Pretty graphics! It was hard to track the hierarchy and get used to not using an spanish deck (Truco is pretty much the national card game around here (Argentina)) but once I got around it it was a nice implementation! Just a quick tip: It looks weird when you drag and you can see the text elements being selected. Add user-select: none; to your body css to prevent this!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
I thought of using the Spanish cards look and feel, which I think is very prettier, but it would be probably more challenging for players not used to that card style to grasp it.

I have friends who struggle to play truco when I use a Spanish deck myself :)

Thanks for the suggestion about preventing selecting text!

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: I do not know what does the revealed card under the deck means. It is my first time of Truco so I dont know what does it mean by “raising the stakes” tho. I have never drawn the 13 card so I never get to experience the “mimic” function which sounds fun. Otherwise, I like the table graphics you need and the nice title screen you make here. This is like a fun card game I can keep figuring it out.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
I really missed a tutorial mode to help addressing these questions. This is post compo improvement I want to include ;)

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Trying to play Truco feels extremely weird without the Spanish deck (and all the missing “nomenclature” like envido and such, which I honestly don’t know how to translate into english). Also I feel this is one of those games you really need to play with other humans to fully enjoy it.

I like a lot the idea of the added mechanics with the card 13 but I feel it could make more sense in a game that is more commonly known (and better fits the French deck).

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
I see what you mean about the Spanish deck, I might add it as an option in the post-competition version. This makes sense on Europe. Here in Brazil, on the other hand, the French deck is more popular. Its funny that my friends had a hard time understanding my Spanish deck :)

The rules are a bit different from other variations, probably a lot different from the Spanish one. Even here in Brazil, there are several variations. I recorded a video with a quick demo in case you are curious!

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Nice game entry! Nice graphics and controls.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
The graphic, look and feel is really impressive. It could be a casino game already. The sound effects are also nice, but I had to turn off the music (thanks for adding buttons, that’s something I need to do myself.)

The controls are good, but it was sometimes difficult to drag the card on the board, maybe having larger “collision” boxes would help with that.

The video with how to play the game is helpful, because I don’t know the rules at all, but I still didn’t understand why I won sometimes. Maybe it would be easier if I knew Truco. I think card games are fun, and I would like to play this game more once I have a good grasp of the rules.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Theres some really interesting and obscure html/css/js going on here. Lots of little details I havent seen implemented before - like the cards blurring out. And I noticed the text was highlightable on the cards, even though they are rendered in a fake-3D perspective. So TIL there is a ‘skew’ method in CSS.

I have never heard of ‘Truco’ either, so a bit to learn there. I like how you added in the curveball with 13 too, which really makes you fear the number 13. So a solid use of the theme.

Thanks for rating my game too, fun to see another poker-like game in this gamejam!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Woaw, it is a good looking card game! I was not familiar with Truco, but you did a good job porting it and adding your own twist on top of it. It has sounds, fine controls, AI, 2 player mode, etc. For a game that is less than 13kb, it is quite comprehensive! Thank you for submitting it.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: I’m not familiar with the game Truco and although I read the instructions several times I still didn’t really understand why I would win and lose some rounds. So I can’t comment on how accurate it is to the card game. However, consider this is 13KB and you managed to have an AI player, or multi player, music, sound effects, a lovely 3D style game board, nice graphics and what felt like a solid core mechanic there - that alone is just amazing. And for a change, it was on-theme! Unlike so many entries this year you genuinely did fear the number 13 appearing.

api.name: The Fear Factory
api.description:
All Demons have one thing in common, on their dreaded 13th birthday they must face their fears! This little demon must tackle 13 levels of spikes, ghosts, jumps, and falls to prove their worth.

Music by AdamTheWilliams

Controls

If you get stuck at all use the reload level button.

Mobile Controls

  • Direction: On-screen analogue stick
  • A: Reload Level
  • B: Jump

Keyboard Controls

  • Move: WASD or Arrow Keys
  • Jump: Spacebar
  • Close Text: Spacebar
  • Reload: Reload Level
  • Mute Music: M

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: The logic of some games truly can be repetitive but I think I saw those characters from another game which is a house game place.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Which are stolen from another game?

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Game looks good and sounds great, but unfortunately the platforming just didn’t work for me. I seem to jump past things a lot, or get stuck in the geometry on the other side of the platform, or fall into the space between platforms and have to restart.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thanks for trying the game, the collision testing on the platforms is not perfect; it’s mostly about timing. Watching the shadow gives a hint when you are above the platform. Those edge cases could really be ironed out to make this a better experience.

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
Really enjoyed it, nice work! I made it two the final room and I overcame my fears :)

I was afraid I’d have a lot more trouble with platforming and controls, but in fact, I managed just fine and I found that the character handles pretty well. Playing on keyboard, I mostly had to press two arrows for each move to align the jumps with the world’s isometric grid. I then connected a gamepad which turned out to be a much more natural input method for this kind of game.

The attention to detail here is great: the particle effect, the encouraging words of the narrator, the wobbly floor effect after clearing a level. And the soundtrack is part dark, part trippy — a great choice which matches the gameplay and the neon visuals.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you for playing and the detailed feedback!

I should have said the game has controller support. As always could be improved in many ways but happy you made it through.

comments.author: Antonio Salvati
comments.text: nice graphics, fun to play, levels are challenging now, definitely needs more levels to play with :P

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, level design is always the hardest part, it’s something I’d like to improve on.

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Good music and nice sound effects. Clean graphics and nice particles. One problem for me was that the player couldn’t move in a direction that matched the isometric tiles. I got stuck with the keyboard trying to press the two arrows at the same time to jump.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: The controls on isometric are challenging; for mobile I tried to make it easier, also asked in Slaxk preference on movement; I should have added an option for iso movement on keyboard. Thanks for the feedback

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Neat game, nice to see your gfx-style from last year again (Fort Knight) I had troubles landing on raised platforms, but managed to progress after some retries. Hard to tell, but it seems, that the player does not bounce off the right side of a pile of stacked platfroms, which looks like jumping through it.

Moody music, and I played quite a while. Good entry!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you for playing!

I’ve been trying to find a style that people recognise in my games; if someone plays and thinks it might be one of my games then that is great! I will have some new characters for next year!

Platform code needs to be rewritten; it’s not ideal and requires the player to perfect it (unfairly). There were a few edge cases that allowed the player to pass through a block. I added retry due to that bug.

Thanks

comments.author: Jesper Rasmussen
comments.text: Great game. Awesome graphics and style. A few problems with falling thru some platforms. But, highly enjoyable :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you Jesper, I a determined to rewrite the isometric code and address the clipping issue, just ran out of time sadly.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Nice stuff!

  • Good tie-in with the theme
    • Narrator also helps with this
  • Awesome music
  • Great graphics
  • Overall nice gameplay and level variety
  • Agree with some previous commenters that the character seems to clip through the platform at times

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Those pesky edge cases! Thanks for the feedback, plenty of positives :D

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Got stuck in between the platforms just below the key at level 3. Similar thing happened on the second stage with ghosts on the taller platforms. The game requires quite some coordination effort from player - I could not play with one hand at all, but moving with one hand and jumping with the other was ok. I feel like it could work also with fixed 4 straight diagonal directions and movement with one key only.. like Right Arrow triggering down-right movement, Down Arrow triggering down-left, etc.? At least the level design suggests that you don’t really need to move straight up or right but only diagonally.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I did implement this movement and took it out, lesson learned here is to add options for the player. Isometric looks good but has some design decisions to make that really do affect the players.

One issue with being the coder and the tester is getting too good at your own game, I did have my daughter test also but then she plays a lot of Roblox and is good at the parkour type games.

Next time will get more testing in early on!

Thanks for playing and the feedback.

comments.author: sdeseille
comments.text:
I really like the fact that the player never loses the game. The ability to restart the level when the player gets stuck at a certain point on the board is also very useful.

The music suits the pace of the game. The special effects and graphics are magnificent.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, a lot of games now are about completing games no matter how many tries; I didn’t think punishing people for losing X times was worth while. I was hoping people would complete the game.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: Great graphics, and the character is absolutely adorable! I love how you handle the animation of ‘death’ and revive the character. The tutorial is also well done, guiding the player smoothly through the gameplay. One minor issue is that it’s a bit tricky to tell if the character has landed on a higher platform due to the isometric perspective.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Platforms were the tricky thing to get right and they need work!

The death animation was going to be a ghost floating up, I put the speed too high and loved it 😊

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Don’t fall into the void. I a-voided it. Gotta love the comedy (and the techno vibe.)

Impressive that you’ve made an isometric 2D game, with several layers, making it feel 3D. My only problem was that I got stuck inside of the platforms a lot of times. I think I learned when it happened, so I was able to a-void that too, and completed the game.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thanks for playing; each year I try to improve the overall game; but each year I don’t quite get one part right 😅

The platforms are my nemesis this time around.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text:
I had fun playing this. Really like the isometric approach, great gfx and sfx, too. Main character animations are great!

Occasionally was tricky landing on higher platforms but overall a fun, challenging game.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thanks for playing and the feedback :D

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I did it! The 2nd to last level is a doozy. It was fun to watch your progress on this over the course of the competition, it’s clear that you put a lot of hard work into it.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thanks Cody, a lot of effort, way too late into the night and early in the morning :D

glad you enjoyed the process, battled with those platforms and didnt quite get that right! Level design is really tough.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I’m happy with the overall game. It takes a little getting used to on the platform jumps but everything is fun!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thanks Kenny, glad you had fun! I cannot ask for more than that :D

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
The music and sound really stood out to me.

Although a little buggy, the isometric physics system was awesome.

Controls were a little hard to use, and the physics system glitching was a little frustrating.

But overall, awesome job.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
2024 is the year of the glitchy isometric platform, I live and learn! I will work on the rendering and physics side of this game as it could have been a lot better for sure!

Thanks

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Loving the 8-bit graphics, pink and mint colours go nicely together. Love a good introduction scene, nice work, good story building.

Nice use of music and sound effects, and love the fact you made it controller-compatible; even to the point of having the controller buttons in the on-screen instructions.

Great work on the graphics and dynamics, ie. having the little cloud appear when you jump on the ground on a box. Found a little glitch on the third screen/level where I can’t jump onto the second box; just kept playing around with different pixels to try and get on the box; also the character can get stuck “in” the box. This happened on all boxes. Needs a little tidy on this, but otherwise super fun!

The character reminds me of the red/pink guy from Futurama 😛.

I love how much you thought about the different aspects of the game and use generic platformer things like fire, boxes, spikes etc. as obstacles. I liked that the obstacles went away once you got the key.

The controls were well done and very intuitive. The animation for dying is very funny!

Got a nice little GitHub README, add some more info in there and it’d be perfect.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you for the detailed feedback, very much appreciated. Sorry you got stuck on the jumping!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: The graphics and the gameplay are very good and I liked the progressive increase of the difficulty. The music is also great! I just found the controls very challenging for jumping between platforms. Good work!

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
A fun platformer, I like a lot of things going on here, like the way a level clears (and becomes safe) once you complete it. Thats a nice element for players to experience. Some fun challenges though, I some of those levels are tricky! The only glitch I encountered was sometimes falling between tricky platforms. Although that might have been a skill issue on my part too.

Enjoyed watching this one being developed over on twitter, good work!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Not a skill issue, I can jump most times and not get stuck but the collision for the platforms is not great, which is a shame as the rest of it mostly works quite well! Thanks

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Nice game! Lovely graphics, awesome character design and really nice level variety. I do see some graphical glitches on the edges, more apparent on mobile (iOS). The sound and music are also very fitting!

I played first on mobile, and got stuck on the spike stairs jump level. The jump controls never felt precise enough for those time critical challenges. So I decided to play on desktop too, to see if mobile controls are funky, and got stuck even a level earlier, with the first stair jump thing. So something about the jumping is trickier than it should be.

Great use of the theme though, I think this one comes closest in terms of being about phobias and the number 13 weaved in very neatly.

Congrats on a nice game, and it could be made great if you decide to improve the jumps/collision stuff after the competition.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thanks Jure, appreciate the feedback. Lots to learn from this years jam. Mobile controls work but need to improve or be simpler! I have some collision issues with the platforms which need sorting before I attempt iso parkour again!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game is really cute, and I see a lot of improvements from the time I first playtested it!

I think i don’t have the dexterity to move diagonally and jump at the same time. I found that really difficult. I also tried on mobile, to take advantage of the joystick, but the game was really tiny on mobile. I feel like this game could have comfortably have been adapted to work in portrait mode on mobile. But the joystick was not as responsive as the keyboard, so it didn’t help me much with the jumping (granted, i have rarely ever played mobile games).

The music was on point.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you, Mobile was not well tested sadly, still not perfected the mobile gaming side of the jam! I will keep trying and improving.

Appreciate you taking the time to play and feedback.

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: This is a lot of fun. Nice straight platformer, snappy interface. I like that it starts back up right away after you die (…got kind of a lot of use out of that :P )

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: I like the visuals and the BGM of the game. But the weird collisions and judgement whether the player is standing on the platform is a bit off. Sometimes I just stand on the walls of the stairs. Also there is 1 level that I mysteriously proceed to the next level without clearing it, Also there is one platform that I just cannot jump. Overall the isometric tile collision needs some work and this can be a really fun game.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I made the mistake of only testing with my daughter, should have had more people test the jumping.

Given more time I could have had more people test the controls and fixed the collision bugs!

Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the game, really fun to play.

The graphics were really good, as was the music - very atmospheric.

For me the controls were a little tricky on the PC doing diagonal movements with pairs of keys and at times timing a jump to avoid a hazard. Controls on mobile worked better for me.

I did get stuck a couple of times, once between two blocks that were touching and once outside of the level playfield.

Overall an enjoyable game, my kids loved it

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thanks for playing, glad your kids enjoyed it too!

I originally was focusing on a shooting game but after adding platforms pivoted away and removed the shooting all together, at that point I should have commited to more testing on the jumps!

Thanks

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Nice work!

Really fun to play with! The UI and the pixel art are really great. I love the charadesign and all the effect, particle that you have created.

I saw on the other comments that the game is compatible with a gamepad but sadly I don’t have one that I can use. I played with the keyboard and it was difficult to align to jump, I succeeded by pressed two buttons at the same times but I ended jumping in front or behind the tile and it was really difficult to climb the tower to get the key.

But what you have build is a really good base to iterate on! Congrats.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you, this is the year I talk about one bug haha, lesson learned, do not let your main mechanic have a significant flaw.

Maybe I could have added a mode with no platforms!

Appreciate the feedback

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I always like Prince of Persia trapdoor games. So I had quite a lot of fun playing!

What I would like to add is that when the level ends and the tiles start to move up and down, the demon does not move up/down with the tiles but stands at the same height. I liked the motion trail :). The music was nice!

Also noticed several bugs. In training level, when I got my first key, I was told to go to the portal from the unstable level as soon as possible, but I took my time and got a crash the same in later levels. Oon a higher level of the test, I don’t know how, but somehow I didn’t take the key, but the portal opened as if I passed the level.

Nice game, congratulations!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Really polished, great tune really matches the game. I liked that you can control the jump in mid air although it does make the jumps quite hard to get sometimes especially diagonally but thats the all part of the game afterall imo. Didnt complete yet!

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Love the tiny details! Well done!!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Very nice, loved the beat and the style.

Glad I got it to work. Took me a couple of times before I realized that full screening it using the top bar, stopped all keys working.

Hated the controls on desktop, made it almost unplayable for me, but truth be told, I always stink at platform games, so I could have just been me :-)

Great entry!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Great isometric platformer, after the first easy levels to get the grip, it starts to really challenge you with pixel perfect jumps and millisecond accuracy.

Also I liked a lot the ways the little demon dies, good job!

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
I thoroughly enjoyed it — and enjoyed watching it come together. I recall playing it (or rather: attempting to) while it was still a draft. Tricky at times (and yes, a few minor glitches make it harder than it needs to be), but ultimately it felt rewarding to beat.

Where just about everything would be praise, I do have one minor nitpick: Something’s off with your canvas sizing (or I don’t get it). On fullscreen, it doesn’t actually scale to my entire screen (2560x1440) nor is it centered, while on lower resolutions — notably lower height, like within the iframe — it results in a vertical scrollbar. It feels like you are forcing an aspect ratio, which is perfectly fine in itself, but the actual sizing only reacts to window width, and not height somehow. I.e. when window height doesn’t exceed your ratio, it results in a scrollbar instead of trimming your canvas size down.

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Love the graphics and gameplay on this!

I’m not sure what else to say other than this game is quite fun. I had a great time playing through, and I’m sure you know what level I got stuck on for a bit haha

You’ve packed quite a lot into this one, esp including the ghosts too! Thanks for the controller controls btw.

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: Really fun graphics and gameplay. I had some issues with jumping off platforms (or not) where coyote time would have been nice, and a few times where I wasn’t sure where I was from a z-index pov (like infront of or behind a raised block?) but overall really fun and great mechanics. And the audio fit really well.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I really enjoyed your game! Great graphics, solid game track, and good controls (feels a bit like Battletoads to me 😀). I think the game could use a little jump sound. It sounds like you may have been using SoundBox 😎, if so, I bet you could make a jump noise by tweaking around with the Bass drum. The second-to-the-last level was awesomely frustrating!! I think it would’ve served better as the final stage. Great work!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
A tad too punishing on some platforms (I fell so many times), but it has a very cool progression. Nice game!

edit: in my original feedback I mentioned a bug but I’m having the same issue with all games now, so I’m assuming it’s a bug in the js13k web, not your game. Sorry!

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: Oh… I’m too bad in this game! Anyway… Nice demon! (I must to say that I play it more than I should 😄)

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
Nice little game. I couldn’t get very far, not because of difficulty but because I think ground detection is a bit wonky which makes jumping less than great. For what is worth, I think jumping in isometric games is always a problem and it is always difficult calculating the landing spot.

Other than jumping, pretty awesome game!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Excellent game! Cute graphics, cool musics and fits the theme! Loved the messages between each level, cool immersion in the game! Gameplay is cool with multiple mechanics. I went to the 2nd “challenge” level but couldn’t achieve it. I don’t know if I missed a lot of content. But sometime there are lack in hitbox detection I think, between ghosts and some jumps which killed me many times.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I loved the isometric pixel graphics, funny dialog, and music.

I tried it on mobile first and, to be honest, the touch controls were very hard to make precision jumps in the direction you want it to move.

Otherwise, a fantastic game to play!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Pretty graphics!

Neat experience on desktop, not so great on mobile though.

Great work!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: nice isometric game the puzzles are very challenging and scary well done

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: Nice platformer game!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Fantastic music, and the visual style is spot on! Very lovely attention to detail, the wobbly floor, the portals, dialog boxes, everything.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Beautiful pixel art, nice music/SFX and good game design. I like how new game mechanics are introduced, and it gets harder and harder. It’s quite amazing you managed to package that much under 13kb, great job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: I love the design. It is pretty fun to play! A bit difficult at times, and I did get suck in a wall sometimes, but nothing that can’t be fixed in a future release. Love isometric games! Congrats!

api.name: Apollo-13kb
api.description: Join the crew of the unlucky Apollo-13 mission to the moon in this simplified historical simulation. Listen for commands from Mission Control, and control the spacecraft by simply pushing buttons. Keep the crew alive, and bring them home to Earth.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Love the idea and the presentation. Im also a big fan of Apollo13 so I new most of the actions to perform :D

I feel some animation would had made the game more alive. Also the zooming felt more annoying than helpful.

Also I would had love to see some indicators of CO2 or Electricity to see if Im running out of time.

Anyway, one of my favourite entries this year.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Liked this straight away. Someone had to do an Apollo 13 game, its perfect for the theme :) Played it to the end. Really nice. You cant really stray away from the story but might be interesting to have other stuff go wrong and you have to work out how to fix it rather than follow the flow.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Apollo 13 was in my ideas list, glad someone made it into a real game

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I made it home, and received the Medal of Freedom! Nice job, this was a pleasant experience. Brilliant interpretation of the jam theme. My biggest feature request would be tooltips, so I could find the thing I’m looking for without zooming in and out all over the place.
