Other than that, this was a pretty polished experience, and a great use of the text to speech features. Nice job!

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
Great concept and a great interpretation of the theme! The gameplay is interesting and engaging, and I felt invested in bringing the astronauts safely back home (which I did).

A few improvements that I might suggest:

  • I really wish I saw the Moon when I slingshotted around it!
  • I had to turn the sound off because the speech synth assumed my system’s default voice was English, which it’s not and which produced jarring audio.
  • When disconnecting modules, I was hoping I’d see an animation of them flying away :)
  • Zooming into a particular component was at times too aggressive, too close. I wish I was able to pan around the spaceship and zoom myself.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text:
Nice interpretation of the theme! And the gameplay is quite interesting! Well done!

Bonus points: text to speech sounds extremely funny when your default language isn’t english :D

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Some good innovation here, I’m a big fan of the Apollo missions so this was a fun one to get into and playtest. I’m liking all the detail in the controls and interactive options overall, and the way youre playing through this historical event.

It might need a bit more work on the states/state machine setup here. With so many controls I imagine its hard to cover every condition and state along the way. I managed to jettison the lander with the crew in it, and the game half kept going and half broke haha

I could see this being the basis for a very interesting and fun educational game, which steps you through the history of what happened here in the level of interactive detail space geeks would love to see! Great entry

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: “Oh no, what have you done?” noooooooo!!! I blew us into space again!! This is a very cool puzzle game and familiar real-life adventure, awesome idea. I wish it zoomed in much slower, and if I could pan around I would have felt more in control. I really like the line art you created of the appolo-13 craft itself, and though I disliked the tts voice, the game was much more immersive because of it; I just pretended it was like a HAL9000 emotionless computer rather than a person at mission control in houston or whatever. really great work!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Clever idea, I did not manage to win as I cannot help but press all the buttons :D I would bot make it into NASA! Nice graphics and a decent simulation.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Really original idea to manage a ship. Not quite easy by the way! Tips helped to know what to do, which was mandatory as I don’t know many things about how to control a spacecraft

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
What a take on the theme!

I like this historical NASA mission simulation.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
Really great use of the theme! Can you help the crew of the Apollo 13 mission survive? Me, definitely not. It didn’t matter what I did, I couldn’t get Tom Hanks to move into the LIM, even with the hatch open. After clicking around I managed to detatch the LIM entirely, consigning my crew to a painful 15 minute death.

Oh well, can’t win them all :) Graphically, really nice! Works very well given the limitations. The text to speech was pretty funny and although I would have liked to be able to pan around while zoomed-in, it was fast enough to get where I wanted for it to not really matter. Good fun, and a clear indication NASA should never employ me, ever :)

comments.author: Grzegorz Tańczyk
comments.text: OMG, wow, great fit into the theme!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
I loved the concept, but I found the zoom on clicked elements a little disturbing.

Seems like this game has a lot of potential with some polishing!

Great work!

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Interesting concept.

I like the ship’s presentation (it reminds me of some simplified schematic from some manual) and zoom in effect on selecting ship’s component.

The voice synthesiser assumed my default language which gave a rather funny effect.

It has some problems with running on Firefox.

Tom Hanks would approve.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I liked the graphics and the exploring the ship.

The voice-over was cool, but sounded like somebody speaking with an accent and very quickly, so was hard sometimes to discern what was being said?

In my frantic clicking to find out what things were, I released the lunar module by accident - oops.

Dissapointingly - the sim didn’t run at all on latest FireFox browser.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
The controls can be improved, i kept zooming in and zooming out forgetting where are the modules and which part of the spacecraft refers to what.

I enjoyed the game in general - taught me a bit more on how spacecrafts work!

api.name: MineCSSweeper
api.description:
For the “fear or avoidance of the number 13” I did something to be the antithesis to the JS13K, but still feet in the rules. This is a true JSZeroK Minesweeper game, and all game logic is implemented in CSS.

This is a pair game. Ask you friend, or mate, or smart dog to place the bombs, and start the game, so you can enjoy this classic with a nice style on your desktop or mobile.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text: I couldn’t get this to work in FireFox I’m afraid, it didn’t look like your screenshot, just a load of text tick boxes and radio buttons on the RHS, and no obvious way to start the game playing?

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: That is sad… I’m a Firefox user and I test it in FF on desktop (Linux) and mobile (Android). I would be grateful if you give-me more details http://t.me/aurium

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Good looking minesweeper, the fact that this is CSS-only is amazing!

The click-menu and the board animations are a little bit slowly paced, I would prefer a faster animation-speed.

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text:
Thank you so much! I really enjoined to prove it is possible. 😊

Speed up the ctx-menu. Noted for 2025. ✍️

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: This is amazing! It would be fantastic if clicking a block with the number 0 revealed the neighboring blocks with 0 as well. Nice entry.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: I have always loved MS flags! Looks really cool, impressive to make with only CSS.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Wow! Huge respect for the implementation in pure CSS. Also it looks great. Cute details on the lady bug 🐞

Though with the limited capabilities of css, gameplay and controls suffer a bit (and Audio too obviously).

Nevertheless very impressive 👏

Maybe there needs to be a separate css13k jam? 🙃

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
It’s possible to cheat by resizing the window, as the elements move to their place slowly and with different speed / easing, you can see where the bombs are.

Cute ladybug!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text:
this game is so stylish! and smooth! and the graphics and gameplay are extremely impressive considering they don’t use any js code!

the thing I loved the most about this phenomenal js13k entry was an extra little detail; a ladybug that crawls across the table, with animated legs, and wings that open and close! 🐞 when I saw that, my jaw dropped to the floor! wonderful work.

p.s. looking for a fun future project, maybe for js13 next year? make a css game starring this lovely ladybug! perhaps landing on flowers gently, like lunar lander on physics spring bendy branches… =) lol sorry I love to dream up game ideas and give them away! use or ignore freely. peace.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Implementing all the logic of a minesweeper in CSS is an outstanding achievement. Love the ladybug moving around.

Controls are a bit uneasy, having to click open / flag for each square, and the empty squares not automatically opening the neighbouring ones. But I guess that’s a limitation of pure CSS.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A minesweeper game intended to be two-player. I would have preferred a way to play single player, and for the theme to be more tightly integrated into the gameplay. The CSS animations are very cool, the gameplay is clear, and it’s overall well made. Nice job!

title: The Thirteen Storms of Divine Wrath
author: Tleffelman7

api.name: The Thirteen Storms of Divine Wrath
api.description:
You are a part of a tribe that has a horrible storm come annually over the course of 13 days. As a coming of age ritual every year young members are sent out to brave the storm each day and return to the village to sleep, going further away each night.

Your future success as an adult relies on coming back in a timely manner and getting a good night’s rest.

Are you up to the task, or will you be kept up all night?

Gameplay

Hop up mountainous terrain as clouds try to blow you away. Reach the lit hut to sleep to the next day.

Controls

WASD ESC to pause M to Mute/Unmute

Notes

This is the first time I’ve programmed anything outside of excel.

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
If you haven’t done anything outside of excel …. THIS ISN’T HALF BAD!

Pretty cool little game.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Storm braved in 04:01, Bedraggled. Nice job, fun series of challenges. I enjoyed getting thrown around the clouds. Great sound effects. The accelerating music created a sense of urgency. Well done!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to move the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: That’s a cute game! Well done! I liked that the clouds are enemies. Very creative!

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Well done! I enjoy the game and the clouds add a nice level of difficulty. Great work getting this completed and in!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little platformer.

The graphics were good, but the game played in a tiny square off to the side.

The music was nice, and I found it amusing that it got faster and more perilous as I progressed.

I liked the little jumping/hopping animation.

I thought the cloud (despite dumping rain on me) looked pretty cute, until I touched one :)

I liked that the levels seemed to add an extra platform to the level.

Much more fun that spreadsheets!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I got Insomniac in 11, after the second stage the game started to lag a lot and became almost unplayable at the end. The rain that most likely caused it was not needed at all. It was fun climbing the mountains though and I like the mountain graphics.

comments.author: Antti Haavikko
comments.text:
Pretty cool! Especially considering the last note about being the first time! 🎉👍

Would have loved alt key scheme (arrow keys) too with a separate jump button. I always feel awkward playing a keyboard only game with just my less dominant hand. It only makes sense to me when you’re controlling the mouse with the right one.

But the controls felt good though. Nice amount of velocity and control even during jumps. And even when you got hit, it was cool that you still had some amount of control over yourself and could potentially save yourself from falling all the way down.

The track to the mountain each level felt a bit unnecessary. Seemed like just a waste of time. Don’t think anything would have been changed for the worse if you spawned right at the base of the mountain at least after the first one. Or maybe if there actually was something happening on the ground level before you stated your ascend could work too.

Visually it was pretty nice. Quite clean and cohesive art style and a pleasant enough looking color palette even though it was a bit murky and dark. Audio wise it could use some more love. There were very little different sound effects (just win, jump and getting hit I think) with no variation too. Made it feel quite gamey and desolate. And the music didn’t help that much either. Having the tempo increase was nice way of giving more sense of progress but the music piece itself was pretty monotone and short. Didn’t really feel musical enough.

Anyways, good job on finishing your first game 👍 (unless you’ve made some excel games 😅)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
This is amazing for a “first game”!

Would be nice if the canvas was bigger (fullscreen). I like the use of AudioContext-oscillators for music and sfx.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Great first game, congratulations! A lot of very good things: scrolling, music, physics… and i liked the graphics. Just I recommend you to implement controls with arrows and/or qzsd for people, like me, who does not have a qwerty keyboard :) Good job!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: I like how I can search for lasagna recipies as i type when I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser. It would be inconvenient to have to disable this when playing games or use one of my other 5 browsers

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: Simple and fun game! I liked how you bumped up the tempo with each level. Florida Man approved.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: Great work as a first-time game!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Thank you for submitting this cute little game. It is easy to control, and the art style is nice. My favorite part is the sound accelerating as the levels get harder, nice touch!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
I honestly didn’t have a clue what I was doing at first! I ran around and jumped a bit, got hit by an angry cloud and then after a lot of jumping the next level started (I had no idea why). After a few levels it became clearer - you need to ascend to the top of the village by jumping up the platforms and avoiding the clouds, which send you crashing back down again. Repeat this 13 times and I assume you win. The task gets harder with each level, as you have to climb higher and higher with more and more clouds getting in the way. I made it to around level 7 but couldn’t get beyond that.

I liked how the music got faster with each level, upping the ‘frantic’ nature of making the ascent, even though I don’t believe there was any kind of time limit or reason to move fast. Not entirely sure this is on-theme, either, and the game window was teeny-tiny, but it all worked, the platformer action was quite nice and it was strangely compelling up to a point.

api.name: Not Warehouse 13
api.description:
You’ve just got hired to organise boxes in a warehouse.

These are no ordinary boxes… they merge when they collide and their labels change accordingly. Nobody knows what’s inside these boxes but when they’re labelled with “13” they cannot be moved. Luckily, “-13” boxes can nullify “13” boxes.

Your job is to ensure this warehouse is clear of “13” boxes.

“Not Warehouse 13” is a puzzle game in which you must swipe to merge boxes until you get rid of all the dreaded boxes labelled with 13.

Use the Arrow Keys or Swipe to move the boxes.

comments.author: Matt Light
comments.text:
I spent way too long trying to win–I never did! But this is fun gameplay and the logic for determining which numbers to populate next must be intense. There were a few situations where I expected to never get back down to zero but then the game gave me a box I needed.

Cool game!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
The logic to determine the next numbers is pretty intense: It takes the whole universe to run it!

I’d love to take credit but it’s mostly random (with a few restrictions)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Fun to play, difficult to master. The graphics worked well, I liked the way they bounced around.

Not sure that I managed to get rid of any 13 boxes, but it was hard to know what was going on when the warehouse filled up as multiple sums were being applied at once when multiple boxes were merging.

Funky popping bubble sounds.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
You’re right. Sometimes when multiple boxes merge, it makes it seem like there’s some visual bugs but it’s really bad feedback to the user.

The moviment and collisions are all fake though

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Took me a while to win, but I finally succeeded.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: Nice one! Hope you enjoyed it!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Great idea, but needs balancing. Like now it’s nearly impossible to win reliably.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
It does indeed.

As a user above suggests, it could do with a something more clever to come up with the next box number.

Additionally I had the thought of either showing where there next box would appear or which number was coming next… But I didn’t have time to implement that.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I played for a while and mostly slowly moved to getting more boxes stuck, is there a win state?

It all works very well, the crates look nice!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
There is! But you’ll need a bit of luck, unfortunately

Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
A 2048-adjacent game, but much much harder.

Storing only 1/4 of the sprite by making the boxes symmetrical is a neat idea.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: I was never good at sliding puzzles or threes. Wasn’t able to remove any of the 13s. Any float that wasn’t .5 felt like it was impossible to get rid of. The audio was nice though.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Somewhere between Sokoban and 2048. Great graphics and bouncing effects. The random values of the added crates makes it hard to build a strategy – you basically keep one integer and one float crate, merging the incoming ones, and hoping to add all that to -13.

Oh, and once you win, the overlay with the message never disappears even as you hit the reset door, you need to reload the game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I found the concept very interesting but difficult to master: I was only able to destroy few “13” boxes :( I would recommend to start with a first level without “float” boxes to help the player winning :) The game is very polished and I especially liked the tutorial and how smooth boxes move.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: I must say it perfectly fits the theme. I really enjoyed playing the game.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A well built game that’s similar to 2048 or other sliding-block puzzle games. The theme is clearly integrated, it has a nice look and feel, and satisfying sound effects. Nicely done!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: I would recommend to always start with the tutorial and instead have a “skip” button if you already know the mechanics of the game. The tutorial was very helpful to learn how to play the game. If I failed, the game “Nice on rookie” text stayed on top and I wasn’t able to restart the game.

api.name: Prime Guy
api.description:
1, 2 or 3 to change attack power SPACE to attack

The attack operation changes after every swing

Add –> Subtract –> Divide –> Add –> …

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: Nice one! Cool retro graphics. I ran into trouble when too many numbers were in the field, they overlap each other and become illegible.

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: Very innovative and a cool idea. The core of this is really good, but it feels a bit rushed (which is understandable of course). Great job regardless!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Graphics and controls are really nice! Feels very smooth. Cool game concept overall, too.

Gameplay was a little bit slow, though? Maybe have the numbers move faster, or add some additional obstacles on the map or something?

But ya, overall cool game!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: The concept is very interesting and the game really fits the theme! Thank you for the tutorial that helped me to start the game, it is very appreciated:) This game would be even better with some audio effect or music behind.

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text:
The art style is very cohesive and looks great! And I really appreciate the amount of effort put in the tutorial.

Controlling the character felt a bit clunky though, for example if you press and release left while holding up, I would expect it to still go up.

I found myself doing lots of attacks in air just to get the correct operation each time, I think there would have been a lot more strategy involved if attacking in air did not change the operation.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game looks great, love the retro graphics. The animations were good.

Having he first “0” as a non-threatening tutorial type NPC was handy as when those other numbers start chasing you and you need to quickly determine what “attack” to go at them with, things get harder pretty quickly.

It was a little odd to have almost the whole game in low pixel resolution, but then the hearts in high resolution.

Once I realised you could swipe away from your target if the sum on offer wasn’t beneficial, the game became a lot easier.

A good entry all in all.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not properly play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to change the attack power the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Nice Hyperlight Drifter visual vibes.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Loved the look of the game.

It was difficult to attack the number I wanted when multiple numbers were on top of each other. It was especially difficult to parse which number was which when they overlapped.

I found myself attacking in the air a lot just to go through the operations hunting for the one I needed. Kinda made it feel like the changing of operations wasn’t a problem.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game looks great. I love the art style and the animations.

It’s personally not my genre and I don’t want to be doing maths in my head while playing games. But as far as games for learning maths go, i think this one is great! You have to make compromises with atacking one enemy or another to spend some of the operations you might not like.

I think it really missed some music though. Also right in the tutorial, i pushed the first number off the island, and it was not in a hurry to come back, so I had to refresh the game. Maybe put the first enemy closer to the center.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Interesting concept. But 13 should not be good things to match theme exactly.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: The pixelart style is great! The game mechanic is also quite unique.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Very cool concept and graphics! I did not realize that it is a math game and my enemy starts as the number zero, but later you get to figure out how to play the game. You can easily push the numbers out of the island, then you have to wait for them to slowly float back. It might be nice to limit the moving area.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Thanks for submitting this game! I love the art. It is sad there is no sound to go with it, though. The game mechanic is good. The controls (1, 2, 3 keys) are not super natural, there is probably a better way to do it. Really great job making it under 13kb!

api.name: Triskaidekagenesis
api.description:
Play with numbers. Help Nina get rid of the triskaidekaphobia. Get yourself the triskaidekaphobia.

Main mechanic is selecting a certain number of the cards from the set which CAN’T be converted into 13 by arithmetic operations.

I have lovingly added some unsettling effects for when you fail. Hope they have made your uncomfortable. It’s a horror game, after all:)

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Less than 5kB! 🤯

Nice intro screen with good narrative building. Nice work on some emojis too. I like the little animation you did with the pulsing boxes on the intro screen.

Said there was sound, but I didn’t hear anything until I lost. Add some music and sound effects to make this game really shine - you have plenty of kBs to spare. Nice animation when you lose, very cool screen tilt.

Incredible amount of maths here, nice work! On the 7th round the numbers went off the screen, and there was no animation to say I had lost or won… so couldn’t end the game.

Add a GitHub README and some info.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Thanks! I have added the postmorted as you suggested. Pity you couldn’t see the end dialog, it’s quite funny, I think. And it’s strange that there were no sound - I tested it in chrome and opera, and it was there. Did not have mac to test on it. Game indeed could use more testing, but there is not much that can be added feature-wise, imho. And being simple and to-the-point was one of the objective for this game.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Woo, I did it, final time 7:02. This was pretty tricky. The combinatorial explosion is too much for my simple brain. I ended using brute force search, starting with bigger numbers, and exhausting out possible combinations. Who knew there were so many ways to make 13?? The sound effects succeeded at making me uncomfortable! Nice job, this was fun!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Thanks for playing! I forgot to mentionit, but the game writes the correct solution to the browser console, so it can be used to pass the levels you get stuck on:) Also, seeing the way Nina gets 13 helps figuring which cards to avoid.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: It’s an interesting puzzle, but the number of possible combinations is just too high with 4 or more cards. I’m not sure who Nina is, but she must be a genius to be able to figure this out in a split second.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Good job on the submission and the size. 4Kb is very impressive.

My poor brain couldn’t do math well enough for the game. Making sure any combination of arithmetic wouldn’t end up at 13 was hard, especially with 4 cards.

When you lose a round it would have been nice if the game reset or something? Right now the timer just keeps going and you can’t do anything else.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: You can’t “lose” in this game. If you get the wrong combination, just click the card again to take it back and continue trying other combinations until you get it right. Sorry I did not state it explicitly.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The visuals are good. The concept of the game is interesting.

It’s easy to understand and start playing.

Given that the game says it will show you all the combinations and do the maths for you, It would be good if you had a submit button, so that you can experiment with selecting a few cards to see what happens.

Having the game essentially “stop” when you get it wrong isn’t ideal, and the text telling you to check the console for the correct answer is just a bit too tiny and not useful for non-devs.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Haha, nice one! I also made a math game last year 🤓

Great twist on the story. I guess, the last level isn’t solvable without it? And nice story in general.

First I was a bit “afraid” that I would need to do all the calculations in my head and was relieved then that all possibilities are shown on the screen 👍

The one audio effect was nice. Would have been nice to have a sound for success. And maybe some background music.

Could be a great game for school kids 😊

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: It looks pretty and interesting! Sadly it gets pretty hard for me to foresee all the possible future arithmetic combinations! Also, when I lost I kept waiting and waitng for it to continue (I thought from the story at the beginning I just had to wait until “Nina recovered”) but I had to reload the game to play again.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Interesting card/number game, but in the end I got away with trying a few random combinations until no operation gave me 13 as a result.

It would have been much more interesting if there had been a game over after a certain number (maybe 13?) of errors.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Cure story! Nice UI effect when an operation would result in 13!

I think there’s room for mechanics improvements though: I found myself just trying a couple of combinations until I find the one that wouldn’t result in 13.

Congrats on the submission!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: interesting way to use 13 in fearsome way, right to the theme and the graphics is cool

comments.author: Benjamin Brown
comments.text: I liked the preamble and postamble, and the sound/effects that play when you get it wrong.
games: XIII Days To Save The Kingdom

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing, helped Nina become the queen of numbers ;).

I didn’t immediately realize that you could reverse the selected numbers, so when I got to 13, I just reloaded the page to start over, but when I realized that you could reverse the selected numbers, everything became much better, it would be nice to somehow animate that you can return the card, or describe it in the game description.

Nina can calculate a lot of operations! It’s almost impossible for the average person to do it that fast, so I just went through all the combos until I got the right one to move on :D.

I liked the story, it fits the theme very well. I also liked the sound that emphasized the result of 13.

It was fun, but I think that not everyone can count like Nina, so the gameplay comes down to a simple went through all the combos for me, or I’m just too lazy to count it :).

When I went through the whole game to go to the final dialogue I had to zoom out to see the button to go - Windows, Chrome.

It was fun, congratulations!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: I loved the story here! Help Nina with her math by giving her cards, but don’t let any of those cards, in any combination, equal 13. Sounds easy, but given you have to factor in the use of the operations - + * / and () it’s pretty much impossible for a regular human to play beyond 3 cards. As a result, you end up just clicking random cards until Nina is happy. I liked it was on-theme! And the story and concept is cute, too.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Yes, it’s not a trivial task, but if you play long enough and notice how exactly Nina makes the 13, you start to see the patterns, i.e. which cards are more “safe” (1,2,8,9), which are more likely to produce 13 (3,5,7) etc.

api.name: No Flight Zone 13
api.description:
Maths handicap airplane shooting game.

Enjoy an arcade game of air combat while avoiding the number 13 in your wallet.

How to play

Use the arrow keys, mouse, or tap on mobile devices.

Eliminate all enemies to advance to the next level.

When you destroy an enemy, they may drop a numbered coin.

As you collect coins, the value will accumulate in your wallet.

If the accumulated total exceeds the value of 13, you will receive a bonus.

However, if the accumulated total is exactly 13, the coins will turn into an activated bomb..

Special thanks to Martina for artwork, Mar and Víctor for testing

Zona de exclusión aérea 13

Juego de disparos de aviones con hándicap matemático

Disfruta de un juego arcade de combate aéreo mientras evitas el número 13 en tus colecciones de monedas.

Cómo jugar

Usa las teclas de flecha, el ratón o toca en dispositivos móviles.

Elimina a todos los enemigos para pasar de nivel. Cuando destruyas a un enemigo, puede dejar caer una moneda numerada.

A medida que recolectes las monedas, se acumulará el valor en tu monedero.

Si el total acumulado supera el valor 13, recibirás una bonificación.

Sin embargo, si el total acumulado es exactamente 13, las monedas se convertirán en una bomba activada.

Agradecimientos especiales a Martina por el trabajo artístico, a Mar y Víctor por las pruebas.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Really well made, love this type of game. Quite difficult to dodge the bullets on a narrow play area.

Nice sounds and take on the theme.

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I need to balance and make the game as fun as it deserves. I hope I can find some time to finish it.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I liked playing your game and it reminded me when I was much younger and used to play “1942”. It’s really fun to play, the contols and the scrolling are perfect and I liked the sound effects. Well done!

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Solid arcade-feeling here. Pretty much bullets in the air, quite hard to dodge them. Some music during gameplay would add a lot to it.

Good game!

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! Level 13 deserves a final boss and some music, but I ran out of time and space.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game looks great, with an arcade feel, reminded me of a 1941 type game. The sound effects were also good, maybe some music could have made it even better.

I liked the victory screen animation. The concept of the game was good and fitted the theme pretty well.

Perhaps it would be good if you could shoot down enemy bullets? Also the text in the pop-up status sections didn’t fit the box completely.

It was a bit laggy on my pc, but ok to play. Due to the portrait nature it feel like a better fit for mobile than desktop.

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Thanks for your comments! Good suggestions for the next competition.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Is it normal that the game feels like it’s in slow motion?

It looks really good, but the game play is kinda too hard from the get go. It’s almost impossible to avoid bullets, and the hit box of the plane is huge.

I didn’t understand what the effect of the bomb was. Sure, people on the surface died but why should I care?

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! It really depends on the browser you use. I’ve tested it on Chrome and Safari for desktop and mobile. As for gameplay, I ran out of time in the 13 days I spent developing it and I couldn’t balance and make the game as fun as it deserves. I hope to find some time to finish it.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: nice and smooth and fun! I wish the collision bbox for the player was half the size of the sprite itself, like in many arcade vertical scrolling shmups, to allow for close calls that are generous for the player! overall, really polished and fun and great fps for me. thanks for joining js13k!!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: It’s a fun arcade air combat game! It’s a bit hard, but I do like the explosion graphics. It’s hard to dodge the bullets and the planes. Adding sounds/music effects would be great, but overall, it’s well done!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Crisp and easy to use UI. I was expecting some 3D flight shooting game when I saw the menu, very cool with the interactive background. The gameplay is simple, but effective. The bullet hell can be difficult, but I like a challenge. The effects are nice and gives the game some character.

It would be cool to see many different kinds of enemies as well.

Overall a polished game.

api.name: Exterminator
api.description:
The Exterminator scans for the number 13 hidden within the patterns of your room so you can eliminate them. Like in the Ghostbusters movie. Hurry or they will overload the circuits.

  • Controls: click/tap
  • Tested on: Android (Chrome/Firefox) in portrait mode.
  • 💃 Live
  • 🍔 YouTube
  • 🍕 Code
  • 🍨 Post-mortem
  • 🍩 Developer Notes

Note:

  • This device uses the camera so make sure it has permission for your browser.
  • Make sure the lights are on and that you have camera/video enabled in apps/chrome and chrome/settings.
  • After you click Scan, you should get some detections. If not, then move your phone back and forth. When you get some detections, you can click the scope to Capture them. Once captured, you must quickly smash them because they are not happy and will begin to zip around faster and faster until they overload your circuits.
  • Tip: If you point it at the ceiling, you will capture less 13s.
  • Warning: Consumer protection groups have found that this device doesn’t actually work to eliminate the number 13 from your environment. But if it makes you feel better, that is all that matters 🐲

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Less than 4kB!!!

Very interesting take on the theme, and using AR/camera scanning to get the basic blocks of what’s in the camera’s view. Since I was doing this on stream and had my camera in use, played this on mobile phone, and super easy, intuitive, love the touch screen enablement so I could smash all the number 13s.

The use of humour for “Consumer protection groups” was very funny. Nice job. Great GitHub README.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Have tried on mobile and desktop but only ever see a blank screen

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Innovating idea to use the camera in your gameplay, but the execution feels hazardous (on Desktop for example, the Scan phase is instant but the Capture phase empty of any 13s)

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: The idea sounds very innovative, but the execution was a bit underwhelming. I don’t understand the use of the camera, if all we see if a black background in the background, and dozens of 13 floating around. Tapping on all floating 13 was also not exactly hard. Took a few tries until I finally saw anything other than a black radar.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: i have no idea what to do after giving permission and clicking and clicking nothing happen

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Good use of the camera, but it does not work on iOS devices, at least on my iPhones.

By the way, nice clicker game although you should ensure a smooth cross platform experience.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Reading about your game it sounds quite interesting

I wasn’t able to play it however because it required camera permissions.

Maybe think about adding another game play option for people who can’t use their camera

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Very creative game! I cannot play it on my desktop though, but it works smoothly on my mobile device. It is an interesting game, and it would be even nicer if it could be played on more different devices.

api.name: Primonumerophobia
api.description:
The fear of prime numbers. Fear them all. Especially 13!

Please read all instructions below. An introduction and tutorial were not added in game.

Don’t let the timer get to 0 and avoid PRIME numbers in your score! If your score ever equals a PRIME number, bad things will happen to you, especially if it’s 13!

If your score is doubles (11, 22) you will have a chance to grab a power up! Collect as many power ups as possible to aid you in higher levels.

Advance to the next level by getting to 100 or more score.

Beware of prime enemies. Contacting with them will deplete your score and freeze you for an instant, along with their shots as well.

Place bombs to destroy enemies on contact.

If your timer gets to 0 or you make contact with a large enemy (not the common prime enemies), you will die and will restart from level 1.

Small hints around player: Bottom left green is what you should collect to get the next double, Bottom right red is what you should avoid for next prime, Top center white is your current score.

The game is quite challenging, but is quite possible to win, especially collecting lots of powerups and playing with a gamepad! Hint/spoilers at very bottom of this page.

Controls:

Press R, Space, Enter or gamepad South button to begin

Use arrow keys or wasd or gamepad left stick to move around and catch numbers.

Left Shift or gamepad West button - Thrust

R or gamepad East button - Place Bomb

R, Space, Enter or gamepad South button to start over

M or gamepad Select to mute/unmute music

P to pause/unpause game

Power Ups: U - Magnet Increase, T - Thruster Increase, S - Player Speed Increase, B - Bomb Increase

Hint/Spoiler territory below

The mid-boss at level 7 is quite hard, but defeating it is not necessary to advance to the next level.

Highers levels do get quite hard but the final level is, you guessed it, 13. This involves a final boss fight; fortunately if you get this far dying respawns you at the final boss instead of making you restart the game.

comments.author: Tero Jäntti
comments.text:
Phew! After some 10-20 tries, I reached level 11 with score 1726. What a unique concept you have here, fits perfectly with this year’s theme! I had fun with this one. I think it has replay value since you slowly grasp what numbers to pick and how to avoid primes. Like, at first I was just picking even numbers but then, as I got a refresher on prime numbers, I started collecting odd numbers as well. And I did’t even focus on getting the doubles yet!

After several attempts, the game seemed very very difficult, as even at levels 4-5 the numbers were really fast and scarce. Also, after starting the game again from start, a previously ongoing disability was still active. You guys didn’t have some global variables that were not cleaned up, now did you ;) After getting the habit of refreshing the browser after each game, the difficulty seemed properly challenging and the middle boss spiced things up nicely.

Just a minor thing, but when having the fast moving “disability”, it was quite difficult to catch the numbers. Perhaps the game could be a bit more forgiving when collecting those (in player’s favor, not so eager to catch enemies :)? But yeah, I guess that was meant as a punishment so maybe it’s just me being clumsy :) Also, I did’t manage to use the power-ups when I happened to catch those, so it would be helpful if some help text would appear at the time that I could use them. But I guess time- and size constraints were limiting here :) But these things were really minor, didn’t spoil the game for me.

The minimalistic graphics style fits the game spot on (at least I wasn’t missing anything fancier). Together with very good music and sounds, playing this is a rather meditative experience.

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Thanks so much for the thorough and kind feedback! We may have used a few globals ;)

You definitely found your stride in this game, just as we wanted. At first it is a bit difficult even with the helper numbers (green and red) below you and score right above you, but as you play a few more times you realize what you want to get and what you want to avoid, and even sometimes to get your score subtracted by purposely running into enemies.

We really wanted a tutorial level to show and flesh out some of these concepts before the game really begins, but time and space constraints limited us here.

The fast moving is supposed to be a punishment, like slow moving and reverse control.

I’m not sure if we explained power ups correctly or at all, but each one builds some stats little by little so it’s not really noticeable except over time. You will eventually notice that you are faster, and that your boost button actually works for a time after you collect enough power ups, your magnet attract gets stronger, etc.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Interesting game! My best score was 815 on level 6. There are many different mechanics and systems to try to juggle in your head! I found the “don’t let your score equal a prime number” rule a little frustrating, so my strategy was to only go for even numbers. If I ever accidentally hit an odd number or hit a yellow number, then my goal was to patch my score and get back to an even number as quickly as possible. Personally, I’d recommend easing the player into the mechanics rather than enabling them all on level one. Impressive amount of content for a js13k entry! Nice music and sound effects too. Great job, congrats!

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: Thanks for the input, and solid score! I agree with your sentiment, it can be frustrating/overwhelming at first. I played long enough to where it became natural, but agree that we could have eased the player in. Thanks again.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The instructions are way too long.

The game is kind of cute in graphics and music, but I found the mechanics of the prime numbers too complicated for the game to be fun. Basically what I did was catch only the even numbers to avoid the primes without having to think about the current total. Because while I’m playing, I’m not paying attention to the total.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I totally get it, that’s how I played at first! Solid strategy. The game does get easier and somewhat intuitive if you play it longer, but we don’t necessarily expect people to become experts at PRIMONUMEROPHOBIA!! Thanks for the feedback, and thanks for playing homie!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Neat idea, and the implementation of the theme isn’t subtle. :)

Intuitively, the score should start high and decrease with pickups, so that the highest density of primes is towards the end of the level.

Good idea with the hints, too. All in all, a solid little game, nice work!

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: Thanks for the input, and thanks for playing!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Excellent idea to fit the theme! I loved the concept (I love maths, I don’t know if everybody would love to calculate prime numbers ^^) Sound is nice, espacially the one used to compute score at the end of a level!

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: Glad you enjoyed it friend, we especially liked the SCORE tally as well! I just found a tone I liked in SoundBox, and monkeyed around a bit. Thanks for the input, and thanks for playing!!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Interesting game! A lot of concepts applied in the game… Also, very nice music, congratulations!

I could reach level 13 but I couldn’t figure out how to beat the boss. By reading the game description, it seems I should drop bombs by hitting “R”, but it didn’t work, or I didn’t understand how :D

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little game, with a heavy maths element.

Graphics are basic, but enough to get the concept

Music and sounds are good

Having the controls flip was fun, was that in relation to being a prime number?

I felt the character moved a bit slow, and there was a lot to try to remember from the rules and instructions when playing.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: managed to finish level yet having no idea what was going on, inverting controls was annoying but is a good way to make a bit challenge

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This is clever, I quite enjoy this, it feels a bit like a lost BBC Micro or Arcade title. Good work on the new-feeling mechanic.

title: XIII Days To Save The Kingdom
author: Benjamin Brown

api.name: XIII Days To Save The Kingdom
api.description:
A point-and-click strategy game about managing an army, exploring uncharted land, finding lost treasure, and crushing your enemies to oblivion.

The Kingdom is in trouble and only you can save it. You must bring the Crown of Light to its rightful place in the Capital, but there are many who will try to stop you! Fight your way through them, earning valuable battle experience, finding ancient items, and gathering gold to improve your army to have a chance to finally complete your quest! Ages 10 and up.

Controls: Mouse to navigate. Space to confirm dialog and re-initiate events on same tile.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Very nice little game. I couldn’t make it to 13 days :(
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: Really enjoyed this. It felt great to explore the hex map. There was a good balance of meeting enemies that seemed impossible, then adventuring for a while and coming back and defeating them. I managed to win on my third try, which is just about right for difficulty.

comments.author: Jani Nykänen
comments.text: I have played about half of the games this far, and I must say this is my favourite so far. I would definitely love to see a post-compo version with more polished graphics, more content and maybe fully randomized map.

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
Welp, there goes my weekend — I’ll be playing HoMM3 again :)

I had a lot of fun playing this game. I think it’s perhaps a bit more a puzzle game than a strategy? Regardless, I still enjoyed exploring the map and getting ready for the final battle. I saved the kingdom in 10 days. (It took me 3 or 4 tries.)

I was a bit disappointed that the map was the same in every playthrough, which limits the replayability factor. I’ll admit, however, that it’s how I was able to beat the game in the end — I followed a path that I devised in earlier playthroughs. On the plus side, the positions of the critters changed, which made me change my plan slightly :)

The beasts (or stone behemots as I dubbed them) were quite tough and the fire mages were almost impossible to beat if they spawned as the last opponent in the final battle. Awesome work :)

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: It is a very cool game I love this type. I think it would be better for the font size to be bigger and for the rest very well done amazing work. GZ!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I love the look of the game, the pixel graphics are great, and the hexagon tiles remind me of a boardgame like Settlers of Catan.

The way the map reveals happened was good, so that you can explore the edges of your known domain before committing to battle.

It appears to matter which order you attack in, a few times I was killed straight away, so it’s good that you get the option to retreat. Having said that even when the odds seemed stacked in my favour I got defeated a few times.

Perhaps as a hint when about to start a battle the player could be advised to recruit or level up?

I feel like I very nearly got to the end, good fun.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I finally reached the end, due to good RNG that gave me more defense (first runs I had many speed items). I’m a big fan of heroes of might & magic, I might not be objective, but it’s a great entry

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This was really fun! I haven’t been able to beat it yet after 3-4 attempts. Really challenging, but addictive enough that I keep trying!

The movement/pathing is very nice! Nice retro graphics and sound effects.

Sometimes it wasn’t quite clear why I was winning/losing a battle. I understand it has something to do with my troop rating / how many troops I have. But yeah, maybe a bit more clarity about the math would be good.

But anyways, very nice work! Awesome game

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
This game was hella fun to play. Risk-style gameplay with a few extra stats. Had to try it a few times until i was able to see the castle. Once you know the map, it becomes easier. But then i died on the last mage, and I couldn’t bring myself from playing it allover again. It was going so well!!

The random enemy stats keep each retry a bit fresh, while also giving you a chance to try different things. But that also means you might be soft locked and its impossible to survive. The art is captivating, though minimal. The little animations give it a nice dynamic touch.

The dooming 13th day was a good use of the theme.

Would like to play different maps.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Oo, fun! Nice collection of mechanics, exploration, and combat. The permadeath is kinda brutal, especially late in the game. Fantastic pixel art, UI, controls, and sound effects. This is a really nice package. Great job, congrats!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Enjoyed playing through the game, did not win sadly but had fun trying several times.

Great graphics, simple controls and intuitive to play.

Would play a longer more balanced version of this.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
It is amazing what you can do with only 13K, here we have a hexagonal movement strategy with excellent pixel art in which you can save your kingdom in 13 days.

The enemies all seem invincible at first, but once you figure out how to earn money, recruit allies and buy equipment, it gets a little easier.

I also enjoyed the combat screen very much.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
awesome artstyle, the exploration-part is great!

The battles… hm… sometimes it was not clear to me, why i lost. bad luck i guess (like in the boardgame “risk”)

Good game!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Nice homage to the classic HOMM, I enjoyed the controls, very straight forward, and the presentation was very nice. It felt a little bit - optimize movements to win - but still, fun to play.

Maybe adding some powerup to spend on a battle could have made it more interesting.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: Nice tribute to Heroes of might and magic 3!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Incredible!

I’m amazed by the graphics you managed to end up with. The sounds are very well in the theme as well.

The map, the enemy fight, the menu, the fog, etc.. It really feels like you’ve spent a lot of time on this, very well done!

comments.author: Ben Gillbanks
comments.text: Lovely game, worked well in the theme and was enjoyable too!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
The pixel graphics and surprisingly fun turn-based game is great! I don’t normally care for turn-based clicking, but it was fun getting familiar with the map and figuring out the winning strategy.

It’s such a solid experience from start to finish. For a game type that I was complete unfamiliar with, the simplicity of the UI made it all so easy to understand.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Nice strategy game with a fun spin on this years theme. Reaching the Eastern capital is a really fun and clear goal, but getting there can be quite challenging though. Enemies can make unexpectedly high rolls and take your army out pretty fast. And with little options to do extra damage, it can sometimes be very difficult to progress. If you’re lucky enough to pass all enemies though - this can be a very fun adventure to experience!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Excellent yet difficult strategy game. Controls are very well thought out and gfx are excellent. At the beginning I believed it was a game of speed, but after losing several times I realized you need to take time to build and strengthen your army.

Support for zoom in and out is great, however, I managed to zoom out the whole game to a pixel, with no way to zoom back in. Oops.

api.name: Phantomicus
api.description:
“In ghost hunting, one man’s noise is another man’s data.” - Dr. H. Spookworth

Welcome to Dr. Zaphod Beeblebrox’s spirit-infested lab! Armed with your Spook Siphon and a PhD in Paranormal Pest Control, ascend 13 floors to defeat the spectral infestation.

Overview

Navigate challenging levels:

  • Collect Spectral Sparks (gold cubes) to power your Spook Siphon
  • Use your powered-up Siphon to capture ghosts and interact with objects
  • Find keys to progress through locked doors

User Interface

Watch your UI for crucial info:

  • 🏨 Current floor
  • ❤️ Health (resets each floor)
  • ⚡ Vacuum power (increases with gold cubes)
  • 💰 Score
  • ⏱️ Time (go fast for bonus points!)

Controls

  • ←↑↓→: Navigate
  • SPACE: Activate Spook Siphon
  • R: Restart level
  • M: Toggle music

Remember, fear nothing but fear itself… and that slime-dripping ghost behind you. Happy hunting!

Acknowledgements

  • Kang Seonghoon for Roadroller
  • Rob Louie for Roadroller configuration recommendations
  • Salvatore Previti for Terser configuration recommendations
  • Brandon Jones for glMatrix
  • Nicolas Vanhoren for sonant-x
  • Title music based on Luigi’s Mansion [Remix] by Qumu
  • Game music based on Dark Hallways (Luigi’s Mansion Cover) by Traceuse
  • Andrzej Mazur for organizing js13k

License

MIT

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Great game! I really enjoyed the Luigi’s Mansion vibe, and the music was fantastic. I beat the game, I honestly wished it was more difficult, I didn’t have any trouble with the ghosts or feel like I was low on health ever.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thanks Ryan! I agree that it’s too easy. In past years, my games have been too hard, and I think I overcorrected. Thanks for your kind words 🙏

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Really enjoyed it! Love Luigi’s Mansion, love how this turned out.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thanks Rob! I’ve actually never played Luigi’s Mansion, but it seemed like a fun source of inspiration 🤣

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: Really nice entry! The different levels, the 3D models, the audio, this game has it all, it was really pleasant to play.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you Gauthier, I appreciate your kind words! 🙏

comments.author: WHITE6982
comments.text: Really Great Game!! There are lot of things in 13kb.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you! I did the programmer thing, and just kept adding features, ha.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: This game looks seriously impressive! The amount of content, the 3D elements, the music. I unfortunately only get a black screen on the 3D part on Firefox, had to switch to Chrome. Also after playing for a bit, the computer started getting very slow and FPS dropping quickly. Had to shut down my browser as it was causing my whole laptop to freeze.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thanks João! Bummer about the performance issues. I need to add Ubuntu to my testing process next year. Thanks for playing and your kind words 🙏

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Quite incredible hommage to Luigi’s Mansion, is that a real 3D physics engine that handles the boxes?! If yes, please release it as a standalone library :p

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thanks xem! Yes, I have you in mind for a standalone version that is stripped down to just the physics. Stay tuned!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Great 3D game, and a nice version of Luigi’s Mansion. I enjoyed playing the game. Impressive that you’ve added so much.

The controls were a bit slippery for my taste, but I was able to complete the game. Appreciate the final boss being more difficult than the rest of the levels.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thanks John! Agree, the player character needs more friction. In the frantic final days of testing, I started tweaking more physics constants, and I unfortunately the player character got slippery. Thanks for playing, and thanks for your kind words 🙏

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My result is 75170, 13/13, but without end scene or I lost he somehow :).

If you’ve ever wanted to hunt ghosts but couldn’t find them, here you can :).

I liked the level design, as well as the music that harmoniously accompanies it. I also really enjoyed climbing the pipes!

From what I noticed, the start of the game can be started by pressing any key, not only Enter, but then everything converges, and also at the end of the game, there is no possibility to restart, that is, it does not work on the key R. I think I would like to see some effect of the end of the game, or a transition to the start screen, or there is a feeling that there may be something else, and it is not clear whether it is the end or not.

In general, very nice, congratulations!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text:
Thanks Roman! Congrats on your high score, that’s very respectable!

Yes, there are a couple of funny bugs where the vacuum will shrink a ghost, but not completely, so then there is a tiny ghost bouncing around that looks very similar to a dust particle 😱

And yes, any key will trigger the intro sequence. I was trying to save bytes by reusing the same “Press Enter to continue” message in multiple places 🙃

Thank you so much for your feedback and your kind words! 🙏

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
I enjoyed the game a lot.

The lighting and music really add a nice touch.

Awesome job.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you! I appreciate it, coming from a fellow redneck fullstack

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Very great game, very polished, graphics are really cool, have a PS1 kind of feel to them. Music was really good, plus nice sound FX.

All ran really smoothly!

The controls I found a little tough, mostly facing the ghosts correctly, I have played Luigi’s Mansion and the controls are very similar to that.

I also see you are super active on all the games commenting!!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you for the kind words! I’m so happy to hear that it ran smoothly for you, as it sounds like a number of people had performance issues. I agree, the controls are still a little loose. When the vacuum is running, the game gently rotates the player toward the nearest ghost, but I don’t think it’s enough. Oh well. Thank you for playing! 🙏

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Incredible game! Everything is very very well done: music, 3d, controls, gameplay. I was impressed by the 3D boxes physics and I really had fun playing. Congratulations, one of the best entries this year according to me!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Ahh, thank you for such kind words! 🥹

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text:
Some interesting things in graphics (ellipsoid shapes, flashlight).

Gameplay and level design are, well, not the strongest part of this entry. I haven’t played Luigi’s mansion, maybe then I would perceive it differently.

Most interesting part is the physics. There is a serious lack of the lightweight 3d physics libs for js (for 2d there is xem’s), and seeing that someone is addressing it is very inspiring. But it’s usage in this game was very limited. i wonder why.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text:
Thanks Alexander! You’re absolutely correct that the 3d physics are not adequately used. There are two reasons. One, the physics engine was not well optimized, and complex scenes ran quite slowly on underpowered hardware, which is difficult in js13k because underpowered hardware is surprisingly common. Two, the physics engine lacked enough stability to rely on it as a core mechanic. By “stability”, I mean stacking boxes and having it work as expected. There are many small tweaks necessary to fully stabilize everything.

I’ve been chatting with xem about extracting the physics as a standalone library. Here is a stripped down version, with some additional notes about implementation details and known issues: https://github.com/codyebberson/js13k-3dphysics-for-xem

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Really impressive graphics! And really good gameplay too! Couldn’t stop until I did it! :D

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Lovely game! The final size of 12.98kB links it very strongly to this year’s theme :)

Love the tech you put in this, the lighting and physics sim in particular. In terms of difficulty it might be a bit too easy, as others have pointed out, but I also got my ass kicked one too many times this evening while playing other entries, so something being a bit easier where one can simply relax and play is a very welcome improvement.

I think you could improve level design and variety a bit, but you’re also right on the limit, so I can appreciate it would have been tricky to pull off. Congrats on a great entry!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
When the galactic president Mr. Beeblebrox’s calls, I am ready to vac the spirits from his lab!

Very nice game with astonishing graphics for 13 kb

I also like the gameplay and the narration. Controls are fine too - support for a gamepad would be a “nice to have”

Good Job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Hi! Congrats on the game! Really nice!

  1. Loved the music
  2. The controls feel pretty good. I liked that you don’t need to be super close to grab the boxes.
  3. Great effect to vacuum things.
  4. And the levels are fun. Would’ve liked to play more levels! Good entry!

And good to see others based in the Bay Area!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Amazing work, 3d engine and animation was really smooth. Solid music and sound effects. Maybe simplify some things like get rid of the score and current floor display, you could show score after they win. Some shadows would help ground the character, even a simple fake spot below them. For controls you might want to make it so when holding down spacebar the player is locked in the direction they are facing. Great entry, I really enjoyed this one!

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: It doesn’t render anything on my browser (Firefox/Linux). It works on Chromium though. Everything is amazing! Awesome Job!!!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Wow ! How did you manage to pack that much data into 13k ?

Nice music, sfx, simple but polished graphics, 3D with shadows, efficient physics, a complete story (yet the theme is a bit overlooked) and smooth controls.

The game is a bit too easy, I shouldn’t be able to finish it on the first run ! And major drawback, the 3D doesn’t display properly with Firefox, you only get the overlays. I had to switch to Chromium.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Fantastic audio! The sound for traveling via pipes is especially lovely :)

Thanks for publishing the physics thing, will be happy to take a closer look.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Impressive graphics with a cool physics system. Ghostbusters had better watch out. Loved the music as well! Finished with a score of 82600 (with the time bonus)!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Wow! The physics, the rendering, the music - it’s amazing! Very nice game :)

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: Not only technically excellent, given the jam limitations, but great execution and really fun to play. Amazing job!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Excellent 3d game! Great experience that fits the theme. Audio is smooth and adds a good vibe to the game! Cool physics with boxes, light effects and phantoms! The only little improvement detail should be about controls: sometimes the character seems floating and turn slowly

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Love the music! I’ve actually been playing Luigi’s Mansion 3 a little lately, so it’s pretty cool to see a game inspired by it.

I like the mechanics and the controls are good also. Played all the way through and I can tell you struggled to make levels and content about halfway through. Kudos to going on and making an ending too! The last room was pretty surprising to me, but fun!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: 3d games will always be my fav good work on making one under 13kb, the graphics are epic and dialogs are fun and entertaining.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is really fun! Really impressive amount of stuff packed into 13kb!

Apart from the solid gameplay, the sound effects and music are really nice, as are the minor story interludes. I like that the UI invites a “speed running” mindset, too, with a running timer. Would be fun to try and get a fast finish time on this game.

I could beat the game with a score of 51,560.

My only thought is that I wish the vacuum attack had some sound effect or more obvious visual cue? Sometimes I couldn’t tell if I was actually performing the attack or not.

But yeah, great game!!

comments.author: Emre Guneyler
comments.text: I think the best game of all Js13k history. Loved the extra health so I don’t die quick, Juicy mechanics, great audio. Top starts all the way. Good job.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Wow, an amazing game with great 3D graphics. I really appreciate the simple controls and perspective. It was fun collecting gold cubes and vacuuming ghosts and colorful cubes!

Aiming at a ghost sometimes felt really clumsy and kept facing the wrong way but I didn’t die. Perhaps an auto-lock to face the nearest ghost would help. Otherwise, it was a totally enjoyable experience!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game is pretty neat, and definitely has Luigi’s mansion vibes. I love playing that on the gamecube.

The tune is good and pretty faithfully recreated.

The torch works really well to illuminate the scene.

The vacuum kept stopping working, was there something I was missing here?

The game didn’t work at all on firefox (except the music), but was ok on Chrome.

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
Feels and plays solid all round. Chapeau bas for tackling 3D in 13KB so well. Even if I tried to nitpick, I’d have a hard time.

It’s awesome to see you participating again after last year’s absence — and to see you clearly building on the experiences and tools from previous years, improving on them each time. Here’s to hoping to see another game of yours next year!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you so much for kind words ☺️ Likewise, thank you so much for all of your hard work on the website and managing the chaos in Slack and Discord. Looking forward to next year 🥂

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Wow, wonderful 3D graphics!

Really fun to play! I loved the scientist characters and his initial instructions.

Congratulations!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: This is a unique 3D game in such a small file size! I was impressed overall with the simple controls and fun experience. The game might need some additional attacks, but it’s already amazing to play. The execution of the game is perfect. My colleagues here at Poki are also impressed!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you for your feedback, Joep! I’m thrilled that you and your Poki colleagues enjoyed the game. The Poki playtesting services were invaluable in refining the game, and I’m grateful for Poki’s support of the js13k community - thank you!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: 👻♥ this is my favourite game of this year’s js13k competition, in terms of game-feel and fun factor! I love that almost everything is made from scaled spheres. I love the mario pipes and sounds. Love the luigi mansion ghostbusting. Love the lighthearted vibe and music. And I really loved the smooth satisfying movement. This game has little to do with the theme except the number of levels and general spooky-ness, but I don’t care, it is really fabulous and I enjoyed every minute. incredible work.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Wow! Thank you! Your feedback has me over the moon! I’ve been a fan since “Wolf King”, and your work has been a direct inspiration for my js13k entries over the years. Thank you for the kind words!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
If 15 minutes ago someone had asked me “Could you make Luigi’s Mansion, in 3D, with music, in 13KB?” I probably would have smiled politely and quickly left the room. And then I played Phantomicus.

Let’s get this out of the way up front: Phantomicus is nothing short of remarkable. Upon entering the lab you are immediately presented with a very Nintendo-esque vibe, especially the great music that plays through-out. Move the character around and they will swing their torch, illuminating the darker areas. Find the character that details the task to you which involves ridding the lab of the ghosts. And, sure enough, there is a handy ghost which you can vacuum up by holding down the space bar. Then you need to find the golden key that unlocks the green warp pipe to the next room. When I said this was inspired by Nintendo, I really meant it.

The rooms get slightly more complex as you go. Some have more ghosts, some have basic puzzles, some have stacks of cubes you need to knock down to find the key. And, once you reach the 13th floor you’re beset with a monster wave of ghosts.

You will probably complete the game in your first sitting. But you know what? That’s such a great thing! Because I had great fun doing it, and while a few of the higher floors did tend to repeat from earlier ones, you still have to bear in mind: 13KB! Game jam! Short deadline! With a little more time I have no doubt all of the levels could have been really varied. As it stands, it’s a technical triumph. My only criticism would be it’s quite weak link to the theme. Given the subject matter on display here, it feels like the theme could have been better integrated into the game itself.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Thank you so much for your kind words! I’ve been a fan of yours since Cat Astro Phi! I’m honored 🙏

api.name: The Emerald Serpent
api.description: You are trapped by a superstitious puzzle master who will test you with Physical, Mental, Skill and Mystery challenges. Reach the 13 in each one to banish the puzzle master and win the game.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Very good game, played and completed in Browser but will come back to try in VR. Really nice challenges, all fun and stressful even in a browser!

Could see this extending and being a larger game, was like the TV game The Cube!

comments.author: Alex Swan
comments.text:
Ha it’s funny you say that, two of the challenges are “heavily inspired by” the British game show The Crystal Maze.

Thanks for playing! I’m glad it works out well enough on desktop.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Great combination of a couple of solid mini games. Good visuals, with attention to detail like using bloom.

There are perhaps no real surprises here, but what is best about this game is how everything is at a good level, where most games are lacking in one or more areas.

Great work!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Interesting puzzles. I think the one with the grid of numbers if confusing since is makes some weird floating point sums (0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3 = 1). The puzzles themselves are fun but the game left a bit too rough for me: the sudden scene changes, the loud harsh noises, the fast animations.

comments.author: Alex Swan
comments.text: Thanks for playing! I agree, doing 0.3 wasn’t ideal but I liked it more than making fractions for every tile (25/3, 16/3, etc.). I do appreciate your other feedback too, this would be better with a bit more polish.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Lovely pack of mini games! I’m a big fan of the clock game, feels quite intense. I had some difficulty playing the snake game, as if the targeting didn’t quite sit right with me, I couldn’t be precise enough - but that’s likely a personal preference.

Nice sound effects, and the minimalistic graphics are doing a good job too. Well done! I do wonder why you decided on a combination of games instead of polishing one of them to a shine? Of course, my favorite for polish, levels and twists would be the clock game :)

comments.author: Alex Swan
comments.text: Thanks for playing, and thank you for the kind words! I’m not sure why I keep trying to do many mini games and end up with 2-4 each year, I guess I’m doing my best to create an escape room experience. I’m glad that I didn’t have to reduce scope this year and got the number of games I planned this time.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: I’m left wanting more puzzles! (but I know generating content is hard)

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game flips the narrative surrounding the number 13. Instead of invoking fear, it embraces the number through fun challenges, such as creating a snake with 13 segments or achieving a sum of 13.

Gameplay: The gameplay is enjoyable, but there’s room for enhancement. Introducing additional elements and a more engaging environment could elevate the experience. Additionally, players should have the option to exit mini-games at will and return to the main game seamlessly.

Audio: The audio complements the game well, but some sound effects could be smoother. A few adjustments would enhance the overall auditory experience.

Innovation: This game stands out as a good example of puzzles in XR. With further refinement, it has the potential to become a must-try title in the genre.

Graphics: The visuals need improvement. Adding more dynamic elements and ensuring the mini-games align closely with the central theme would greatly enhance the aesthetic appeal.

Controls: No issues here; the controls are responsive and intuitive.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Some fun mini games in 3D.

These played really well on desktop also.

Very well made puzzles which used the 3D well - great job.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: Is it not more a reverse Triskaidekaphobia ? Despite the nuance there are a lot of references to the number 13 which I appreciated.

Gameplay: I love mini-games and puzzles. I wonder if smoother transitions between games could influence the overall feeling.

Audio: Nice audio effects but missing an immersive background music.

Innovation: I enjoyed the minigame challenges and never encountered the clock mechanics in a room before.

Graphics: This is great for 13k and maybe better lighting could help.

Controls: All good and responsive.

comments.author: Alex Swan
comments.text: Thanks for your feedback and thanks for playing! I’m glad that most of your suggestions are what I would have done if I had more time to put into this project. I intended to have a puzzle master become more angry/afraid as you solve each puzzle (which would have helped with the theme as well as transitions between puzzles). I’m glad to hear that what was there worked well.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Innovation: I liked having a variety of games. It’s worth noting that you’ve come up with a few different minigames, each of which could have been its own submission. There is a lot to explore here, good work! The wall timer concept was surprisingly fun and challenging.

Audio: I enjoyed the audio style, it reminded me of C64 or “bitunes” audio which complements the retro feel.
