Theme: I liked the concept of a retro game collection, and most games adhered to the 13 requirement, but games seemed to be somewhat random and didn’t have an obvious thematic element connecting them together.

Gameplay: There’s a good mix of easy and challenging elements, providing varied difficulty levels but some minor issues like NaN errors when adding up rows/columns (after removing numbers) detract from the experience.

Graphics: The visuals seem to intentionally embrace a classic low poly game style. Some minigames are nicely polished, some have room for improvement.

Controls: The game works well with a single controller which is a positive. I liked the control scheme for the snake chase game, but some games within the collection were confusing and required a few tries to figure out.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: I loved the possibility of having different and very original challenges within the same game. The interaction feels great, and the sound really helps, especially in the clock challenge. Without a doubt, one of the best this year, and I would love to see a more polished version because I think it has a lot of potential.

api.name: 1:13 AM
api.description: Chop the metal dudes before they get you. They have 13 spikes

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Strong visuals, good atmosphere, glowing eyes,fun controls, great how the metal dudes get sliced up the right way with your sword!

Gameplay was good, but these 360 attack games always feel a bit random since you die from the metal dudes you don’t see.

comments.author: hybr85
comments.text: Thank you! I was considering limiting the range of attack to mostly in front and only a few from behind, but forgot about it before submitting.

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: While the title hints at the topic, it would be beneficial to better connect the metal characters to this year’s theme of fear or avoidance of the number 13.

Gameplay: The sword mechanics feel immediate and satisfying, but it’s a bit disconcerting that a simple sword touch causes metal characters to split apart. Adding more features and button functionalities would enhance the experience. Additionally, having all metal characters spawn at the same height can make gameplay less dynamic; varying their heights could create more interesting challenges. Furthermore, having them approach from behind adds difficulty, so introducing a front spawn or a mini-map to indicate their proximity would improve playability.

Audio: There is currently no audio, so I can’t provide feedback in this area.

Innovation: The game has some similarities to the Moonrider, but it would benefit from incorporating music to elevate the experience.

Graphics: The visuals are impressive, but adding more objects to the scenes would create a richer environment.

Controls: The controls are intuitive and work smoothly without any issues.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
It’s a shame I don’t have VR, but thought I’d test your game in Desktop just in case it also worked without.

However got this error…

TypeError: navigator.xr is undefined

I’ll come back to the game if I can get a VR setup going

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Theme: The game effectively captures the silly spooky genre, setting a nice theme but the use of 13 seems a bit shoehorned

Gameplay: While it’s immediately clear how to play, there’s uncertainty about bad guys spawning – where do they come from – all angle or from a specific radius? The game ends upon death, which may be abrupt especially when I didn’t realize an enemy came from behind. Nice use of XR space to allow players to walk around the room and attempt to avoid enemies, but the goombas aren’t fooled and still come toward you.

Audio: No audio elements present

Innovation: The game combines elements of Fruit Ninja with a wave shooter, creating a unique hybrid experience.

Graphics: The visuals effectively convey the theme. The sword design and breaking apart animations are particularly noteworthy.

Controls: Excellent implementation of controls, with seamless handling of both the right controller and left hand fallback options.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: You are in the theme by avoiding the 13 spikes but it is a bit far fetched :-)

Gameplay: The game was enjoyable and intuitive to play but it seems I had too many random death (enemies I did not see) making it a bit frustrating. A sensor audio could have helped a long way.

Audio: Not Available.

Innovation: The game looks a lot like all hack and slash/shooting games in Web VR.

Graphics: This is amazing what you did for a 13k demo.

Controls: Intuitive and reactive.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: The atmosphere is really well achieved, I also like the enemies quite a bit, and the detail that when you cut them, the sphere is sliced at an angle similar to the one used with the sword looks really good. I think that with a bit of spatial audio to know where the monsters are coming from, it would be quite nice. Great job!

api.name: 13 Squared
api.description:
We need help.

See, people don’t like the #13. They seem to be a little… stitious.

We’ll need to blow up all the #13s we have. But there’s a problem: all the #13 squares are not visible. If you can get them to appear, we can blast them all to smithereens!

Will you help us?

comments.author: Matt Light
comments.text: Simple concept and yet still a great challenge. Nice work!

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text: A nice puzzle that becomes surprisingly difficult at later levels, I really liked it!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Simple concept like seen in many games, but last levels are quite challenging!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Nice game. I’m not very good at it, but it was still fun. Glad there were hints, otherwise I don’t think I would have made it very far.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: This is like the classic ‘lights out’ game - except you need to make each square contain the number 13. Clicking the different numbers changes the value plus of the adjacent cells. You know the drill - see how far you can get! The graphics are all you really need from a game like this. The fireworks are a nice touch as are the small sound effects. Challenging, but fun and on-topic.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Love the attention to detail! The fireworks, the dialog at the beginning, the animations in the background, everything. Clearly a passion project, this.

The starting dialog, and especially the “I’m intellectually lazy, but very patient” button gave me a good chuckle.

Simple concept, very well executed.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Clever but tough game! I had to use the hints until I just got totally stuck and there were too many moves for the computer to help with.

Nice colours and shaders, plus some sounds too.

Well made and clever game!

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: the sound after completing a puzzle took me offguard! It was fun, but I got stuck at Level 4. Steep difficulty curve

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Nice little game, felt a bit like “lights out”.

Really odd that you give people the option of not playing as the first choice they get to make?

Sometimes the number didn’t fit completely inside the box, maybe a font issue?

The little sound ditty on level completion was fun.

I really like the fireworks too, which at first I thought were out of focus, then I looked closer and saw they were all 13s - nice.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: the concept itself is not bad but there are a few graphical issues

comments.author: Benjamin Brown
comments.text: Fun game. The hint button helps a lot. And that last screen is worth all the hard thinking to complete it.
games: XIII Days To Save The Kingdom

api.name: Xiicur Surviivors
api.description:
2133 AD: The curse of the number 13 inflicted on earth is found to originate from the exoplanet Xiicur.

You are humanity’s best hope, tasked with gathering data from this planet to help rid everyone of this phobia.

Can you survive this harsh planet long enough to uncover the secrets behind the curse?

How to play

  • Attacking is automatic
  • WASD/arrows/touch & drag to move
  • Click/touch/space to use menus & select items
  • The game also supports controllers

Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
I win, I’ve lifted the curse!

Almost hypnotic, really great little game!

If there was another enemy type that could shoot at the player then it would make them move about a bit more which would mitigate the fact that after a while you can pretty much AFK and come back to powerup more.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Woah nice game! Sci-fi version of Vampire Survivors kinda!

Plays really well, and the different abilities are fun. I maxed out the “Plasma Field” and felt pretty untouchable. Very satisfying to collect all the gems.

I think the different enemy types could have been introduced faster maybe? It wasn’t totally clear if I was making progress in the game at times

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text:
Thank you!

I wanted players to choose their own difficulty by making some powerups(like the plasma field) intentionally overpowered. Perhaps in a future version I could incentivize picking harder powerups and discourage the easy ones somehow.

I agree that enemy waves were not spaced in the best way, I thought it would be cool to have players survive 13 minutes, I guess just 4 enemy types may be a little less for that.

comments.author: WHITE6982
comments.text: Really Great game, there is a lot in 13kb.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
13 mins and I win!

score: 9649

attempt 4

Finished with 6 death orbs, a large and deadly death field and poseidon’s rod!

The first time I was not lucky at all with randomness, but later, when I discovered a plasma field for myself, it was no longer clear who was hunting whom :D.

Very playable, the music also adds to the gameplay. The characters look cool, good balance too!

Of the improvements I’d like to have, the ability to mute/unmute and more theme matching, everything else is great!

Very good, congratulations!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Woot, I win, I lifted the curse! On my first playthrough, I lost at 3:26 with score 182. On the second playthrough, I had final score 9698. I started to feel invincible around 9 minutes in. Lightsaber felt powerful (maybe too powerful?) in the early game. I basically ignored all heal, max health, and movement speed powerups, and went 100% damage. Very fun and engaging experience. The music and sound effects fit perfectly. Great job!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I’ve lift the curse! I love vampire survivor’s like, it’s really well executed in 13kb! I’ve taken some damage at the beginning, but “Heal” power up was about 5% healing, it’s not enough I think. Everything else is fine!

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
My first playthrough ended quickly due to an unlucky random roll: my first few updates were speed, healing, and faster shooting. Hardly enough to deal with the quickly growing horde of enemies!

On my second playthrough I got the Plasma Field as my first update, which completely changed how the game played. I quickly maxed it out (although I didn’t understand the difference between Plasma Power and Plasma Damage upgrades) and then went for Laser Orbs (ending with 6 of them), magnets, and lightsabers. This build was so good that I don’t think I lost HP during the entire run and I barely had to move at all: waves after waves of enemies died at the perimeter around me, and when I moved, I just vacuumed the dropped gems. I felt powerful and invincible, which was fun, but at the end of the day, I think it was a bit too easy and can hurt replayability.

I loved just how many enemies there were on the screen. It resulted in total chaos and mayhem—just what I expected from the genre! Nice work on balancing the critter spawning curve and on the AI following the player. In particular, when I moved a lot, a huge menacing swarm would form just off the edge of the screen, and then I’d charge into it, plasma field around me. Mmmmmm :)

Very nice enemy art, and great music which develops steadily to match the pace of the gameplay. I also liked the subtle CRT effect.

Just before the timer hit 13:00, my entire screen was filled with enemies and suddenly, they all disappeared. The game continued for a few more seconds: I could move and collect the gems, but the UI was gone. It looked like a glitch, but perhaps it was intentional? And then, the victory screen appeared; my final score was 10,273.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Very competent Vampire Survivors game.

The progression is nice, I always felt ahead of the curve so there was nothing challenging / threatening the run (to be clear, this is perfectly acceptable balancing, just wanted to point this out: power spikes are more impactful if the player struggles at first.)

Re: CRT effect: I’m a big fan! Please lean into it more. Make it prominent.

Anyhow, a very solid title, GG!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
This deserves a much better Thumbnail!

I’ve played it so many times and could not vote for it because i was disturbed (kids, phonecalls, reaching my trainstation to get off, etc.) and then did not find the game in the list again, because i could not remeber the thumbnail-image…

But now, i finally managed to vote for it :-)

Good game in the style of “Magic Survival” ( or Vampire Survivors for the younger among us ;-) )

I love the pixel-art and the masses of enemies at the same time on the screen.

Good controls, i preferred the mobile version a bit.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Cool little game

I like the pixel graphics.

The little fights work well, although there’s a lot of enemies.

I played a few times but didn’t complete it

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text:
Nice little Vampire Survivors clone with great BGM and also character design.

I managed to survive up to 5:00 and I found that the difficulty ramp is a bit high for me. The enemy themselves are pushing each other and they are pretty cute.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
But look, you can make a Vampire Survivors like in only 13K. This is one of my favorite games in the competition, there are weapons, gadgets, powerups and even a fantastic video effect.

How could it be improved? I don’t know, you have to ask the author, this game is great and as far as I can see it is already perfect as it is.

Maybe the triskaidekaphobia theme isn’t part of the gameplay, but I’m enjoying it madly.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Some games just work - yes, it’s a mini Vampire Survivors, but it got me hooked anyways, meaning if a formula works, it works :) Well done!

comments.author: Ben Gillbanks
comments.text: Great survival shooter. I love anything with upgrades. Nice retro screen effects too.

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: The pixel art style immediately draws the player in with its charming simplicity. The use of light around the player’s character creates a great sense of focus, especially in a survival game, where visibility can be a key part of the challenge. The soft color palette is pleasant and doesn’t overwhelm the screen, helping the player focus on the action. The environment could benefit from a little more texture or variation to break up the solid colors of the background. Adding small details like rocks, plants, or minor animations in the background could enhance immersion and make the setting feel more dynamic. Overall, the game’s visuals do a great job of supporting the survival theme. The use of lighting effects adds a layer of tension to the gameplay, which complements the pixelated aesthetic. A solid effort that effectively conveys the atmosphere of exploration and danger!

api.name: Small Mahjong
api.description:
A variant of Japanese mahjong for beginners

  • Suits: ▲ (Angle), ■ (Box), ◉ (Circle)
  • Ranks: 1 to 9
  • Turns: 13 (for js13kGames 2024)

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Wasn’t clear at the start if “1 player” was me against AI. It would be great to have instructions for, or even just link to a Wikipedia page or something on generic instructions for playing Mahjong. Been ages since I played and had to look it up 😛.

I did notice the link afterwards, but maybe make it a little more explicit; such as, “check out the rules for Japanese Mahong here” or something along those lines.

Good work on the game. I just got lost trying to remember and figure out the rules of Mahjong again and was wayyy too much for my brain to figure out atm 😛.

Add a bit of music to make it more immersive; you’ve got plenty of kB to spare - even some Japanese music would have been a nice touch. Could have leaned into the theme a little more if you wanted, such as 13 different tiles, all with themes from spooky 13 things, such as other things relating to bad luck; black cats etc.

Nice GitHub repo and README.

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: This is very different from the mahjong I know as I usually draw tiles from the center. Not playing from your hand. This game can benefit a lot by spending more time explaining the game play and write more on the player AI.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I have no idea about the rules of Mahjong, so I definitely don’t know how this variant differs. I wasn’t able to play.

Good job on the submission, because I know it’s always challenging to build board games.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Great job on the submission!

Unfortunately I don’t know how to play Japanese mahjong. I tried reading up on the rules, but didn’t feel like I figured it out. I would have loved if the game had taught me how to play mahjong, helped me know when and how I could call tiles, and taught me what Reaching was.

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Congrats for releasing the game.

Sadly I don’t know how to play mahjong, a tutorial or a help screen could have been useful. Or maybe some hints on what could be played.

Some audio could add a great ambiance to the game. But again, releasing it is already a huge step so gg!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I don’t know mahjong rules, I was not able to play a lot. Simplified rules should have been added in game description.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
An interesting game, looked good.

I can see this being more fun the longer you play it.

I’m afraid I didn’t totally understand the rules enough to get much out of it, the info panel here just stated what the cards were, but not how to play.

When I saw this game was a variant of Mahjong I was excited to try it because I enjoy playing that. I think I shouldn’t have had the expectation that it would be similar, as this variant is additive rather than subtractive.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Well, I’ve never played Mahjong before!

I have to admit I didn’t take the time to look up the rules. I think that if you want more players to play your game, you should add a little tutorial in-game, or a way to learn.

The graphics looks very simple, yet it’s probably good enough for such a game.

Well done!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: well done on the game and having multiple player give a touch to your game, if I was a mahjong player I would dig it.

api.name: The Demons 13 Floors
api.description:
The sound of Pathetique, Op. 13 echoes in the air, endlessly weaving through joy, sorrow, and chaos.

A mysterious 13th floor has appeared in the elevator.

Alice, a witch, who has 13 lives, has been tasked with investigating the terrifying disappearances related to the elevator.

However, as the elevator ascends, it pulls Alice further and further away from reality, one floor at a time.

Perhaps, by defeating the dreadful 13 number demons on each floor, she can escape this nightmare once she reaches the 13th floor.

But… is that really possible?

How to play

Dodge enemy bullets and defeat them. Do not let them hit your heart. Defeat all demons before 13 life points are consumed.

  • ⬅: Move Left
  • ➡: Move Right
  • ⬆: Move Up
  • ⬇: Move Down
  • Shift: Move Slow
  • Escape: Pause The Game

Tips

  • Shift is very useful, moving slow and show the Red Point which you only need to focus.
  • Each time you hit bosses, will get 1 score, every 130 score will get a new life point.
  • Each time you loss life point, you will in god-time 3s, moving wherever you want.

The game looks hard, but it is easy. Welcome, and, Have fun!

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Great use of sound effects and music. Love the 8-bit graphics. A very interesting take on a space-invaders-like game. Good narrative building.

It’s nice to have the controls and game info right there on the screen, great work. It did feel a little simplistic; like I didn’t really have to move around much and the ‘bullets’ seemed like they had little effect on me.

The second level is SUPER trippy (and even the ’bullet’s from the enemies are very light/graphics intensive). Might put off some players that have visual impairments; I would have a disclosure at the start of the game saying something along the lines of “this game contains strobe-like lighting effects that may be sensitive to some viewers.”

Extensive GitHub README - well done.

comments.author: skyatmoon
comments.text: Thanks a lot for your love and play! Thank you very much for your suggestions too! The collision determination of bullets is actually set relatively loosely, but in fact, my friend still said it is very difficult, so he can only try to find a balance of difficulty. Thanks for your playing again!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Reached level 8!

From the beginning I didn’t understand how I was losing life until I finally read the description, it would be nice to have this information in the game.

I like the transition effects to the next floor, looks cool! Loved the rain too!

The music is not bad, but I would like to be able to turn it on/off. Also, I don’t really imagine how it is possible to win, given that there are a lot of enemy bullets, so I guess I would like to see a little easier levels, although I can imagine how they can be played, it was a bit difficult for me, or on the contrary, I stood still for several levels without damage: ). It would be good to have arrow keys and WASD keys :).

Good idea and game, congratulations!

comments.author: skyatmoon
comments.text: lol, thanks for your love and play, hopefully have fun as well! Except for the last level, each level actually has “invincible” locations, so you may have found them! Also thanks for your suggestions.

comments.author: LongRobin
comments.text: Fantastic game! Great audio and amazing ideas!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Interesting SHUMP and good music. Got to level 6.

I didn’t understand what triggered get hit or not. Sometimes bullets would go through me and not take away a life, and other times they would hit me. Also a lot of attack patterns from the enemy seem impossible to dodge.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I had fun playing this entry. I made it to level 12, but only because I was able to find a spot to camp out level 11. I really liked the way the blocks flowed in each level. Sometimes, it became quite hypnotic. Solid game track too. Great entry!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is pretty cool! Nice job making each level feel unique with different enemy attack patterns and backgrounds. Cool that each level has a little story interlude, too.

Interesting that you can move up and above the enemy. Makes for some unique repositioning strategies.

My main nitpick with the game is that I wasn’t sure what was hurting me. I guess there’s one pixel (“the heart”) that insta-kills you? But otherwise you’re invincible (like getting hit in the arm doesn’t do anything)? Maybe that aspect could have been a little more clear

But yeah, overall, cool entry!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game was fun.

I liked that if you were brave enough to go closer to the “demon” you could defeat them quicker because the next shot came more frequently (due to cutting out travel time).

The feeling of moving was good, with the streaks going past on each side.

I didn’t pay particular attention to avoiding bullets and still seemed to always manage to complete the levels. Very generous to have so many lives.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Thank you for your submission to js13k! It is great to see completed games under 13kb :)

I really liked the demoscene graphics you added to your game and the story behind going up on the building. I didn’t manage to finish the game on two attempts, even though I figured that the blocks only collide with me when they go down.

I enjoyed the first levels while I was trying to figure out the best way to hit the bosses, but then once I had it, it was a bit repetitive. I get this might just be the rush of the month in a game jam.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Challenging, but a really fun take on a bullet hell game with an interesting nod to this years theme! My one piece of feedback for the game would be that while playing, it was difficult to tell at times wether I was being hurt by the bullets. This made it a bit unclear what my current status was in the game and wether I was close to getting a game over. Other than that - very nice job!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
A Touhou-like!

The QoL features such as the shift key for precise movement, and showing the hitbox on the player, are very nice. GJ!

api.name: Hauntriska
api.description:
Oh no, someone left the portal open!

Catch all the ghosts you can!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Hey, pretty nice game! Good ramping up of difficulty and explanation of the game mechanics. It gets pretty stressful as different colored ghosts start appearing.

Maybe it would be cool if there were some ability to release ghosts to avoid numbers divisible by 13; that could add another layer of skill to the game.

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text:
Thanks so much!

I’ve been thinking about adding a way to release ghosts but I felt that would make it too easy to avoid the number 13 and I could not come up with a fun, fair and also predictable downside for that mechanic - like release a ghost but a random one, release the last one, etc.. But you’re right, if I could came up with it it would have made a new skill/challenge in itself.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: Well done, it’s a good entry that fits the theme of triskaidekaphobia well. I think it’s great idea to have players collect ghosts while avoiding the number 13 – it’s well designed! However, sometimes I had to wait quite a while to collect a certain rare ghost, so maybe adjusting the spawn rate would improve the experience.

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text:
Thank you, I am glad you liked it!

I agree, the spawn rates feel off sometimes but I think only on 1 or 2 levels, though it didn’t feel too bad after a few test runs - at least you have more chance to collect too many ghosts ;) (Okay, maybe I should have really tweaked it a bit more… :))

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Nice game. Got more into it the more I played it. Love the graphics. My brain couldnt cope with the maths after level 3 :D. Nice challenging game

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text: Thank you, I am happy you liked it! Sorry about breaking your brain, though… :)

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I really liked how you have interpreted the theme, according to me this is one of the best game this year in terms of theme. I like the progressivity of the difficulty from levels to levels and I had fun to play. I suggest one improvement: implement the keyboard controls that according to me are suitable for this type of game. Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game, with easy to pick up controls.

The graphics are funky and the ghost catcher ray looks amazing.

Sound effects were good alongside the beat of the soundtrack.

I got a real Guitar Hero vibe from this game, maybe having the ghosts picked up in time with some music would be neat for later levels where they are positioned based on their “note”?

Maybe let people know it’s a mouse control game.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I like the artstyle and the effects!

Gameplay gets stressfull soon with the different ghosttypes and the multiplies of 13… :-)

Good job!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Cool game, loved the particle effect. Easy to play, ghost got different movements after some levels. Avoid getting 13 ghost is a nice idea (cool animation when ghost are leaving!)

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Hey, great game! I think the level progression is very well implemented.Too bad the story was so short. I like that the ghosts have different behaviours. The blue one is specially tricky. I think after the last level instead of the game just ending it could continue endlessly. The implementation of the theme was very relevant, but a bit annoying. I was also surprised i could finish the level with the alarms beeping; I felt that shouldn’t be allowed somehow!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Great particle effect, even greater transition effect between levels, and good game.

Interesting use of competition theme, the gameplay is really simple but a good hyper casual game is exactly what we expect in just 13K!!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: It is a fun game that I am playing longer than expected. I like the purple pixelated animation. I’d see this game on mobile doing well. Overall, the navigation is smooth, and I’d change the menu / continue buttons to more obvious placements.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: I like the pixel art, and the transition screen between levels. The “shooting” effect was also pleasant. It didn’t take long until I understood the game mechanics, so I paid careful attention to the number of ghosts I captured.

api.name: 13attle TanKs
api.description:
13attle TanKs is an top-down tank controls shooting game.

Controls: WASD/ZQSD/arrow keys

Shoot: X, J, or Space

There’s only one weapon and enemy type. Advancing through more levels makes the level longer with more enemies.

comments.author: Sonny Qin
comments.text: Nice art style, I love it. Great ideas.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: Thanks! And thank you for playing!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This plays really nice! “Easy to learn, hard to master” type of game.

Graphics look nice, too.

I guess not too much tie-in to the theme… But yeah, the game itself is fun! Nicely done

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: I appreciate the feedback and thank you for playing!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Reached level 3!

Good old tanks.

Fun controls, a little more difficult, but quite smooth. Pleasant, recognizable sounds for such a game. It was a little difficult to shoot with several tanks (2-3) at a time, but it was nice to know that you can repel a shell with a shell. I also liked the graphics (I like the 3D effect).

I would like a little more relevance to the theme. It would also be nice to be able to turn the music on/off.

Nice game, congratulations!

P.S.

I also looked at your archive, it looks like you didn’t use any compression tools. However, even so, it’s already impressive that you were able to add so much to the game without it! I suggest looking at different code minifiers, then you won’t have to maintain the code during development in a less readable form, instead have a well-formatted and readable code, and the minifier will already compress the final version for you.

In my game I used webpack in addition to minifying the code, but there are many other minifiers out there, so I recommend checking them out, because then I think you’d definitely lose half the size and have room for more fun stuff!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Yeah, after getting feedback, including yours, I do realize that the game is actually very difficult. Being that I’ve played the game a lot more than anybody else, it didn’t seem too hard, lol, but I literally “finished” the game just before the deadline. I forgot how I should have made the game easier so that reviewers of any skill level can play enough of it to enjoy it. I feel like most players have not gotten past the first stage. I wanted to add more enemies and weapons, but there was too little time.

I also appreciate the suggestion to use webpack. I definitely should have used a compression tool from the beginning because I only looked at compression when I was “finished” and luckily I was just below the limit.

Thank you very much for playing and leaving a review. It’s very much appreciated!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I like the pseudo 3D of your sprites and especially the energy bar ! Controls are very good, the scroll is perfect and the game is fun to play. I think a better background (not a simple grid) would improve a lot the global aspect of the game. Well done!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I agree, the environment could use a lot more variety.

I appreciate the feedback and thank you for playing!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Fun game, I reached Level 5 and could continue further. I used the same tactic - retreating with the backpedal and shooting while the enemy tanks were trying to catch up. I was getting hit but the health drops were more than enough. Perhaps game could’ve been more interesting with more complex level design and enemy spawns and ambushes that could break my tactic, or maybe activating the engine with a second of delay when trying to go backwards.. of course all that for the later levels, not to scare the rookies ;)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
It’s awesome that you got so far. I wanted to add more enemy types to make combat more interesting so that you have different tactics per enemy type. So an ambush type enemy could be implemented.

activating the engine with a second of delay when trying to go backwards

Interesting, that could give a more tactical and “tank” experience. Maybe it could be implemented for the chonky tank you unlock later, lol.

It feels great to hear ideas for my game. Thanks so much for your feedback! I’ll take what I learned from feedback like yours and hopefully put it into my next game.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Nicely done. I like that you can shoot the enemy bullets. The movement is spot on and I like that the enemy dont see you at first then turn to shoot you when you get closer. You should do I directors cut I hope. Got to level 4

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Hehe… the bullet collision was not intentional but I left it in. After the final push, I guess I got exhausted from coding. I hope to add more later to a director’s cut.

I wasn’t sure if it was good that the AI would be “surprised” when you approached them, but I suppose that rewards aggressive play styles which is what I was going for.

Thanks so much for playing and leaving feedback!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Fun, I like the fake 3D graphics, all works well, decent controls and sounds.

Once you work out the best tactic is to move back and keep the enemy at the right range, becomes a rinse and repeat battle. Made it to level 4. Its fun but needed more variation.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: Agreed. Thanks for playing my game and leaving a review!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I made it to level 4. It seems like the safest strategy is to peck and back away, almost kiting the enemies. It’s kinda slow and tedious, but it does seem to get the job done. I like your art style, it’s an elegant minimalism, but quite attractive. Nice sound effects too. Great job!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Thank you for playing! I’ll definitely work more on interesting enemy combat in my next attempt. I also originally wanted mission tasks and more enemy types but I ran out of time.

I really appreciate you reviewing my game!

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Cool game, cool controls. Love the minimal graphics and the UI elements, they fit together well. The game is very difficult, however! Some kind of shield would be interesting maybe, one that slowly recharges when not used, but depletes quickly when in use. Just an idea, and I’m sure I could also improve a lot as a gunner.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
A recharging shield is a great idea. It allows players pace themselves when needed ad not be punished for it.

Difficulty is definitely a concern I’ll address in my next games.

Thank you so much for playing and leaving a review!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: a very stylish game! I love how it looks 3d with the offset grey outline and stacked rectangles. that is a cool effect. the movement was nice and smooth and sounds were good, and I was impressed that there was AI moving enemies to battle. I loved the waypoint indicator brown line which kept me going forward without getting lost or bored. awesome work!!!!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: Thank you so much for your kind words and for playing my game!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun art-style with the solid colour being offset from the outline. I liked the little cubes of health following the tanks too.

In each of my mini battles I struggled to keep the direction of shot lined up with the enemy, as to avoid their return fire I needed to keep moving, but because I had to face the whole tank at them rather than rotating a turret it meant I would often just go towards them, which meant I didn’t escape return fire at all.

What was linking the game to the theme, I wasn’t sure?

Given that it’s a desktop game, maybe having mouse click to shoot as an option would be more familiar, and rotating the turret to the mouse cursor for a shot direction.

Overall a fun game

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Just getting a game submitted from start to finish was a struggle so I decided not to sweat the theme. But I do realize how it’s not in the spirit of game jams, so I’ll try to fit the theme more next time.

I guess not everybody likes tank controls, but it was my intention to not include a mouse aim mainly because I wanted to add onscreen mobile controls, which I never got to and I enjoyed the idea of playing a tank game with tank controls.

The idea behind defeating the enemy was to drive backwards and move forward and shoot after the enemy fires. I do realize that it’s not easy for everyone to do though and I intended to create more enemy types but I ran out of time. This enemy type should have been less common and easier to defeat.

Thank you very much for your honest feedback!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: the brown line I think can be replaced by signs on floor or something similar, it cause a bit of get of my way screem inside me. tank itself have a very cool view 2.5D and stacking of the health as boxes on top of each other, would have asked from some upgrades if possible to keep things moving and have some reason to keep going. well done

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Yeah, definitely wanted add more to the game but I ran out of time.

The brown line was a last-minute addition, so I couldn’t get what I wanted, which was a marker at the edge of the screen. But a more subtle-colored symbol on the ground would be better.

Thank you so much for playing and checking out my game!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice little tank game. Good job on the graphics (stacked layers, pseudo 3d) I love this type of rendering :-)

The controls are pretty fast (I’m using a gaming laptop with a 144Hz display, maybe you are not calculating and applying a delta-time between the updates in the gameloop? ) But it’s still playable and fun!

EDIT: I played it again today, on my laptop for work (60 Hz, non gaming) and the speed is normal and very playable. So somewhere the deltatime is missing in an update as the 144 updates per second speed up the gameplay in comparison to the 60 updates :-)

But i like both speeds of the game, good job!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: There is an acceleration to the rotation and speed of the tank, so it takes some time to rotate to simulate “tank” movement. All my stuff is time-delta-based, so it’s probably the ramp up to rotate top speed. I could be wrong since I’m not very keen on the details on frame refresh-rate. However, the game targets to run at 60 fps so I suppose there might be a noticeable delay in 144hz monitor.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Super nice 3d-ish rendering with canvas. Fighting against multiple tanks at once is a nice challenge - trying to dodge their attacks while hitting them. A very nice little game :)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: Thank you Almar for playing my game!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Very playable tank game with an original and cool-looking 3D renderer! My only complaint is that I was craving dual-stick style controls, where I could be moving in one direction but firing in another. I also didn’t notice the theme integrated, but it was fun to play and reminded me of classic arcade-style tank games. Nice work!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Fun little game. I like the simple 3D graphics and the sound effects.

While the difficulty ramp up was smooth, I left like my strategy didn’t change. Move around close enough until they shoot, then turn around and shoot them in their cool-down time. I wish the movements were a bit faster. I know they are tanks, but the game felt a bit sluggish.

The level transition was very abrupt.

Doesn’t seem to fit the theme.

api.name: The Encroaching Darkness
api.description:
Earn the most points at the end of 16 rounds

How to Play

  • Each round you choose a Tile to place.
  • Each Tile has a building (the image), a value (the number), and a shape.
  • You cannot place a Tile if any part of its shape overlaps the Darkness, Forest, Mountain, or another building or shape.
  • Press “R” to rotate the shape, or press the “Rotate Tile” button below the Tiles.
  • When placing a Tile, if the total value of all buildings in the same row or column as the placed building is equal to or great than 13, the Darkness will grow.
  • At the end of every round the Darkness will grow.
  • Be aware that Darkness can overtake other tiles and even your buildings (removing them from the board) unless you have a Light Tower nearby. A building that is covered by Darkness does not score points or contribute to points of other Tiles.
  • On round 13 the Darkness will grow twice.
  • For scoring purposes, nearby and adjacency is calculated using the building square (the image) and not the shape of the Tile.

Credits

Thumbnail / cover image made by Sanskarans from DeviantArt.com

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
The game is logical and very interesting to play! Especially when you figure out the rules so you can maximise the score from placing tiles. Managed to score 29 so far, but I believe can do more :D

Took me sometime to figure out controls — click on the tiles to place them instead of dragging those. And missing reset button in the end.

Congratulations on submission!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: It took me about 3 games to figure out the basics. Then I started paying more attention to the scoring system. On my next 3 runs, my scores were 20, 22, and 30. Nice feeling of agency and progress. Lighthouses seem like the workhorses of the game. Lakes, farms, and sawmills seem like the bread and butter. Houses are great, but only if you get lucky and you can find a wide open area. Churches seem the hardest to play? But maybe that’s because I haven’t figured out how to tame the darkness. Overall, really clever game, feels like something from a professional boardgame designer. Nice job!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Got 25 points. Not sure how good that is!

This reminds me of a really fun pen-and-paper card game called Cartographers. The darkness seems to be really intense! I didn’t get anywhere near enough lighthouses to prevent it from expanding, so half the board was covered by it. I wasn’t even sure if they were preventing darkness from expanding at all?

I dig the bare bones game aesthetics, reminds me old old web games.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Nice! Cartographers was one of the games I took inspiration from. I’m also not sure what a good score is yet, but it seems that 20+ is pretty decent score. Hopefully everyone keeps letting me know what they got and that’ll give me a better sense of what a good score would be.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
I got 21 points after few games. But as other players here, it took me a while to get basics. It was very beneficial to play one game and see sequence of numbers calculations and highlight areas, it helped a lot to understand rules.

I liked the daily seed idea. As I got, all players have the same map for a day, as a daily puzzle to solve.

You probably already thought about it, but it would be very nice to have some additional info on hover, pre-calculation of how many points you will get before setting the tile. And the same action for lighthouses to show where darkness will not come in any scenario.

Congratulations with a great entry, I see a lot of potentials here. A few animations here and there, a bit of sound FX, some additional info on hover and boom we have a daily puzzle game!

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Why 16 rounds? Could have been 13 to lean more into the theme as a suggestion.

Very cool image on the home page, but didn’t really related to the style of game play. Needs to have an “objective” at the start, not just “place titles” but what my goal in placing the tiles. Do I want more points or less points or lots of darkness or lots of mountains etc? How do I get more points? What is my goal? Found this all out AFTER scrolling down, have it in a game window, not just the whole browser.

Once I figured all this out though, made a lot more sense! I would have it so it’s all in a game window, and have an intro scene that explains each thing as you scroll through the introduction, then have a “cheat sheet” on the side with how much each tile is worth - similar to the way they do this in Settlers of CATAN.

The controls were a little tricky; I placed the first three tiles not where I wanted because to the click and drag function… Wasn’t clear whether can place tiles over, as the tile was read when it was on the board, red to me means “no go” not just the outline of the tile.

The counting at the end of the rounds was really good - nice work on this aspect. Overall, cool concept, rather different, needs some music and sound effects (you have plenty of kBs to spare), and just tidy up a few aspects on the UI and it’d be so much more fun to play. Nice work on everything and congrats on a fun entry.

Add a GitHub README 😛.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
OK, this is pretty cool! Reminds me of Carcassonne a little bit!

At first, I wasn’t quite sure what I was doing, and I got 12 points. On my second playthrough, I started getting the hang of it and got 22 points. I’m definitely going to play again later to try and beat my high score– so yeah, this game has some great replay value, I think!

One small thing I’d change: in order to see the “available tiles” and the game board in one screen, I had to zoom out the browser window (I’m playing on a laptop). So, I guess some other UI arrangement would be nice

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing your game. Lots of strategy in the placement of the tetris style pieces.

The graphics looked good and the input interactions felt natural.

I wasn’t sure if I did something wrong but I only got to play one round of the game which ended with it counting up my scores, then the game stopped without an option to retry or progress to a harder level.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Interesting game, reminds me a little of Carcassonne! I’ll have to come back and give this one a few more tries, got a measly 17 points my first try.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I loved this game, excellent gameplay. Easy to learn, hard to manage :) Really cool to have rules/reminder on the side. I didn’t see the “rotate” feature on my first game, it’s cool to have it. it should have been cool to be able to place tiles by drag and drop in addition of 2 clicks

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Incredible game, very nice work. Maybe a tutorial could be great, I needed a first game to try before really understand how it worked.

Displaying the rules near the game board is really great, it helps a lot.

Really fun to play, nice work!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
This would do well as a tabletop game, you should consider adapting the rules. The graphic style is already leaning in that direction.

Placement is a bit complex and it takes several games to fully understand it and contain darkness. Got scores between 26 and 37 so far. A great idea.

Is there a way at the end to restart a new game without reloading the page ?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Interesting gameplay-concept, this could be a real-life boardgame!

It would be nice to see everything on one screen, and to have a little bit visual “magic” (effects)

I played a few rounds, reaching 29 as best result.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Interesting idea! A very cool way to quickly prototype a board game. That being said, the presentation needs a lot of work so it’s easy to just see at a glance what each type of tile does (I had to be constantly scrolling up and down which got in the way of me thinking where to place tiles).

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Nice entry. Although there are many rules, the introductions provided are simple and clear, making it easy to understand.

I love the concept of placing blocks—it reminds me of a board game! Adding a computer opponent could make it even more interesting, especially if it includes a mechanic for occupying tiles.

I also like the score calculate animation. it’s simple and clear.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text:
Took me a while to work out what was going on - I didn’t realise I had to scroll down to get the tiles at first.

Interesting concept and certainly has a lot of potential!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: variation of tetris with fear of 13 or more, the page is so full of information I lost focus couple times not to complain about having information on how to play but would prefer something simpler

title: The Sixth Prime
author: Zachary Rankin, David Fagan et al

api.name: The Sixth Prime
api.description:
In the Prime Kingdom, in the 13th century, a certain evil — the Sixth Prime — Triskaidekaphobia Demon King — grew fat from the sin of the land.

Soon It’s infernal cage could no longer contain it. It yearned to be free.

The key: The 13 sacrifices of hope’s flame — 13 Levels of Sacrifice.

Once this light is extinguished, the 6th Prime shall harvest the souls of shadow to his will..

And rule eternal, by the darkness of man’s sin.


  1111111    333333333333333   
 1::::::1   3:::::::::::::::33 
1:::::::1   3::::::33333::::::3
111:::::1   3333333     3:::::3
   1::::1               3:::::3
   1::::1               3:::::3
   1::::1       33333333:::::3 
   1::::l       3:::::::::::3  
   1::::l       33333333:::::3 
   1::::l               3:::::3
   1::::l               3:::::3
   1::::l               3:::::3
111::::::1113333333     3:::::3
1::::::::::13::::::33333::::::3
1::::::::::13:::::::::::::::33 
111111111111 333333333333333

How to Play

  • Use 1, 2, and 3 on your keyboard to control your warriors/destiny
  • Use Q as as your 13k ability
  • Kill the skeletons, get to the 13th Level to release the Sixth Prime upon the deserving world
  • Micro ftw

Goodies

  • Sixth Prime

comments.author: Matt Light
comments.text: Great art, sensible controls, and fun game play! I probably will not be able to be all levels because the AI is so good.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Really interesting foundation here. I wasn’t able to get past level 7. I’d be curious to watch your strategies. I assume 1=tank, 2=dps, 3=healer? I tried positioning everyone at the top in a triangle formation, with the tank in front. That seemed to work ok, but the enemy AI likes to target the healer. This has a lot of potential! Nice job.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Art looks great and the concept is pretty unique and interesting.

However, it was very micro intensive. My hand was getting tired from all the switching between characters and using their 13k ability. I also wasn’t sure what all the abilities did, but I spammed them anyway.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Love that you had so many people work on the game.

Cool 8-bit graphics. Would love it if when you click “Play game” there’s an intro screen or something so it doesn’t just go straight into you being hit by enemies. Be also good to have a pop-up for “dying” and also a “Pause” menu to go back to main menu, pause, restart etc. When the “end” and “R-etry” came up, I wasn’t able to click or select either of these.

Add some music and sound effects for an extra bit of immersion, but I also understand you’re right on the 13kB limit.

Interesting concept controlling multiple characters, make it start a little easier, so you don’t have heaps of enemies coming at you at once whilst still trying to figure out controlling each character. Great job though and I love your ascii art style 13 on game overview.

Good GitHub README, love the emoji, 🍭 but it didn’t seem to be 100% related to your game. Make the README relate to yours and you’re good to go.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I really appreciate the pixel art and seemingly working controls, but nothing else. I’d have to say that this is an unfinished game.

It appeared like my team fought automatically so I just spammed the Q ability on all of my characters, which didn’t have any indication of what it was. I didn’t know what was really happening except I’m fighting waves of enemies with a team of 3.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice game, but I don’t know how to beat it. After some waves I never can save my sorecerer who is focused. Maybe adding a shortcut to launch abilities directly instead of select character then launch ability should help

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Wonderful artstyle, music sfx would add much to the game experience.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I liked the pixel graphics and it was fun to have to control the different characters.

The thumbnail image looks cool too.

For me there was a lot to do monitoring each of the characters and switching between them to move and attack the hordes with either the click or the “Q”.

There was often a time when one of them had finished an attack but was just sat there over the skeleton whilst they could have possible gone to help another character?

Still, it’s got potential, and I did like the game dynamic.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I had no idea what was happening for the longest time but it looked amazing! the sprites (and also the idea) is really cool, and I would love to see a more fleshed-out version of this, full of sounds and particles, and tons of hud/gui indicators to help new players familiarize themselves with this very unusual concept! nice work

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: The strong point of this game is how it looks! Fantastic art! But I’ll be honest, I had no idea what I was doing. I ended up doing button mashing (1, Q, 2, Q, 3, Q in a loop as fast as I could). Is that how it is supposed to be played? I really don’t know. Also, it was lacking audio, so sad. Overall, it looks like a good game but as I had no idea what I was doing, I cannot really tell!

api.name: Bubble Burst
api.description:
Reach the goal by tackling obstacles and avoiding enemy (XIII) bubbles in this sparkling adventure

Controls

  • Hold finger or mouse down to inflate the bubble
    • The bubble goes up the more pressure it has stored
    • Pressure above 13 will burst the bubble 🫧💥
  • Release finger or mouse to release pressure
    • The bubble falls down when pressure is low
  • The bubble latches onto objects to move back and forth
    • Attach to the floor to move forward
    • Attach to the ceiling to go backward

Challenges

Embrace the challenges

Decentralized

  • OP Guild Challenge
    • NPCs which gives the bubble a makeover
  • Avalanche Challenge
    • Created my own ERC721 Smart Contract, and deployed it to Avalanche
    • Fetch 3 NFTs from the contract, which acts as NPCs to give the bubble some colors
  • Thirdweb Challenge
    • Use the Thirdweb Typescript SDK
    • Use the SDK to fetch my 3 NFTs from the Avalanche blockchain
    • Use the SDK to fetch bonus levels from Pinata (IPFS)
      • For example: Bonus 1
  • Arcadia Challenge
    • Upload the same game to Arcadia, Bubble Burst

The player has to click the “Web3” button in the game to see the challenges. Clicking “Web3” will dynamically import the Thirdweb TypeScript SDK, and fetch NFTs (My NPCs) from Avalancnhe, and bonus levels from IPFS. Clicking on any of my NPCs will change the color of the bubble

If the game for some reason is not able to fetch the NPCs when you click web3, try the web3 version of the game here

Mobile

The game was made mobile first. Hold finger down to inflate the bubble. Be careful though, if pressure exceeds 13, bubble burst! 🫧💥

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Great game, at times I just wanted to be able to have more control over the bubble but I just had to relax and be patient! Really cool movement and interactions with the other bubbles and environment!

There were levels that looked tricky but were easy and then the opposite! Too a little while to complete but was an enjoyable experience, another great entry from yourself! on a roll :D

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the nice comment.

The game requires patience, that’s for sure. I sometimes get angry playing the game myself, because I’m not patient, haha.

I’ve played the game multiple times, making it difficult to create easy and challenging levels. I’m sure we’ve all been there.

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: Clever concept, and the physics are really satisfying.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. I’m happy to hear that.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Awesome original concept!! Enjoyed it on my phone.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much. I’m glad it worked well on your phone. It was my main target, so it makes me extra happy to hear that.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Great job on the one-button puzzle platformer! The use of floors and ceilings to change direction is such a neat idea.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks. I hope it was easy to understand the mechanics.

I kind of turned a bug into a feature by designing puzzles around the “bug.” I didn’t intend for the bubble to spin to begin with, but it turned out all right.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice one button game. I really think the bubble physics are amazing.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the nice comment, I’m happy with the bubbles myself. It didn’t turn out the way I was imagining, but it turned out good enough for the jam. I wanted to create some soft body physics, and quickly realized it would be impossible in such a small amount of time and space.

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: Interesting gameplay, I got hooked and played it for quite a long time.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks! I’m happy to hear that. Makes me want to work on a post-jam version and improve the game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I love this entry because I have no idea how your physic engine works behind: to me it’s really magic :) The concept, the music and the gameplay are very good and I particularly like when the bubble gets tangled!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback. The music is highly inspired by Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks. It’s almost the same tune.

The bubbles are a series of “springs” and the “pressure” will apply a force to the springs to inflate the bubble.

If there’s a point inside another shape, the physics engine will move the point to the closest point outside of the shape. But this is not perfect, and bubbles collapsing happens more than I want them too, haha.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Congrats it’s super cool! Curious to know how the bubble is implemented

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much. The bubbles are a series of “springs” and the “pressure” will apply a force to the springs to inflate the bubble. I didn’t mean for the bubble to spin when I started development, but I wasn’t able to fix it, so made it part of the gameplay instead.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: Great game and interesting gameplay!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the nice comment. Appreciate it.

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: Very unique game play. I really enjoyed this one. The music is wonderful and the puzzles are very thought provoking.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you for the nice comment. The music is highly inspired by Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks. It’s almost the same tune.

comments.author: Adrien Guéret
comments.text: One of my favorite entries so far. Concept is very simple and very clever, and physics is outstanding. The background music reminds me the aquatic theme of Super Mario 64: is it a nice coincidence or is it intended? :p On the downside points, I don’t really get what “yellow”, “green” or “purple” powers are, and I had a (very funny) bug where my bubble turned into a very messy noise of random lines. But nevermind, this is a very solid entry, very well done!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thank you very much. for the nice comment

I tried to copy the music from Mario 64 (Dire, Dire Docks,) but added a few elements to make it slightly different. Maybe it’s too similar still.

I wanted to do more with the powers, but ended up just giving the bubble a makeover. I was thinking about giving the bubbles different abilities or similar, but I wasn’t able to get that far. I’m happy you tried the web3 optional bonus though (I don’t think many does.)

I’m aware of the bubbles getting tangled or collapsed, but I chose not to fix it (not sure if I would be able to either.) If I ever make a post-jam version of the game I will do a better attempt on the “soft body” physics to improve this.

Thanks again!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Awesome. I completed all of the levels. I love the unique bubble physics mechanics, very innovative, very clever. Most of the puzzles had a satisfying “aha, I get it” moment. A few of the puzzles had a frustrating “just… go… here…” moment. But it was definitely more fun than frustrating. Strong music and sound effects too. Thanks for sharing your progress on Slack and Twitter, it was fun to see it all come together. Great job!

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: This is a really cool, original game that deserves to be fleshed out. The physics are really fun, and the core of the game is so deceptively simple. One of the games I played for the longest, even though I didn’t get very far!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks. It’s hard to to know which levels are difficult and not. So the order of the levels are probably not according to difficulty, especially after level 5.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Hard to managed, but funny game Excellent physics, chill music is nice too, this game is really smooth!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks. Controls are probably not easy and takes some time to get used to. The music is highly inspired by Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks (almost a copy.)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Very innovative concept of blowing a balloon up and how it moved around the map. The physics made for some tricky levels!

I had difficulty mastering moving up walls. Most of the time the bubble would just bounce off instead of climb it. Never did figure out the trick to do it consistently.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks. The bubble can’t go up walls, it will bounce off. I’m happy you mention it, because a post-jam version should probably allow the bubble to climb walls too, making it easier and more predictable to control it.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
The creativity in the game is fantastic! The concept of using inflating bubbles combined with triskaidekaphobia is really innovative. I love this idea!

The game is easy to make user know how to play and also incorporates some puzzle elements, which adds depth to the gameplay. The music is quite relaxing as well. Great entry!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the nice feedback. I’m also glad the mechanic was easy to understand. The music is highly inspired by Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks (almost a copy.)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I love the bubble-physics! And the one-button-control feels perfect.

Also great music and the simple graphics-style is nice. I wonder what it would look like with superfancy gfx, it might just be too much…?

Awesome game!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the nice comment. The music is highly inspired by Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks (almost a copy.)

It would be nice to do something more with the graphics post-jam. I want to try to construct a mesh and use the edge of the bubble to deform the mesh, making for some fun animations (hopefully.)

comments.author: Daniel Cohen
comments.text: This game is super fun. I was playing on my phone and I couldn’t stop. The graphics are really basic but communicate well what you’re supposed to do. And the gameplay was a compelling. Great job!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. I had plans for more shapes and also some underwater effects, but time, space, and energy ran out (I think we can all relate.) Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Really cool little game.

The theme worked well, and the gameplay mechanic was quite innovative.

The music and sound effects worked well. The levels were good and quite challenging.

I’ve played it a few times on both mobile and desktop.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the positive feedback. The music is almost a copy of Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks. I wanted to create an underwater atmosphere.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Really innovative game! The visuals are very well made, and the music is great, as well as the sound effects. I just found the transitions between levels somewhat broke the vibe of the game. The transition sound seems out of place in this game. Good theme fit!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. The music is very similar to Mario 64 Dire, Dire Docks. I wanted to create an underwater atmosphere, and I think a different transition style and sound effect could be better too.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Impressive physics in a jam like this! Loved the ambiance and audio, I found it relaxing at first… until the difficulty got the best of me :D Great job!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the comment. Some levels can be a bit tricky. I tried to make the levels easier this year, but some are still a bit difficult.

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
Seemingly simple and straightfoward, but in no way simplistic. IMO You did a great job with the difficulty ramp-up and the introduction of mechanics over time, without making anything feel like a tutorial slog.

I’d have maybe enjoyed a slightly faster in/deflation rate, so as to speed up retries a bit? I spent quite a bit of time neither figuring out how to progress, nor actually “controlling” gameplay: merely waiting for the ball to “reset” after a failed attempt — and that downtime felt a bit unproductive and static to me (relative to the rest of the game, of course).

But that’s just a nitpick anyhow. Lovely job overall and thank you for participating again!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback. I have the same feeling with the inflate/deflation speed. If I ever make a post game jam version, I’ll probably look more into this to make the game more fast paced.

Thanks for improving the js13kgames site every year. It gets better and better.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text: Very innovative and fun concept for a game. It delivers on gameplay, graphics and audio. Bubble physics is really impressive.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the kind comment. The music is highly inspired by Mario 64 Dire Dire Docks.

I had lots of fun experimenting with the bubble physics, though it was kind of an accident that the gameplay ended up the way it did. I’m happy with it.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much for the nice comment. I’m happy to hear the implemented theme was relevant, as I struggle to keep my games on theme.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: I love the sound on this one! Great rendition of music from mario, good choice of tune, very vibey. Very fun and original mechanics too. Great work!

comments.author: John Edvard

comments.author: Emre Guneyler

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you for the nice comment. I’m happy to hear that.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the comment. I still want to fine tune the bubble physics, but I’m glad you liked it.

comments.author: Jack Le Hamster
comments.text: The controls are intuitive and original. I feel I have perfect control of the bubble, yet I have a hard time describing how it works, which is quite strange. Good game for innovation!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: I’m happy to hear that you felt in control. This is something I was truly concerned about, because we are often pushed away by the environment. Thanks for the feedback.

comments.author: Harvey Seager
comments.text: I really liked the bubble physics and the sound effects + music. I hit a bug on the “Falling is OK” level where I infinitely fell through the floor, falling is not OK!! Interesting concept and I enjoyed it overall, nice one!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the comments. I guess falling isn’t always OK, haha. The music is heavily inspired by Mario 64 Dire Dire docks

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text:
Very innovative! Some ideas:

  1. Visually show the currents (as I understand, there is rightwise at the bottom and leftwise at thetop?)
  2. Put some limit on the bubble’s deformation. One first contact with the “friendly” bubble it has first compacted both to small blob, then unraveled bubble to entre screen (without popping)

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback. Both are great suggestions. Number 1 I didn’t thought of, but I think that could help convey the gameplay mechanic for sure.

For number 2, this is something I wanted to fix, but I just wasn’t able to. I decided to keep the “bug” in the game. If I ever make a post jam version, I’d like to improve this.

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: The minimalist design of the game creates a calm and focused atmosphere, which works perfectly for the mechanics of inflating and controlling the bubble. The soft colors of the background contrast nicely with the interactive elements, helping players focus on the gameplay without being distracted. The subtle design choices, like the transparency effect of the bubble, contribute to the game’s overall elegance. The game could benefit from a few additional environmental elements, like simple floating objects or slight background animations, could also add depth and make the gameplay feel more dynamic. The simplicity of the visuals works in favor of the gameplay, creating a polished and cohesive experience. The focus is clear, and the calming aesthetic helps immerse players in the task of bubble control. Great job on crafting a clean, visually appealing game!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much for kind, thoughtful and useful feedback. I could get some good ideas about what to improve for next year. I’m happy to get feedback about design choices, and the advice about adding more elements and depth are great. When you mentioned depth, I can already imagine how I would add it. Thanks again.

comments.author: VincentBounce
comments.text: Incredible idea and SO addictive since the first level, a banger!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much for the kind words. It’s a positive surprise to get feedback after the jam!

api.name: The Forbidden Number
api.description:
The game features an 8x5 grid of numbers that are randomly blurred, with the level of blurring increasing as the player progresses. The goal is to click on the numbers, but the player must avoid clicking on the number 13. When a player clicks a number, time is added to their time bar. The game ends when the time bar is completely full.

Game uses mouse as control, and numbers are selected by clicking right mouse button.

I started this project initially with Kontra.js, but I decided to switch to DIY “engine”. I am not sure was this good decision, but I use Kontra.js next time. :)

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Very simple and very interesting approach I like it but very hard thou it would be cool to have a power or something that makes all visible for a fraction of a second if you succeed in a long strike if that makes sense.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Nice little game! Fits the theme well. A pang of shock went through me whenever I hit the “13”

Cool music, too!

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Congratulations!!

I really liked it, one of my favourite at the moment! Really simple to play, it’s fun and catchy. Good idea to write the rules before launching a game. Sometimes I got time added but without really know why, maybe removing the blur on the 13 tile that has been clicked or adding a visual effect could be great.

Really nice work!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Solid game, that comes to an end really quick as the blurriness makes it virtually impossibke to ditingush 13 and 15 :P

Nice that you added music!

Would benefit from some graphical effects (particles, etc), when cleaning a numberfield or when hitting a field with the number that must not be named.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Found the game a little strain on my eyes, but an interesting concept.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Fun to find a game about how I see text at a distance :D. Maybe that helped me, but I only got a few levels in before everything was too blurry to distinguish.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Good job on creating a complete game with clear aesthetic and nice music. However it felt somewhat like one of these “prove you’re not a robot” mini games when logging to sites. Perhaps there could been some game play elements to increase the fun factor, like why not hunt a 13k monster hiding behind these number tiles (you capture it when you reveal it’s full body) or something in that vain.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I had to put on my glasses to play :) I think the concept is interesting and I had fun to play. To me it would be nice to add the score in the main screen (not only at the end) to challenge the player. Well done!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: noise is a bit noisy and loud and no switch or option to kill it, game itself is very cool and promissing

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: lolol that was so fun!!!! this was a really cool idea, executed perfectly, so simple and playable and original. the only thing is I wish the audio was as fuzzy and soft as the graphics. it is currently razor sharp. overall, great work! I’d love to see this with emoji / patterns / letters / faces / etc!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A very simple, but unusual game.

The concept was good, and had me squinting as it progressed.

The layout was interesting although it was a little small compared to the browser size. Perhaps resize to fit?

The music was well made, but a little loud.

For me the mouse interaction was all with the left mouse button - not the right as stated in the info.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: Oh that’s such a great idea for a game! Just click any number that isn’t 13. Easy, right? Except with each level the numbers get more and more blurred. Which is ok when you’re looking at a blurry 6, but the difference between an 11 or 12 and a 13 is super-hard as it progresses. Really nice concept and nicely on-theme. My only suggestions would be a little bit of polish here and there - a small delay between the levels would give you time to breathe, and when it’s game over I would have liked to know which ones were left and how close I got to completing it. Overall, very good though!

api.name: Night-at-Hotel-13
api.description:
Escape from a looping nightmare staying at a haunted hotel

Story

You checked into the 17th floor of Hotel-13.
You noticed that 17/F is actually 13/F if they haven’t skipped 4/F, 7/F, 13/F, 14/F,
so you couldn’t sleep the whole night.

You finally slept, but can you wake up from the nightmare?

Collect clues and deduce the actual 13/F, and/or the skipped floor numbers!

Controls

  • Use WASD, ZQSD, or Arrow keys to move.
  • Press Up or Down to take lift.
  • Press Up at the EXIT to attempt exit.
  • Click numbers to jot notes
  • Click notes to change color, click between note items to insert blanks.
  • Click blank notes to delete them.
  • Press M to toggle audio.

(Managed to not use road roller, at the cost of no bgm)

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
Interesting concept, but I didn’t really understand how the game works.

Nice job

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
