I think there needs to be more cues on what to do and how to “collect clues.” I have no idea what I’m doing except avoid the black shadow of my character.

The game looks very nice though. I like the pixel art and side scrolling art of the hotel.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I liked the animations and graphics overall, the play window was a bit small on screen.

In your screenshot some of the numbers at the bottom are coloured, but when I clicked in the game they all were white. What was the significance of this and why would I want to change colours - was it to note the existence of floors and rooms then to rule them out?

No matter which exit I went in on any level it always said I was wrong, then rather than let me continue my hunt it restarted and randomised the hotel so all my progress was lost.

The death falling animation and sound effects were good.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Good job on the submission.

I couldn’t figure out what to do though. I clicked the room numbers, but I wasn’t sure what that did for me. I couldn’t figure out what I needed to do or how the clues related to figure out the true 13/F.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I like the dynamic animation of the player and I was scared to be reached by the enemy :). I guess the concept is interesting, but I have to admit I did not really understand what I was supposed to do with the numbers. Maybe you could start the game with a kind of tutorial level in order to help the player? Anyway, good job !

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: So you have to escape from a nightmare-ish looping haunted hotel, by finding the ‘true’ 13th floor and leaving via its exit. Right. Sounds easy in principal. Turns out, it’s a little less so. As you run across the hallways you’ll see doors. Some have numbers over them. You can click those and they’ll get noted down in your ‘note area’ below. You can click those to change their color, so you can start to figure out “Right, on what the hotel claims is floor 10, I saw doors 11+12” and then ride the elevator to another floor to start deducing the layout of that one as well. All why trying to avoid a gloriously animated ‘shadow’ that chases you and ends the game if it hits you. I’ll be honest, I tried and tried, noted and joted (and died and died) and never once managed it. I enjoyed the lovely animation in the process at least!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A haunted hotel game. I get that buildings and elevators avoid the number 13, and I appreciate the elevator usage for that reason, but I didn’t feel the theme integrated enough. I gave this game a fair swing but couldn’t really figure out the goal or how to win. However it has a nice aesthetic and the animations were especially cool. Good job!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Vey clean presentation, loved the art style. The gameplay was strange, it took me a while to get the idea. I loved the setting.

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: I actually really enjoyed this, what a fun little thing you’ve created with this claiming of numbers.

api.name: The City Without That Number
api.description:
A WebGL2 city-building puzzler in 9,965 bytes.

This is the city without THAT NUMBER. Breaking the city into tiles, you may never have THAT NUMBER of anything (or any multiple of THAT NUMBER.) 12 buildings built? You may only place a new double or quad. 11 stories across three towers? Sorry, that new double tower will have to wait until you place one more single or a quad. Also counts these statistics separately between the two kinds of buildings – residential versus commercial.

Controls:

  • WASD or Arrow keys or Click&Drag: Pan around the map
  • Shift: Accelerate movement, accelerate demo mode
  • Number keys: Select tool
  • Click: Select a target tile
  • E: Confirm placement
  • Q: Rotate double buildings (You can also rotate by clicking on the tool again.)
  • ;: Toggle an automatic random placement of buildings “demo mode.”
  • Escape: Open/close the level select menu. (You can also open the menu by clicking on the level hamburger icon.)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I’m sorry, but I have no idea how to play this game.

There weren’t even words that displayed when I moused over the buttons. Is tut a tutorial for those shortened words?

What exactly do the green check and “no” symbols do?

The graphics are cool though. I do like 3D cities and grass textures. I just wish I could understand what I was doing.

comments.author: 4onen
comments.text:
Hi there!

Yep! The “tut” levels are supposed to be simplified examples of the context. Seems I didn’t do the greatest at explaining that, likely because a lot of the UI implementation was finished in my mad rush to make it by 2:00 a.m. the night before the deadline (6am in my timezone – and I had work in the morning.)

The flow for placement of buildings is two-step so that it is compatible with mobile platforms. You select the tool that you would like to use, a tile on the game grid, then the “check” confirms your action. (For desktop confirm is the “E” key as well – realizing now I should have included “Enter” and some others.) The “no” symbol is for canceling your selection.

Unfortunately I also didn’t get to the point of implementing any “level goals.” I felt there was still enough there in terms of personal exploration, kind of like Sim City 4, that people would be interested in playing nonetheless.

Hope you get another chance to take a look with this added context! (A bit of is in the desc.)

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: It feels like this game is incomplete - I tried to solve some puzzles but was not able to (or at least there was no indication, maybe I’m missing something?). However it was satisfying to put these stores and buildings around in the area. It’s quite impressive on the technical aspect - viewing the 3d plane, placing buildings and raising them with a cool animation.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Great concept for a game.

There’s a lot of different controls to remember.

The graphics were good, I liked looking around the town.

I built something and it started to grow, but then stopped and made it self smaller again until it disappeared - not sure what I did wrong.

I didn’t understand the point of the tick and cross symbols, as no matter which I clicked nothing seemed to happen.

Still all-in-all a fun little game to watch or explore.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: I faced console errors when I tried to play on chrome, but it was fined on firefox so I got to play it a bit. Loved the prespective but hated the UI its not too friendly. I had no idea how to play but seeing the houses build is a cool sight, reminded me a bit of cities skyline it have something in it to make it go next. hope you get something of it

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I think I understood the puzzle but maybe too late. The goal is to maximize the available space, while not stepping on the level 13 or multiples of 13. Like a bin packing problem but for buildings. It’s really hard to understand what’s going on and the intro screen is reaaaaly confusing and it looks like the game already started and all the buttons with shortened words make absolutely no sense to the player.

What is the point of there being different types of buildings? I think the game would be simpler without just one type. Also without labels on the buttons, it’s very confusing. And what comes after I packed the city with buildings? There doesn’t seem to be a game end.

It was very frustrating that I couldn’t rotate the camera or zoom in and out.

I want to love your game, because I think it’s a fun idea. You could make it a daily game sort of thing. But the gameplay and UI needs some work. I think you already achieved a lot with 3D graphics in under 10KB.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice visuals. Intro screen with animations is pretty cool too, but the “level” selection menu should have been centered and clearer, I was confused by this at the beginning. Multiple maps to select, a tutorial a bit confusing too, and I think no real objectif on each map? The game as a potential but seems a bit unfinished

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
A good 3D construction set for sure, but at this point it is more an open world than a game. The initial screen lacks clear instructions, the abbreviated options on the menu are not explicit (how about using a smaller font and uncut text?) until you figure out that they stand for tutorial and puzzle.

There is not much challenge though, a scoring system and goals would be welcome. A a few bugs remain, such as the texture on selected multi-stories building, or buildings vanishing once you attempt to build on their square. Oh, and the controls are missing a rotate (orbit) option, it should actually be the first choice before pan.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: I am not sure I fully understood how to play this game. The menus are quite confusing. Is that a sandbox game? I managed to build a bunch of things, but I don’t know what I am actually supposed to do. It looks good though, and I appreciate it has sounds. But without any guidance, I can’t really tell if it is a fun one or not. Good job making it under 13kb!

api.name: Gecko Blaster
api.description:
Mile 13 in the Desert of Doom is feared by all Geckos. After all, they know that evil things lurk there …but, Mile 13 is also home to the best donuts.

Can you brave your fear of Mile 13 to munch on sweet treats?

Drag your finger on the screen to move our little gecko friend. You can also use the mouse.

  • Collect power-ups to increase firepower.
  • Eat all the donuts.
  • Defeat the evil dude at the end of the desert.
  • Oh, and try to stay alive.

Good luck you’ll need it!

Credits

  • Music by ‘Snabisch’

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: Super fast paced and energetic. Clean graphics and a blasting soundtrack. It might be a bit too unforgiving for my taste; dying happens real fast and sometimes you don’t know what killed you. Just so much stuff going on at the screen. But that’s just personal preference, and the game is still great!

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: Thanks for playing and appreciate the feedback!

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text: Super nice game! excellent choice of music too.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: The controls are nice and tight, and the music and art is great. Trying to cross the bridge is what always killed me since it was so hard to kill the enemies in the way before getting to it.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
This is a fun entry, lots of good things going on. Simple but effective gameplay, a helpful and very quick tutorial, great graphics & a nice challenging game loop. Music and SFX are also solid!

I did play it on mobile too, which also handles really nicely. The way youve implemented a ‘grab area’ around the gecko works very intuitively, and helps make the player movement a lot nicer than if you’d just had a simple single-touch location.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Really nice game! Had lots of fun playing this, lots of attention to detail, easy to play, easy to fail, rewarding to beat! Will do well!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Oof, hard boss. My high score was 4400. Really enjoyed the pico8 look and feel. Excellent music and game feel. Nice job!

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: Thanks! You may have noticed the music is powered by your awesome pico8-music - saved my skin at the last minute!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Easy to learn, hard to master. Great recipe for a game. Tons of juice and effects. I especially love the tail movement of the Gecko. I wish the music loop had slightly more variation, but I think it was clever to mute the music during the boss fight. It added some additional tension.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: Everytime I played this game I ended up going on a power trip! Lol Will play again even post game jam!

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: Very polished entry. Easy to learn gameplay. Very fun

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Love the juice! Explosions have weight to them, so satisfying. The aesthetics are brilliant, to me this is peak video game graphics, easiest 5 stars. Great job!

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
The graphics and sound really stood out to me.

Gameplay was great.

Controls were easy to use.

Awesome job.

comments.author: Adrien Guéret
comments.text: A very cool game, but I think the difficulty curve could be improved: it’s way too punitive! We should have the right to make mistakes ^^’ Just give us 3 HP and slow down the game a bit and it would be close to perfection. I love the juice of the game and I would love to see more than few seconds before dying miserably x’)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Really awesome game! Super cool music and pixelart.

Very fast gameplay and a bit unfogiving (what might be on purpose…)

It seems to me, that it’s easier to play on slower computers, as it was ok on my working laptop, but nearly unplayable fast on my gaming pc…?

Anyways: very good game!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: The game art is very pretty and the sound is nice! As other commentators mentioned, I’m in favor of a few extra lives before dying, but that doesn’t diminish the game! Great work!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: wow! this is so polished and juicy, with awesome art, sweet smooth tweens aplenty, and all the particles, flash, and screenshake you need to really feel impactful and awesome. I like the way your tail follows you. amazing work.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: What a super addictive game. The pixelart, colors and scale is really well picked, and the enemies really cute. Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Really fun shooter.

The pixel art ands animations were really good.

I appreciated all the power ups!

I liked the little areas of the level and the bridges.

Definitely makes things easier with an apparently infinite supply of ammo.

comments.author: Nicklas Löf
comments.text:
very well polished game! :)

one note is that the game is more difficult to play when the browser is running faster than 60FPS.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: Thanks for playing and the feedback. Should be an easy fix - what kind of setup did you get more than 60fps? Did most of my testing on mobile devices so didn’t really think about the other end of spectrum :/

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: The retro, pixel-art style instantly gives the game a nostalgic arcade feel, which complements the fast-paced shooting mechanics. The bright green enemies stand out against the dark background, making it easy to focus on the action and distinguish targets. The cute gecko design adds personality and charm to the gameplay. While the minimalist background works for focusing on the action, it could benefit from a bit more texture or subtle animations to give the environment more depth. Adding small details, like moving shadows or ambient elements, could enhance the immersion without distracting from the core gameplay. The game’s visual style is both fun and inviting, successfully capturing the feel of classic arcade shooters. The character designs are memorable, and the color palette is vibrant and engaging. You’ve created a delightful experience that draws players in with its charm and action-packed style!

api.name: AUD13ND
api.description: AUD13ND is an sound sequencer game. Sounds are created by selecting selecting boxes in a grid as the sequence progresses from left to right. Enemy voids will attack from both sides, and if they collide with a highlighted cell, that entire beat will be ended. Go for high score and try to survive all 13 levels to earn your freedom (to make more music).

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
This is interesting! There is a game here somewhere. After a couple of rounds it seemed obvious to me the better way to go was to keep the beats in the same notes and what starts as a nice melody ends up being a monotone mess caused by this strategy.

Neat idea though! perhaps start the level with nothing and have the enemies lead you into a melody, instead of the other way around.

comments.author: Mike Helgeson
comments.text: Thank you, I really appreciate your feedback. I had intended to make the generated audio more melodic, but time got the best of me.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: One of the most enjoyable game so far! I like the default song you made there at first I didn’t understand the game but when I got to lvl 2 it all became clear. It was very fun, was a bit frustrating destroying the default sound :))

comments.author: Mike Helgeson
comments.text: Thank you for playing, I appreciate your time and feedback!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I unlocked the sequencer! I ended up with the same strategy as Federico, just moving all of the notes to a “safe” row, and moving them all together. I don’t know how it would work, but it would be amazing if the “song” started as one thing, and then over the course of the level it ended as another song, which could be a very satisfying experience of resolving the audible tension rather than creating it. Still, a very fun and innovative entry. Very polished. The controls handled great. The UI was clear and easy to understand. Great job!

comments.author: Mike Helgeson
comments.text: Thank you for your feedback and for playing the game all the way through!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Very interesting concept! I love your beautiful musics becoming each time worst and finishing in a sound mess, it was very fun! The synthetized sounds are well made and the sequencers are perfect. Congratulations!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Fun! The game gets pretty chaotic pretty quick. And I appreciate that the voids attack the rows with the most notes, to keep you on the edge of your seat. I really like music games.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Original concept. I didn’t understood in the first levels what was enemies and why some columns were desactivated. You should add more feedback or visual effects to show voids

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: Very interesting! Turning a basic sequencer style interface into a game is certainly unique. As the tune plays, these squiggly voids appear on the sides and will flow along a track. If they hit a cell, they’ll remove it from the tune. All you have to do is let the tune play to completion to finish the level, although of course it will change how it sounds as each part gets blown away by the voids. The first 5 levels or so don’t really require much input from you - although it is fun to change the sequence just to hear the impact it has on the tune, but the voids basically line-up with cells that are filled anyway, so there isn’t much work to do to ensure they always make contact with something. I feel like as a concept there is something very different here, that is well worth exploring in a larger non-13KB environment. With a full-on soundtrack and voids everywhere, this could be something pretty special.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Deriving music from the playing field is a cool concept! I think it works better if the music is part of the gameplay, and / or if the simulation is more chaotic, so that the tune is constantly changing.

If you press play and never touch any notes, most of the levels complete successfully by themselves I think?

The drums sound meaty!

A small bug in the sequencer: when I change bpm while it’s running, things go out of sync.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
I love the idea, very original. Gameplay wise it feels sometimes a little bit forced, but I feel there is space to make a more interesting gameplay, like limiting more which notes can be modified.

Anyway, it was refreshing to play it.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Fun to click around and make different sounds and rhythms.

I got through a few levels by clicking the row where the next void appeared, but on later level it wasn’t enough?

Sound effects are good.

I think the concept was really innovative

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A very original and interesting game! A sequencer but with enemies that collide with selected cells. I could see this becoming a full game. Great work!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: You should have put this in the mobile category also as it was working for me on my Samsung. Would be a perfect mobile game 👍

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Interesting game! Levels seems to progress well!

Congratulations on the submission.

api.name: Dont step on thirteen
api.description:
Navigate your spaceship through a mesmerizing cosmic grid, but watch out! The mysterious number thirteen lurks along both the X and Y axes. Avoid these treacherous coordinates at all costs, or risk being blasted by the lasers. Try to reach as many stars as you can.

You navigate with the [arrow keys] and dont forget the booster with [space]

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: A nice looking game. The controls are smooth and respond well. The graphics are nice, and visual feedback is very helpful. My only complaint is that since the number 13 comes up what feels like randomly, it’s impossible to avoid getting hit when it comes up on the square you are currently on.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: My high score was 13 (coincidence??). Nice graphics, nice sound. I tried to stay on black squares as much as possible to avoid the randomness. It’s interesting to think about how you could use the “13” concept to foreshadow which squares will turn red. For example, put the “8+5” on the square for 1 second before it actually turns red, that way the player has a chance to avoid. It’s a solid entry, I had fun, great job!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Nice game, simple but fun, I was not very good at avoiding being shot down! Kept focusing on collecting the planets.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Nice presentation, and handling of the spaceship. The appearance of the fatal 13s seemed a bit unfair and random (especially on the border stones) Maybe it would be a nice addition to the gameplay, if the target-fields are highlighted in any way, before the fatal blast. But its a full and playable game in unser 13 kb, congrats!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Fun to play, nice graphics and easy to work out how to play.

The highlights seemed to go in patterns which gave a slight chance of avoiding the lasers.

It’s handy you can go diagonally as you fly around the grid, really smooth

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Game should have be really more fun if hi/death wouldn’t have been random. We can’t predict where it is going to be hit and it’s instant death. Just frustrating and random, not based on any skill. If you had let 1 second before getting hit or mopre and decrease it with score to make game harder :).

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Pretty neat entry.

To echo what others are saying, it would be real nice if the mechanic wasn’t so random and resulted in unfair and/or twitch control gameplay.

If there were patterns you had to figure out or some mechanic to stop or move the intersects that would be cool.

I do like the overall idea (fairly innovative!) and the boost is nice too.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game for js13k!
    • Adding visual effects and sounds.
  • I liked that you:
    • Added the red tiles highlights to the tiles that were 13. At the beginning when I saw the numbers on the sides, I was certain I would need to sum them myself and it would not go well :P
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • I scored 21, and that was it? Usually with “infinite” games it is nice to anchor the value into something. Like mention “can you do 100?” or give your high score – then suddenly I’m on a challenge and will try more, there is a game to be beaten.
    • Was there an order or the numbers were just randomly generated? If they would slide across, the results could be more predictable and you could make levels where more numbers would match 13, and others where less would.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: A novel idea, but in reality pretty hard to play. I struggled to keep an eye on the numbers and how they changed while also navigating my ship.

title: Laundry In Debt
author: Fazle Adyuta U. and Zac Sawyer

api.name: Laundry In Debt
api.description:
You are a laundromat owner in a place where every 13:00, an absurd amount of bills will be given to your laundromat account. Installing more machines in your laundromat will increase your income and daily bills. You also will be accompanied by a mysterious and calculative Wallow who will gladly trade some of his washing machines with you.

Rules

  1. When the clock reaches 13:00, some coins will be deducted from your laundromat account based on the amount of machines you have installed.
  2. When someone sells a machine to Wallow, he will pay less amount of coin for the next player that sells that same machine to him.
  3. When someone buys a machine from Wallow, he will pay more coins for the next player who sells that same machine to him.

How To Play

  1. Double-click the tile to install or uninstall machines at your Laundromat.
  2. Click the store icon to go to Wallow’s Store to trade machines with our mysterious and calculative NPC Wallow.
  3. Click the return button when you get lost.
  4. Click the “+” icon to deposit.
  5. Click the “-” icon to withdraw.

Mobile device

Yes! You can play this game on your mobile browsers. Landscape screen orientation is recommended though portrait is not a problem.

Decentralized

OP Guild

This game has Wallow, an NPC that will gladly trade his washing machines with you. Wallow’s logic is written in a smart contract so you can read its logic and state, ensuring fair game for every player.

Avalanche

Avalanche is implemented at the core of this game. Wallow and billing rules are written in the Avalanche smart contract to ensure fair play for every player. We also implement ERC20 and ERC1155 for in-game items.

Thirdweb

We use Thirdweb SDK to interact with our smart contracts.

Arcadia Challenge

Yes, we are doing all of the categories!

Test it on Testnet

Have fun

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
I did the Metamask thing, but sadly I only get “Transaction failed” and “Account not found” messages, and can’t take any action in the game.

However this won’t stop me! :)

The aesthetics are solid! Love the pixel art. Smol people running around are fun. Red on white just works so well for the UI.

The background music, on the other hand, is torture.

Too bad the game doesn’t work for me, it looks like a passion project. Maybe delete all of the shitcoin code, and make a post-compo version? I’ll gladly update my ratings based on that.

Good luck with the decentralized challenges!

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text: Thank you for trying so hard to play our game 😭. Thank you for your feedback. We’re glad you liked our game style. Unfortunately, without interacting with the smart contract you can only watch the walking customers, It seems we should’ve only joined the decentralized category after all to avoid all of this confusion. Thank you for playing our game. _

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Tried on mobile (iOS) and it unfortunately got stuck on “Preparing your laundromat” screen.

Tried on desktop, it connected to Metamask ok and I see little folks running around but can’t continue the game as I only get transaction failed and account not found errors.

It looks like it’s a really cool game though, based on what I can see. Nice graphics and polished UI! Cool music and sound effects too. Let me know if you address these issues or provide further instructions needed to get the game working, and I’ll be happy to re-review.

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text: Hi, I’m sorry for that. I’m not sure what causes that behavior on iOS. Also, unfortunately, without interacting with the smart contract you can only watch the walking customers, It seems we should’ve only joined the decentralized category after all to avoid all of this confusion. Thank you for playing our game. _

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Congrats for releasing your game!

Sadly since I don’t have a metamask wallet, I haven’t been able to test it, but this is what I can tell from what I saw :

  • The UI and the pixel arts are really good!
  • The audio ambiance are top too

The decentralized and smart contract aspect are really interesting, I don’t have time to dig into it right now, but there’s chance that I check your code later on to learn more about it!

GG again.

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text: Thank you. We’re glad that you liked our game. I know right! I’m also interested in these smart contract concepts. This is actually also the first decentralized app I’ve ever made too :D

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I had a wallet, so I was able to connect (but got transaction failed?) It would be great if connecting the wallet was optional, otherwise it’s difficult to get people playing the game. Is it possible to play the game without buying machines?

The UI looks great

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text:
Thank you so much for your feedback. Unfortunately, without interacting with the smart contract you can only watch the walking customers, It seems we should’ve only joined the decentralized category after all to avoid all of this confusion. Thank you for trying our game. _

You can try it on our testnet version if you don’t want to spend any real AVAX for the blockchain fee. You can grab some Avalanche Fuji AVAX from faucets to play.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: I love tiny people in such games, I did not have a clue on how to play tho

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text: Haha, Glad to hear that. It seems we should’ve only joined the decentralized category after all to avoid all of this confusion. Thank you for trying our game :D

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
It says please click to start loading but for me nothing happened on mobile, no matter what or where I pressed the game didn’t start. Sorry

I was playing on iOS if it helps?

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text: Hi, I’m sorry for that. I’m not sure what causes that behavior. The game should’ve continued to the main scene after loading the assets. I’m sorry. Thank you for trying our game _

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Do you really need an Ethereum account to play ? Since I don’t have one, my wallet got stuck at 0 SANC with no possibility to buy any of those fantastic machines to expand my laundromat.

Pixel art is nice with the meeples running around, sfx nice, music would need a melody in addition to the drums. Hope you can release a version that does not require xxxcoin to play.

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text:
Hi, thank you for your feedback. Unfortunately, you need to interact with the smart contract to purchase machines in this game. You can try it on our testnet version if you don’t want to spend any real AVAX for the blockchain fee. You can grab some Avalanche Fuji AVAX from faucets to play.

Releasing a version of this game without the smart contract feature will be interesting. I think I’ll do it in the future. Thank you! :D

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: I just open the game In mobile since it says for, but doesn’t matter where I press start I can’t play, nothing happens just a sound playing when it’s touch the screen. Could be nice to finish compiling the game to see how it works fue the cover image is attractive graphic

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text:
Hi, maybe you can try to check your internet connection or just go offline after assets loaded, because this game will try to fetch some web3 libraries. Or maybe check for any action request in your crypto wallet.

But unfortunately, without any crypto wallet you can only watch the walking customers, because you need to interact with the smart contract to purchase machines in this game.

Thank you _

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: The UI and the walking people are cute. But the requirement of connecting with an Ethereum wallet is turning the players. Also players who are risk-averse would not like to connect their wallet to an unknown game. The connection of the wallet should be optional and make more players be able to enjoy the game.

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text: Agreed :⁠, ), well, this is a good insight for the next web3 game at least. It’s actually quite hard to balance the web3 aspect while keeping it below 13kb. Thank you for playing our game by the way ⁠_⁠

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text:
Unfortunatelly I wasn’t able to play the game. Based on the visual, the game seems to explore an interesting concept around managing bills and finances. The visual design appears clean. The UI elements like the timer for the next bill payment, a currency indicator, and wallet connection suggest that the game focuses on time management and financial pressure.

I hope next time I will be able to play your game.

comments.author: Fazle Adyuta U.
comments.text:
Yes, this game is challenging players to maintain their laundromat income and operational costs. Sorry that we can’t make the blockchain interaction more friendly. It’s really an interesting challenge. It was really hard to even fit this game into below 13kb.

Thank you for your feedback. _

api.name: Thirteen Terrible Stunts
api.description:
It’s 1924 and you’re trying to make in big in Hollywood as a movie star. But you have to start somewhere, so you work as a director’s assistant’s assistant and move up the ladder.

Experience the fast paced movie industry under the wing of 4 terrible bosses who will give you 13 increasingly terrible stunt jobs to perform. Can you survive the pressure? Watch out for level 13!

Any resemblance to real people or events is entirely intentional.

🕹Controls: WASD, ZQSD or Arrows and follow the instructions on each micro-game

In story mode, each boss has a set of fast paced micro-games to play. Your progress will be saved so you don’t have to repeat from the first boss if you die. In endless mode, you can play all 13 levels in increasing difficulty and in random order.

The melody was adapted from “Batty McFaddin” by Kevin MacLeod (incompetech.com) Licensed under Creative Commons

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Really nice style, love the simple but fun graphics. Like the concept of repeating the same tasks (mini games) over and over with little changes.

The landing safe was quite tough, thats what caught me out mostly, like when I got to level up I could literally climb the ladder :D

Nice entry, lots of fun!

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: I love this so much! The music, the graphics, the peppy UI, oh it’s just perfect!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Really cool. There’s a strange feature with the land safe mission where you can sometimes fall between the carriages but land safely :)
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I mean, so long as you land safely, you’ve succeeded. That’s what sells popcorn!

comments.author: Antonio Salvati
comments.text: love the classic look and feel and the music, story and minigames are fun, brilliant!

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: I love the whole feeling of this game. The great music and classic look. The minigames are very simple but it does not detract. I laugh every time I have to fetch coffee.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: This game is very fun and it made me laugh a lot! I love the style and the music which fits the game perfectly. The endless mode is cool to discover all the different levelf: it is perfect for people, like me, who does not land safely :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
What a neat and fun to play collection of minigames!

I love the music and the feeling of beeing a tiny wheel in the silent-film industry, back then. The controls are on point.

A very well made game! Had much fun as aspiring hollywood star “Buster Keaton” :-)

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Haha this is a fun one, I love how it speed up!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
I loved the career mode idea, but I was always getting the same stunt (the train one), which almost made me quit the game. Then in endless mode I saw all the cool stunts. I think they should be in the career mode from the beginning.

I missed some audio fx but overall it was quite nice.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Actually there are sound effects, but because it’s a silent film, you couldn’t heard them! :)

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text: Interesting concept with the minigames, but the repetition easily gets old.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: That game made me smile! Lovely graphics and music!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Fun! I made it to the 3rd “speedup”, and then things got crazy. It was great to watch your progress on Slack over the competition, and to see the addition of more mini games, and more polish. Sound effects and music are perfect for the game, excellent choice. Great job overall!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Very fun Warioware-style mini games in low-pixel art to the theme of working for a Hollywood studio. The animations are fluid and rewarding for completing the mini games. I think I experienced every game and they were very creative and mostly fair.

The story mode was too hard, so I went for the endless mode and found that you can play more of the mini games there. The one game that I felt was unfair was the falling house wall. You would only figure it out how to win after losing a few times knowing generally where you need to be. Sometimes the controls were too tight so I’d move too quickly.

A slight acceleration in movement before going top speed would help with allowing players to move in smaller increments. But other great mechanics like floating off the edge and late jumps off the edge were there, so that helped.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback. It was the first time I’ve implemented a platformer from scratch, so your feedback about the movement is very valuable.

I guess you have a point with the falling house level. I tried to have different mechanics, and there’s only 2 levels where you die if you don’t act quick: the falling house and the wheel. I think both suffer from that problem, but on the other hand, that’s a feeling I often felt when playing WarioWare. I’ll try to make it more clear from the get go in a future iteration.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: I love how the game offers so many mini-games, each with a fresh and unique experience. I can also sense the creator’s humor throughout the game! The music is also quite fun. Overall, it’s very well done!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Well made, art is good and musics fits the mood. Gameplay could use some variety/complexity, though. EDIT: Why have you removed half of the level types from the story mode!? I assumed that’s all, and would vote accordingly, if I’d not notice a mention of more level types in the comments.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
But I haven’t removed them! Simply each of the 4 bosses has a subset of all the levels, so if you play the whole story mode you will see all the levels. I did it like that because a) I thought it would be too much to have all 13 microgames at once to learn how to pass, as testers complained the game was already too hard, b) I wanted all bosses to provide some level of freshness to the game.

In a future iteration, I would like each boss to actually have 13 distinct microgames, for a total of 13x4=52, but that was impossible to achieve in the limited time and space, so I settled for 13 games split among the 4 bosses (with some repetitions).

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Ha, super fun game! Endless mode is a lot more fun than story mode, due to the much increased variety, which might not be obvious from the choices available.

Really nice gameplay, polished graphics and style, and solid minimalistic but fitting music and sound effects. The link to the theme is not the strongest, but it’s there.

Overall a great entry, congrats!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Story mode has all the same games as endless mode, but each boss only has about 5 distinct microgames, as I wanted the story to feel fresh with new games as you progress.

I guess I forgot that most people would not play the whole story mode (granted, it’s pretty tough even for me haha), so I should have made the first and second bosses more engaging. I’m taking notes :)

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice game warriorware like. Mini games are pretty simple to learn. I just failed so many times on the landing one. Nice sound and graphics vibe!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
João, it’s great entry! I love that someone finally implemented a game in 13k in this genre.

The difficulty curve is insane, the game is hard, but I bet you already know that.

Great music and the tempo of the game, everything works exactly as I expected. Putting all those mini games + story in 13k is astonishing, job.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Thank you so much, and yeah, I know! 🥹 For my director’s cut I’m working on smoothing that entry barrier before I absolutely destroy the player.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
A solid little game build around the theme. I like the theme integration with this one, and the strong gameboy throwback style.

Music worked well, story mode is interesting and a good challenge. Kept me engaged for quite some time. And amusing that some of the stunts were just terrible, and ended in a quick but amusing demise. Impressed you basically put a stack of minigames into your entry overall!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: This is just so fun. Love the style and the humour and the simplicity.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Quite a fun little game full of mini games.

Some of the mini games were really fun, others challenging.

I like the silent movies meets warioware vibe.

The music was pretty good, as were the minimal graphics.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: As a Wario Wares fan, I loved to see this done! Great job!!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: I loved the music! At first I thought the game was a little too fast, but once you get used to it and the game becomes very fun!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: If you ever play the WarioWare games, it’s supposed to be fast! :D

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
This is one of the best games I’ve played this year. Fun graphics, varied gameplay, many very different minigames.

Congratulations on a great entry!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
The music is really addictive. It helped me stay in game. The coffee mini game is my favorite one. Reminds me a lot of Wario Ware.

I tried endless mode as well, and I think the variations in endless mode help hook the player and get their attention.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Yup. I already took this advice from other players and restructured the story mode in the director’s cut to provide more variety earlier in the story instead of spreading out the new levels equally.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Very cool idea, great music, great graphics!

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I had fun playing! Loved the graphics and the old-film/cuphead style. The tea/coffee staining of the white background is perfect. Music was good, and speeding up the tempo really made me feel the pressure. My biggest complaint would be DAVE. That guy sucks! Nice entry!

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Love the style and the WarioWare style gameplay. However, you have to account for different screen refresh rates. I have a 120hz monitor so the game was double speed for me and extremely difficult to control. I’m guessing some of the other reviews complaining of the speed also had the same issue.

This was a rare game where I liked the concept and polish enough to go right click on my desktop, choose display settings, choose advanced settings, choose 60hz, wait for screen to reset, choose “Keep”, and then go back to the game and try again. Then do that all again to revert my changes back to the correct refresh rate. But I shouldn’t have to do that.

I think that one little fix would have helped a lot because not everyone will know to do that, and may think the game is just too fast? Still, I think it’s a very good entry.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: That’s odd, because I did take into account different refresh rates? I’m both limiting the frame rate as well as doing all animations and timers relative to the time delta. I’ll have to investigate why this is still an issue. Thanks a lot for the report

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: I love mini games! Quite a few of them were really fun. The music and the choice of palette also help set the mood of a 1924 Hollywood silent movie with Charlie Chaplin or some other moviestar of that time.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Very nice idea! And I like the storytelling aspect!

Also noticed a small issue in safari (v17.6) — text and icons were not rendering at all for me, but in chrome it was good.

Good job and congratulations on the submission!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Thank you for the report. I don’t have access to safari, but i’ll try to investigate what’s going on and fix it in the directors cut.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: A brilliant game, so much fun! Glorious visual style, too. Amazing work! 🤩

api.name: Six And Seven
api.description:
Can you escape the dungeon of Six and Seven? To escape you must light the torches, and keep them lit! The gremlins will thwart you at every opportunity. Light more than Six and Seven torches to activate the portal to to the next floor…

Whatever you do, don’t light exactly six and seven torches.

A twin stick dungeon shooter playable with mouse and keyboard, or a gamepad.
WASD/ZQSD + mouse, left click to fire, right click to deploy a bomb. P to pause the game and show the full floor map.

gamepad supported: left stick moves, right stick aims and fires. right should button and right stick button: deploy bomb

kill ghasts to collect mana. Spend mana on bombs, or accumulate it to upgrade your gun.

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: Awesome! This is really polished, hard to believe it’s a jam game. And thank you for supporting gamepads! That makes a huge difference.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Thanks for the kind words! Yeah I really enjoy having gamepads for dual stick type games, especially since a lot of people don’t have/use mice anymore.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: One of the best games of the jam so far. Loved it! Art style is wonderful and gameplay is fast and fluid. Only one very minor complaint which is when I died the first time, I couldn’t restart using the controller, had to go over to the keyboard. After that though I played all the way through to the end and enjoyed it all the way through!

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Yup the controls could have used a polish pass, I made the decision to add gamepad support pretty late in development. Thank you for playing!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Very nice. It’s dark and atmospheric!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: I worried that it may be too dark. Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Seems like a fun game. I really like the way it looks. Unfortunately I had trouble with the controls on a macbook laptop. I would click and hold the trackpad and sometimes it would shoot, sometimes it wouldn’t. When I did shoot, I couldn’t move the cursor otherwise that would stop shooting. It made it really difficult to fight so I wasn’t ever able to fend off the enemies.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: I’m sorry you had a bad experience with the controls; I did not thoroughly test touchpad for playing at all (I should have but ran out of time/energy) just gamepad and mouse. Thank you for playing!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Awesome. Great visuals, great music, great sound effects. The enemy design is a brilliant use of simple primitives to create something terrifying. The enemy alert sound was perfect. I had a true jump scare the first time I hit 13 and all of the enemies turned red and went into overdrive. Reminds me a little bit of 2018’s Underrun (in a good way). Great job!

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: I had a lot of fun with the enemy design; originally they had IK legs but I had to cut them due to size. The simple ‘orbiting circles’ trick ended up looking better in the end anyways! Thank you for playing.

comments.author: Fábio Santos
comments.text:
Great graphics! I love the dark and mysterious palette, and the dithering that makes the whole theme work.

I liked the gameplay as well. I can’t see the enemies coming up on me, so I always feel a bit nervous about moving.

The map on the lower right was very helpful when I wasn’t sure what rooms I’d visited.

All in all the best submission I’ve seen so far. This link is going on my list and I think I’ll be playing it long after the contest is over.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: I’m flattered by your kind words! I did put some more thought and work into making the game both have an ending and be replayable this year. I’m glad you liked it, thank you for playing!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Graphics and game feel. I had a really smooth experience which for a game jam is already a bonus.
  • I like that you:
    • Reticulated the splines!
    • Added a bunch of juice and details into the already tight budget of 13kb.
    • The lighting of the game really expands the world without adding content. A lot would be lost if there was no lighting.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Balancing (a classical issue with game jam games). This might be your first rounds of playtesting and the game right now is quite hard if you don’t play the single strategy I found of staying inside the altar and hoping the globins aim for the fire, not you. Then you go around trying to collect mana to improve the gun. Once you have a good enough gun, the game is done – just kill everyone on spawn and move on. On level 2 it gets even simpler because the maps seem more spacious and you keep the updates from the level 1 – you kill everything before it even tries to shoot. Also, there’s no reason for using the bomb? The mana goes down – making shooting harder and at the point you have the mana for the bomb, you’re better just shooting everything.
    • Progression – I don’t know if there are more than 4 levels. The game mentions escaping, but it leaves me to figure out if that happens. The mechanics of the three levels seemed quite similar, so it didn’t convince me there was an actual end.
    • What with the number six and seven? I had at some points the number 6 on the top right, but nothing really changed? I was confused and expecting something – maybe it didn’t work out in the end?

I also took some screenshots https://x.com/javascripl/status/1837978518535246313

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Title of the game was cleverly also avoiding saying Thirteen. :) Also, if you get exactly 13 altar points the enemies go bezerk, chasing you fast. Six and Seven is one of the ways to get exactly 13 altar torches. Fair notes, I switched from melee to shooting way too late in the jam and really didn’t playtest with folks at all. Agreed on the bomb mechanic, it was a bit of a tack on to add variety without giving the risk/reward of it much thought. Thank you so much for playing, and for giving your play-by-play on X, I enjoyed seeing the play-through. :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Really nice look and feel to the game, love the art style, the funny little walk too.

Seemed quite easy to keep HP up, just keep killing the mobs and collecting the health but I did manage to die on level 3 or 4. I liked the areas you had to activate and protect to complete the level. Some of the levels were pretty large!

The enemies looked great.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Completely fair notes, I struggled in the last day or 2 after switching to shooting from melee to balance how often health bits spawned, simply ran out of time. Level generation, despite consuming a great deal of the first 2 weeks of the jam, was still sometimes unpredictable, but I liked the wide variety of map sizes it produces. Thank you for playing, and the compliments!

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text:
You strike again with a great dungeon crawler in your whimsical style.

For Victor Debone: lighting exactly 13 torches gets all enemies into a red frenzy!

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Thanks for playing and the kind words Jerome! I sadly will be unable to play yours this year, sold my quest2 a few months ago.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
This game is visually stunning. You really implemented a consistent and beautiful art style. The music and sound effects also perfect complement the game. Nothing feels out of place, annoying, too loud, or too glaring.

With that said, I found the gameplay a bit on the boring side. For starters the game play is very slow. Maybe it helps setup the vibe of the game, but it could be a tiny bit faster.

I played through 3 dungeons but I didn’t feel a progression in the game. I presume the dungeons are procedurally generated. But they all felt very samey, and I never felt like I was in danger of being swarmed by enemies. Because i could precisely point my gun at the enemies, they were very easily killed ta a distance.

In summary, the game looks great and is technically very impressive, but I would try to make it more engaging and provide a better sense of game progression on each dungeon.

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: Fair notes, I continue to struggle with creating gameplay that’s deeper / more fun than the first 3-5 minutes. It would be interesting to team up with a more accomplished game designer and be solely responsible for the visuals and sound. Thank you very much for playing!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Beautiful art and atmosphere. cool gameplay. I stopped at level 3, don’t know if there is an inifinite number of levels due to procedural generation? A bit too easy many times (don’t know if monsteres are heavier later). Just a bit frustrating sometimes when enemies spawn just on player, it should have a minimal distance I think And on a “ZQSD” the “Q” didn’t worked for me (I used arrows instead, it was fine)

comments.author: Elias Ku
comments.text:
Solid entry! Love the art style, sfx, idea, gameplay!

  • It took me a while to realize that you can shoot and detonate bombs.
  • It wasn’t entirely clear that green lines from monsters were bad for me.
  • The camera have to be a little ahead of the movement or the direction of shooting, or at least centered on the character (a little uncomfortable for just for me)

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: The dithering on the lighting is amazing! Art style, controls, music, gameplay - it creates a wonderful game. On the bad side I managed to reach level 7 and one of the torches spawned right on the edge of the level, meaning I couldn’t get to light it completely and move to the next level. Despite this, it’s a great game, good job!

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Great entry! The visuals are STUNNING, and the game creates a great sense of tension. I really like the graphics in the game.

It’s very well polished, and I really like the concept of lighting the torches. Perhaps making it easier for players to reach 13 could enhance the triskaidekaphobia element even more!!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Cool twin stick shooter with a horror theme, keeping the lights on is not easy because enemies run quick!

Loved the filter and the dark palette, good job.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text:
Very atmospheric; gloomily great visuals and soundtrack complements perfectly.

I enjoyed this one!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice twin-stick shooter, with interesting artstyle!

I especially love the gamepad-mode! That feels great!

One tip regarding gamepads: i have a headset plugged in, which also identifies itself as gamepad (maybe because it has some buttons, who knows…) You are checking only gamepads[0], which currently is my headset, and thus, the game is not controllable by the additionally connected xbox-controller. So I had to disconnect the headset to play ;-)

I had the same issue with my game, and solved it by searching for a connected gamepad with mapping == “standard” and using this for the input.

Great game!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Cool looking game.

I liked the sound effects.

The graphics were really well made and the lighting effect worked well to create atmosphere.

I never made it off the first level. Perhaps the focus was wrong, I went after the ghasts, rather than lighting torches and always ended up overwhelmed.

Still, it’s a pretty good game!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: Really impressive work considering the restrictions. Colour-palette was impressive - a lot of other entries haven’t messed around with as many groups of colours.

api.name: King LongLegs
api.description:
A small, challenging platformer set in an bleak, empty world. Journey through tunnels and caverns to find and defeat the evil thirteen-legged king, who lurks somewhere far below. Along the way, find secret paths, solve puzzles, and learn to face your fears.

Use WAD or the left/right/up arrow keys to move around.

Troubleshooting

This game is mostly framerate-independent… but if you’re having issues making jumps, try changing your display refresh rate to 60 Hz. Also, while it’s been verified to run on the latest Firefox and Chrome, it might cause issues with some drivers.

Interesting technical details

The legs are drawn using inverse kinematics techniques (specifically, the easy two-segment case) and automatically adapt to the terrain.

Secret areas

Two. They’re not particularly hidden, but they’re easy to miss if you’re moving quickly.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Interesting game, the legs are very cool, the hurt sound is quite sad, I did try not to hurt the little guy too much!

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: This was a lot of fun to play. You’ve really struck a distinctive look, and with such simple ingredients.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Strange platformer action, pretty cool!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Quinten Clause
comments.text: The aesthetics immediately jumped out from the thumbnail and did not disappoint. Great job on the legs!

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: The smooth animations really sell this game. It feels great to control. I really like the dark but fun aesthetic. Really nice game.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Really fun to explore the caves.

The water worked well, I wanted to dive down but couldn’t figure out if I could.

Getting the double jump made things a bit easier.

I love the look and feel of this game, the movement feels quite lifelike, not one for those scared of spiders.

Some sections had brutal spikes and the leap of faith was tough when the health bar was small. I couldn’t get up the tower.

Overall a great entry, I’ll play this lots more

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Cute platformer! I got stuck, impaled on a screen where the fangs run from top to ceiling, left to right.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Very interesting platformer with some depth

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Wow, this is one of my favorites so far.

Here are the things I like:

  • Leg animation is really well-done and vaguely creepy
  • Black and white palette also adds to the unsettling tone
  • Lamp posts refilling health is bizarre but “somehow makes sense”
  • Gameplay is challenging but not frustrating
  • Checkpoints (in case you die) are much appreciated
  • I think there’s a message of “facing your fears” embedded in the game, too: jumping from a high place into water, walking through what appears to be a tunnel of spikes

Yeah, really nice stuff!

Only one thing I would change:

  • I thought the jumping sound effect sounded a bit cartoony compared to the rest of the game.

But yeah! That was awesome! Nice work!!

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Cool game, great art work, the leg technology is pretty amazing, really curious how you did that. If you write a post-mortem explaining some of the tech I’d love to see it. Only two complaints really:

  1. You should account for different refresh rates in your code. High Hz screens are very common, and I shouldn’t have to go change my screen to play your game. It’s really easy to lock the frame rate in your game code.
  2. This game would be great with controller support! Having to move sideways and effectively press “up” to jump is not ideal. Having a jump button would be nicer.

You have room to do both of these thing, and those features would definitely make me stick with the game more. I still think the game is amazing, those two things though would make it a lot nicer imo!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Really nice game. Was fun to play and explore. Very nice graphics style and the platforming was fun.

There were a lot of times where I couldn’t see what was below the screen but needed to fall, and would end up falling into spikes. Would be nice if you could see further down before making a jump. Also I got trapped in the tower by falling down either side which I couldn’t get out of, so ended up dying.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
When I first saw the screenshot I wasn’t expecting such a fun game! Figuring out how to pass the lake was challenging.

I spotted the king but couldn’t finish the game just yet. But I will try again later!

I wasn’t sure about the theme connection, but after checking the source, I got spoilers :)

comments.author: ric
comments.text:
Best game. fun to play.

  • Richard

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Neat platformer! Interesting choice of aesthetics. IK legs are fun.

I love the sequence with the spikes coming out of the ground as you run past, very nicely done!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I’m not into platformers with double jump, but this one was so fun I had to learn and improve on that aspect. There were some very clever mechanics (not telling which one to avoid spoilers). Found a few hidden dead-end rooms, good that there was always a checkpoint around. Jump sound became a bit repetitive and I missed some foot step sounds, but other than that - excellent!

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: Nice work adding in IK to this game, it works quite well. Reminds me of flash games from years ago!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Legs animation is really cool. I loved the game, but even if I’m familiar with platformers and metroid like, it’s sometime tricky to double jump at a perfect frame.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: That was really cool! I liked how there was some exploration with secret areas and it didn’t feel completely linear. The art style worked well here and seemed to allow a lot of content to fit in though it would be nice to see a bit more variety. Legs animation gave it a unique vibe. It felt very playable and I wanted see everything. Great work!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I love the graphics. Reminds me of the old flash games. The spikes look terrifying.

The leg animations and inverse kinematics are well made. I sometimes get stuck in the platforms, but it’s not an issue since I can get out.

comments.author: ckolin
comments.text: That was great! I love the style. Couldn’t get up the tower until I lowered the refresh rate. Really well made and the animations are impressive!

comments.author: Elias Ku
comments.text: Great short adventure! It made me feel nostalgic for Flash Player era. Also for small true indie metroidvania games. As for platformer, it could be great to improve more platforming physics/feeling

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
wow, such a nice feeling with such simplistic graphics (i like the spikes, especially the growing ones :-)

I like this game!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
Ohh, this has heavy Flash-era vibes! You’re a mere two-legged creature that has to work their way around the frankly terrifying landscape. Your legs stick to the world in a strangely satisfying and spidery manner thanks to the nice IK implementation. As you go, you’ll encounter other characters like you (except with more legs!) that will give you advice. You’ll also find plinths that give you a piece of the story along with a much-needed health pip. The transition as you gain this is wonderful. There are also lamp posts you can light, which form as checkpoints.

And then there is the landscape itself. There are plenty of ‘platformer’ puzzles to solve here. From gaining the double-jump, to figuring out how to plunge deep enough in the water to swim under the rocks, to getting through the nightmare-ish forest of spikes.

The graphics are simple but effective, the gameplay downright addictive and the whole vibe borders on the freakish in a distinctly unsettling way. Excellent work.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: This reminds me of Daddy Long Legs. Overall, it’s a fun platformer that plays smoothly. At the start, I wasn’t really sure how many times I could die. An animation would help. Besides that, excellent job! The thin legs are sparking joy from the start.

api.name: Data Warrior: 13kb limit
api.description:
In “Data Warrior: 13kb limit”, you take on the role of a cyborg fighting off hostile data blobs in a digital battlefield. As you collect data, unlock powerful JS modules to enhance your abilities, but with a memory limit of just 13kb, every byte counts. Your mission? Reach the 5th level of the protocol and defeat the Data Blob Big Boss.

Controls

  • Touch devices: Tap to run in the direction you want to go.
  • Desktop: Use WASD or arrow keys to control movement.

Stats emoji meaning

  • ❤️‍🩹 - hit points
  • 💥 - damage amplifier
  • 🏃 - movement speed
  • 🕒 - attack speed
  • ❤️‍🩹 - hp regeneration

Special thanks

  • @killedbyapixel
  • @roblouie

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice, another one of these this year.. very cool. I like the ram expansions so you have to add more ram to accommodate for more powerups. I also like how there are multiple levels so you can choose when to increase the difficulty.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Thank you for highlighting good parts. Will appreciate list of bad parts too!

Also, thanks for last year results table you implemented!

PS Please answer me in slack, I want to know what our VS clones we have to play them =)

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
A katana in one hand, a machine gun in the other hand, a mortar in.. uh.. the third hand?, another katana in the fourth… another machine gun in the fifth… I’m starting to see a pattern!

A very nice VS-clone with a simple inventory management system fitting this game jam. I like it that the it’s easy to tell that the enemies at every level are more menacing and difficult to defeat, despite the simple graphics.

The gameplay itself seemed a bit slow. On multiple occasions I managed to position my characters against a wall and just leave him there for a minute or two, and he’d kill everything in sight with no issue. I think I’d try to increase the number of enemies on the screen and make everything faster — a true bullet mayhem, so that there’s no time to take a breather.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Hi! Thanks for playing!

In multiple occasions I managed to position my characters against a wall and just leave him there for a minute or two, and he’d kill everything in sight with no issue.

As my friend said during play-test “it’s the first IDLE VS clone, you invented new genre”. So yeah, it’s pretty easy to find the spot to grind XP (data). In the game defense (I haven’t found the way to fix this problem fast before submission) - longer you play, harder it takes on lower levels, so you can grind for a while, but it will harder to kill the boss in the end.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice survivors-style game! I ended with 782 data on level 4 (I think). I went with the forcefield powerups, assuming they were like Vampire Survivors Garlic. It seems like the right strategy would be to stay on level 1 longer to powerup before progressing? Nice graphics, nice sound effects, good level design. This is a pretty polished experience. Well done!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Thanks for playing Cody. Heh, yeah force field is the garlic =)

The right strategy (it’s counterintuitive) for this current version is to run to the boss as fast as you can, ignoring all enemies and collect as much free Data as possible. Grinding on the first level also can work too, but it’s boring and the boss fight will be harder.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I like this kind of games. Overall it’s a fun one. I really like the theme of having a 13KB limit - it’s both relevant to the theme but to the game jam itself!

I quite liked the visual effects of the different weapons. I wasn’t sure about the auto-firing at first, but it’s actually well implemented.

I feel the power ups happen too often. I got several before I reached the exit, and I didn’t feel much danger.

The level up UI was a bit confusing because i was using the mouse, but clicking didn’t work - only space to confirm.

Later pop-ups had some UI issues with overlapping elements.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Hey João! Thanks, I liked that you got the meta thing about 13kb limit in 13kb limited game.

Later pop-ups had some UI issues with overlapping elements.

Thanks for reporting it in Slack, I will try to fix it in the postjam.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
A brilliant take on the bullet heaven genre! Love how the character actually wields weapons, I dig the attention to details :)

Really nice game, I’d play the full / post-compo release with more everything.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text: Thanks Mark, I post jam version is on the way!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I suck at these kind of games if I have to aim, but with the auto shooting it’s so much fun and satisfying! The collectibles could be different colors than the mobs (or different shape). At first I thought I must destroy everything and the level will end, but after a while I realized there is a level exit. Good that I had grinded quite a lot already. The levels could have more impactful static collectibles to encourage exploring the edges. Also there could be rooms full of bonus, but with ambush spawn on the exit of the room for more intrigue. I’m not sure if healing is only done on level up, but if so then perhaps a heal potion somewhere on the level will be cool to have. Reached 7104/8000 but the level I ended up started to lag very quickly..

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
An interesting take on a Vampire Survivors like game, having to navigate a maze while avoiding enemies. I did appreciate the little icon that tells you where the level exit is so I didn’t have to wander aimlessly. I also liked that there was variety to the monsters, both in size and ground and air units, and that they all had unique graphics.

I didn’t know how to attack at first. I was trying to shoot the enemies but that wasn’t working. Took me a bit to realize I had a melee weapon. There was also one point in the game where the enemies all got stuck behind walls and I was able to just sit back with my mortars and kill them all for lots of safe xp.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This was really fun!

I liked the 13kb limitation; I was trying to max my force field, so at one level-up, when force field was not an option, I chose nothing and felt like a genius

Also, I managed to find the exits really fast; but I guess there’s some strategy about whether or not you should leave immediately or wait and gain more power-ups.

One thing I might adjust: it wasn’t clear when you were being attacked/losing HP. Maybe adding some visual or audio cue would be good

But yeah, nice work!!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Fun to play! Reminds me dungeon crawl (one of the best games I ever played, but on console).

The weapons combinations are cool and the experience is very positive, makes me want to play more!

As others mentioned, I found it a little easier. Maybe spawning one or another higher tier monster earlier, or stopping spawning at some point so I’m forced to dig into lower levels.

But if the game would be too difficult, we would be complaining about that too :D

Nice work! Congrats!

Even though, it was fun to play!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Fun, lots of fun! Love all the power ups and weapons. I can see a lot of the comments cover all the positives and there are many, including the cute enemies.

I think the map could have been smaller or more details added, a vast grey grid is not the most interesting. I like seeing pixel art games a little more zoomed in and maybe a touch more juice, screen shake etc, you have some nice particle FX but could have added more in.

Over all its a decent auto shooter!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A good little game, I like the powerups and maze exploring.

I was happy to get the speed up power, whenever I saw it.

Lots of fun when you can see where the exit is with the indicator, more of challenge without it.

I did progress through what felt like several levels but apart from one bigger enemy I don’t think I got to see the data blob big boss?

I was glad that the character auto-fired their weapon as that was very handy.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Nice entry! A classic Vampire Survivors-style game. I love the cute characters and vibrant colors. I also appreciate the way the game incorporates the concept of JS modules!

One small suggestion would be to add sound effects or visual cues when the character takes damage, which would make the game experience even more perfect!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Very nice tribute to Vampire Survivals!

I think at some point I managed to get 3 katanas and was missing “skip power up” button, so I could get another one :D Also I’m feeling like that it would be nice to have a slightly higher HP for the character, so it will be easier to survive impact from the enemies.

Love playing it and congratulations with the submission!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Feels like playing a cyberpunk/shadowrun netrunner on a risky data-heist :-)

Nice game! i like the powerups

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Fun game! I liked that you can rush to the exit, but if you do, you’ll probably be too weak for the boss when you get there. Adds a nice layer of strategy if you were trying to speed run the game. It wasn’t clear that worms can fly, so it just looked like a bug. Some audio would really seal the deal, great work!

comments.author: Lucas Drummond
comments.text: Fun gameplay! Simple and enjoyable, with a focus on gameplay. My main critique was the enemy pathing was a bit too simple, making it too easy to hide in a corner and avoid getting hurt. Flying enemies was a smart idea for that problem.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: That was super fun! It was cool to see the insane number of enemies at the end and ran super smoothly. The audio was good too and fit well with the game. Great use of LittleJS!

comments.author: Fábio Santos
comments.text:
I played this one a lot :)

The diversity of weapons is cool, and I like how ridiculous you look after you have 4 weapons.

I liked the difficulty of this game, though I wish enemies wouldn’t spawn all at once at the beginning of levels!

I noticed that I can’t pause the game, but I can sit around auto-killing everything if I’m high level enough! This showed me why games like Risk of Rain will increase difficulty as time goes on, even if you do nothing.

I wish I could direct the fire in some way, because sometimes I want to clear a path forward, instead of defending myself.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
Ahh there’s nothing like the smell of a level 2 machine gun and ground spikes in the morning! This is basically Vampire Survivors done in 13kb and with the superb concept of collecting bits of data from the fallen foe, in order to buy extra modules to fill your massive 13kb of memory!

Those modules can be improvements, like extra hit points or speed, but let’s face it, you’re more likely to splurge it all on grenades or a katana :) You roam around the map, laying waste to all the bits chasing you, avoiding contact with them and powering-up as much as you can along the way. You’ll also see an Exit on each level. There are 5 levels in total and just when you start thinking “oh this is easy! bring it on!” and you decide to jump down the exit portal, the next level will surely bring you to your senses, with new baddies that are even harder to kill.
