An unusual take on the theme, and also visually interesting vertical gates — I think there’s a lot of potential here.

Would fit beautifully in the WebXR category.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Really cool graphics and minimal game. Took me a moment to realize the point of catching the yellow cubes. I found the hitbox variations a bit annoying because it seemed very easy to crash into mountains but hard to catch cubes. I was genuinely afraid of the last closing gate, so good job!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Nice game I didn’t manage to pass the lvl 5 :( I love the minimal UI, but the control inputs feel a bit hard maybe if you’d had the option of playing it with the mouse it would been better.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Well done!

The 3D is well made and immersive. I was a bit puzzled by the physics of the ship, it felt like it was too heavy! Also the idea of jumping with a ship is a bit uncommon, I would have felt more of a ship if I could go up & down as I wish.

Menu is well made and introduces well the game.

Very cool submission

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: Nice 3d effects and fun gameplay. It felt a little sparse for some reason. Some ambient noise or music would have helped a lot.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: Just wonderful! Plays kind of like Starwing - control your glider as it slaloms down the course full of spiked mountains. Grab the yellow orbs to increase your speed, and you’ll need to do that in order to get through the gate at the end which is slowly being closed piece by piece. You can also hit space to jump into the air, which is very useful because contact with a mountain is instant death. But it costs you a bit of time as you glide back down again. The controls are lovely, the graphics fit the style perfectly and it has basic sound effects. The author said there was 4KB remaining which could have contained enemies, music and more - but I think the core concept here is spot-on without any of that. The only thing that may have elevated it would have been music, or maybe some kind of thundering ‘slamming’ effect as the gate pieces drop down. But that’s a tall ask for a 13KB game anyway.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Nice graphics / 3D models, all runs well. Could not beat level 2 even when I collected the gems and used the boosts what am I doing wrong?

comments.author: Mezriss
comments.text: The main purpose of the boost is to increase your speed - which you need to reach the gates before they close. Using boosts for jumping is for emergencies - when you’re about to crash - and comes at the cost of losing a boost, which reduces your speed. Using boosts right away would have the same effect as not collecting them at all. I wish I’ve articulated this clearer in the game.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A great looking game, easy to understand and pick up the rules.

The courses were challenging, but the speed vs response seemed about right.

The menu system was good and easy to navigate.

The info suggests I should be able to jump? But didn’t go into details on how to achieve this.

For me the hitbox for the boosts was less generous that the hitbox for the obstacles.

I saw some boosts placed overlapping obstacles, which were not possible to collect.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
If it had a bit faster gameplay it would be like “Race the sun”

Superclean good graphics, coluld benefit from music.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I really like that this game is in 3D, the perspective is very good. I think it’s a simple idea, and do not see what it has to do with the theme, but do like the polish in the game.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
This has some really neat elements to it. I especially like the way you can see the end gate, and the parts of that gate closing. A constant reminder of your progress, and a fun element to overcome once you reach the end as well.

I feel like this is setup really nicely to be a neat mobile game too, with some fun music and a bit more graphical polish, this would be a real blast on mobile (and the kind of game you play throughout a busy day whenever you have time for a few quick rounds).

Good dev!

api.name: A Deadly Bet
api.description:
You died ! But that won’t do it for you, so you bargain with Death to live a little more.

Death invite you to play a deadly game where 13 will become your worst nightmare.

Win and live, loose and you’ll regret it.

Information

The game plays with the mouse or by touch. Instructions will be displayed on the first run. For better readability, play the game in landscape.

Credits

Thanks to all of the Creeps au sucre for testing and suggestions. And a very special thanks to my partner who let me finalize the game while taking care of our new born baby girl.

comments.author: Jerhyn
comments.text: Cool and pleasant card game, with neat assets and clear texts to understand and enjoy gameplay.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
I enjoyed it, although it felt a little bit random sometimes.

I think having extra special move would had helped.

Anyway, great presentation and loved the intro.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Pretty cool game. I’m a bit shocked at how long you managed to make the intro within the size limit, specially with the large images. The game design is nice and so is the music.

The intro was a bit too long and i got lost in the explanation, but fortunately you lose fast and can start again quickly. I wasn’t able to win though.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Wow this game looks great but is tough.

Reminds me of the mini-games in something like monkey island where unless you know the trick to it you fail pretty quickly and often.

First game first card drawn - died. Second game second card - died.

The music was good and I love the little animations.

Congrats on your daughter, well done for getting the game done with all the distractions in your life :)

comments.author: Gérôme Levoux
comments.text:
I really enjoyed the intro with the little jokes and loved the music. Pretty impressive quantity of text despite the size limit.

The game itself can be tricky as it depends on your luck, it’s not easy to win but after some trial I managed to clear the pile a couple of time!

Well done for the game and congrats for your new family member ;)

Btw… How did you know for the watermelon? x⸑x

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Great intro, cool game. Nice music too. Death design made me think about “Have a nice death” I was not able to win after several games. Didn’t get the intereset of green vs red treasures. Sometimes I had no more “protection” or other benediction cards so I couldn’t do anything

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text:
Nice polish with the intro, The reaper is so cute!

Gameplay is interesting but feels limited by what benediction cards you draw. A general action to lower a card by 1 without a card would be nice, and add some additional risk reward/strategy. Nice work overall!

comments.author: soline-b
comments.text:
After several games, I win one game out of two.

I learned the hard way the importance of keeping credits, and really like this management part I was not expecting in a card game.

I’m so happy when I draw a green card, and so sad when it’s a red one. I have to admit this is quite addictive. 😁 Well done and thank you!

Finally, I really love the Death design and the detail of the way of dying changing at the beginning of the game ❤️

Congratulations, for your game and your baby

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
What a cozy card game. Usually i dont like cardbased games on computers, but this one is really nice!

I love the pixelart!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Innovative rules for a card game, took me one full game (that I lost) to figure out how to keep enough karma points to get those beneficial cards coming and eliminate the treasures.

Of course if you get a 5 or a 6 locked, the game suddenly becomes much more difficult. Music is well in theme with the game, gfx are nice. Well done !

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: This is a great entry for this competition. I like the atmosphere of this game. The music and the graphics are really nice, and I appreciate the story telling, even if it is just a card game in the end. The gameplay is also strong: it is kind of a twist on blackjack. The only thing that bothered me a bit is that I feel wining is mostly a matter of luck, not really of skill.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I really like the intro to the game! The little story is funny and goes well into explaining the card game. It’s just a nice, well polished game!

api.name: Aurora Snap
api.description:
You’ve been sent out into the wilderness to photograph the aurora borealis. Capture the best shots and improve your ratings among your colleagues. Who knows where your career may turn if your photos are top-notch!

Your camera film only has capacity for 13 more photos (because yes, you’re still using that), so don’t run out before you’re called back to HQ!

Click the shutter button (the circle) to take a photo. As soon as you’ve taken your 13th photo or when 8am arrives, the game is over and you will see the average scores given for your photos by category.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text:
The Reviews Came In… Sharpness: 3.00 Contrast: 0.85 Color Distribution: 2.00

A nice relaxing experience. Nice job!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: It would have been cool to be able to steer around, or have me decide the time of exposure, or why not even steering while exposing to create some kind of mixed images?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Hm … 13 clicks for a game. Well i always dreamed of seeing and taking pictures of the aurora boralis, but if its like this random noise photo taking experience… well… then i can make a mark on the bucketlist ;-)

I was wondering what the black parts on the picutes edge should symbolize, by reading yout code on github i learned that these are the trees! Now that i know this, i can see them in the pictures too.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
An interesting game concept. We had some aurora recently and it was hard to get a good photo.

When I played the game the camera button wasn’t very responsive - not sure if that was intentional but it took a good few seconds before a picture appeared in the gallery.

It would have been nice to have a back-of-camera view with some options to tweak like exposure, ISO, duration e.t.c.

Was the ring around the centre to give the impression of being in a forest and looking directly up?

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Interesting submission. Definitely breaks the mold!

It wasn’t clear what was going on after i took the picture. Some sort of visual feedback, shutter sound, or something, to tell me that the picture was coming would have helped. This idea would be great for an more immersive full screen experience, if you’re interested in further developing it.

It’s also not really clear what the goal is, so I think my score was bad (0.3 on the contrast).

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Taking photos of things is a brilliant and clearly underutilized concept.

An interesting way to do the noise, I was expecting to see a Perlin implementation or something.

A very intriguing experience.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
I wasn’t expecting that!

Really unique concept, didn’t really feel like a game and more like an experience. Impressed by the quality of what you’ve built.

Well done!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
So, you need to photograph the retro plasma effect. Sorry, I mean the aurora borealis. You can only take 13 photos and at the end they will be rated on criteria such as contrast, sharpness and color distribution.

Graphically I guess it kinda looks like an aurora. A little bit. I like how time slows down after taking a photo (as I assume it’s processing the pixel data to make the image :). There’s no audio at all, a ‘click’ sound would have been fun. And it’s a bit arbitrary as to ‘how good’ the photos really are. Even so, it’s a novel concept - even if not really on-theme at all.

api.name: Thirteen
api.description:
On September 13, 2024 a body of a homicide victim was found. Crime scene analysis suggests odd similarities to series of 13 murders commited 13 years ago by unidentified serial killer (see case no. 20110913/013). We don’t know if we’re dealing with the same killer, copycat or it’s just a coincidence. What we know is that killer won’t stop on only one victim.

You take a role of a Police officer investigating a case of series of murders revolving around number 13. The killer is leaving clues about the next victim. Your objective is to identify the killer and protect their potential victims.

Gameplay: On each day you can review police report and check personal files. You can protect a person from serial killer or arrest if you suspect them being a killer. You can arrest and protect only one person at once.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This was pretty cool! I like the art style. The gameplay and premise of the game is pretty unique, too.

I was able to solve the mystery (I think… is it possible I chose the wrong person..? Haha)

One thing I would have liked is a “next/previous” button when looking at each person. That would make it a bit easier to scan through people, instead of opening/closing each profile.

Overall, cool entry! I enjoyed it

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Interesting music choice, nice work adding that in, and I feel it worked in well with the theme.

Great work on the story here, it would have taken a while to figure all this out so nice job. I like the way you built it up. Probably needs some sort of feedback after you click either “arrest” or “protect”. I wasn’t sure if it wasn’t working, or if it just didn’t register, but it turns out something was working because I ID’d the culprit. But having something on each of the cards (like you did with the red X) would be useful, such as a P or A, or even a symbol or emoji so we know that we’ve selected to protect or arrest a person.

Nice GitHub README.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I was not immediately captivated by the game and was just clicking around when I started to understand the pattern and started protecting the next victims. Caught the killer in 9 days with only 4 victims. It was fun in the end.

What frustrated me the most were the controls. I had to click soooo many times. Way too many. Open file, close file, open, close, open, close, open, close, open, close, open, close. I wish the file opened on a sidebar. I wish i could press Escape to close. I wish i could use arrows to move between files. Improving this one thing would have made the game so much more enjoyable.

It was confusing that selecting “protect” or “arrest” didn’t highlight them in any way. I initially just tried… protecting everyone. I’m not even sure what that did.

The music was a bit too repetitive.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Nice whosdunnit game! I would love a shortcut summing up all the suspect clues so far, a search field to lookup possible dead faster, and access to the dead victims to verify where I went wrong.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: A fun twist on the game of Guess Who. I liked the giving of a clue for the next victim so you can try to protect them. The notepad was the hardest clue though because so many jobs could use it. On that note, having to click on every single person to find the job that applies to the clue was tedious. Would have been nice if there was some way to reduce the amount of clicking you need to do.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
An interesting concept, plenty of scope.

At first I thought it was a bit of a guess who type game where I’d need to rule out a number of people as victims or suspects based on the info being supplied.

Then as clicking the people just brought up their stats, and I understood more about what needed to be done, it became a bit trickier as I’d have to read up each file to narrow down my theory.

It would be good if the person you are protecting or arresting was highlighted on the grid of files.

Maybe hovering over people could give basic stats in a popup so I didn’t need to remember lots of details about each person, as they did look fairly similar to one another.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Fun idea!

Though a bit much reading involved. I think it could be more fun, if the occupation would also be represented with graphics. And I found the text itself a bit hard to read.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I enjoyed catching that murderous b@stard! Lost civilian or two, but so it goes. My biggest complaint is the having the CLOSE button so far away. Nice entry.

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Cool quick game!

I really like how everything is randomized in it; potentially good replay value.

I wish there was a mute music button, and also like some others said it is quite a bit of reading to go about. If it were quicker to click on each person’s profile and close it that would be great.

Love how you were able to give it the look and feel of an old DOS game.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I like the concept of the game. The case files gave me enough hints to know which to protect, and who to suspect and eventually arrest. The graphics and atmosphere is also made well.

It was not clear to me if my guess was correct, and I didn’t understand why some people were X-ed out. Maybe those were the ones that got killed?

It was also confusing if I was looking into one murder case or several murder cases. Because when I arrested someone, I didn’t get feedback if it was wrong or right. (Maybe this will be more understandable after a few tries.) It would be great with some UI-indication of the people I protect

Overall, I think this game has great potential, I only think it needs to improve the feedback to the player to better understand the game. It was a fun experience.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Nice game! I quiet enjoyed puzzling together the evidence. I would have liked if it was possible to navigate between the people using the keyboard instead of having to click and close every time.

api.name: The 13th Door
api.description:
“The 13th Door” is a point and click animated adventure game. You control Pip, who suffers from Triskaidekaphobia.

Pip’s doctor has recommended a session within the “Exposure Therapy” escape room as a way to cure his fear. The escape room is a series of 49 rooms connected in a maze. In these rooms, Pip will encounter 13 numbered doors of varying colours, 13 golden coins, and 13 ghosts protecting the locked doors.

Will sustained exposure cure him?


Controls

Control Pip by using the Walk to, Look at, Pick up, Use and Push icons at the bottom of the screen.

It’s a classic Point and Click adventure game interface.

Walk to: Used for moving around rooms and moving to other rooms.

Look at: Used for examining an inventory item or an object in the current room.

Pick up: Used for picking up items that you find.

Use: Used for interacting with certain items within the room.

Push: Used for pushing objects around within a room.

You can also use the items in your inventory by clicking on them and then clicking on the thing you want to use it on.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: great graphics and ambiance but I’m stuck at the beginning. ghosts, urns, coins, doors… nothing seems to be doable

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text: I’ll give a small hint to get people started. Are you able to walk through the left exit from the starting room? If so, then try moving an urn in that second room. Hopefully some puzzles will start opening up after that.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Interesting music choice, I thought at first it didn’t fit in the theme, but then the spooky music kicked in and made a whole lot more sense.

If you make it so that when you hovered over the emojis they said what each one did, would make it so much easier to pick each one, rather than having the go through and click on each action to see what it does.

There was a bug where I got suck on the wall as I went through the door and had to reload. Happened twice.

Liking the overall concept. Nice work, and good job on the graphics too. Just got stuck with the wall bugs, thought I’d be able to pick up the coin, and can’t find the card for the 7th gate. Great job though! Maybe a “hint” button so if people get stuck they can get a hint to continue on with the game.

Very extensive GitHub README with lots of information and screenshots - winning!

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback Michelle, and for the tips on the usability features. I will definitely look to add those.

I hadn’t realised that the coin was so difficult to pick up until I saw your stream. I think it is because the object itself is quite small on screen, so the player has to click within quite a small area to “hit” the coin with that click. In hindsight, I should have implemented a distance threshold beyond the coin and assume that clicks near to the coin where intended for the coin.

As a work around for now, I would recommend to people reading this that they click the “Pick up” icon then hover over the coin until the sentence below the room image changes to “Pick up coin” and then click. It should work then. I must have been so used to this myself that I didn’t see what now seems like an obvious usability issue.

I should also note that collecting all the coins is required in order to finish the game. It will become obvious later on where the coins are used.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text:
Oo, old school point and click adventure, both the good and the less good :)

Loved the open ended puzzles, and the high degree of interaction. I definitely got stuck a few times, and had to cheat by looking at the source code for clues. For example, I had to look at source code to figure out how to get the key card, and that I needed to refill the water pistol to fill up the vase, I missed the “faint marks”, etc.

At this point, I’m stuck at door 9. I have both the “key” and the “old key”, and it seems like I should be able to progress, but I must be missing something.

This is a very impressive amount of content for a js13k entry! I usually try to spend 5-10 minutes per entry, but I’m 30 minutes into yours. Nice atmosphere, the music and sound effects contribute perfectly. The graphics have a nice thematic consistency. Overall, very impressive, nice job!

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text: Thank you for your kind comments Cody. I thought that a few people might check the source code. I really should have made the puzzles a little easier. A point and click entry for something like js13games is a bit of a gamble in that regard. As you say, most people spend 5-10 mins trying a game out, which isn’t enough to get much of the way through a traditional point and click adventure. It was hard to know how difficult the puzzles really were, me being the designer and all. It seems that the keycard puzzle is a bit evil, and the refilling the water pistol perhaps even more evil! That’s probably the hardest puzzle in the game to be honest. Maybe tied with the faint marks puzzle. I was hoping I didn’t make the marks too faint, but at the same time I didn’t want to make them obvious. I wanted to create those moments when the player says “wait a second… there’s something there” and feel the satisfaction of realising what to do. I wonder how long it would take someone to finish the game without cheating.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Nice game, I got stuck after the first door. Couldnt cross the card door.

I felt I needed more clues, or context sometimes.

But nice work overall, smart use of emojis.

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text:
I’ve been using emojis in my last three point and click adventure entries for js13kgames. I think its a good way to be able to build a decent sized point and click adventure in 13K. If you haven’t seen my “The King’s Missing Page” entry from a few years ago, that one was using emojis for almost everything, including buildings, vehicles, trees, etc.

Hint: Yeah, the keycard door seems to be where most people got stuck. Maybe with only a couple of days to go, I can add another hint here: There is a keycard in one of the urns where door 13 is. The trick to getting it out though requires putting water into the urn to make the plastic keycard float. Everything required to do this is in the door 13 room, including the water and the means by which the water can be transferred into the urn containing the keycard.

I agree that there should have been in game clues like this though, a bit like how an escape room attendant is able to give clues when required. Next time I’ll keep this in mind.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: I like Pip’s adventures. This year I made it to get the old key, but it didn’t work on either of the two doors I couldn’t open. Well done apart from the sometimes too small hitboxes (especially for south exits).
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text: Thanks. Wow! I think you got further than most people. I think the old key is quite a bit towards the end. Yeah, the hitbox comment is the main improvement feedback I’ve had on the game. I’ll definitely improve that for Pip’s next adventure. I’m glad that you’re a fan of Pip!

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Nice point’n’click game. I went as far as picking up the umbrella and 9 coins, but couldn’t open door #9. (I even tried cheating by looking at the source code but couldn’t figure out what would open it). Game saves in localStorage would be nice so I could resume Pip’s adventure without starting from the beginning.

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text:
Hey, well done! I thought that one of the others had got the further-est so far, but actually I think you may have that distinction. Nine coins it getting towards the end, with 13 being the total. You’re the second person to get stuck at door 9 even after checking the code. It must be well hidden in the code then. This is another difficult one, maybe on par with the faint marks and filling the vase.

Time for a hint: Unlike the other doors, door 9 needs a door handle. It isn’t locked as such, but just needs the door handle attached. If you keep your eyes peeled while walking around the various rooms, you’ll notice that one of the plain, unnumbered, already open doors has a door handle. It can be removed and attached to door 9. - Evil, I know : )

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Excellent concept, fits the them perfectly! Impressive to get all those graphics in 13kb, but I was stuck at the second door. I’m not familiar with point and click. I think one improvement should be to keep the selected action active after using it. I want to show or push multiple items, not going back to move action any time. And maybe bigger hitbox to interact with objects, many times I used the wall

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. I agree with your suggestion regarding the bigger hitboxes. That is definitely something I want to keep in mind for next time, particularly with smaller objects. Its even more difficult on mobile to click some of the smaller items. I also agree about keeping the selected action. For some of my previously entries in past years, I did have it like that, but changed it after feedback. I think it depends on the game though. For this one, I think it would make sense to keep the selected action. Thanks again.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Clever use of Emojis, and the atmosphere is creepy (which is good.) The music sound familiar, I think I’ve heard it on other games this jam, or maybe past games in previous years. It fits nicely though.

The controls are a bit difficult to use. I was able to find the water pistol (eventually,) but it would be great to have obvious hints as to where things are, or how you can use them. I don’t play point and click games myself, but I have this feeling that the “push” action and “use” action could be the same, just to avoid having to use both actions on everything, which can be tedious (at least for me.)

Overall I think the game looks good and fits the theme.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: Nice graphics, cool theme/premise, and enjoyable concept. I couldn’t figure out how to put the coin into the coin slot, or even if that’s what I was supposed to do… Nice entry.

comments.author: EWCW
comments.text: It was so great that I fell in love with it!

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Good entry!

I got stuck pretty early on like others; couldn’t figure out anything with the card door.

I really like the look of the game; wish there were hints or something.

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text: I wish now that I hadn’t put such a difficult puzzle early on in the game. I thought I was being clever with how the keycard is obtained, but in hindsight it is one of the more difficult puzzles and should have been later on, maybe. I’ll keep the hint idea in mind for next time. Thanks

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Looked ok, but got stuck quickly with not being able to get the keycard. But this happens to me in Zelda continuously, so it must be me ;-)

Still if there are indeed 13 doors / rooms with items and combinations, that is a pretty good entry!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Yo, I freaking love Point & clicks!

I’m impressed, must not have been easy thinking about the plot of the game! I think you thought well of the decor and the plot, and the story as well.

It did feel a bit slow to play, probably the fact that you have to select the action before doing it slows down the whole game, instead of just having 1 click to do on the object you’re interacting with.

Overall, pretty impressed, well done!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I spent a solid hour trying to finish it. Only to go through the whole open map twice (I didn’t know how to unlock the door 9) and get stuck on the room of the door 13 – there is a bug where you use the vase twice and the games sends you character to the top left of the screen, making it impossible to move or get out of the room, game over :(

I had to check the source code to know what was the thing – and I completely don’t remember any bolts on the door.

This game is a solid work for 13kb, with a very smart use of emoji to shave a lot of kb from the assets :)

comments.author: Tom Copeland
comments.text:
Very interesting game! Loved the graphics, the use of emojis for graphics was very fun. I ended up spending a lot more time on The 13th Door than I expected!

I got past the bolted door, but gave up after that. I thought some of the puzzles felt pretty obscure, like the hidden sheet music and the door handle thing. I spend most of the time wandering between a bunch of basically identical rooms, which wasn’t the most gripping gameplay. I actually think this might be more fun with fewer levels.

The puzzles were clever. I liked the multi-step ones, like the the platform. Pip’s various exclamations were fun as well. The controls were super intuitive (though having to double-click all the time was kind of annoying). Nicely done!

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text:
Thank you for the kind feedback Tom. It sounds like you are one of only a handful that made it that far through the game. It is great to hear that people got so far and saw most of what I’d created. I don’t think anyone has finished it yet, which is certainly achievable… but I think there are even more obscure puzzles towards the end. One of them is tied into the theme though… I just wasn’t sure if it was an obvious tie in to the theme (the exposure therapy turns out to be letting each 13th ghost hit Pip, over and over until he’s cured… which allows entry through the 13th door).

I know what you mean about the number of rooms. That is something that I struggled with early on. I wasn’t sure whether to make it 5x5 (25 rooms), or 6x6 or 7x7. In hindsight, maybe I should have gone with 25 rooms rather than almost double that, and reduced the number of different coloured areas within the maze.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: solid point and click adventure. the amount of content and graphics and sounds is incredible!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Very innovative, and amazing to have a whole adventure to play through.

The graphics were really good, and the whole adventure game vibe brought me back to the LucasArts ones of days gone by.

Atmospheric background sounds and effects.

I collected lots of items, but no matter how I tried I was unable to get them to do anything much. Like what was the water pistol for?

The texture of the game and the CRT effect looked good, as did the whole dialog system.

I liked the interaction verb system, but perhaps a right click on things could make it select a relevant action automatically?

comments.author: Lance Ewing
comments.text:
Thank you for the kind feedback. Yeah, the puzzle difficulty is probably what let the game down in the end; that and the tiny hit boxes. I’m convinced now from the feedback I’ve received that I should have provided a “Hint” icon. That would have kept people playing for longer, perhaps even to the end.

Great idea about right clicking. That would have made it easier as well, e.g. right-clicking on the urns suggests push.

Regarding the water pistol, it is used in two places in the game to transfer water from one container/vessel to another. One of those places is physically not far from where the water pistol is found.

api.name: Unnamed
api.description:
I intended to write an AI-driven game until life priorities took over.

Therefore there is no gameplay, but a focus on research with this entry.

comments.author: Ronica Singh
comments.text: Tell me about it! Would like to eventually see what could have been as I’m learning more about AI-based games! Even a possible post-mortem on your GitHub. Because I’ll speak for myself and say, I’m still learning.

comments.author: Ryuno-Ki
comments.text:
Hey Ronica,

my Postmortem will show up in https://jaenis.ch/en/blog/2024/ (I’m currently writing it) and be PRed to https://github.com/js13kGames/resources#post-mortems-10

Given https://sfconservancy.org/GiveUpGitHub/ I want to avoid GitHub for the reasons stated there. That being said, it’s still a rule to this competition, so I wasn’t able to delete my profile. You can find more work on Codeberg.org or my own Forgejo instance at code.jaenis.ch.

We’re all learning all the time :)

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
Sorry other priorities bumped this down.

For what it’s worth, we are discussing the potential of a whole GenAI-focused category for the next edition of js13kGames and are very open to ideas and suggestions on how to make it both fun and educational.

Edit: Yep, my use of the term “GenAI” was an oversimplification. My hope is for a category which gives access to ML-based tools and APIs in general, so GenAI would be just a subset of that. That said, the focus would still be on the use of them (in games, obviously), not on building them from scratch.

comments.author: Ryuno-Ki
comments.text:
No worries.

I’m not a fan of GenAI. But that wasn’t the limit of the AI category. It reads:

The goal is to create a game that makes use of NPCs in a way that hasn’t been seen before in most games.

Non-Player Characters (NPCs) have mostly been used as side characters, companions, quest givers, or enemies in games. But do they have to be limited to these roles? Now that we have the affordances of new technologies such as generative AI and blockchain, we should start to re-imagine the role of NPCs in our games.

To be considered for this challenge, your game must simply make use of NPCs. To win the challenge, your game must make use of NPCs in a thoughtful and innovative way. Subvert the traditional roles of NPCs in games and show us something new!

So „such as” are examples, but not boundaries. Instead, I would see something less wasteful (in terms of energy and water) such as expert systems. Those have been known to be hard to scale. But js13kgames is already imposing a limit.

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: Interesting game! Not sure what is going on but I like where you are headed!

comments.author: Ryuno-Ki
comments.text: Thank you :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Come back and finish it next time around!

comments.author: Ryuno-Ki
comments.text: I’m definitively to take another shot on this. Improving from one submission to the next.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I hope the research set you up to where you can finish next time!

comments.author: Ryuno-Ki
comments.text: You bet :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The music was interesting and bouncy.

Not sure where the game was heading because there’s not much there. But the premise sounded fun.

Good luck if you continue the game or try again in another jam

api.name: THE THIRTEENTH OFFERING
api.description:
A survival game where you’re trying to keep alive while fighting a dark cult.

On every 13th kill, a demon will appear and the difficulty slightly increases. After killing the demon, a random item either to power up your sword or to heal yourself is spawned.

Control the player using arrow keys and the attack will follow the movement direction.

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: This is great! One thing I’d recommend is blackout sound effects after they play, so you don’t get two of the same sound effect within 50 ms or so. When you kill a bunch of goblins at once, there’s some unpleasant reinforcement.

comments.author: Dimitrij Agal
comments.text: Great feedback to be learned as a lesson for me. Thank you!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
Pretty neat. I didn’t like how you could only fire in the direction you were facing and there was no way to face diagonally and be stationary at the same time.

I liked how the big demon spawned on the 13th enemy death.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Love the gameboy-esque color pallet. I got so far into the game that my weapon turned into an automatic machine gun, and then turned into a static image of weapon projectiles :D.

I wish there was more variety to the enemies though as after a few levels it gets repetitive.

comments.author: Dimitrij Agal
comments.text: Oops my bad on not handling the weapon firing rate. Thank you for playing and for the feedbacks!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: This was great! My eyes are literally watering right now because I forgot to blink while playing. The game starts simple, but ramps perfectly. Never boring, great pacing. Excellent music and sound effects. Excellent graphics. Clever interpretation of the theme. My biggest request would be to show score and/or level on the game over screen, because I kept forgetting to look at those during the endgame. This was fun, great job!

comments.author: Dimitrij Agal
comments.text: Thank you for playing and for the feedback! Well noted and greatly appreciated

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Solid implementation, but could use more depth. Something like, choosing the upgrade when leveling up.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Supercool gameboy artstyle! Gameplay gets pretty hard after a while

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: The pixelart is cute. It’s an odd choice to attack only in the direction of the last move.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This was cool! I like the limited color palette. Gameplay is very smooth, and the difficulty scales up nicely. It’s very satisfying destroying multiple enemies at once as you progress later into the game.

There’s no ending, right? Just play until you die? In that case, I agree with another commenter that having a “high score” visible at the end would be nice

Overall, cool game! Really fun!

comments.author: Dimitrij Agal
comments.text:
It was originally meant to have an ending (the 13th demon being the big final boss) but due to size constraints combined with my lack of experience in making the game smaller it turns into infinite survival.

The “high score” is a good idea, should have added that. Thank you for playing and for your feedback!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: I spent like an eternity on level two and I really with there were some powerups - but the music, sprites and feel of the game are awesome. Very nice game :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The graphics were good as was the tune and sound effects.

Quite atmospheric, and dark.

Did SPACE make an attack happen? It felt like it, but wasn’t listed in the info. But moving did seem to trigger a echo in the direction of travel (as stated)

How could I use the sword? Or even better, heal myself? Was it a case of walking over stuff? Perhaps I need way more practice.

The demon was hard to kill.

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: Very fun, I’m quite a fan of this Archero/Survivor.io/VS style of game and it fits great with the art style. The sound effect choice was also a great fit for it too.

api.name: Fear Species 13-K
api.description:
For too long we’ve lived in powerless terror. Hidden inside our minds. Consumed in order to fortify the strength of our enemy.

Wake up and earn the trust of our captors. When the time is right we will strike a resounding blow… and make species 13-K begin to fear US.

Overview

Master maintenance tasks of increasing number and difficulty.

A rank of “B” or higher will unlock the next type of shift.

Earn a position in the the Command Ship’s Systems Core and you’ll be in position to “repair” the heart of the enemy forces into nanoparticles and end their reign of terror over the galaxy.

Controls

Control with a keyboard or via the on-screen touch keyboard.

  • Q, W, E, R, 1, 2, 3, 4
    • Select a maintenance shift
    • Select a part / action for current task
  • K / L - Hold to open one side of available parts / actions
  • ↑ / ↓ - Navigate maintenance tasks
  • X - Select a maintenance task
  • A - Submit work order / Close shift results

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I removed the head of species 13k! This was fun! I haven’t played a game of this style or genre before. The time pressure makes “easy” tasks quite a bit more difficult. Especially the escalating “Wires” tasks. At first I tried playing with both mouse and keyboard, but keyboard alone is much much faster. Nice variety of puzzles, catchy music. This was a clever game, well done!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Atmospheric music and some nice effects with the graphics!

It gets pretty stressful to accomplish the various tasks in time!

This might be random feedback, but I think having some more feedback after “accomplishing” a task would be good (some sound effect or visual cue). I kept forgetting to hit “A,” and maybe that would have helped me remember

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game is very confusing and starts really hard. I think you should ramp up the player with simpler tasks with a lot more time. The gameplay was confusing even after reading the description, but after a while i guess i got the hang of it. But it was very stressful to use all the different keys.

The tasks start sliding away so you can’t read their names any more. Would make more sense to represent that in another way (like a progress bar).

The music is a bit annoying after a while.

On the bright side, I can definitely appreciate the amount of thought and work put into this. There are several mini games and a compelling storyline. The game is pretty complex. But I think the UX needs some work.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Very interesting atmospheric game. I appreciated that the same task was always the same so I could use muscle memory for the solutions, and that the tasks slowly went away if I didn’t complete them quick enough.

The game was unclear at first what I needed to do. The controls helped to understand what was expected but it took me some time to figure out how everything worked. Especially the first task of Hard Reset I didn’t understand that I had to press 2 after opening the panel with K. After I got what I needed to do the rest of the game went smoothly.

There were a lot of times I didn’t have any tasks to work on because I had completed everything and would just have to wait around for new ones to show up. It would have been nice if the tasks were a constant stream even if I completed them faster than expected.

It also might have been interesting if during the later shifts that clicking on the wrong buttons during a task would result in failure. For example if during the Hard Rest task clicking 1 before 2 would fail it, or trying to screw in the Loose Panel before it was on. Without that I didn’t really have any need to worry about accuracy as I tried to complete tasks (other than the Wire task).

Lastly the music, although fitting of the game, got a bit annoying at the end as the repeat is so short. The CRT effect also was a bit too distracting. Making it a bit subtler would be nice.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I can see how this could be fun, the concept was good, and there seemed to be plenty of tasks.

I could only select one thing from the list, but having the pulsing items as prompts to know what to do was useful.

I kept getting stuck on pressing “K”, was I meant to hold “K” and press something else like the numbers?

Why were the left hand tasks scrolling off the screen, was there a time limit?

The graphics were a little flickery, was that intentional?

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: I’m sorry, I don’t get it. Task asked me to open (k) and press(k), Even when I use “K” button it opens the “K”, but I can’t press it again has it closes it

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: It took me time to understand how it works, but I think it’s part of the game:) An interesting thing to me is the stressing atmosphere due to the music and the limited time. I think the game is hard and I was not able to pass level 3 even after several attempts :( Good job!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Very atmospheric, great music! I actually like the fact that the game is very cryptic and doesn’t really tell you what you have to do so you have to figure it out on your own, but it does feel confusing and punishing in a way. The concept though is great!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • The tasks! They were repetitive enough to allow me to learn them.
    • Flow. The game ramps up slowly and at the later levels I could feel I mastered some of the tasks and I could balance between doing the hard ones and the easy ones.
  • I liked that you:
    • Created a background story for the game actions. It’s minor, but Advanced Systems Repairs is much more enticing than “press these buttons in this order under this time”.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Keys detection. Like in platformers, having a “coyote time” for allowing minor mistimed presses of button could improve the experience a lot. The tighter the keyboard presses will feel, the more joy the player will get. I can see tasks getting completed at a flash.
    • I really liked the keyboard usage of the game – you could build on it and make it a keyboard training game for VIM bindings for example.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Great concept, there are a couple of bugs if you play with the mouse, click one of the letters then move away keeping the button clicked.

At a first glance it may look a bit confusing, but after some trial and error it’s a fun game.

api.name: Bee / B / 13
api.description:
Introducing Bee, a minimalist game created for the js13k competition! The name “Bee” is inspired by the digits “13,” which resemble the letter “B,” and when spelled out, it perfectly captures the spirit of our winged hero.

In Bee, you guide a diligent bee back to its hive, navigating through a world filled with obstacles and tricky flowers. The gameplay takes inspiration from the classic “Flappy Bird” style—tap and hold anywhere above the bee to fly higher. But beware: gravity and the bee’s growing tiredness will constantly pull it back down.

As you journey, keep an eye out for flowers with nectar to keep your energy up. But not all flowers are safe! Avoid flowers with 13 petals—these are either empty or, worse, poisonous! Running out of nectar means no more flying upwards. However, if you find yourself grounded, don’t worry—you can recharge by resting on the ground. Just keep in mind that while resting restores your nectar, it also costs you precious time, limiting how far you can travel.

Will you guide your bee back to the hive safely? Navigate carefully, conserve your energy, and plan your route strategically in this buzzing adventure!

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Nice story building and narrative in your entry. Plus one for instructions on game launch.

Love the metrics at the top of the screen. At first I thought you needed to continually click the mouse button, but it was nice to only have to move the mouse. Probably a little too easy, make it harder with levels or obstacles, or make it more of a challenge with leader boards, or even enemies attacking you (like wasps or something).

Add some music and sound effects for a little more immersion. Nice work though. I love the bee graphics, super cute!

One of the links (View Bee) in your GitHub README is broken.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I was able to fly 230m which is good for a little bee :) I found the concept interesting and I had fun to play. I would recommend to add some variations (change speed, add other types of flowers, enemies, …) and some sound effects. Well done!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I’m not quite sure I understand the controls, as what you say in the description and my experience feel different. I never have to click the mouse a single time, just had to move the mouse up and down.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: All worked well, simple game, good base to add more detail and variation.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Adding the petal count as a number in each flower is a nice quality of life improvement.

It’s interesting how you inlined manifest.json, I didn’t know it was possible.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Cute Bee, the game would benefit from sound (Music) and some “eye-candy” in form of particle-effects when successfully extracting nectra from a flower, or getting ill from the poisionous 13 petaled evil flowers :-)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I like the concept. Funky graphics.

Fun playing the game zipping along.

Was I supposed to land on the non-13 petal flowers? It was tricky to count tree petals as you went along

The bee was buzzing along nicely, but didn’t exactly match your screenshot. Possibly looking a little glitchy as it animated?

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: its a nice game and I liked that you can keep going after falling on ground, all you have to do is avoid the flowers with 13 on them. but I would have prefered not have 2 flowers with 13 on them next to each other so nectar would last more. all in all is a good game well done

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Simple but fun and well executed. Some audio would be a nice touch next time! Very cute bee

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A cute little game about a bee looking for nectar. Could use some polish and work on the feel of the gameplay, but overall easy to play and runs great. Nice work!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: It’s a cute little game, thank you for submitting it. The link with the number 13 is really a stretch, though. I think a background music and a bit more challenges would have made the experience a lot better. Controls and graphics are fine. If you keep working on it, it could become quite good.

api.name: How it started
api.description: A game in which you have the opportunity to experience a little adventure, namely - to see how and where it all began, and also how it all ended or did not end?

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I defeated 13 (or did I join forces with 13?) I love the intro sequence and the flavor text, that was great! Excellent writing and storytelling. Fun little adventure game. Part of me wishes that the 13 AI would have been slightly more aggressive for more challenge, but I also understand that it’s impossible to find a difficulty level for everyone in a game jam like this. Overall, very creative, great job!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text: From the beginning, I won 13, but then I understood and accepted :). Thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text:
Woah, didn’t think that I would get so emotionally invested in a story about numbers :p

It’s really cool that you managed to make this with very simplistic graphics, the intro and cutscenes were smooth.

I think the difficulty curve was a bit on the linear side and maybe making 13 more aggressive in the later stages of the rescue levels might have made it better.

But very nice game regardless!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Oh, I was hoping the story would hook you ;).

In the last level (final battle) you can fight a little harder, but I agree that the balancing in the rescue level could be better. I also didn’t want to complicate this level to allow the player to read the notes, so I left it as is :).

Thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
The cutscene and story were awesome.

The boss fight with 13 really turned things up 🔥

Nice job

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text: Thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing the game to the end.

The initial levels were fairly easy, just avoiding 13 and pressing E when over a cage.

The animations at the start and end were great and really set the scene. Although I thought I was going to have to battle 14 when it appeared - haha

The boss battle with 13 was fun once I realised what LMB meant :) after playing the the keyboard the whole game I wasn’t ready to have to use the mouse.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Glad to hear you had fun!

The menu has a separate section with controls, where you can see which keys are available and their designation. However, I understand that it may not be completely obvious :).

As for 14, it’s like a good idea for the continuation of the game (part 2 - soon) something like that :).

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Really nice entry, sometimes I just a game too early and maybe I did early on with this game but I stuck with it and glad I did.

Enjoyed playing through and was determinded to beat the DVD menu style enemy (13), it never did hit the corner of the screen!

Was not expecting the boss at the end, was a nice twist / change of pace. Liked the story too.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
It’s nice that you stayed and tried my game :). DVD (13) randomly chooses its next route after completing the current one, and theoretically could get to the corner, it’s actually a really cool idea that could be tweaked somehow to make it even more fun! Nice to hear for a change of pace too :).

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: Great story and strong ties to the game jam theme! The game is clean with subtle effects and animations that make it fun to play the game to the end.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text: Thanks for playing, I hope it was interesting and fun!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
I really liked the story! It was like a numerical fairy tale. It’s one of the best interpretations of the theme I’ve seen, too. The twist at the end was also great :)

I enjoyed the last boss fight, but clicking the mouse on a track pad got a little tiring, haha. I wonder if for the sequel (perhaps titled “How It Ended”) there could be an auto-shoot function (like holding down the mouse button).

Overall a lot of fun!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
A fairy tale is not a fairy tale if you don’t believe in it :).

If you use a track pad instead of a mouse, it can really get a bit tiring :D, however you can warm yourself by converting kinetic energy into internal energy on those cool autumn evenings :).

Actually, you can come up with a lot of plot twists, since the ending is specifically left open, so… :).

I’m glad you had fun, thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Hu?? That final battle took me by surprise!

I like the story of the game and the plot-twist at the end and the one after the end too :-)

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’m glad you liked the plot twists, I hope they weren’t obvious and I hope you were pleasantly surprised by the final battle :).

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Interesting game, nice story about number and cliffhanger at the end! But as 13 moves in screen boundings, it was way too easy as I played on a wide screen. you should maybe reduce space and/or make 13 bigger depending of resolution

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
As for move 13, you are right about it being limited by the screen. I didn’t want to make the rescue level too intense to make it possible to read the notes, and as for the final battle, I think it would be appropriate there, thanks for the possible improvement!

Thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Incredible! This is the best game I’ve played so far. You’ve done an amazing job with the story, and it ties perfectly with the theme. The gameplay is really engaging, and the way the UI complements the storytelling is fantastic. I wasn’t expecting a game of this quality here!

Congrats on your hard work—truly impressive!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’ll be honest, from your feedback I couldn’t fall asleep for a long time, rejoicing like a child :). I am very glad that you enjoyed playing it and also to hear such a high rating of my game. And it is doubly pleasant to know that all this adventure was not in vain!

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Fábio Santos
comments.text:
I loved the story in this game! It made me laugh really hard at the end, and was very whimsical and fun the rest of the time.

I wonder if this story is still going on since the big bang, since there’s an infinite amount of numbers to go through!

There’s no audio so I don’t know how I would rate it!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Glad to hear you had fun, laughter is good :).

As for the never-ending story idea, it’s actually a good guess, maybe so, maybe not, either way, here’s how it started, and what happened next? This is another story ;).

Unfortunately, I didn’t have time to add music, but maybe it’s for the best, I think it would be difficult to choose such music that would deepen the immersion in the story, so grab what you have and don’t give it away ;).

As for evaluation, for this, criteria were introduced from different directions, one of which is music, so the evaluation depending on the criterion can vary greatly, I think this is the idea of ​​​​evaluation criteria, in order to be able to divide the evaluation by criteria, instead of the average for everything .

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Fun game, had me laugh a couple of times. Great texts and fun to play.

Very well done!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Making people laugh is one of the directions I wanted to achieve, it’s nice to hear that you laughed :).

I’m glad you had fun!

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Loved the story, you really put some effort into it.

A few feedbacks, Too bad you’re missing some audio, a nice little touch would have made the game very enjoyable to play. I feel the “Press space to continue” is a bit cumbersome when reading the story as it keeps popping up. Maybe always showing it with lower opacity, or in the corner of the screen would have made the intro more enjoyable.

Overall the game, is really well made, nice animations, nice story. Love it

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’m glad you liked the story :).

I agree that well-chosen music could increase the enjoyment of the game. Also good advice on making the hints less distracting. Thanks for the feedback!

Thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Funny idea, loved the cinematics you did just with characters, I really felt captured by your narrative! I Was expecting more gameplay, but at least the game felt completed.

Cheers!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’m glad to hear that you were fascinated by my story! I’m also proud of my movie effect, thanks :).

Hope you got some gameplay in the final battle :).

Thanks for playing my game!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game in 13kb :)
    • Kept your graphics simple, but consistent.
  • I liked that you:
    • Kept it short and easy. You also let it clear of when the game will end. This let you get away with a very simple mechanic, but with enough going on to keep me wondering – what’s next? I don’t think I’d stick for so long with a game that’d be counting how many numbers I’d unblock. Here, I did almost having fun, and the payoff of going through it was well worth it.
    • Had a good story and humor at the end of it, 14 will come for all of us!
    • Also, let’s talk about that advertisement placement? ;P

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’m glad you noticed the consistent of events, I tried to achieve this! As for the graphics, really simple graphics were used here, moreover, no media resource was used, only canvas, only hard core :D.

This time I wanted to make a game with some context so that the player knows exactly what they are playing, not just how to play it, so I came up with and added a story to the gameplay. It was also necessary to somehow show the player when the story is going on and when the game is going on, so the cinematic effect helped a lot here :).

I’m glad you were able to play the entire rescue level! I knew that just rescue could be boring, so I tried to add some notes to keep it interesting :). It’s a pleasure to know that your idea worked!

Humor is an integral part of this game, so I’m glad to hear that people were able to laugh a little :).

Regarding advertisement placement - of course, any suggestions, I am open to communication ;).

Thanks for playing and feedback, it means a lot! I hope it was fun!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A cute text-based game about rescuing numbers. Nice job!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Oh, I hope you played not only the level with rescuing, because it’s not the end, there’s still a final battle ;).

The game also has autosave progress, so you won’t have to start from the beginning, but from the place where you finished the game. Therefore, suddenly, if you did not finish the game - welcome :).

Anyway, thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Congrats on sending your game! I loved the words of encouragement, so I had to keep playing until the end!!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’m glad to hear that the words of encouragement encouraged you not to give up and to see the end ;).

I hope you also had the opportunity to take part in the final battle :).

Thanks for playing, I hope it was fun!

api.name: Liminal Gap Runner
api.description:
Welcome to the World of Liminal Gap.

Evil corporations have taken control of our data and placed it all in the cloud. Personal data possession is illegal now.

Only you, the Liminal Gap Runner, can save the day by traversing abandoned spaces looking for data, and uploading it to private FTP servers. Lucky for us, the Liminal Gap Plumber Union is not part of the Cartel. They control the Liminal Gap jump pads and allow data couriers like yourself to use them in exchange for data uploads.

So keep enough data to use the jump pads. But be careful! Do not exceed your capacity! Maybe famous data couriers like Johnny could carry 80 or even 160 GB. But you should never exceed your 13MB limit, or you’ll have a very bad day. Always upload excess data.

Controls

WASD - Movement Mouse or Cursor Keys - Look around

Jump Pads

Active jump pads are orange and can be used to upload data along your jump across the liminal gap. You need some data to be collected to unlock them. And they’re single use only - after the upload jump they will not be active again. Consider that while planning your route across the Liminal Gap!

comments.author: Sonny Qin
comments.text: Nice graphic and scene. A bit too hard to control, seems like sliding on ice.

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text: Thanks. Yes, I should’ve spent more time adjusting forces and friction to make it more solid.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice job. Lovely 3D graphics. The controls are a little sensitive, but manageable after a little practice. The sound effects were great too. I uploaded 22 GB of data. Well done!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Hi. Uploaded 27.4MB. I could put a lot of entries in 27.4 =)

I don’t see any problems with control and movement, I think it’s predictable enough to get used to it in 1min.

I like the background story, I wish to have a bit more in the gameplay itself. But I know that it’s impossible to put 3d and gameplay in 13k.

Nice entry!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Very cool game and an interesting concept. it would be cool when entering the game to lock the pointer from the start.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Nice 3D graphics, nice concept, the controls were a little tough. Managed to upload quite a few MB of data, is the game never ending?

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
Thanks! Yes, the game is like endless runner.

I wanted to increase difficulty along the run (that was the initial plan), but never managed to do it :)

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
OMG it’s floppy disk! Really fun concept. The graphics platform load animation is super cool. Love the random load for all the platforms and floppy disks. Would be cool to have levels too, maybe start a little easier for the first bit to get familiar with the in-game dynamics and keys, then make it harder and harder; also I didn’t know if the game had an “end goal” for if it was never-ending. Great job though, had some fun playing this one.

Great sound effects. One or two might have been out of synch, but great work. Add some music for a little extra touch, but I know you’re at the 13kB limit.

Nice GitHub repo and README, could probably put some of the files in a ‘src’ folder if you wanted to make it a little tidier.

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
Thanks Michelle!

Yes, sound effects add up when you upload a lot of data, I should’ve done something to limit it.

And music is something I really wanted to include but I totally missed my space budget and had to cut a lot of content to squeeze it back into 13k :)

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to move the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Interesting concept, nice and clean first person graphics. And i never thought, that anyone in the world would know Johnny Mnemonic! :-D

A little extra music would add much to the atmospere of the game. And maybe some cyberpunk enemies? Like ICE… (Intrusion Countermeasures Electronics)

However: smooth controls and overall good game-feeling. Solid entry!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Cool atmosphere platformer. Controls were a bit tricky, especially with how slow the look sensitivity was, but not terrible to get use to.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Cool concept, nice platform game with graphics and light. Is it generated procedurally? I don’t know if the games has an ending

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: Nice 3D game! I didn’t have problems with the controls.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The game looks great, i think you nailed the liminal space vibe, but it’s really hard to control. The acceleration is too low, and the floor feels very slippery.

Making a 3D engine from scratch is very impressive on its own.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: so fun! I really enjoyed it, and didn’t have any trouble with the awesome physics (though of course I wish I could jump a bit higher and stop a bit faster! =) but that would make it less challenging). a very well done js13k entry with a cool look and feel. good work.

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
Thanks, Christer!

Yes, physics and movement dynamics are something I wish I invested a little more into. After all - it is the core of any endless runner game. Initially, I was looking at the bunny-hopping mechanics of Quake, but I never managed to get it quite right.

P.S. About the jump height - the game has a bug/feature when you land on a jump pad and press Space at the same time, the pad launches you anomaly high, almost in the stratosphere. I decided to leave it as is since there is some enchantment in these powerful jumps.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game looks great, the 3D works really well to create a world to navigate.

The floppy disks are quite retro, but look great.

The jumps are quite generous, so getting across gaps is fairly easy, but with the power of orange jumps your range is so much further - which is neat.

Because you can’t see yourself (i.e. 1st person view) it can be tricky to know just how close you are to the edge, and falling off the edge is a bit perilous.

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
I instinctively attempted to crouch-jump several times to speed up, as if I was parkouring over Satisfactory foundations~

So no, I didn’t have any issues with the controls nor pace. In fact, I’d have loved it a bit trickier and more dynamic. Possibly with the occasional platforms moving, rotating, disappearing?

Shoutout to the - if I understand it right - cross-generational family effort :) It’s great to see nearly the entire age range in the competition.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
A very strong entry! I had a lot of fun finding floppy disks and uploading them. Brought me old memories when I was zipping and cutting things in pieces because all I had were floppy disks to store stuff. For real :D

I wish the “punishment” for going over the limit was a bit stronger - or as soon as you upload too much data, bots (drones? security counter-hackers?) would chase you down or something. But in general I have to say this is the game that better fits the theme: I truly felt bits of triskaidekaphobia while jumping around, grabbing data, checking the 13MB limit… in a way it felt a bit like working on a js13k entry where you are constantly trying to push your limits without going over the size and time constraint.

Very cool game overall :)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Wow, such amazing smooth 3D graphics! J13K at its finest; matching a theme and tying it into an infinitely expanding world. Simple and satisfying gameplay.

I felt like I was a “digital” dog because of the sound effects.

Invert mouse Y would be appreciated as an option, but I know it will only add to your limited code available.

Overall, great game!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text:
Nice looking game. Good controls. Pretty fun but sometimes quite challenging. I would have liked to have a higher movement speed in the air when launching from a jump pad.

Bonus points for the nice reference to Gibson in the instructions!

api.name: Terra Mobilis
api.description:
NOTE: There are cheats left in game. Don’t use them if you want “authenthicate” experience. Cheats are: click item in store to get 100 of it. And move or zoome camera with mouse, excapt for the first week.

Terra Mobilis is an experimental game. It is alike to Civilization, but is set on the planet with ever-changing geography. Local nature (including people) has had to adapt, living constantly on the move. People have learned to get by with minimal amount of things with flexible usage. So, a wagon can be used as a ship (with a help of some fabric as the sail), engine can be taken from the car and used in the factory, etc.

You can do one of three things here. Move from the location to location using map. Use one of the recipes on the right to craft things, collect things from location, or choose the travel method. The last one is picking the research focus ⚗️ once you have the respective tech - it accelerates the book effect on the research of the respective tech. Each action takes time, even focus switching.

The more stuff you have in store, the harder it will be to move around, and some percentage will be lost each week. On the other hand, just by having thing (or being on location with them) you make people happier (attracting more of them) and advance the research on relevant topic (check top right of the recipe buttons for what’s “relative”, and books📙 accelerate all research)

Year on the planet has 13 months. On the last month of year, and also throughout each 13th years, various “calamities” 👹☣️🌋 appear. Remove them to reclaim space for the more useful location (as the total number of spots is limited).

“Tool” type resources (🛠️ tools,⛺ housing,🛷 wagons,🐴 horses,⚙️ engines and 🏹 weapons) are expended only at 10% of the listed at recipe rate, but travel requires full horse etc. per each unit of weight. Each pop👨‍👩‍👦‍👦 weight 0.5🏋, and each resource - 0.05🏋

Recipes with 🐾 and 🍃can work differently with different resources.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
Interesting use of emojis for this kind of game, must remember that.

I kept getting a lot of lag whilst playing, maybe all of that console logging doesn’t need to be there.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Lag is from the terrain updating. Words about the “ever-changing geography” was not the flavor text:)

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Awesome. The initial experience is a little overwhelming, but I suppose that’s true of all Civ-like games. I’m pausing with 1586 population, 11705 happiness, on the 641st week. I unlocked all jobs and bonuses. My main strategy was to focus on Grasslands and Forests for food and sticks, then opportunistically grabbing other nearby resources once food was stable. This is a very impressive amount of content for a 13kb game! Great job!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Thanks for plaiyng! I was late with the features, so I had only about a day and a half for balancing. Was not sure it will not all fall apart about first hundred weeks or so. Good to know it’s playable after that :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I feel like I need a tutorial to fully understand the game, the map looks great and nice use of emojis!

Can see a lot of effort went into so many items!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Thanks! Tutorial sadly was out of scope because I was running out of time and space. Though now I am thinking that I could fit some visual mini-guide into the game screenshot…

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I’m not able to play because i’m unfortunately missing some emoji

edit: i didn’t vote for this game because i didn’t think it was fair, but FYI it’s within the rules to allow external emoji fonts.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text:
That’s unfortunate. Could not include custom art because of the space limitations. Maybe only some very primitive ones… It should work on chrome and chrome-based browser, looks like you had something else.

I’ll try to not rely on emojis next years.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
“Terra Mobilis is an experimental game” seems legit. I’m totaly overwhelmed by the sheer amount of buttons and things and stuff :-D

Some kind of introduction or tutorial and soundeffects/music would have been nice (probably didn’t fit into the zip)

The changing landscape and the rendereing of it is very cool.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: The terrain looks awesome! I couldn’t understand the ui fully. I’m not sure what your initial locaton is and how you move around.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Great game, coding a quite complex resource management packed in 13Kb is outstanding, and the final result is fun too!

The UI should be improved a bit, by the way.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Visually very impressive, the “world” you’ve created for this is quite large and detailed.

I’m afraid I didn’t get what needed to be done or how to play. There was no tutorial or hint at what to do. Was it a sim that I just get to watch unfold?

I clicked on loads of things which just seemed to zoom in to that thing and then give stats and not actions. So it appeared that I had no control over anything on the map.

Like some other people testing the emoji didn’t all work for me, some places have unicode value, others had “UNKNOWN”

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Impressive to have a small 4X game in 13kb! Nice art and a lot of things to manage! It’s a hard part to develop, but a short tutorial would have been nice, it tooks me time to understand the mechanics, but it’s a cool game!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
