comments.text: this world map is absolutely fascinating! I love all the little details, like being able to zoom in and inspect the various denizens of a vast landscape. It was truly overwhelming. I did not end up figuring out what to do, but I could sense a delicate balance between dozens of interesting systems that, given time, I could perhaps peel back the layers to and eventually find the game hiding in this vast (cool looking!) mountain of information. I feel like I missed out in something really cool that I would enjoy if it was presented one piece at a time rather than all at once, which scared me away. I WANT LESS! =) but absolutely amazing work here, the potential is very exciting I can tell where you’re headed with this incredibleness!!!!!!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This is a phenomenal effort, unfortunately it’s quite hard to get the hang of, but it’s still an incredibly impressive feat, especially the quality of the map and (simply) how many interactions and elements you managed to squeeze into that UI with the limitations.

api.name: Bitty Jump
api.description: Jump from wall to wall and comple all 3 hard levels. Collect all 31 conins and avoid 13 monsers and traps to complete level.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I liked the simple controls which make easy to play in this 3D world. The 3D is very well done and the game is fun to play. I would have appreciated to have some checkpoints in order not to have to restart the level from scratch when you loose. Very good entry!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The graphics are really cute, I like how wobbly the yellow one is.

I feel like I would much prefer to control this game with the keyboard or gamepad than using the little 3D arrows.

I wish there was some music and sound!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
My favorite in the WebXR category this year!

I typically hate the WebXR scenes that only use primitives, but you nailed it! Great color pallet, robots and player character look cute, the satisfying squish animation when moving, solid level design, great overall.

Add sound an 20 more levels and you’ve got a classic, fit for the Meta browser main page!

One minor gripe, the hit boxes for the cones were way to big, which killed me several times, hitting the wrong hitbox while the ray cleary hit the right cone.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Very well polished 3D puzzle game. Weird that only the left hand controller worked on my Meta Quest 2.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I could not move; using Quest 2.

The graphics look really good and its looks fun, just could not figure out how to interact.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: nice game concept and fun gameplay, 3d games always are fun

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A really polished looking game. Plays fine on other mediums but just webxr.

The graphics were good, the puzzles were also really well thought out.

Great job

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I wish i had a VR headset. But it plays well on Desktop too!

Good job, nice game

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
Thank you for making it playable beyond WebXR devices! I feel like adding basic keyboard controls on top would go a long way for very little relative effort (and you were well within the size limit, too).

The gameplay is straightforward while making good use of 3D space without overcomplicating things. I dig the mesh transformations (wobbly wobbly~) and feel like you could capitalize on that flavour by making the environment and monsters interact in a similar way.

Good job and thanks for joining in this year!

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game effectively captures the main theme, featuring 13 thorns (both moving and static) that players must navigate.

Gameplay: The gameplay is engaging and requires logical thinking, making for an overall enjoyable experience.

Audio: There is no audio. It would be great to have some sounds, apparently.

Innovation: This is a clever logic game that effectively incorporates physics and geometry concepts. There’s potential for further expansion, which could elevate it to a truly standout game.

Graphics: The graphics are well-suited to the game, featuring a clean design without unnecessary elements.

Controls: The controls are excellent, and I have no additional comments.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: Avoiding the 13 traps is definitely in sync with the theme.

Gameplay: I love platformers and puzzle games, and this is like a nice hybrid of those.

Audio: Not Available.

Innovation: I do not think I ever played a game with the same effective yet simple mechanics in VR.

Graphics: Amazing color choices go a long way in 13k.

Controls: Well done on a really intuitive and enjoyable game.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Innovation: “3D Puzzle Platformer with Monument Valley vibes” this is a unique take on a multidimensional platformer with simplified controls. Despite the limited character movement options, the 4-sided levels surrounding the player provide rich puzzle + platformer challenges. Adding a few more platform movement features, elevators or other dynamic.environment elements like Monument Valley could result in a very unique XR game worthy of a full release.

Gameplay: Once I got started the core gameplay was excellent and absorbing. I really liked being able to “look around the puzzle” like one looks around the room – the level platform blocks are optimally placed around the default user view. The 3rd dimension is a very unique experience to this style of game that I’ve only seen in 2d, and the depth of multiple levels gave a satisfying playtime for only 13kb!!

Graphics: While not fancy, the visuals are nicely designed, highly polished and worthy of a simple but legit mobile game. Characters, enemies and spikes are precisely placed and represent hit boxes well.

Controls: I had issues with the control scheme – 2 of the 3 times I tried launching I could not control the character. I didn’t see instructions on how to handle this case so I kept pressing buttons and restarting until it worked. I also would prefer using joystick to control character directly for rapid movement a la super meatboy (speed run!)

Audio: No audio that I could detect, would add a lot to have environmental ambient and basic responsive audio to character.

Theme: The game didn’t seem to strongly tie into a specific 13 theme but I liked it anyway.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: I love the level design, aesthetics, and the enemies. For some reason, the hover of the rays didn’t work on my Quest, so I had to try it on the computer (bonus points for supporting both). Overall, great work! I think it has the potential to become a complete game with just a bit of extra work, like adding more sound effects, background music, and perhaps allowing the use of the joysticks to move the character in the direction of the arrows instead of having to click on them.

comments.author: SolidCapo
comments.text: Pretty original for me, loved it, and would like to play it with some sfx and music…and maybe a timer to try to solve it faster.

api.name: Triskaideka-Cat's Elevator Caper
api.description:
Triskaideka-Cat is the star of this purr-fectly cuddly elevator caper. The story begins when our weary traveling hero seeks shelter for the evening at a Totally Normal Hotel:tm:. It turns out this hotel is anything BUT normal, and our hero finds themselves trapped by the nefarious treiskaídeka-ites.

Now, you must challenge the denizens of each floor and find a way to escape before your nine lives are up. Jump, collect, flap, and fight the rascally rats that fill the hotel floors–throwing balls of yarn, bopping, and even gambling your way out of treachery. Reach the 13th floor and a fat cat boss is waiting to annoy you.

Use W, A, S, D or ARROW keys to jump and move, and CLICK or use SPACEBAR to shoot yarn at enemies

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: “fin” Nice job! It took me a few tries before I realized that the fish grant double jumping. The surprise on level 12 was truly unexpected. I only had one life left for all of level 13, which made it quite tense. This was a great obstacle course game. Congrats!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Could use A LOT of polish. Even 1984 Alley Cat game (was it the inspiration?) had better graphics and more variable gameplay. In spite of that, was fun, with the exception of the 12th level. Was I supposed to do something there, or it’s pure random?

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Fun game, a little glitchy at times. Took me a while to work out the fish game double jump!

Fun and funny, little more polish, maybe more forgiving hitboxes too.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the look and concept of the game.

What is the yarn for if you can just fall on the rats to dispatch them? Perhaps using yarn to span gaps or climb would also be cool?

It took me a while before I realised I had to click the number button to get that level to load.

Some of the levels were tricky to get back up onto the platforms once I’d gone below them.

I enjoyed playing it, well done

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Heh, I’m not sure how I got past level 12 gambling, but I passed the game it seems with 1-2 lives left in the balance :).

It was a lot of fun to play. The gameplay was intuitive, so I understood what and how without a description of the game almost immediately. I almost didn’t shoot balls of threads, I just went through the heads ;).

I liked the music! But unfortunately it starts from the beginning every time with a new level, I would like it to play all the time regardless of the beginning of the levels. Therefore, I stayed at the level for a certain time to finish the music, it turned out that it really has an end, it would be good to loop it, when it ends - start again. It would also be nice to be able to turn the music on/off.

Repeating half the levels only with more intensity is a pretty cool trick!

It was fun to play, congratulations!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Nice cat design! Congrats on the submission!

I had to read comments to understand I could double jump though :D

Not all the emojis worked worked on my chrome browser on Linux, though they worked on Firefox.

Also, pressing space bar didn’t do anything, while clicking did. In general, I would consider doing some work on the keyword. For instance: if I die, I would continue or not by pressing Space/Esc.

comments.author: Fábio Santos
comments.text:
I was really afraid at level 13, though I had to stop since my finger pain started acting up on me.

I thought this game had interesting levels. I liked how they came back with slight variations. I liked level 12, because it made me go into another level to beat it. I chose level 1 for some easy yarn.

I think the movement could have used some more work. 2D platformers are all about the feel. Some inertia would have helped a lot, I think. Shooting yarn was a random but welcome option, and I was surprised to see that it bounced off walls.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Nice variation on the platformer levels! I would have liked a bit more cohesive art style. A pixel art style for all of the game would have been better than the current mix.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
What is it with spooky hotels in this years games?! But here we have another one. This time, you’re a cat who must escape. Use the controls to platform your way around each level of the hotel. You can collect yarn, which you can shoot at the rats, or you can just jump on their heads to kill them (which is often easier). There are also fish floating around. Collect these and you gain a double-jump. Collect lots of fish for lots of extra jumps!

Graphically it’s a bit patchy, but the cat and rats are cute enough. And bonus points for using the Zzxm music from the Cuddly Demos, which was a nice nostalgia trip for me.

api.name: National Accident Day
api.description:
13th September 2024. Helsinki, Finland.

You were supposed to give a speech at the National Accident Day, but you are very very late. You need to make it there on time, but the traffic is horrible.

Navigate through the traffic and avoid accidents to make it to the venue on time.

Good luck!

Controls:

  • On desktop, use the [Left/Right Arrow] keys move and [Up/Down Arrow] to accelerate or decelerate.
  • On mobile or desktop, use [DPad ← / → ] to move and [DPad Up / Down ] to accelerate or decelerate.
  • Press F11 to toggle fullscreen.

Credits

  • Developers: Sayem Shafayet
  • Music: Mysha Azfar

Acknowledgements

  • Sprites by ipixl (https://ipixl.itch.io/city-cars)
  • TypeScript js13k starter kit (https://github.com/mtmckenna/js13k-webpack-typescript-starter-party)

License

GNU General Public License v3.0

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
Kind of interesting, very touchy controls.

I found that if you ride the lines you are never hit by any obstacle.

Also my car is on fire but the smoke is moving up the screen, faster than my car. It would look nicer going the other way :)
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice job! My high score was 24300. Pretty fun endless runner. I need lane assist, ha. I liked the smoke and fire particle effects. Congrats!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Intresting feedback damage, after lvl 6 is becoming very hard:)) !

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Fun little game. A classic genre.

I’m a bit confused, am I driving in the opposite direction of the traffic? Why do cars have fire coming out of their head lights? Also how is this “bad traffic” if the streets were mostly empty? I think you need to rethink the story :P But good job overall

The sound effects seem to have some delay when I crashed against other cars. The car images look kinda blurry, you might want to disable imageSmoothingEnabled in your canvas.

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Damn, I guess traffic was so bad you had to drive on the opposite side of the road!

I really liked the pixel graphics and fire effect.

I do wish changing lanes was done with a tap of a key and not veer across the screen or end up on a traffic line. I didn’t know about accelerating or breaking so maybe some visual indication of your speed, accelerating, and breaking could be displayed in your UI.

Perhaps you wanted to do your own sound effects, but you could use ZzFX for a quick and good solution.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Simple game, all works well.. my advice, go check out an arcade version of this genre. Look at what makes it fun.

I would have had the car drift into the centre of the lanes automatically, have cars beep when you are close, add in some power ups, oil on the road etc.

Anyway just some ideas for you.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text:
Well done! That’s a nice take on the theme!

I accidentally managed to position the car right between two lanes, centered on one of the dashed lines, and none of the other cars could hit me anymore :D Sweet spot!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Simple game which become really hard after some time (cars are going too fast :P) but if you stay between 2 lines, you never get hurt nice particle effects, lines are accelerating with time too. It would be great to have a “trail” after cars to add the speed sensation

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
It was fun :).

The idea itself is cool, I liked it :).

Nice acceleration and burning graphics! And also fun sounds :).

You mentioned the full-screen mode in the description, but when using it, I expected more visibility, instead it scaled and the car models became more compressed visually :).

It would be nice to have information about the controls in the game, because people don’t always read the description before the game, as well as the ability to mute/unmute. I would also like to have control of WASD.

Congratulations!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Nice sprites and particle effects. The difficulty is a bit steep, from easy on level 1 to almost impossible on level 5 (even with the brakes to the floor, you need lightning reflexes). Incoming cars should appear on the top of the screen, not 1/4 down.

And the audio is a bit annoying, I was expecting the staccato of your own engine and the wooshes (and honks) from the car in the opposing directions. And if you are in a hurry, why drive on the wrong lanes to start with ?

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text:
Well. Huge violation of traffic law as you are driving against the direction ;-).

Other than that, the game is pretty nice tho I prefer the driver to switch lanes when pressed Left and right. One press of left arrow makes the car go all the way to the left.

The fonts are too small for me since I am playing with desktop and the screen is vertical.

Keep on improving the visuals and it would be a pretty good game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I had fun to play, my best score is 16300. I found the difficulty increasing very fast, I would have appreciated a more gradual increase. The particles effects and the car graphics are nice. I suggest you to remove the fps in the top right of the game (seems like a debug mode)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Graphics are good, both in terms of sprites and game play area layout.

Sound effects were nice, but didn’t sound much like a crash.

One of the times I played through the game I got really far just by staying hard over to the right side.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: It’s a classic lane racer. Avoid the oncoming cars, or they’ll cause damage. Too much damage, and it’s game over. There’s a quite nice little particle effect as you accrue damage and you do have some semblance of control over the speed of the oncoming traffic, although it feels a bit arbitrary. I managed to make it to Level 3 by staying in one single lane and not moving :) By Level 5 the speed was so insane I stood no chance, and the other cars were spawning a 3rd into the screen! Well done on entering. The concept feels almost on-theme, just needs some more gameplay polish.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
I loved the visual effect after the car has been hit one time.

The music doesn’t seem to have worked well, it is kind of choppy.

I see a lot of potential on this game. Congratulations!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice endless runner… eh… racer.

Cool particle-effects, it only looks like the smoke is going the wrong direction, when the car is moving forwards :)

One thing: on displays with higher framerate (144Hz / FPS) on my gaming laptop, the other cars and the controls are incredible fast. You dont calculate the delta-time for each animationframe in your tick() method, whichresults in more than twice the speed copared to yout probably targeted 60 fps.

I like, that you added gamepad-support into the game!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: The instructions mention that I have to reach a location on time, but there is no visible timer. I think it would be nice to have some visual cue on my own car that it is decelerating or accelerating. Moving the car down towards the bottom of the screen when decelerating and moving it slight upwards when accelerating. I also noticed that sometimes the other cars seems to be spawning on screen (at the top) rather than outside of the visible area.

api.name: HAUN13D HOTEL
api.description:
Can you escape this Haunted Hotel?

Welcome to Haun13d Hotel, where a ghost hunting expedition to the infamous 13th floor has gone horribly wrong. You awaken disoriented, surrounded by eerie silence and the lingering presence of the supernatural. Your mission is clear: eliminate the remaining 13 ghosts with your trusty photon gun and find the elevator to escape this haunted nightmare.

Navigate through the haunted corridors using the right thumbstick on your VR controller. Be prepared for surprises and spectral encounters as you attempt to cleanse the 13th floor and secure your escape route.

Find the elevator to escape.

Explore the haunted hotel and eliminate the last 13 ghosts.

Are you ready to face your fears and conquer the Haun13d Hotel?

comments.author: Chui Hin Wah
comments.text:
that jump scare successfully scared me 7 times out of roughly 7 playthroughs.

the dark hotel has really good mood, but having a gun against ghosts seem too strong. However, since most of the time i don’t know the layout and each corridor aren’t truly cleared, it kept me looking left and right all the time.

i am not used to using the right stick to move. i usually use the left stick. also, my play area is quite restricted, so i would appreciate being able to rotate my camera 45° in place by tilting the “right” stick sideways.

i dunno if it is a bug or not, but i get game over even just after i check my back. could the ghosts touch me thru the wall?

also, fantastic graphic. the torch’s limited range really added to the atmosphere.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Looks good, flashlight works great, insane jump scare (enhanced by the loud sound).

I never did find the elevator with a couple of playthroughs because of the sudden death head.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: The jump scare is really effective in this dark horror game. The thumb-stick motion caused me instant nausea (because the view changes rapidly which contradicts my body feeling no motion).

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Works really well, graphics are slick! All looks great but I cannot cope with jump scares :D

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Scary atmosphere. I’d like to be able to turn. Good graphics considering the file size limit. Nice job.

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game effectively captures the main theme, where players must eliminate 13 ghosts.

Gameplay: The gameplay is enjoyable, engaging players with more real-life movement rather than relying solely on controllers. I also appreciated the complementary use of the second controller as a flashlight.

Audio: The audio is nice, with no additional comments needed.

Graphics: The graphics are nice, with no further comments needed.

Innovation: The use of the second controller as a flashlight is a clever innovation that could be extended to other ghost-themed games.

Controls: There are no issues with the controls; they function smoothly.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text: I don’t have VR so the game wouldn’t let me play in desktop mode, which is a shame as this looks like a really cool and atmospheric game.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Theme: Excellent thematic coherence. The thumbnail, title, and gameplay all strongly reinforce the concept, creating a unified experience that aligns perfectly with the intended theme.

Gameplay: The gameplay evokes a nostalgic feeling like a classic FPS like Doom or Wolfenstein, and I love the addition and the feeling of having a flashlight in the dark, although the light didn’t appear to be a critical part of the gameplay.

Audio: The minimalistic audio design is effective. It complements the gameplay without overwhelming the experience, though there might be room for more auditory depth.

Innovation: The flashlight implementation stands out as a surprisingly fun mechanic in a spooky hotel, but it didn’t seem critical to gameplay success.

Graphics: Surprisingly high visual quality and fidelity that directly enhances the gameplay experience. The graphics not only look good but also serve a functional purpose, contributing to the overall immersion and spookiness of the 13th floor.

Controls: The controls are generally well-implemented and usable. The use of a joystick for movement works for this case but not all users may enjoy joystick locomotion

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: So much attention to make it all go through the theme: this is perfect.

Gameplay: This is too scary for me but the gameplay is really good and feels intuitive.

Audio: Helps a lot with overall immersion.

Innovation: The flashlight is an amazingly nice twist.

Graphics: The use of lights and details creates a quite immersive environment for only 13k. This is impressive

Controls: Intuitive and smooth.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: The atmosphere is magnificent, the addition of the flashlight gives it a very original touch, and the gun dynamics with the sound are also very well done. I also really like the design of the gun itself. I’m not sure if it’s possible to turn using the controller, since I was only moving with the joystick, but I was missing a way to rotate without having to physically move myself. Great job overall

api.name: TetromiGo
api.description:
A battle between humans and demons begins on a remote island. You will become a captain and expand your territory with the formations selected by your king.

Rules

  • 12 rounds match.
  • Take turns expanding own country’s territory.
  • Advance 4 troops at once.
  • The formation is decided by the king.
  • No enclaves allowed.
  • Avoid having exactly 13 territories..!
  • spin = [Shift], ok = [Enter], skip = [ESC]

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Fun! My best score was 146-58. The “avoid 13 territories” rule was tricky, because I didn’t understand the capture/fill rules. I’d love to see a version with more advanced AI. The current AI was great for getting started, and learning the game, but I imagine it could be much harder. Nice job!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is a cool idea! Once I caught on to the game mechanic, it was fun to expand my territory. I could crush my demon opponent!

I think it might be cool if there were more scenarios where 💀 tiles were created, because having 13 territories possibly only happens once per game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I like the idea that reminds me the GO game and I think you could develop this concept in a post version of the compo. I was stuck several times because I had 9 territories, and, as you have to add 4 territories each time, I always reached the 13 territories :( Except that, the game was fun to play.

comments.author: Logan Tempez
comments.text:
Nice game! Good work for releasing it.

I’ve a little problem with the UI, when I click on the down arrow it make my page scroll too, a little annoying during the game. Some ideas of improvement :

  • Adding a score counter during the game could be a good thing too.
  • Some difficulties level could be a good way to keep the player hook after some game.

Nice idea and working well!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
My score is 262-75!

It wasn’t obvious for me initially, that you need to capture empty tiles to extend the territory. Without that it is not possible to avoid 13 rule and usually you start losing tiles on turn 4 and after. Might be nice to make a hint for that. When I figured the idea, the game became easier :)

Nice idea and congratulations on a submission!

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Fun game idea with lots of potential!

I had to read the comments though to understand how to avoid the 13 territories.

To really make it fit with the theme, there could have been additional rules, e.g. avoid rows+columns=13, or avoid the territory size to be 26/39/52/…

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Interesting concept, although at the beginning I believed this was a game of conquest, with the goal being to steal opponent’s land.

I got flustered at the inability to position my troops on enemy land .. or on my own to thwart the 13-rule (since 1 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 13). Having the score displayed at all time would help make strategic decisions. For an extra bonus, have it fit to screen, independently of the resolution : it seems optimized for 1024px high screens, but the host bar at the top eat some real estate, causing the game screen to scroll.

Good game overall, a bit more polish would help it shine.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Interesting concept!

It does feel like it’s missing a bit of polishing, especially:

  • a little tutorial explaining how to play on a simple map, instead of bullet points
  • a little bit of background story in the game
  • some cool music :)

Otherwise, the concept is cool, but didn’t really feel like my type of game! Well done on the submission!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Interesting game

Lots of strategy with a Tetris style vibe so changes every play which is great

No matter what I tried in the first play it kept doing a “computer says no” noise and left me with no option but to press skip to continue.

Sounds effects were very loud and a little coarse

On mobile not everything fitted on the screen and it wouldn’t let me scroll around the page

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: interesting game mechanic with these tetris-blocks

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: I enjoyed the clever use of tetronimos here, that and the enclave rule. Something fun here, even if it is a little light on polish.

api.name: Lost City Of S
api.description: In search of the lost city of S, S for superstition, where exactly that was invented, as legend has it. And to this day, a mishap happens here every 13 seconds…

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text:
Very clean vector graphics and a nice art style. The monsters are great, but might be a bit too fast. I just face-tanked the scorpions and fish. I didn’t quite understand the 13-timer to the left, but I guess it just kind of staggers the character when it has counted down?

All in all: looks good and is short but sweet!

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Yes, exactly, that’s my interpretation of the theme: a “mishap” happens every 13th second so the 13th is to fear ;) Sorry for not making this more clear.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
I was bitterly disappointed. I had saved this game till last because I love your point’n’click adventures but it wasn’t to be this year :(

As always however, amazing use of SVG!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Oh dear, now I really wish I made another adventure! Sorry for the disappointment. There will be more!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I found the lost idol! Fantastic art. Challenging enemies, but thankfully you were generous with checkpoints and respawns. Nice job!

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: Nice vector-style art, simple and intuitive controls, and the addition of a checkpoint feature is really thoughtful – it helped me finish the whole game. Currently, it seems the main character can only move to avoid attacks. I think if there were options to move objects in the environment for cover or have some kind of active attack abilities, it would make the game even more enjoyable!

comments.author: Quinten Clause
comments.text: Great graphics! Always nice to find a game with a clear quick ending, when I have to rate jam games.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Short and simple, nice graphics, all works ok. Some of the enemies were brutal but the game was forgiving.

Fells like it needs a few more features.

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
control with the mouse was pretty awkward.

Short but super clean game.

Nice job.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Sorry for the awkward mouse input - it’s really designed for touch input on mobile devices. I really should have disabled this on the desktop.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Great looking game.

I couldn’t figure out how to control the character movement (was playing on desktop). Kept dying and couldn’t move out of the way.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Ah, sorry, mouse input wasn’t really meant for the mouse but for touch devices, and I just left it in there because I thought everyone would use the arrow keys on a desktop. Sorry, I should’ve mentioned this somewhere.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Really nice graphics, but gameplay is not really clear. I just went straight up, got killed many times but without feedback or punishment. If I understand the game, every 13 second I go back. If it has been easier to avoid creatures, and a visible life bar (maybe with heal items), it should have been really more fun. and when killed, respawn at some point or at start maybe, so the “back in time” every 13 seconds should have been really scary

comments.author: Mezriss
comments.text: Is mishap a control reversal when the timer runs out? I guess it makes sense in a game about avoiding enemies, but is still very confusing. Otherwise - very cool art, props for the river crossing part.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game of avoiding the obstacles.

Felt a bit like frogger or cross road.

Having thought the gorillas were hard to avoid, the scorpions proved even trickier, but the fish OMG they were something else.

I got to the end and found the rock carved with the face.

The graphics were really well done.

I’m glad that in death the character got back up and carried on, without having to go back to the jungle each time.

Some sounds would be good. Also moving faster than the enemies would help

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Well that was a silly one. I really love the graphics. I was literally lost in the jungle without knowing what to do, but eventually figured the only way was north. The animals are ruthless. I didn’t understand why, at the 13th second, my movement speed suddenly would flip and instead of going up I would go down. That made me die almost every time. It’s missing sound effects and music.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Very impressive visuals for less than 13k. Gameplay wise, I’m not sure what the goal is – survive? And the “mishaps” that happen every 13 seconds include changing the controls, which isn’t clear or fun. Still it’s very playable and looks great. Nice work!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
pretty short game. at first i thought i should do something against the gorillas, but it turned out, that running away is the best option i have .

So i ran upwards, discovered the desert and the sea, and the lost idol. the end. hu? that was fast :)

I like the artstyle, and the different biomes

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: I played this game using a trackpad, so it was a bit challenging (unless it supports keyboard and I didn’t figure it out?). But the graphics are super impressive! It is fairly short, but yet there were a bunch of different environments. I am not sure exactly what the goal of the game is, but I know I finished it somehow :-) Thanks for submitting it, and good job! Note: I wish there was sound in this game.

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This was a hoot, fantastic visuals considering the limitations as well.

api.name: GOLFROG
api.description: GOLF + FROG = GOLFROG

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: I enjoyed playing and also the surprise at the end.

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: Very cute little game. Given a bit more polish it could easily be a casual mobile game I’d pick up while waiting for the bus or something. Maybe it could have a procedural infinite mode. The mechanics are simple but effective, and it’s always fun to throw things across the screen. The surprise ending was a bit sad to be honest! That green little guy was my friend!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Only on my second playthrough did I discover the ability to roll! :)

Great little game, I totally dig the art style. Fantastic ending.

Moar levels please!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
Super flat minimalist graphics, really nice!

Rolling is fun!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Fun ending! Really love the flat graphics. You made a unique style with excellent palette and minimalism. Nice job

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
Nice graphics and concept. I think it’d be nice to have more complicated physics (wind, bouncing…). I think I got my frog to roll once but couldn’t figure out how I did it.

The ending alone is a really nice touch, I hope people get to see it :)

One thing I noticed is that when dragging, the trajectory doesn’t match the actual jump, which makes it a bit frustrating.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Super cute. I wish there was more to do

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: Ha! That was a cool wee casual game. Loved the ending!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
This should be olympic! Nice golf game, pretty clean graphics (cute frog) and good controls - at least on desktop. On mobile i would prefer the trajectory to be inversed (like angry birds), so that i can see the expected jump-path wich otherwise is hidden below my finger.

The unexpected ending in the 13th level earned you a bonus-point for the (otherwise ignored) theme ;-)

Good game, could be a casual gaming hit. needs more levels ;-)

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: It took me 59 moves to finish the game :) The concept is simple but addicitve, I really had fun to play!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Nice game on desktop, a fun and cute looking game.

The game didn’t work on mobile for me, the frog had no eyes and the drag operation had all the dashes hovering over the green triangle.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Very well made, super simple yet stylish and effective. I did not realise I could turn into a ball at first! Was much more fun when I realised this.

You had so much space left, maybe room for more holes?

comments.author: Jesper Rasmussen
comments.text:
Really nice game. Cute graphics and good controls.

Was really missing some simple audio.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice idea, made me think about “what the golf” in graphics I’m more usede to have reversed controls for golf games (moving mouse in opposite direction of where you want to send the frog)

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
A simple concept on first sight, but the cute character and the slick controls make it a really catchy game, I actually could not stop until I reached the end. Loved the final animation (not disclosing).

The frog is a bit optimistic about its jumping skills and falls a bit short, you have to compensate by aiming further. I only wished you had some audio there (sfx or background music).

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
The art is so cute. Really well made and fits well altogether. The helper at the top on each level is just enough to understand how the game works (thanks for that!) The mechanics are well added and really fun to tinker with. Adding some cute sounds would make this game almost perfect.

Well done!

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: Very very cute! Add it to mobile app stores!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Great (G R E A T) use of physics in this little artillery game; all in all this is an artillery game and you are the bullet.

I liked the animation when you roll down a hill, nice game.

By the way, no use of the competition theme.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: very fun game and nice graphics, pysics work really well

api.name: Triskaidekapotamus
api.description:
Triskaidekaphobia - from Ancient Greek τρεισκαίδεκα (treiskaídeka) ‘thirteen’ and φόβος (phóbos) ‘fear’, together meaning ‘fear or avoidance of the number 13’.

Hippopotamus - from Ancient Greek ἵππος (híppos) ‘horse’ and ποταμός (potamós) ‘river’, together meaning ‘horse of the river’.

Triskaidekapotamus - from Ancient Greek τρεισκαίδεκα (treiskaídeka) ‘thirteen’ and ποταμός (potamós) ‘river’, together meaning ‘13 rivers’.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I don’t know what I was doing, but I managed to rotate the “boat” around 180 degrees into a nice looking pentagram :)

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: A Verlet-based simulation, written entirely in a functional style, with a fixed-step main loop! I’m a big fan.

comments.author: aselitto
comments.text: I love the name and direction this was going! I would love to see it completed!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Looks like this had potential! Hope you come back and complete a game using your wireframe and physics engine!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Looked very promising! I can see nice ideas there. Finish it!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text: I liked the wireframe interaction as I moved it around the screen and they moved almost like weighted pendulums

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text: This reminded me of an old Google doodle where you had to hammer the left and right keys to row. Hope you can finish it next year!

api.name: Mai
api.description:
Congratulations, it’s a MAI! You must care for your MAI’s needs and raise her big and strong - as fast as possible. Friday the 13th is just around the corner, and anything could happen.

You can control the game with mouse, touch or keyboard arrows.

Design by Marja and code Viljami.

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: Doesn’t works in phone, if a category says is for mobile also might to work. I recommend the next season truly recognise many games under the same situation for mobile specially iPhone.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Sadly, I can only push the “z” button on my phone, but after pressing it a while nothing happens.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I wasn’t able to get past the initial “z” screen. I looked through the assets, and it seems like there’s some great robot pixel art in there.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text: I’m afraid like a few others playing your game on mobile, there was just a z in the screen but no obvious way to progress.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I worked out holding down on the Z wakes the character, took a while! I played some music and also played the catch game.

Cute game, some sounds for feedback would have been a good addition I think!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text: Clues would have been appreciated. Keyboard seems to work better than mouse, I got stuck with the latter, all three buttons were giving music notes. Still not sure how it is supposed to play after this and the cherry catching game.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Woke the MAI by holding the button, and then made it to day 10. MAI evolved into something big and then just “danced” a bit… hm I have the feeling, I’ve missed something.

The artstyle is neat. Music/sfx would be cool.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
I advanced in the calendar by pressing Z a lot of times but I think I missed the point.

Please provide clearer instructions and I will be back to play tomorrow.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text: Thanks for your submission to the js13k! It seems though that your submission is a bit on the shorter side and lack gameplay. I did enjoy the tamagochi style you were going for here, but even with that, I couldn’t make progress and the buttons seemed not to work at all times. I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month.

api.name: Counting Mind
api.description:
You have to click the button only when the equation does NOT equal 13. You gain a point by clicking correctly and lose a point otherwise.

Play as long you want to excercise your math skills.

You can put your 3d-glasses 🕶

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: Love the blue-and-red 3D! We hardly ever get to use that :)

comments.author: Łukasz Lityński
comments.text: Thanks. I just wanted to make simple game and I remembered that I have pair of 3D glasses. So… why not? ;)

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: My kids enjoyed this! I need to get them a pair of 3d glasses.

comments.author: Łukasz Lityński
comments.text: Thanks. Nice to know :) I got mine with a book (Brave New World - there was 3D glases edition)

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I don’t have 3D glasses but I found the idea very nice :)

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Nice simple math trainer. Graphic effects are interesting, but a bit annoying. I think it would make more sense adding visual effects to when you do solve (or fail) the task.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k!

You have a good prototype here. I got engaged in the mechanic and I could see this being part of a game loop.

You striked a good balance between the size of the equations, going from “you can do it blindfolded” to “please slowdown timer I didn’t even finish multiplying the first part”.

For me it was exactly on the flow – not too hard I gave up doing the math, but not so many too easy that I turned off. Before I realised, I was at 30 points and could continue had it been part of a game.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
Well written math exercise, but this not really a game.

The 3D effect is a bit distracting.

How about labelling the button « not 13 » to make the goal clear, and measure the time needed to answer for a challenge ?

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Couldn’t test with 3D glasses, I suppose it would have been nice :)

I believe the game wouldn’t grab much attention from adults, but my kids enjoyed it!

The balance between the equations could be improved though. Some equations would need more time to calculate. I believe you could explore some kind of level advancement: simpler equations first, more complex equations later…

Good work so far! Congrats on the submission!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The 3D idea was pretty neat

The idea was good. Some of the equations were a little tricky - hahah

Well done for submitting a game

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: good game to test your math skills, i dont have 3d glasses but 3d idea is neat and seemed worked on alot, well done

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Good match with the theme of this year! It requires a lot of brain power, so I couldn’t play for long. But it was a good experience. A background music to add some pressure would have been great. Thanks for submitting it!

api.name: Paraskevidekatriaphobia
api.description:
You are playing as a robot-spider and your task is to help your creator build factory

Controls

  • WASD to move
  • SPACE to interact

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I made lasagna! Love the fun writing style. The chicken chase was weird – I don’t know if it was a bug or by design, but the chicken always stayed out of reach, until I stopped moving and then it came to me on its own. Very impressive 3d graphics. Looking forward to seeing more of your work. Congrats!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: not sure what to do to beat the game

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: haha love the robot-spider design!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: I could not find the chicken. I saw the chicken at the edge of my screen, but the it disappeared. I love the 3D graphics and that spider-like robot, but I was definitely afraid of getting lost because the map had no landmarks nor arrows to help me back to the construction site.

comments.author: hvox
comments.text: I expected player to lie low, observe, memorize the route of the chicken (it walks in circles around the construction site), stand on the route and grab the chicken as it passes by. It’s kind of a stealth mission. In practice, no one has done this, everyone either catches it by accident, or can’t catch it at all. This chicken is a complete failure that has ruined my game. I hate it. 😡🍴🍗🍗

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Graphics for the hero image are super cute! Love the use of Blender.

Need some sound effects and music for a little more immersion. Also add a way to speed up the text on the intro scene. Good narrative and setup though. The chicken ran away so fast - and the game scene felt like it was a bit too infinite, felt like I was never going to catch that chicken… or find the construction site. Funnily enough the chicken came back but ran away so much faster than my character could ever move.

Add a little more into your GitHub README ❤️.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: I think I completed the game. I was able to make lasagna after being teleported away. Impressive 3D graphics.

comments.author: ckolin
comments.text: That was cool! Some cute models and funny narration. Also, your implementation of the plot line looks very elegant!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game was really nice looking, the spider model and movements were pretty impressive.

I never did find the chicken - I’m still looking. The playfield was pretty big. Also it took me quite a while of reading what the creator said before I realise I was able to start moving around.

The pick-up/put-down mechanic for interacting with objects was a bit flickery, maybe some kind of debounce would be good, or make it so you need to release SPACE and press it again to toggle states.

comments.author: hvox
comments.text:
Thanks for detailed feedback. You brought a couple issues to my attention that I wouldn’t have noticed without you. The way SPACE works right now is a bug. It is supposed to do one pick-up(or put-down) per press, but instead it does it repeatedly if you press and hold it. Thanks to you I noticed and fixed this bug in the post-compo version of the game.

Sorry about the chicken: it hangs out near the construction site and runs away only if player chases it. To catch it, player have to stand on its route and grab it when it passes by.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: I cant find the construction site so I wandered aimlessly for a while and left the smol in a field somewhere after a chicken ran away. Then I couldnt find either of them again. I persevered though, and managed to make the pizza somehow. I love that you can move diagonally and no one has complained about the inability to skip the dialog. 🐔🍽️

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: I couldn’t find the chicken so couldn’t progress very far. I also had picked up the person in the beginning and couldn’t figure out where it wanted me to place it back down (didn’t know what the factory was). Game looks interesting though.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • Am I the spider of factorio?
  • Great work on:
    • The dialogues! It kept me going and they were unexpected for a game jam.
    • The animation and making a 3d game under 13kb! That’s no small feat :)
  • I liked that you:
    • Had a whole setup about chickens, robot, portals and lasagna! All for the power of lasagna!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: It was too hard to catch the chicken, but graphics are really nice, so do the animations. Game seems interesting

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
This is one of the best games I’ve played here!

I really liked the text at the beginning until I started moving, it was very interesting and funny :). Then it turned out that I am a robot! Robot!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! I love robots :). It was nice to have someone besides you on the game stage, moreover, the creator himself! Somewhat reminiscent of the pleasant atmosphere of helping in cooking in the kitchen :).

Little Troubles That Happen on Friday the 13th is a very good theme match! And playing as a robot as a secondary character who has to help is innovative!

3D graphics with well-drawn objects, the creator, me and, of course, the lasagna machine - very cool!

I didn’t immediately understand how to catch up with the chicken, I ran after her for a long time :), but then I realized that before I ran after her, she was circling nearby ;). So then I had to turn back, it’s good that I ran diagonally and came back the same way, because without navigation I would have gotten lost :).

And in general, my creator can turn off the sun at the push of a button! Even if not the first time ;).

The movement of the robot is very smooth, the only thing I noticed is that when I press the S button, that is, I go down, the eyes are not visible, but then I realized that this is probably due to the way the shadow falls on the object. In any case, this is a cool mechanic!

I really liked it, congratulations!

comments.author: Michael M.

Great robot spider!!

api.name: Mini13 : A very minimal fps
api.description: A very minimal FPS. WASD to move. Space to jump. Click to shoot.

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
First off I will say it’s pretty impressive for this to be made and fit into 13k bytes, even compressed.

Now as a js13k game entry:

Barely a tech demo but at least it does says so in the name.

Move, shoot and jump, no score or goals.

No sound or music that I could hear.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice wee 3d engine there, has some potential for future expansion.

Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke

Game looks good and is fun to play.

Reminds me a lot of a JS13k entry from a previous year.

I wasn’t sure what the purpose of the monsters was as they didn’t seem to pose any threat to me.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I defeated all the monsters! Nice job, impressive work, especially considering you used less than 5kb! Lots of different directions you could take this game.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The fact you could fit this almost three times in the size limit is very impressive!

And it’s also written in Haxe, such a unique submission really.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Is seems impossible to have a 3D FPS in less than 5kb! Congratulations!!! It’s a pity you did not create a full game (maybe you were running out of time?) as you have all the 3D techonology in your hands and have more than 8kB left. Maybe next year we will have the pleasure to see a full impressive FPS game from you ?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
The impressive fact is that its only 4.88 kB! The movement-controls are great. The gameplay is a bit… erm… short? :-)

If you had filled the rest of the 13 Kb with gameplay/levels/leveldesign, this would be an overwhelming game for the jam!

Good job, anyways!

comments.author: Michael M.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: You seem to have the basis for a great game and enough room for expansion!

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Nothing new by itself (especially compared to the last year entries), but interesting thing about it is that it is made in Haxe. And compiles in a rather compact code. Could be a useful boilerplate for similar projects.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Thanks for another submission to js13k!

You have a 3d engine running here, and it’s not even half of the final size, so that’s impressive! I do hope this can be a base for a game next year and that you learned from it!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
It was fun :).

It would be interesting to have a volcanic lava instead of the ground to jump by stones :). It would also be nice to be able to lose when the enemy faces you, and I can also go beyond the borders of the earth and fly in the sky :).

Congratulations!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Fun little game. Technically impressive, but with very limited gameplay. There’s no threat of dying, even if walking off limits, and jumping on the blocks gives only a slight better view, but otherwise not much of a point. I couldn’t see the connection to the theme.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text: Gameplay is really smooth, now that you have the Minecraft engine up and running, how about packing a scenario and extra features into the remaining 8 kb ?

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Nice tech demo, but not so much a game. Perhaps you could make the red “monsters” move towards me so that I can move around, dodge behind pillars etc

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: Pretty smooth as far as 3D entries go, youve got a good few features into this game. I feel like its the perfect setup for some platforming too, and of course some infinite terrain would do nicely. Good work with the 3D though!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Well, as described by the author: it is a very minimal FPS. In fact, it is more a tech demo than a game, but you do need to kill enemies to finish it, so I guess it counts. Graphics are good. I am not sure there is any link with this year’s theme though, but no big deal. It doesn’t have audio, sadly. Everything else works as advertised. Good job!

api.name: Millennials
api.description:
Did you grow up in the ‘80s and early ‘90s? Do you miss it?

Craft your deck during a series of card duels against the childhood villains: Suke Lywalker against Varth Dader, Bombles against DojoBojo, and Matban against Poker!

Features

  • 60 cards, for a total of 2,176,782,336,000,000,000,000 deck combinations!
  • 20 locations, each with its own effect, for a total of 8,000 possible battlegrounds.
  • 3 unique enemies with different strategies.
  • A challenging endless mode.
  • Turn-based battles.
  • Hand-drawn pixel art.
  • Track your card collection and unlock all 60 cards.
  • Build powerful combos and observe as they unfold in the beautifully indented battle log, which contains the record of everything that happens during the battle.
  • Power-efficient — it’s just HTML with no game loop!

How do I play?

  • Drag & drop with your mouse, Windows 95 style!
  • Each turn you have 1 more energy to play your cards.
  • Score more points than your opponent across all 3 locations.
  • For each location that you win, pick one card from the shop before the next battle.

How does it implement the theme?

The theme inspired us to build a deckbuilder with a twist: you can only ever have exactly 12 cards in your deck. When choosing new cards for your deck, you don’t add them, making the deck larger (and slower). Instead, you exchange one of the cards already in the deck for the new card. You could say your deck is superstitious and really doesn’t want to have 13 cards.

Why is there no sound?

Look, we both know you’re going to play this game at work, or maybe while putting your youngest to sleep. We could have added some audio, but you’d play the game on mute anyways, and we’d need to drop a few cards to make room for the sound data. Plus, we’d need to also squeeze in the logic to toggle the music. Which you’d promptly switch to OFF. So instead, enjoy the extra cards and go build those sweet combos!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Really impressive deck-building game! The retro pixel art is fantastic, and I love the concept. It’s clear you’ve put a lot of effort into this project. I did find some of the mechanics and rules a bit unclear, which made it challenging to fully grasp the gameplay. Clarifying these aspects could really enhance the experience. Overall, great work – I’m excited to see how you’ll develop this game further!

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text: Thanks, Cody! We do plan to keep working on this game and without the size limitation I hope we can do a better job of explaining the mechanics. I’m so glad to hear that you liked the card art. I spent a lot of time researching what was possible given the number of cards we were planning. In the end, 75 sprites weigh 1262 bytes, which comes out to ~17 bytes per card. I’m pretty proud of this :)

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Implementing a card game is really complex, so major kudos on the innovation and technical ability!

Despite what you wrote, I do missed greatly some sound effects, as well as animations. I felt like so many things were happening all over the board, that I couldn’t keep track. It’s still impressive for 13KB, but it was hard to follow the first 2 rounds.

It’s still fun, and I would say totally worth continuing.

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
Thanks for playing, João! Designing the engine for this game was a lot of fun. I spent the last 5 editions of js13kGames building my own 3d rendering engine, just to completely ditch it this year and build a new one from scratch. And it’s a completely different kind of engine: the challenge is not perfomance, 3d maths, or lighting effects, but instead being able to implement gameplay mechanics we designed and ensure correctness.

I acknowledge the feedback about the audio and more generally — about explaining better what happens on the screen. Personally, I usually read the log once all cards stop moving, to see if there was anything I missed. But even then I sometimes feel a bit lost.

We do plan to continue working on a post-jam version! Thanks for the kind words of encouragement.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
I feel like each time I play this, I will enjoy it more and more! Seems like it’s quite deep, so you need to spend some time to absorb all the rules.

Really nice touch the title screen shows the cards you’ve used/seen! That’s awesome. Clicking the card to get a “3D” preview of their abilities is also really nice.

Gameplay-wise, it’s interesting that you have three “battles” to keep track of at once (left/right/center).

I’m probably just not paying attention, but I’m not entirely sure what causes the game to end..? At first I thought winning one “zone” (left/right/center) ended that game, but I don’t think that’s correct. Is it after x turns?

Anyway, my favorite cards were:

  • Toy Astronaut: helps you use more powerful cards
  • Coprobo: get access to enemy cards, very useful
  • Moher: no special abilities, but only 3 energy for a pretty strong attack

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
Thanks for playing! I know card games can be a bit hard to get into, which is why, I think, we don’t see many of them during jams. However, we took the risk and our goal was to design a game with a high replay value. I’m happy to hear that you enjoyed it!

Replayability is the reason why the card collection is featured prominently on the title screen—unlocking all cards is a ‘quest’ and you’ll need to complete multiple runs in order to achieve it.

Thank you for the feedback about battle rules not being clear enough. We could have done a better job explaining them. Battles go on for 6 turns. In order to win a battle, you need to score more points in total across all three locations. Each location has an effect and can hold up to 4 cards.

And yes! Toy Astronaut is great at accelerating your deck, making it possible to play a 6-cost card on the 5th turn. I also like Choody who plays the top card of your deck for free. Do try some of the other 5-cost and 6-cost cards—you can build sweet combos with them 😎.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
This is a great board game! I love the way you incorporated deck-building mechanics, especially having three places to construct cards with different effects depending on the enemies.

I’m curious if the characters are tied to their abilities in the design. I’m really looking forward to seeing how this game develops after the competition! Great job!

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Really cool game! I love rogue like deck building decks. Lovely art and cool references (or maybe not, I feel old know! :D) Sound would have been great, you still have 0.01 kb left in your zip for that :P Excellent effect hover the cards. I had a similar one on my entry “Cardice” years ago :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun card game.

Lots of recognisable characters on the cards which was neat. The graphics were really well done.

I loved the shiny card mechanic as I looked at them close up - very cool.

I’d have liked to have seen a tutorial or hint system to help me get the hang of it.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Great idea and implementation. “Look closer at card” effect is very cool. AI is pretty dumb, but that’s expected. Looks like you can unlock more cards, but I don’t see how, exactly.

comments.author: Michał Chojnacki
comments.text:
I have yet to properly sit down to play it, so I’ll update if I manage to play sufficiently before the vote deadline.

That said, in one of your replies you mentioned the sprite size at 1262 bytes. Well, you’ve got some free 200-300ish bytes laying on the table and waiting to be grabbed — since that’s base64 encoded. That’s one of the bigger things that immediately jump to mind looking at the implementation.

Considering you’re Roadrolling, I think you’re optimizing for size, not site load times (and HTTP roundtrip avoidance) — posthtml-inline-assets begone and you got free real estate for additional cards.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Impressive depth of the game! And I enjoyed the graphics a lot.

The drag&drop could benefit from a larger drop area. I often grabbed the bottom of the card, which led to very long distances. And I think it would feel more natural, if I would be able to drop it when the card overlaps with the target area.

After the last round, I was still able to place a card, but could not end the turn. And it took me a while to understand that the game was over. So I think it would be good to visualize the state of the game not only in the game logs, but also have some elements within the game field or some temporary overlays.

Still a great game idea!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A dueling card game with a considerable amount of content and detail at just under 13k. I would have liked to see the theme integrated more directly into the gameplay, but I’m impressed with the depth. Nice job!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Dzień dobry! Thanks for another one of your submission to js13k!

Great work on having a complete concept and game under 13kb, even more with the amount of depth that is coming from 12 cards and plenty of rules.

It took me a while to get going. I thought reading the log on the right side was crucial for playing, but it was going so fast with so many events that I gave up on it. Then I couldn’t tell if there was some battle and understood that maybe not after 2 or 3 games – all it matters is the location. After 10 games I still didn’t get the locals activation phase.

Only after 5 or 6 games is that I understood that there was only 6 turns – I couldn’t understand why the games were ending.

I liked the enemies (not the one with the marble makers and jokers) had their own personality and deck – I wish I could make a deck and explore the possibilities.

Very smart choice of not having any kind of ability that would require adding extra steps like choosing cards or making decisions. Less UI, less code, and I didn’t feel I was losing anything (but gosh those marble makers… do they pick the best card of the hand?)

Last, you mentioned that no music for extra cards but managed to pack a 3d visualization for the cards (joking ;PP)

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: Very clever implementation of this board-game area capture system (I can’t remember what the name of the system is at the moment). I was really entertained by the different cards you put in, just familiar enough. Great work coding the logic. Some sort of explanation pane on the menu would have been welcome, but once I recognised it I was away.

the 13s are attacking earth to invade

they are sending colored ships with 13 written on them

you controll the missle station on earth and only you can defend it

Fun game, nice use of emojis for sprites!

I found the upgrades menu only after reading the description or I would have missed it, perhaps making it a bit more prominent would have helped.

comments.text: Nice job, fun game. I made it to level 14 and then the enemies were too fast. The time between waves could be a little shorter, I felt like there was a lot of waiting between waves. The upgrade system was nice, the music was great. Well done!

Would be nice to have the music on by default for atmosphere. Throw in explosions and particles and it would rock.

One weird thing happened; when i started wave 2, no aliens arrived.

comments.author: Roman Miniv

Reached level 3!

After the first wave, I thought it was stuck, but it turned out that the time before the next wave is quite long, I managed to upgrade myself and just wait for a while, I think it would be good to be able to start the next wave faster. Also, for some reason, when 13 flew into me, I died, although the HP was still 5-10 :).

In everything else, the game is great! Pleasant music, appropriate to the theme, good control and intuitive enough. I like how the Earth rotates with its continents :).

Among the improvements I would like to see are possible moving backgrounds, namely stars, starlight (various intensities), passing comets, meteorites, etc. It would be funny to watch how 13 flying to the planet crash into an asteroid and thus do not reach :D.

Nice game, congratulations!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
A simple and fun game, very straightforward with regards to the theme. Good job!

Sun orbiting the Earth game me a good chuckle :)

The music fits the space theme nicely.

One thing I’d consider changing would be emoji, seeing that you were far from the size limit it was possible to add some graphics instead.

(The use of emoji for graphics makes many entries lack personality and look almost indistinguishable, to me at least.)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: I think the game was broken? I killed all the enemies and then nothing happened after that. The timer kept ticking up but no more enemies spawned, so couldn’t progress after the first round. The upgrades confused me at first because I thought the format was telling me the cost per upgrade and not the increase amount per cost.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: I made it past level one; no more enemies came after level 2, bought some upgrades but could not make use of them.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I played the first wave, but then nothing else happens on wave 2. The first wave was very easy and barely a challenge, so I was looking forward for the next levels and upgrades.

I was surprised, but it seems despite being quite simple, this game is very demanding on my computer, and the animations are not very smooth. It was a bit weird that the moon orbits the earth but never passes in front of the earth somehow.

The time is displayed a bit weirdly, for example instead of 4:01 it would show 4:1 without the zero, which is odd.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I’ve played on desktop and mobile. I think mobile is easier.

I enjoyed playing, and can see there’s plenty of scope for expanding it.

When I dispatched all the 13s the planet kept rotating and the moon orbited but no new 13s came, like the game was over but without a way to continue?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Made it to level 5 by pure patience :-) After leveling up (and upgrading) the game waits too long to send the new wave.

Nice artstyle and music.

The game would benefit from a little bit variation, maybe othe attack-patterns (not all in a straight line) or variation in the missile-types

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
No waves spawned at level 2. Maybe it was because I bought some upgrades?

I like the use of emoji, especially the rockets. Maybe the “13”-asteroids coming at me could have been a mix of emoji ☄️ and the number 13 as well? (Just brainstorming)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: I really like the use of emojis for graphics. Unfortunately, I couldn’t play past Level 1, but I got the gist of it. It was fun though.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Cool animation of earth and moon, should be interesting with upgrades, but nothing spawn after level 1, I was not able to play more.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: Is it just me, or there is only one level? I finished this game in 30 seconds, then nothing happened. It’s sad, because it was a good start. The controls and the background music are fine. Thanks for submitting!

api.name: Odyssey
api.description:
Ahoy Corsair! Welcome to The Isle-Hop Odyssey, where you are stranded on an island with little more than your two legs and a soon-to-be-rusty ship nearby. Hop from island to island like a seafaring kangaroo - or maybe more like a seagull after a cake - conquering forts, battling crabs, and other scallywags as you sail through treacherous seas filled with giant squids and sea serpents just waiting to ruin your day.

Your mission? Siege castles, recruit a ragtag crew (who might not know port from starboard), and loot dungeons, all while trying not to sink your ship or get pinched by a particularly grumpy crab. But don’t get too cozy! There is a mysterious island no one talks about - except when they’re screaming in terror. The Cursed Isle (it’s the 13th one), hides the nastiest dungeon of them all, home to a certain Balrog (or was it Balron?) who’s been feeling a bit cranky lately and definitely doesn’t like visitors. So, buckle up, sailor! Your Odyssey awaits - if you don’t get eaten by a squid first!

Controls: Mobile controls are available in the bottom left of the screen. You can also move with gestures. For keyboard controls, use the arrow keys, WASD, and Spacebar.

Gameplay: You have three main characters: the Hero, the Crew, and the Ship. Combat depends on your location: when sailing, you’ll fight with the Ship; when on land, you’ll battle with the Hero and Crew.

Your adventure kicks off on an island with a castle where you can upgrade your Ship. Scattered across the map are other islands, some with enemy-occupied forts and others with dungeons.

Your first task? Conquer these forts and build up your strength. But don’t get too comfortable! Your ultimate mission is to defeat the Balrog, a fearsome foe lurking in the deepest level of the dungeon on the 13th Cursed Island. And remember, you have just 13 days to accomplish this, so set sail and get cracking!

The game has an installable Progressive Web App version. Enjoy!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: nice strategy game. My crew were just revolting!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Hi, thanks for trying my game! There are sail points that you need to pay attention to (indicated by the ⚙ bar). If you try to sail with no sail points left, the crew will suffer, and if their HP reaches zero, they will revolt and demand money. Sail points can be refreshed at the Castle’s Inn, but this will cost you one of the precious 13 days you have to complete the game. :)

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: I love travelling around and exploring the map. I feel that the combat is too difficult, though. Just about everything can kill you. And there is no way to know how difficult something is until you fight it. The revolting crew was annoying until I figured out they prefer to sleep at the inn. Makes sense.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Yeah, I know the game lacks explanation for pretty much everything. I should have provided completely different description, and I will probably do so after receiving enough feedback. So, thank you for explaining what is confusing to you. The sail bar definitely needs to pulse to be more visible when it’s getting empty, and a dialog when the crew gets hit for the first time because of that will help. Regarding the enemies, the pre-battle screen should indeed be more informative (it was). At first, only the Serpents are overkill (hence the harsh colors) and the Castle siege battle to some extent. There are a some strategies that can be used initially: either search for the easiest dungeons and loot them (only 3 floors displayed in the dungeon intro screen) - clearing a dungeon gives attack and health bonuses that are needed for successful castle siege, or look if there are castles on the shore that can be sieged from sea by the ship. Once you get another castle and upgrade the crew, things get easier.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Oo, there’s a lot of content here. The game seems compelling. I like the idea of going out on treasure hunts and managing the crew. It feels like a near miss, because a little difficulty tuning could make a big difference. There’s a lot of good raw materials here, nice pixel art, nice sound effects, and a ton of UI. Nice job

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game, providing feedback and for the nice words. On the difficulty aspect - I have deliberately left a cheat on so it will be easier for players if they want to progress faster, and that is the Apple healing mechanic which can be exploited - just go to a tree and heal as many times as you want ;)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Interesting little game. I liked exploring the map and the fog of war effect. There was lots of interesting things to discover around the map and figuring out what they did.

I do wish I could tell what my relative strength was to the enemy. The game tells you what the enemy strength and attack is, but doesn’t tell me what my own units strength and attack is. I mostly had to die a bunch of times to figure out what fights I could take. I also couldn’t figure out how to upgrade my hero strength. The shipyard upgrades my ship but I couldn’t find any place that upgraded my hero.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Thank you for playing, and explaining what was unclear. When tapping the units UI a window appears with information.. but, it doesn’t work on the battle intro screen ;/ had to disable that in the last minute because of a bug, sorry for the frustration. Regarding Hero upgrades - that’s the only unit that actually gains experience - looting the easiest two dungeons is enough for him to level up.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
A really nice idea, I love how you hop out of the boat and can travel around and attack other islands etc.

I entered a cave which seemed to go on for some time so I fled, was not really sure if I should keep going.

Quite a lot going on and not sure I full understood it all, I feel with some futher instructions I would have lasted longer. After hoping a few islands and winning some battles I journied too far and lost.
