comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Thank you for the feedback! It was wise of you not to venture too long into the deeper dungeons without a buffed party. There is a clue on the dungeon intro screen indicating whether a dungeon is easy or hard, with a (…) sign on the level display showing there are more levels. Part of the game’s challenge is that players don’t know which dungeon contains the final boss (e.g., the 13th island). I’ll make this clearer in the post-compo update. Thanks again for your insights!

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: I really love the graphics here, and it’s appealing to just sail around the world. With audio and gamepad support, this could really be something. I’m amazed how much detail you were able to cram into 13k!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I’m glad you enjoyed sailing around! I drew inspiration from Heroes III, but I wanted to add my own touch by allowing players to hop between islands as much as possible, while still needing to return to a home island by the end of the day. Will definitely try to enhance player experience further, especially with gamepad support, thank you for the suggestion!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
There are so much content in it, amazing.

I made only to day 3 tho. Combat is difficult. I wish there was a button to run away before death, or respawn with -1 day in the inn instead of game over.

Beside of the difficulty it’s nice, complete and polished game. Congrats.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Thank you so much for playing my game and for your valuable feedback! Your idea about respawning with a penalty instead of a game over is very insightful!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
It seems like a very deep game where there’s so much to discover and strategies to employ and so many sprites! I gave it a few rounds and I didn’t get very far, but I learned that you need to pick your battles and not fight a serpent right off the bat!

I might suggest is to enable fleeing while in Auto battle. I saw my health dwindling too low I thought I could flee, but it doesn’t look like I can.

I should have read your description before starting because it gives at least a few pointers that I should have known but I think that’s how retro games were.

Otherwise, in a massive map with a crew and ship to build, you could go on to experience some interesting and unique adventures.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I enjoyed how the game has so much content packed into it. It was a great experience to go explore things freely and figure out the world. Controls were a bit hard to use on my Android tablet, but other than that, it’s a nice game!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Such a fun little RPG! We don’t get nearly enough of these in js13k.

I’m into the old school graphics, works really nicely here. The lack of emojis is fantastic.

The progression is nice, conquering castles to get better crew. Haven’t finished the game yet, I always die to a beefy castle. :) Will try again though, I want to see the ending.

One UI feature I especially enjoyed is how you implemented the sound button, such a neat idea.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A cool looking game, pixel graphics are ace.

I enjoyed exploring. The auto-battle feature was handy.

I like the animations. But there were some parts of the screen where it looked like it was trying to show a font or emoji that I didn’t have as there were unicode numbers shown instead.

The transition from land to ship was quite smooth.

When out at sea sometimes the boat moved fast, and other times really slow, I wasn’t sure why.

When on desktop and using a keyboard to move around, it was a little clunky to switch to a mouse just for the UI elements.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. Indeed I use a few special characters in the game like the crossed swords (⚔) and I suspected it could be problematic on some devices. Lesson learned to not rely on such symbols, will improve all that in the Post-Compo version, including the slowdown you mentioned and the keyboard controls.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: an rpg, wow nice game, took while to get hang of it but it get easier once cought, well done.

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Very ambitious and interesting game, graphics look great and controls work well enough on mobile. As other comments have mentioned, it’s hard to tell if you’ll win/lose a fight, so takes a bit of trial and error to make progress. Some more audio would be a nice touch. Nice job!

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: Sprites are cute although it takes me some time to understand them. The map are also different every time, so every play is fresh. If there are more SE and music, it would be a pretty charming game. Also liked the UI you made here.

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
omg, the work behind this game must have been immense.

There are a lot of elements, from unit movement management to combat, done with very nice pixel art.

The strategy component is also perfect, the game is very well balanced and this aspect goes beyond the 13k limitation, you can tell there has been a lot of beta testing work.

Kudos.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Thank you for the encouraging words and for recognizing the balance in the game! I failed in explaining some of the crucial game aspects, which made the game seem harder for many players. It was indeed a lot of work, and I didn’t have much time for testing - it was more like playtesting the game in my mind while frantically developing and adjusting things as I felt they should fit :)

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: It took my a while to figure out what to do, but I think I got it. Maybe a quick tutorial would be nice? I wish I could just skip the auto-combat in one click, because I spent a lot of time waiting for the battles to finish. The first few times, I died so easily. It is not an easy game for sure! But the graphics are good, there are SFX, and once you understand how to play it gets more fun. Thank you for submitting this game!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: This is incredibly impressive. I really loved the art style and scale you picked, it gave a sense of visual simplicity that didn’t match the game but hooked me. There’s so much going on here, it’s probably content-wise the most impressive game I’ve seen so far.

api.name: 13 Bubbles
api.description:
13 Bubbles is a twist on the classic Bubble Pop kind of game.

Here, to be able to pop bubbles, not only the group has to be of the same color but the sum of all its values must be a multiple of 13 (0 and negatives included). Reach bigger sums (26 or -26, 39 or -39, etc…) to multiply your score! To avoid headaches from too much calculus, hover your mouse on a bubble group to get the current sum of values and the closest multiples.

Try to survive as long as possible by preventing the bubbles to get to the bottom of the screen, and if 2 or 3 colors are too easy for you, why not try the hard mode?

Controls:

  • Change Bubble Color: Mouse Wheel
  • Shoot: Mouse click
  • Toggle sounds: M
  • Pause: Escape

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nicely done. Clever use of 13. On my first couple runs, I didn’t realize you can change the bubble color. Changing the bubble color dramatically increases the number of decisions! Well done on graphics and audio

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Tutorial is definitely the biggest thing I wanted to add but add no time to do. Thanks for the comment!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thats a clever variation of the bubble-shooter genre!

Nice presentation! Good game!

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Thank you very much!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
An interesting twist on the bubble-popping genre.

Almost impossible without the hover hints.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: You’re right, the hints are mandatory for most, and I wanted to have gradually smaller hints as you increased the difficulty. Maybe an extreme mode where there’s no hint could be interesting for the hardcore players :D

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: This game was a chill experience to me with a sweet music and time to think for each move. I loved the concept wich fits perfectly the theme. Well done !

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Thank you for the comment! Glad you found it chill, it was definitely one of the goals!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
My game is also about bubbles, so of course I had to check out this game. I love the main menu, it’s so simple, and pretty. The bubbles in the background are subtle and nice to look at. The sfx and music also fit the gameplay well. I appreciate the mouse-over hint, otherwise I would have given up, haha.

Using the mouse wheel to change color is a good idea, but it was difficult to operate on my mouse (Apple Magic Mouse.) The color changes too fast. Maybe it’s easier with a wheel with steps.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text:
I’m going to check your game then, can’t miss on anything bubble-related :p

Thank you for your nice comment, the hint is indeed very welcome! I must work on the controls for a post-jam version, for sure! Getting it to a playable state on mobile will have me rethink it anyway.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: Testing my elementary math here! I like the gamification element of combining math and Puzzle Bobble mechanics, with additional layers added in to make it more challenging. One thing I found a little confusing was how the current sum of values and multiples were presented to the player.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text:
It’s definitely an area that can be improved! Tutorial and better UX is one of the main goals of the improvements I’m working on.

Thank you very much for your comment!

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Very polished production! I enjoyed playing this a lot!

The controls were good. Though I think it would have been beneficial, if they were also displayed inside the game, e.g. next to the game field. (Had to leave the game again to remember how changing the color works)

Great audio and graphics. Good idea that the side bars change color accordingly.

Nice to be able to plan with the upcoming numbers. Except when another row appears and all the numbers change 😱 which I guess is a feature not a bug 😅

Sometimes after a row appeared there were already groups that were a multiple of 13. I think it could makes sense to avoid creating these or that they should pop

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text:
Thank you very much for your comment and compliments!

Indeed, that is not a bug, new rows come and add an unpredictable element to the gameplay! But, as you say, when a group “automatically” gets to a multiple of 13 it should pop. Unfortunately I didn’t have time to implement that :/

As you mention, a tutorial is definitely something I’ll try to get into my next jams, but I have to admit I’m often keeping it for the end since it is playable without.

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: A fun progression on bubble pop. Game works properly, and I really liked game track (It has that nursery mobile sound, which is quite bubbly). We also entered a Mathy game, check out Primonumerophobia if ya get the chance. Solid entry.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Will check that before the end! Thanks for your nice comment :)

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Good bubble game, nice graphics, good controls.

Not as mindless as I like my bubble games :-P

Great entry!

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Thank you for your comment! It is not intended to be mindless indeed :D

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
It is a nice use of the theme and a very cool game overall.

I agree with some of the reviewers that an in-game tutorial would help the players to grasp the game. I read the description and still kind of missed the point that changing the color is crucial. But it was an “a-ha!” moment once I got it - it instantly gives you that strategic depth in the gameplay. It would be nice to guide a player to the moment she can start to switch colors. It takes just one successful hit with a color switch to get it.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Thank you so much! The tutorial is not something I’ll be missing next time I do a gamejam :)

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
It’s a good Puzzle Bobble + Math game, butthe game field is really too wide and there is space for a lot of bubbles before you get in trouble.

You should make the game field a bit (quite a bit) smaller.

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text:
Thanks for your comment!

The game field (and many other parameters/variables/values) are 13 or multiple of 13 (13x26 in this case), which was supposed to be part of a bigger easter egg in the game related to the theme, but I wasn’t able to complete it.

I think it’s nice that it takes some time to get in trouble, so that everyone can have fun! But I understand that some challenge is needed, and the hard mode hopefully provides a bit of that.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
This is a really well put together game. Classic Bust-a-Move gameplay but with the theme integrated. Nicely done.

To me the gameplay would have been simpler of the goal was to get each group to 13, or something more straightforward. I did get headaches from too much calculus but the mouse hovering calculations were helpful. The game has a lovely, soft aesthetic and plays very well. The only issue I believe I found is that I was only given bubbles of one color.

The portrait viewport made me think it would work on mobile, and it does! When it first loaded it was zoomed-in too much (not sure why, looks like it has the meta tag it would need), and I wish I could drag-to-aim, but you can long-tap to see the calculations, which is a nice touch.

It even has sound effects and music. Impressive in under 13k!

13 Bubbles is an easy to play game that’s well thought out and nicely polished . Great job!

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text:
Thank you very much!

The game is missing a tutorial and having the choice for the color of the bubble is definitely the strategic part of the game.

I’m working on a mobile version that is more complete, but I’m really glad you found it has some potential!

api.name: SUB13
api.description:
SUB13 is a lane-racer where you need to keep a high speed by picking up speed boost power-ups. Keep as high speed as possible, but do not go above 12. If you do, you will lose speed instead.

Beat the 4 opponents and enjoy the psychedelic visuals and chill music!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
Neat little game. The power boosts looked like walls and I was avoiding them for the first few games. They would be great if they were chevrons maybe.

Really cool 3d graphics and nice changing with the music too. Well done.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text:
Chevrons would have been great, for sure! It wouldn’t have been that hard to make happen either. Oh well.

Thanks for your feedback and glad you enjoyed it!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: 4th, 3rd, 3rd. Nice job. Very clever use of “13”, and the fear of going over 13 was real. It’s tricky to constantly watch the road and your power meter. Nice visuals, nice music, pretty polished package. Well done!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Cool game! Love the music tied to the speed. And nice postfxs, I can feel the speed.

I would had loved to have some uphills and downhills.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I think, i forgot to blink, while playing…

The constant checking of the current speed and counting the booster-bars and calculation of “dont go over 12!” makes it quite stressful, but that is also the motivational thing that lets the player retry the game. The feeling of the speed is good, nice graphics and music. Good entry!

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: It’s a stressful game for sure! Thanks for playing and glad you enjoyed it!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Awesome 3D ans music, too bad the power ups look like walls

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I spent quite a while avoiding the power ups! Much more run when you use them.

The quick / jerky movements was tough on my eyes, smother lane transitioning would have been good.

Nice music, had a retro racer feel to it, kind of like WipeOut!

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text:
Big WipeOut fan, and this is a bit of a silly homage to those games! I’m glad you made the connection.

Sorry about the jerky movements. The camera smoothing isn’t smooth enough.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Cool game, very stressful! Getting that 13th power-up hurts real bad. Great graphics and cool music. I wonder if adding sound effects for power up levels could allow for more hyper-focused gameplay, i.e. that you could listen to how many powerups you have left and focus just on the road. Congrats for submitting this nice game!

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: Thank you! There are sound effects when picking up the power-ups, but maybe some sort of persistent “engine noise” thing could be added to help keep track of your speed level. That’s a good idea!

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Very nice graphics! And good music too! It’s a really psychedelic ride! Like it!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Great graphics, music, easy to understand gameplay, and fast-paced racing! I liked the 3D (psudo-3D?) graphics and the cubes pulsing to the music! I also appreciate you applying the theme to the gameplay.

I also really liked how the controls are press and release to change lanes to help with reaction time.

If the speed boosts are staggered, it should have enough space to hit two of them while lane changing. Some seem too close.

Also, if you change 2 lanes too fast, the controls get a little wonky. Nothing a few tweaks can’t fix.

Overall, great game and thanks for making it an enjoyable experience.

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback and the kind words! Yes, the boosters sometimes spawn together since they are random, meaning they become very hard to avoid. I should probably have had some logic to make sure they’re spawned a bit apart.

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Very cool graphics I love it! At first, I avoided the speed boosters I thought they were obstacles.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I think this game implements the theme beautifully. I’m always impressed with 3D graphics, and this one looks really polished.

The speed also changes enough that I can feel it, which is great.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Graphics were good, and the game plays well.

The music and sound effects were really well done and catchy.

I love the way the course pulses in time with the music - very neat.

The first time around, I thought the big stacks were blockers so I avoided them all only to be told I could to better.

Then I played again and intentionally hit everything on the course, which of course is the point of the game as they are the power ups.

If I could suggest one change it would be to switch view in sync with the player racer, it felt a bit disjointed that the camera switched and then the player did.

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
Beautiful art direction and an original idea to make speed power-ups a double-edged sword. I liked it how you managed to convey the sense of speed and that there are NPC racers to compete against! Great audio, especially the drums track.

Since managing the speed is the core experience, it would perhaps be helpful if the speed gauge was positioned closer to the center of the screen, or integrated in some other way into the gameplay. As it is right now, my eyes hurt a bit from having to constantly switch between looking at the speed meter and at the track. At high speeds, it’s a bit difficult to tell 4+ speed boosts apart—maybe some color coding would help?

I also struggled a bit with the controls: my instinct was to assume that pressing an arrow was enough to change the lane, rather than hold it. It took me a while to get used to it.

I had a lot of fun playing, thank you!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Wow this game looks phenomenal. Looks better than most WebXR games. The art style is really cohesive and engaging, and the animation is super fluid. I found the mechanics of maintaining the speed around 12 to be a really nice interpretation of the theme. The music is very nice and fitting too. The gameplay is fun even though it’s very simple.

I honestly didn’t realize this was a race against other players until I finished 4th on the first try. You can barely notice there were other players. Some of the text, e.g. in the menu, looks a bit out of place and broken. The controls could be also a bit smoother. The camera movement is a bit janky.

comments.author: Igor Khotin
comments.text:
Superb vibe and atmosphere! I love music games and it is amazing that one can be squeezed in only 13k. I enjoyed that psychedelic racing track dancing and colors pulsating and changing to groovy music.

As for improvements, the track switching feels a little jerky for my taste. I feel the camera jumps are too steep and sudden with the camera jump first and then the ship following suit. Maybe it would feel better to move the ship first and then slowly slide the camera onto the appropriate track or sync the ship and camera completely - that way you’ll feel you control the ship and not the camera.

Also, a speed boost looks a bit like a wall to me - I was hesitant to hit them at first. It is great they show the boost number very explicitly, but maybe you can reorient them like laying down on the track and maybe shape them like >>>>, so they would look like something that will boost you and not collide with you.

P.S. After one of the runs I caught an exception in Chrome while trying to restart the race:

Uncaught TypeError: Failed to execute 'setValueAtTime' on 'AudioParam': The provided float value is non-finite.
    at d (sub13/:1:36387)
    at e.da (sub13/:1:37390)
    at Object.update (sub13/:1:35126)
    at c (sub13/:1:39652)

And it refused to restart - I had to refresh the page. I didn’t figure out the exact condition that caused it and it was a one-off event - I never experienced it on other runs.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • Subway surfer on 13kb?
  • Great work on:
    • Music, visuals and having a complete game, all in 13kb!!!
  • I liked that you:
    • Put together so many details that made the experience very smooth. I really felt the speed and the boosting improvements.
    • The early 00s flash websites UI here is a cherry on the top.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • I think I’d remove the slowing down mechanic. This is a fast, great racing game that managed to put me on the flow, even more with such visuals and music. I couldn’t keep up with how many turbo points I had and I was usually messing up the numbers. Absolutely skill issues, but on top of that, the boost just faded away without any explicit feedback, I just had to keep in mind that I might or not need to take the next 1 that pops up and keep top speed – this broke my flow every time. I think the speed and the columns with more than enough boosts to mess up with my speed were enough challenges – I could master this. Hoping that the next group of boosts might or not have a 1 or 2, IF my boosts will degrade to those levels – this is beyond my control and don’t add to the experience.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A racing game with impressive 3D visuals, especially under 13k. The speed power ups look more like barricades to me, so at first I avoided them. Once I started collecting them instead, the game speeds up and is more fun. Looks great and has good sounds/music too. Great job!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Well done! Great looking visuals. And good music. I would have preferred to have the speed meter and counter closer to my racer so that I don’t have to constantly shift focus from the lanes and the top to see my speed.

api.name: XIII Floor
api.description:
Solve the mystery of the XIII floor. I bet you can do it in just 5 steps using only 3 buttons.

User guide: You control the first elevator. The second elevator moves in sync, always maintaining the same floor distance. The building is ancient: if the load difference matches the larger elevator’s capacity, the heavier one drops a floor.

Elevator rules:

  • If there are people on the floor, they enter the elevator up to its maximum capacity.
  • If the floor is empty, everyone exits the elevator.

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: A fun little puzzle with great noir retro graphics. It’s very short though, and could do with a few more levels. But time is tight, I get that.

comments.author: ArtemKorchunov
comments.text: The atmosphere in the game is absolutely captivating—every detail feels thoughtfully crafted. The smoothness of the gameplay and the fluidity of the animations really elevate the overall experience. It all comes together beautifully, making it a joy to play!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I serve my master well! Very clever game, well done

comments.author: Superman
comments.text: Good game! I really liked the effect of opening the door. I solved the puzzle itself in 10 steps. I would like to have more levels!

comments.author: tengri-dot
comments.text: Amazing, I had a lot of fun playing.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Very noir/retro vibes with black and white. Fits nicely into the theme. A little bit of music would encourage players to stay longer - you have plenty of kBs to spare.

Nice little animations. I like that not all the characters are exactly the same. The elevator disappeared for me… is that supposed to happen? I also couldn’t figure out the puzzle so I never actually finished. Would be good to have a hint if you get stuck; someone came in to drop the clue, and it worked!

Got a nice GitHub README - add a screenshot and it’s perfect.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Great atmosphere with fitting graphics, and the game’s logo is really well made. It looks like a professional game logo. Animations are well made, and I felt I was playing a complete game. It was not obvious what I was supposed to do, but I managed to clear the game after reading the description. Would love to see more levels, more floors, more elevators, more obstacles, more monsters. It’s a good concept, and I think it has potential. Add some ambientmusic and sfx for a greater experience, well done.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice atmosphere for this puzzle game. I served the master after few moves.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • This is a puzzle game, or maybe physics based where I need to order people.
  • Great work on:
    • The graphics. Staying on one color is a bless and a curse. It’s easy to make your game unreadable, but you rocked it.
    • An interesting mechanic. You could probably expand from this single idea of connected lifts.
  • I liked that you:
    • Took care of some small details. I assume not many people would try to get the lift below the screen and the fact that you handled it was a bit of joy :)
    • Focused on quality over quantity (even if you didn’t intend to go further).In a game jam context, you can hardly playtest anything, so having a single level and not many ways of going wrong helps people finishing the game up and have a complete experience, even it feels very short. This is much better than having 5 levels that drag a bit and have the majority dropping half-way through because they can’t guarantee they will be in for a good time.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Clever idea, I serve my master well it seems. Was not sure if I followed the rules or got a bit lucky if I am honest.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game looked good.

I didn’t understand how to win however, but keeping them alive by balancing weight was a sweet mini task

It was fun shuffling people around between floors

comments.author: Elias Ku
comments.text: Very nice puzzle game. I finish it in 3 attempts. Great light effect and B/W stylistics. Theme is completely matched by game itself. Great job!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Cute puzzle game which can be solved in five minutes, but I liked the graphics and the overall atmosphere.

You should definitively get to floor 13 to see what happens, then the game has no replayability, anyway I enjoyed it.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Very good theme integration, i very much like the artstyle, and the humor!

The game would deserve a few levels…

comments.author: StormEx
comments.text:
Very stylized pixel art graphics which bring needed atmosphere to a game. Unfortunately this game finished for me unexpectedly. Unexpectedly from ending perspective and game underdtanding perspective :)

So unexpectedly, what force me to pass this game one time more :) I hope it’s ok to serve master not once per day!!!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Super short but SUPER nice idea fitting the theme and the sprites were awesome. Nice tiny game!

comments.author: Ilia Malanin
comments.text:
Having two layers of the same puzzle — great move that perfectly fits a short game. First, figure out the goal and then how to achieve it.

Enjoyed going through the game.

api.name: Do Not Make 13
api.description:
A puzzle game about discovery and learning the rules. Draw a line and see what happens.

On mobile, tap the screen once to enable the music. Best played in landscape mode.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Wow, insanely clever and innovative. Brilliant puzzles. Who knew that there were so many ways to make 13! “45-32=13” made me laugh out loud. Excellent music as well. It’s hard to find music that doesn’t get annoying after a while, but you did it. Well done, congrats.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: This is a brilliant game. It took me a while to figure out the mechanics, but then the controls and presentation melted away and only the challenging puzzles remained. Well done, congrats!

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: This game is really cool! It took me a while to understand it, but then it was fun. I like games that make you think. This is a great game. How did you come up with this idea?

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Very original concept with maths and drawings

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Cool game, only complaint is it really doesn’t work well on mobile at all. Since the start and end points are so close to the edges, it either doesn’t register the touch or it triggers the back motion.

comments.author: Pim Schreurs
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! The “hit box” is actually the entire screen and you don’t have to put your finger near the edge of the screen at all. But I understand that that is not very intuitive. This is one of the reasons why I recommend landscape mode in the description.

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text: Beautiful game and lovely puzzles! There was enough variation between each level that they did not feel repetitive.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: Nice idea! This is a cleverly designed puzzle game that really fits the theme, making me feel the fear of 13 after playing the game, haha! Currently, it does get a bit repetitive. Perhaps adding some variation would make it even more engaging!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A novel idea for a game.

I liked splitting the symbols up to prevent making 13. It started as a simple separate the 1s and 3s, but quickly had to look out for the pluses and other ways 13 could be made.

You did a great job making a fun game with a new dynamic.

It played well on desktop and mobile.

Although having the start point so close to the edge made for things like over swipe navigation, so activated a “back history navigation”.

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Very clever and unique gameplay! Took me some tries to figure out the later levels and understand that the plusses were being used in the equation, but that’s just part of the fun. Nice atmospheric sound effects, and really cool minimal graphics, though I might’ve picked less saturated colours. Nice work!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Excellent puzzle, I thought it was too easy, you just have to separate the 1’s from the 3’s, until with 4 1’s I came up with 11 + 1 + 1, then I realized that those +’s were not simple obstacles.

Great interpretation of the competition theme, bravo!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game under 13kb!
    • Puzzle game! Challenge grows over time and it teaches me how the mechanics work gradually.
  • I liked that you:
    • Implemented what seems to be rather complex mechanic of splitting the level, backtracking and then matching whatever combination the player makes towards 13. Really good job here.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Eventually I just stopped trying to understand the map and I was more inclined to trace a line wherever and let the game tell me what was wrong (great animations btw). Then I backtracked and tried again changing a number or something, without thinking too much – I suspect it is very hard to make the line on my head and get a right answer. All the correct answers I just trusted the game knew what was going on. The two minuses as a plus though was very funny :P

comments.author: EWCW
comments.text: A really great game I love it it is incredible.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: innovative puzzle, i like how fluid the input is working Nice music too

api.name: Scrammbl
api.description:
A fast paced word game. You have 13 seconds to solve the words!

How to play

  • Solve the words before the time runs out.
  • Drag and drop the blue letter tile into an empty grey tiles or a red letter tile.
  • The tile will change color to show if you were correct or not.
  • You can swap tiles by dragging a tile over another tile.
  • 13 seconds is added to the timer when you get a tile in the correct spot.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: drag and drop is capricious on pc

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text: Ok, thanks for the feedback. Is there a particular browser where the drag and drop is worse? I’d tested it on a windows pc (edge and chrome), mac (safari and chrome) and asked a few friends to play it on their phones.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Hmm, the game looks good and appealing, for a word game. But I think there should be at least 1 letter already present (or more on the longer levels) so I can try to guess what the word might be right from the beginning, instead of having me spam place the first letter myself. There were some weird word like “HANDO” and others that I even didn’t managed to read at the end because the level complete screen was placed on top. Also I think it would be much more interesting if this was a puzzle where you have to solve each word(s) in 13 “moves” or less, as this time control doesn’t work very well in my opinion.

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text: Thanks that’s really helpful feedback; I think it would be help to have starting letters as I know the beginning is frantic until you find a few letters. I was going to put the completed level message in the middle of the screen so it did not affect seeing the completed words. But space was a bit tight on mobiles, so I went with the popup, which has the downside you cannot see the words. For the weird word I guess should have explained in the instructions that the horizontal tiles make words and the vertical tiles make another word so ‘HANDO’ is really ‘HAND’ and ‘DO’. I’ll try out switching to limiting the moves instead of time, thanks for the suggestion!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text:
trying to make this better would be through a ton of ideas

1- make all letters visible instead of brute frorce guessing game

2- instead of us adding all make few missing letters that player will complete

3- something that give a clue of the game, make the grid much bigger than single line or two lines its just not worth playing half hour to reach to the interesting intersections make it there right away

4- music sometimes add something to gamaes, even that I’m of those who keep it muted mostly but its a valid aspect and alot of easy ways to integrate sound in the game without costing much bytes into game

5- menu would be great in whatever game, I’d say its essential even so games without a menu are just for dev phase, seeing the low byte size of game I’d say you should have spent an extra hour using this extra space on game.

good luck and hope you learn and become better in next games to come.

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text: Thanks for the suggestions 😀. One of my main problems I experienced was I’m still learning js and I ran out of time. I had ideas I wanted to implement but it went right to the wire submitting the entry.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is pretty fun! I had a few levels where I was running out of time and scrambling to find the right space. Nice stuff

One UI suggestion: when hovering a letter over a space that already has an incorrect letter in it (i.e. to swap positions of letters), it’s hard to see that it’s a valid move. The border changes from white to yellow, but I didn’t realize that at first. Adding a wider border or a more obvious color change would be good, I think.

But ya, nice job!

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text: Thank you, Yeah I should have spotted the css was not working properly for tiles swapping red tiles. Glad you enjoyed it 😀

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I actually like that you used HTML-DOM to display everything.

As it is a mobile game, it’s great, that you display the dragged letter a litte bit to the left and up (otherwise it would be unvisible below the finger) but the drop-targets should then also be activated by the letter itself, not only the finger position.

Some music/sound effects would add to the atmosphere of the game. And maybe some “eye-candy” in form of CSS transitions?

Anyways: Solid game, and only 7kB!

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text:
Yep, I need to figure that out so the drop target is also activated by the letter. My partner also found that annoying that it was based on finger position. There was more I would have liked to have done but I simply ran out of time! I would have liked to put a ping sound when the word was placed in the correct spot.

Glad you enjoyed it and thought that it was good that I used the DOM 🥳.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Fun word game. I like the concept.

When the words were to both sides, it made it more interesting and played a bit like a crossword.

I wonder if there was more than one way to make a word with the same letters?

The game was easy to pick up and control although you have to guess where the letters go and then end up spending ages shuffling them around against the clock until they turn green.

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text: Thank you 🤩. There is only one correct way to order the letters to make the words.

comments.author: Luke Nickerson
comments.text: This is a fun little game. Well polished and usable on mobile. I think adding less than 13 seconds for each correct letter would help a lot because I found it easier to just switch letters around until one matched, rather than actually thinking about the words at all.

comments.author: Rustie
comments.text: Thanks, yeah I need to test out a few different options for limiting the time/ limiting the moves. One thing I was thinking about was loosing time (1 sec) for choosing the wrong slot. So you have to think instead of spamming swapping the letters and having the start time dependant on the number of letters you have to work out (so the start isn’t too difficult).

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Nice work and great game idea!

Contrary to some of the other comments, I think it’s good that it starts with an empty field and the letters appearing after each other. It makes you think: what would be the likeliest position for that letter and which letters could still come. In most of the levels the words already became clear after a couple of letters, so it was already easy enough in my opinion.

Issues with the Drag&Drop did sometimes cost me some seconds.

After the last level there should be some kind of celebration :-) I clicked “Next level” and after a while I was like “wait a minute 🤔 I know this already”

I enjoyed playing this :-)

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: I had fun! Fitting title. I wish there was audio and an ending. I played level 13 twice lol. Nice entry.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Very well done. The concept is great, the difficulty goes up gradually & you even have a little tutorial. I think you mastered the way to introduce players to your game, well done.

A little music would have made the game even more playable!

Works very well on mobile too, I can see it working very well when playing on the tube :)

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Controls work great on mobile! Gameplay ends up being mostly trial and error plus luck however. I think a percentage of letters should already be in place so you can guess the word right away rather than having to randomly choose until you get a letter right by luck. Some audio would be a nice touch! Good work

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Enjoyed playing it. But every time I click on the next level button, the screen was disappearing and appearing again. Also at some level I feel sorry off 1 letter and couldn’t do anything. Seems like some bug.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A word-guessing game. The goal is clear and the game is easy to play. I wish there were something other than trial-and-error to get started in a new level. The 13 second timer works well with the theme. Nice job!

api.name: DECK 13
api.description:
WELCOME TO FRIGG STATION.

Please get rid of the cargo.

That’s your only mission.

Controls: WASD/ arrow keys.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Impressive 3D textures and graphics for a 13k game, really nice lighting too! Smooth controls and rendering.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you Ryan! I wish I could do a bit more, at least more variation for the textures :)

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
Definitely pushes the limits on graphics capabilities in 13kb of JavaScript! The game looks amazing with Dead Space vibes.

Given the limitations on space, I don’t think I could ask for more but having space and C to move up and down could have helped.

Sorry that I did not have the patience to move what seemed like dozens of floating cargo into the yellow squares. I was also constantly lost. The flowing glow effect I saw on twitter could help direct me in the right direction would have helped a lot.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Oh thank you! :) The idea of moving up and down is very good, I totally missed that. As for the boxes: I think there are in total 120 or so, I don’t think I ever managed to push more than 10!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Impressive visuals, your heavy.js looks pretty cool!
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you! My heavy.js is basically a slightly improved lightgl.js (https://github.com/evanw/lightgl.js/). It’s starting to get short on my needs, but it’s truly awesome - if you want to make things in 3d, I totally recommend it.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Beautiful graphics. The zero gravity 6DoF reminds me of Descent. Impressive work!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thanks! :D The only thing I remember of Descent is being completely disoriented all the time, that one handled weird. I will try to replay it to see how they compare - it’s been a while :D

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
The graphics are really really good; does feel like I’m a little low to the ground though (like I’m crawling - that’s how my camera started, but figured out how to move it around). Not sure how it fits in the theme though, and would make it a tonne better if there was some music and/sound effects.

Figured out the objective; pushing the cargo around. You’ve done a really good job of in game dynamics (physics with the cargo moving around). The disappearing animation is really cool.

Would be even better with some levels - ie. start with removing one cargo, and then move up through levels. I got bored after moving just one around (I don’t have the patience, and with no music, less immersive). Cargo count on the bottom is great; I know how many I have to do, I would assume (because of the theme, there are 13, but this isn’t stated anywhere). Could have 13 levels, with +1 cargo per level.

Really amazing job on the graphics and dynamics though! It’s epic.

Would love to see some things in your README.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you Michelle! It never crossed my mind to treat it as a puzzle game, that is a great idea. And about the repo you are totally right - I pushed everything in a rush the last day. Now it has a proper readme and a cool 8000-word post-mortem. Great feedback, thank you for playing :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Awesome graphics! Cool textures made of characters, great colors chosen. I also like the movement, only thing i miss is a possibility to strafe up-/downwards

A in game tutorial or at least an instruction like “push the floating boxes to the teleporter” would be fine. And music/sfx

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Thanks! :D I don’t know how many people realize that in fact a lot of the detail in the textures is just UTF-8 characters! You have a good eye ;)

And yup, multiple people told me already that moving up-down would be great and you are all totally right.

Not having enough time to add sounds is truly painful and I’m totally aware that it kills the mood (I cry a lot about it in the post-mortem ;) ).

Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Hogia
comments.text: Love the graphics, the touch of the lights is awesome!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you! The dynamic lighting is the biggest hack I ever created haha. It’s a fake-ish approximation to light probes via “cube maps”, lerping the vertex color of the boxes as they move around (using the data I precalculate for the “lightmap”). It does look pretty convincing though, and it’s way simpler than it sounds :)

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Nice idea, the presentation is great! Some sound would had improved the atmosphere a lot.

Btw, in the first run I did the portal was blocking the exit of the room so I couldnt leave.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Oh no! I tested a lot and thought that the entrance would never get blocked. The perils of procedural generation :D

I am traumatized of failing to add sound, but I used up to the last minute - my submission went up like at 12:50 or something like that. I will definitely be more careful with this the next time (it’s truly painful, specially since I spent some time tuning sounds for the game but didn’t have time to implement them).

Thank you for playing! :)

comments.author: xem
comments.text: great graphics, but quite hard to push even a single cargo :/

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Ooh :( In certain conditions I noticed that the boxes wouldn’t go exactly where you pushed them - I think I calculated the tangential vector slightly wrong while processing the collision response. I tried making this better but ended running out of time. I will try improving the physics for my next game!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Outstanding graphics and technically very strong game! I think after 7th crate I started to feel like I’m in a horror movie :)

From controls perspective, looks like you need to push the crate at a right angle and let absence of gravity do the job. However, I had a lot of challenges with that. I wanted to have an easier way to move vertically, so I can catch crate in time. Would be nice to have a possibility to push from floor/ceiling to facilitate the movement. Or may be to have a possibility to grab a crate, so it will be easier to aim.

Anyways, managed to get rid of 14 crates so far and realised there are way more than that :D I believe you put a lot of effort into it, good job on submission!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: The horror aspect was definitely what I was going for, too bad I didn’t have time to add a monster or two lurking in the shadows. Moving vertically is something others also pointed out - and it would definitely help the game a lot! 14 crates is a lot! You managed to get rid of ~10% ! ;) Thanks for playing :)

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Love the atmospheric 3D graphics here, you clearly put in a lot of effort into setting that up. I feel like this could be the foundation for a fun gaming experience with a bit more development, I know you were pressed for time (and bytes!) in the end though, but I do wonder where you could take this setup and what optimizations would be possible to allow for more gameplay elements (perhaps in a future jam!)

Good work overall though, 3D entries are always extra impressive here!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you! Definitely, this zero-g box-pushing mini game gave me a lot of ideas (some of them I’m exploring right now, without the constraints of time nor size :) ). And for the next year I will absolutely reuse the code I have - physics and texturing deserve to be separate mini libraries on their own. For my next game I will focus much more on gameplay :)

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Absolutely amazing graphics, congratulations! The gameplay is interesting too, but demands too much from the player as the first step. It might be better to get the ball rolling with some tutorial-like points, boxes close to the teleporter, and then ramp it up. Perhaps a time-limit thing, where boxes from further away get you more points, but the closer ones are quicker and safer? Some sort of balance like that might prompt the player to explore and tidy up further and further places.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you! :) Yeah, the game definitely needs a lot more work on the gameplay department. One can take this in so many directions - a time limit, as you mentioned. A monster chasing you. Perhaps not all cargo needs to be incinerated? Perhaps instead of pushing the boxes towards a weird laser-incinerator thing you need to push it to feed a weird alien monster so it remains sleeping? So many ideas! Thanks for playing :)

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Really cool graphics!

Unfortunately, I don’t know if it’s the FOV or the 3D objects moving, or the fact that the character is flying without being on the floor, but I got motion sickness in a few seconds! So couldn’t really play the game fully.

Impressive work though!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Oh, I’m so sorry that happened to you. Perhaps adding a helmet or some other fixed element around you to help you anchor could help with this (it does help in VR experiences). Thanks for playing!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The visuals are oustanding, but the gameplay is absolutely confusing.

Am I in some sort of space ship, or what are we floating? The controls are honestly very frustrating. I eventually figures out you could push the containers to the bean of light and they disappear, but bumping into boxes makes for a terrible game mechanic.

The game was also somewhat disorienting without any sound.

This feels more like a tech experiment that a fully fledged game.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Absolutely agree. Let me tell you a secret: the gameplay as you see it here (“push crates towards the incinerator”) was born in the last hour of the compo, before the deadline. I had so many ideas… but ran out of time. That’s why there are no explanations basically. Still, thanks for playing :)

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The aesthetics of a spaceship interior are fantastic! The color palette is grand, with blue and orange highlights reminiscent of Portal.

I’m so going to learn from your source code. 🤩

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Oooh I would love you to do something with it! Take it, learn, cut it into pieces - for the next time I hope to release parts of it (texturing and physics) as standalone mini libraries for others to use. It would make me truly happy to see others using it :)

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Graphics + 3D are incredible for a 13kb entry! Light effects are great, physics too! If you’d add a FPS gameplay on it + sound it would be such a good entry! Next year I want to try a 3D entry, I’ll save your to check how you did that!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
It will be an honor if you use any piece of this game to make your next one! :) The game was definitely going to be an FPS, but as soon as I implemented physics it was so nice being floating around in zero-g… I don’t regret ditching the original plan, after all there are thousands of FPS games out there. But zero-g box pushing simulators? Not that many! ;)

Jokes aside, I hope to use parts of this code for future entries and focus more on gameplay the next time. I feel now I’m getting closer to having a nice little engine to do whatever I want :)

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Absolutely great looking game, good textures and lighting made for a solid space ship with monsters lurking around the corners, except there weren’t.

I don’t get nauseated in games even in VR, but there is something about these mechanics that definitely would, if I had kept playing it for an extended period :-)

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
If I had time to put monsters lurking in the shadows, I can bet you more than one would jump out of their chairs! ;)

That was the original idea actually, but setting up all the tech I wanted to make (and explore) ended up eating all my time.

As for the nausea, sorry for that! I am quite sensitive myself but after hours and hours I never had any issues with it (I remember this happened to me playing Descent like a million years ago). The few testers I had before launch didn’t complain either. You are not alone though, so I guess the free-ish movement where the player’s body has some inertia can produce that effect. It’s something I will have to fix if I want to take this idea further.

Thank you for playing and for your feedback :) (oh and your game is super good btw!)

comments.author: Mohammed Saud
comments.text: Absolutely blown away by how beautiful this looks, the ambience and atmosphere the graphics alone set is incredible. This visuals in this game really push the limits of what’s possible in a web browser, let alone in 13kb.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you! I tried my best, hope to make something better for the next jam :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Wow - the levels and graphics are really impressive - visually stunning

Not sure exactly what to do. I mean I can push the boxes around, but where should I be aiming them towards?

The game was a little dark for me, but I guess that was part of the ambience

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
You can push the boxes to the bright yellow lines - that’s a subatomic incinerator! It destroys boxes and gives you points. Or you can just roam around and decide to put all boxes in a single room and see if my physics engine holds up :D

I tried playing it the other day during daylight and it is super dark indeed! For my next game(s) I need to figure out how to do brightness settings. Thanks for playing :)

api.name: The Dandelion Wars
api.description:
Mysterious aliens, waves of attacks, weaponized dandelions: you name it, this game’s got it.

WebXR only game. Microphone permissions are essential and used to determine if you’re blowing hard enough.

Pick up a dandelion with the trigger button on controllers (hands work, but weren’t tested), hold it, pick a target and blow. Blow! If you blow hard enough the seeds will launch.

Beware the dandelions with 13 seeds!

Tested on Quest Pro.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Totally blew me away :) Fantastic job on the scenery, the grass blades and all. The ambient music is very nicely done.

I was skeptical at first about the mic use for controls, but hey, it works great!

VR entries are really a highlight of this year’s compo, I hope we manage to get more people to check them out.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: Looks good, great music, innovative gameplay, swarms of seeds, very solid overall!

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Such an innovating concept to use the microphone to “blow” on dandelion and attach. The health counter on the hand controller is well visible and consistent with the visual style of the enemies’ HP. Sometimes a bit difficult to catch a dandelion despite the hand being right on the model.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Excellent VR experience, reminded me of playing in the fields as a kid, what a vibrant and fun game!

Needed a good sized play area to collect flowers, started a little small and was waiting on flower to grow, love the attack using the seeds, cool to watch them fly around!

comments.author: Nik Lever
comments.text: Great idea to use blowing! Surreal but fun.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the concept of this game, and the whole microphone aspect.

Reminds me of the microphone use on some DS games.

Music is relaxing and atmospheric.

The game looks good, is about all I can say, as without VR I can’t play it.

It does look fun to play after watching the video.

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game effectively explores the main theme by utilizing 13 dandelion seeds to combat aliens.

Gameplay: The gameplay is enjoyable, with an impressive environment that creates an appropriate atmosphere. The mechanics are somewhat innovative, especially with the use of the blow sound effect. I believe this game has potential for further expansion. However, I encountered issues with the blow mechanics on my Quest 2.

Audio: The music and sound effects are really well done.

Innovation: The use of blow sounds could be expanded into other games, but it works perfectly for this one.

Graphics: The graphics are very good and enhance the overall experience.

Controls: The controls are also very good, providing a smooth user experience.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: Amazing fit to the theme.

Gameplay: The gameplay is impressive. I really love how natural it feels to blow the Dandelion. This is also a direct hit into innovation. Last game I remember blowing on was on 3DS :-)

Audio: The overall audio participates to the full immersion in the game nicely.

Innovation: The blowing mechanic is amazing.

Graphics: Simple yet amazing and cute!!!

Controls: Great and enjoyable.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Innovation: The use of microphone for launching is a genuinely new input scheme and game design element that I have not seen before. The closest I can recall is an old school Nintendo DS game but it wasn’t like this! The projectile control scheme was also unique as well and picking plants in a combat setting is definitely unique – sort of like “missile control” but with flowers.

Gameplay: The unique gameplay took a bit of practice before I could focus on improving my performance, offering replay-ability and desire to do better each time.

Theme: The game integrated the 13 component and at first gave me a hint of inspiration from “Duck Hunt” which seems like it’s setting the right user expectation as a projectile game.

Audio: I was impressed by the audio design with both music and sound effects, again evoking the charm of classic 8-bit era games.

Graphics: The visuals are solid – embracing a retro style with thick lines which is both nostalgic and effective for this type of game. Small details like the waving grass and the animation paths of blown projectiles demonstrates attention to detail that elevates the overall aesthetic. Well done.

Controls: The game features unique microphone input, which is an innovative approach to control and the the gaze control scheme for guiding projectiles was effective. The new control method contributes to the game’s distinctiveness, though it might also present a learning curve for some users.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: I loved it! Super original idea to blow to shoot the dandelion. The music is also a great addition for more atmosphere. The details of the grass moving are really cool too. Definitely one of my favorites in the competition. Great job!

api.name: Facing 13
api.description:
Game Description for Submission

Facing 13 is a puzzle game where you help Tessa overcome her fear of the number 13 (triskaidekaphobia). Every 13 seconds, a “13” block appears, increasing Tessa’s fear meter. To stop it, match the 3x3 block pattern in the 5x5 grid below, lowering her fear. Clear all the block 13s to help Tessa overcome her fear.

Controls

  • Laptop: Swipe with the mouse cursor to swap blocks.
  • Mobile: Swipe on the screen to move and match blocks.

Match the patterns before Tessa’s fear meter fills up and game over!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I helped Tessa overcome her fears in 91 moves. Nice job!

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game and helping Tessa overcome her fear.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Nice idea and works with the theme. I think adding in some movement animations would have been good.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game and the suggestion!! I had so many ideas, but forgot about animations. Maybe next time!

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
This was really fun, but the theme doesn’t really flow alongside the gameplay

Controls were easy to use and intuitive

Great job

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game!! I tried my best to link the theme with the game. Glad that controls were easy

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I completed the game with 77 moves, I didn’t see any 13s appear this time, although I’m pretty sure I did when I played before the voting started.

The game was easy to pick up and know how to play.

Sound effects were good.

I think it would be neat if when you match a pattern it highlighted it on the grid and then maybe those blocks disappear with the ones above falling to fill the space (a bit like when you complete a line in tetirs)

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game! I had other ideas for the game, but couldn’t complete, so had to stop it in this stage. Thanks for the suggestions.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
It was interesting that you came up with a meaning behind all the different symbols, and that they all related to trying to overcome fear.

At first I thought this was a match 3 game. It took me some time to realize that the pattern I was suppose to match was next to the number 13s at the top. Once I figured that out I was able to play the game.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game! Glad you enjoyed it and figured it. I wanted to keep it unique so didn’t replicate the match 3 game.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Fun game idea! Nice graphics and gameplay.

I feel like the theme integration was a bit too artificial and the 13 element didn’t fully fit the other graphics. Though it was a good “save” with the story spun around it.

Controls were good, though could have benefitted from additionally allowing to click on two neighboring cells.

Nice that you added a switch for light and dark theme

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game! I spent so much of time thinking about the theme and ended up building this version.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: It’s an interesting puzzle. The sounds were painful and loud. The instructions were not clear, i didn’t understand what the “3x3” was.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game!

  • I have added a mute button for those who prefer silence.
  • Game details are inside the startup popup (it’s a 3x3 grid).

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Interesting puzzle that perfectly fits the theme. I really liked the switch button between dark and clear mode, great idea :)

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game!! Glad you liked it.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Nice game good graphics, fun concept.

I didn’t like the controls much, it needed some feedback, and I feel clicking would have worked better here.

Overall nice, well done!

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game! My initial thought was to have click as the control, but I thought swipe would be more interactive.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game for js13k!
    • The instructions and the graphics. You put effort in the instructions and the theme of the game.
  • I liked that you:
    • Create this small twist on the match-3 game, and added some flavour in the sense of mindfulness and phobias.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • This felt less like a game and more of a test/exercise. I think it’s a bit too easy the way it is right now. You have the shapes you need to put together, but then it’s very simple to put them together. And that’s what goes on for the 13 times. I do think you’re close to it. For example, you could generate the challenges based on the tiles organization and make such that you can only swap one tile at a time – that would force me to look for the solution a bit more careful already.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game and for your suggestions. I spent most of my time in the ideation phase due to which, I was not able to add more game elements or improve the game a bit more, and yes I’m aware about most of the things that you told, but thanks for the improvement suggestions.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: I have no idea what I’m supposed to do despite reading the instructions multiple times and trying out different things.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Thanks for showing your interest in playing my game! I have tried my best to keep the description beginner friendly. The only goal is to swap the blocks present on the 5x5 grid, and then imitate the pattern that is being displayed side to the 1st 13 number( which is present above the 5x5 grid).

api.name: 13s
api.description:
13-S is a pixelart action platformer.

7 levels (wanted 13, but here we are). 13 seconds each. Some are easy, some are hard, all are fast! Can you do it?

This game is designed for desktop. User WASD or arrow keys to move.

Good luck and have fun.

Above all, thanks for playing my game.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice little game, way too difficult for this old geezer though :D
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text: Ha …. in fairness, difficulty should be toned down a bit. Thanks for the feedback

comments.author: Rafał Gicgier - Certified Codeable Expert Developer
comments.text: A challenging, yet appealing game. The author did a great job of catching the essence of old-school, hardcore platformers. Interesting and fluid graphics and music are a huge plus for a game of this size.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice, there’s a lot of good things going on here. It’s punishingly difficult. It took me quite a few tries to get past level 2. I made it to level 5. This would be a fun game to come back to. I like the black/white graphics style. The music was pretty good too, captured the sense of urgency. Congrats on submitting, nice job

comments.author: xem
comments.text: very good but we shouldn’t gat back to the very start when we die

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text: Easily the biggest feedback I’ve received so far! :-)

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Really like the music and sound, and the art style. Honestly it looks and plays nicely. One major problem and one smaller piece of feedback.

Major Problem: The level doesn’t reset itself. I died in level 2 with the spikes falling and it reset me to the start. I got back to level 2 and the spikes were where they left of, they hadn’t reset to the top of the level, so I did instantly and was 100% soft locked and couldn’t continue without refreshing.

Minor feedback: A game like this should really have controller support. If you’re doing precision platforming, include controller support. Your zip is 10.92kb, you have way way more than enough room to add this and it would make playing the game so much better.

If you make these two changes and post a “directors cut” version (there’s a link provided for this now), I’d love to play it some more! I think you’ve got some great style and great ideas, just needs tweaked a little!

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
I can’t stop laughing at the “directors cut” ! … I am definitely doing that!

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
I really enjoyed the simple graphics; they’re awesome!

For a precision platformer, I couldn’t perform controlled jump heights. For example, a long key press and a short one should result in different jump heights. Perhaps this was by design, but it something I would change personally.

I made the same mistake myself, but I would target the casual audience given that a variety of people trying your game simply to experience its entirety, not be punished relentlessly in order to see more of your game. I would make a easy mode and then when they’re done with that, make it harder for those who want to play more with a challenge. Otherwise, keep it easy, but that’s just my opinion; I’m also sure there’s a good way of presenting very hard games such that it would be judged favorably.

I think the (AI generated?) cover photo clashes with your art style. I’d rather have seen the cover photo with just your game.

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
You are not wrong!

The AI generated can-robot was intended evoke the game but not really represent it. Try searching for “Atari Games Cover art” and you’ll quickly notice the graphics of the game have nothing to do with their covers. This trend kind of applied to all gaming platforms until the modern engines took over (source: me). And even today that’s a thing.

That said, it clearly failed its purpose. I’d blame time, but ultimately it falls on me not thinking thoroughly. This deserves a place in the post-mortem for sure.

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Really nice graphics, sounds and music, I like the concept but its very difficult with a slow restart. Each death means going back to the start, I imagine some people like that but I prefer a a few tries, the second level just killed me almost right away and then that was it, game over.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I love the graphics style, and the movement and sounds were really polished and slick. It was too hard for me to progress but I wanted to see more; I wish there were checkpoints or rewind or a cheat mode (or a menu at the start where you could choose any level to retry) only because I have slower reflexes than the average gamer and tend to need a little help with brutal platformers. Very nice work!!! it plays great and a skilled platformer speedrunning master would probably say the difficulty is just right for them!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Dang that’s a hard game!

I think some people will absolutely love it, and others less :) Maybe you should think of adding an “easy” mode, where you can respawn on the level you just died. Also, imho, the death animation is slightly too long, not making me want to try again right away!

Really really cool music, it adds so much to the game. + the sound effects.

Overall, really cool game

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
Interesting! I never thought about how much impact that dying animation would have. I modified it on the Director’s cut and it absolutely makes a difference! faster death makes for less friction on the try-agains.

Thanks for the feedback.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: I love the concept of Minit trimmed down to 13s, but it’s frustrating to start from the very beginning. Also ran into a case where, after dying twice on the first level, the teeth ceiling hasn’t reset position and is waiting for me at ground level, killing me instantly and preventing any further progress without a full page reload.

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text:
Ha! Didn’t know of Minit, but you are right, it is kind of like that!

I’d love for people to play the director’s cut where the dying feedback has been addressed :)

Thanks for the feedback.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
The difficulty level is really high, you have to make a pretty perfect run on the second level.

The music theme first perfectly with the game. Unfortunately, I got stuck by a bug on the second level after a few games, a second spiky bar appears from the beginning at mid-height and you cannot avoid it. The only solution is to reload the game.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice artstyle, good controls, the movement feels “right”. Pretty hard game (thats the intention, i suppose)

The “start from the very beginning” may be better reserved for a hardcore-mode. I mean its good, as it intensifies the fear of the 13 seconds, but maybe not for all players? A “restart the current level” would be nice ;-)

Good game!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Perhaps I’m crazy but I actually enjoyed, even not being a fan of ultra hardcore games myself. The art style was cool and the controls felt nice. I wish I could restart each level without going from the beginning but other than that, it’s a nice little (phobia inducing) game :D

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The graphics and animation are really good

This is one of the games I liked the look of whilst the games were still being submitted before the voting started so have had a few goes on it.

My first few tries it was quite tricky to navigate against the pressure of the oncoming dangers.

The music was cool, and the sound effects work well.

But I certainly didn’t get as far as the levels with the ropes like I saw on the preview video. I’m just a bit rubbish at auto-scroller type levels.

However the game is well made and fun to play

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Great art style! Love the chunky black and white feel. The gameplay is also solid, with the jump having a nice weight to it. That’s not as easy to do as it seems.

I do think this is tweaked too hard, it’s too punishing to always be returned to the first level, and I found I was craving a run/rush ability.

Overall very playable, clear on what to do, and nicely crafted little game. Good work!

api.name: 13C Gauntlet
api.description: Help Juicy, the algae from Antarctica, escaping his dissolving endless iceberg as hostile organisms and flamable gasses are freed from their frozen tomb.WASD/Arrows to move - SPACE to attack - E to pickup power-ups - Q to use the dash power-up. Push Juicy against an ice block to latch him onto it. When Juicy is latched onto an ice block, press Space to slide upwards!Red ice blocks will dissapear after 13 seconds. An enemy or a new ice block MAY appear in its place.Reach the golden wind rose to escape the stage. If there is no way to reach it, wait for the to change, but who knows what else might appear.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Tricky. My scores were 50, 60, and 30. I wasn’t able to reach the golden wind rose. I suspect there’s an easier way to play it, I was frantically pressing W and space to try to jump and climb upward. Seems like it could be a fun game, maybe an easier intro level to help players learn the basics. Congrats on submitting, nice job

comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text:
Thank you very much for leaving a comment. Yeah i was planing in doing a more friendly intro but i ended up not having enough space for a little bit more scripted level. Next time will go smoother :) Yes there are a couple of play-styles one can discover. Let’s just say that i played into not only the fear for the number 13 but also that this fear comes from a matter of luck: waiting for the 13 or Jumping into it might pay off.

Thank you again for playing!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Nice graphics, fun platform dynamic, cute character.

I didn’t really get the hang of what to do to progress. Was the yellow triangle pointing towards where I should be aiming for?

I don’t think I ever got as far as the golden wind
