comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text:
Thank you very much! Happy you liked Juicy. Yes the arrow points towards the wind rose. If you touch it, you will progress to a new stage.

It is an endless survival game. The objective is just to try better your score.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: whoa that was intense! once it gets going, what a frenetic, brutal, tight experience! I was unable to progress very high, really at all, but I could see that with dedicated practice and the reflexes of a teenage mutant ninja coder I could succeed at this awesome game! I few times I got up a ways by moving randomly, just spamming the movements and jump lol, but if I moved with intent I never had the skill to get anywhere and left feeling like the game was just way too difficult, fast, and complex for me to enjoy but looks super fun for someone else who can think 10x faster than this old gamer! makes me wonder - how fun would a slowmo mode be? =) I would love if the entire game ran at 50% speed hahaha but I feel the same way about life in general. awesome work.

comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text: Thank you for playing! Eh yes it came out pretty much like one of those jumpking like games. I run out of time for further tweaking the difficulty unfortunately. Next time it will go better!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Interesting use of the 13s timer.

I could never get very far as the yellow blobs got me every time. Was also not sure where I was suppose to go our how to get there with the changing levels. It felt like I would make 2 steps forward and then 1 step back.

comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text: Thank you for playing! Yes, the indication for the objective has been the major point of critic of the game. The golden arrow was leading towards the windrose. For both the high difficulty and the objectives, i should have done a small tutorial. Unfortunatelly, i did not have enough space nor time for that.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Very cute graphics, but pretty hard game. I never reached the windrose even after many attempts… Maybe some “tutorial” levels would be nice?

comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text: Thank you for playing! Yes, a tutorial level was definitely needed but i could not fit one in it in time. Next time i’ll take better measures for that.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game within 13kb! And with sounds and good graphics!
    • The overall idea and interactions that I could have with the map. I liked the idea of destructive terrain and the jumping powers.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • It was not easy to play. I couldn’t find the golden rose and I was not sure where the arrow was pointing at. A platform game works or breaks on the the responsiveness of the controls – and here I was not in complete control. The side arrows were too sensitive and they sent me flying out of a cube many times, I couldn’t nudge the character around. Also, the jump and the jump heights were not very consistent to what I thought they were going to be. I didn’t reach a place many times out of missing the correct height.

comments.author: Nicolò Giuggiola
comments.text:
Hi! Thank you for the comment and playing the game. Actually it was both the rush and the fact that i’ve reached the 13kb. I’ve identified three major problems with the game:

  • Unclear UI for the objective: the development of the indicator had to be postponed due to other complications so i just hoped for the arrow to be enough to lead the players.
  • Enemies are too strong: a similar problem. They did received one big nerf before release but i couldn’t polish them more due to me being late.
  • Map evolution is unclear: there are actually in-built exploits in how the map works, to reward risky gameplay. But it was not clear how much one could have risked.

Everything could have been addressed by a tutorial to explain how to latch onto walls, how to track the windrose, ecc. But there was not enough space nor time for it. I should have made less content like more enemies/weapons to make room for the tutorial. I’m happy you all liked the graphics. I’m no artist and i was worried my pixel art wasn’t enough.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: The graphics of this game are nice, good job! Unfortunately, the controls and the gameplay are not as good. It’s hard to move, to target or to even finish the first level. Most of the time, the exit point is not reachable. I gave up trying without ever reaching level 2. That’s sad. The sound effects are fine, but I would have loved some background music. Finally, I felt the link with the theme of the competition was a bit weak. Overall, I think it is a good game that could become great with a few improvements.

api.name: Spirit Hunter
api.description:
With a fear of not meeting 13kb bundle size and passing multiple sacrifices and loses I’m happy to preset “Spirit Hunter”! It desktop and mobile friendly idle shooter with a hunter as a main character who brings peace to the suburbs.

Fear nothing and embrace the adventure!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Nice music (the song is “I am back” from ZzFXM examples) and spritework! All the right choices.

(Note that the characters sprite sheet seems to be licensed CC BY-SA 3.0 (Attribution-ShareAlike), so would be nice to include a reference somewhere in the repo.)

Took me a bit to understand the shooting, and it’s an interesting mechanic: you have to stop and face the enemy, but this makes you easier to chase down, so there’s a strategy element to it.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Hi, Mark! Thanks for the tips, I added license reference to the github readme (not sure if I can update description here).

Yeah, well spotted! I was thinking about that you can’t shoot backwards and tried to build that behaviour in to the game to see how it will impact the gameplay. Also, I had a small issue with the enemies velocity making it constant and equal to the player’s speed. Will try to make it better off competition.

Thanks for checking and for the feedback!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Tough! I beat level 1 after a few tries. Pretty sneaky that enemies can go through obstacles, but bullets cannot. I like the NES style graphics. Congrats on submitting, nice job!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Hey, I just realised that I had a bug that makes enemies velocity constant instead of increasing gradually :) And I was planning to draw enemies as ghosts initially (which explains ability to float through the obstacles), however, did not found suitable graphics for that. I have plans to make a v2 version outside of competition and will try to address those issues.

Thank you for the feedback and for checking it! :)

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
This game is hard! :) Beat level 1, but I enter level 2 with like 1 or two lives. Congrats on your game, I really like the vibe you’ve constructed!

As Mark has said though, if you’re using an asset that comes with a license, you should follow the terms of the license.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Good job on the progress! :) I’ll try to adjust the difficulty as a follow up off the competition. I also updated my GitHub readme with the license link, so hopefully it should be good for now.

Thank you for the feedback and for checking my game! :)

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to move the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: Thanks! I totally forgot about this one. I just tested it and it helped me to address some issues on mobile with the “share” button.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I like the main hero sprite and graphics, nice chip tune music. The fighting is really tough.

Am sure that could be easily improved!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: Thanks! You are totally right! I tried to make the game less challenging in the post-compo version, so it be better :)

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I enjoyed the game, but I think the enemies are a bit too fast. In games like this, it’s okay to make the enemy a little slower than the player so that the player can have time to properly run away from a bad situation.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback! I realised it late that I had a bug that was setting enemies speed equal to the player, which makes it more challenging. I will improve it in the post-compo version.

Thanks for checking!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the pixel graphics and soundtrack. The game has a really nice aesthetic.

Did other enemies appear away from you initial spawn point? I didn’t ever see any, plus if I could escape them and just keep walking the level seemed to go on forever?

The game was pretty hard, because there was no way to shoot - you almost needed to just run away and occasionally point at an enemy and hope the character would shoot for you.

It’s a good job you start with quite a few hearts, perhaps you could have more hearts awarded to you after battles.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Hey, you noticed interesting things! To save some effort on level design I decided to go for the infinite map idea, which I implemented through the scrolling behavior. In a nutshell, it relocates tiles from an existing pattern when you go beyond the screen. And as you spotted the enemies appear around the player, so when you go far in one direction you quickly lose the track of those. A bit late I realised that this behavior can be confusing and may block players, so in post-compo version I decided to apply this behavior to all entities: tiles, enemies, hearts and it believe it should be better now :)

I totally agree that the game is hard, mainly due to velocity bug (enemies should be slower). And I like the idea to award extra hards on the level completion!

Thank you for the thorough review!

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: This looks so adorable, and the music really sells it for me! Well done!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: Thanks!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The art is really cute, I love the minimal style. The illusion of pixel art gets ruined by the emoji hearts and non pixel text though.

However, I couldn’t really understand how to survive. I can’t control the shooting, and had to constantly run away, until I eventually died. Tried a few times and couldn’t understand the way to survive.

No apparent connection to the theme. Well, I can give you the “phobia” part because it’s a scary game in a cemetery?

The sound was too loud but the music was fun.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Thanks! I try to add hearts into the assets as a follow up. I agree on the theme topic, the connection can be improved.

The shooting works in a simple way: you need to face an enemy to be able to shoot it. And after each shot there is a small cooldown for the reload. I guess it is worth to think about some visual feedback for that. Regarding survival aspect, the enemies speed a higher than it should be in the released version by mistake, that’s why it is so challenging. I improved it in post-compo version.

Thanks for mentioning the sound as well. I try to think about how to provide a way to adjust it.

Thanks for the thorough review!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
This has a very retro-feeling (Commodore 64), nice!

The music is really fun to listen to (hm… oh… just found it in the examples of ZzFXM…)

The gameplay is pretty hard, as the enemies are quicker than the player, and the player has to face them to hit them.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Yeah, I was learning how to apply the music and went for one of the existing examples :D

You are right on the challenge and I made it better in the post-compo version! Thanks for playing and for the review!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

Great work on having a working game for the jam! Also the graphics are a nice and consistent.

I liked that you added music and sound effects, these add to the experience.

I couldn’t finish the first level. Allowing enemies to not collide with the map while blocking me made it really hard to create a strategy to kill them all. A minor mistake and they were over me – I couldn’t shot them and they just ate me :P

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
No worries and thank you for the feedback!

Yeah, the idea was that you can position yourself properly while enemies are slowly engaging you, however, I had a bug with the velocity making enemies run at the same speed as a player.

Will make it better in post-compo version :)

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: It is a good looking game, with music and SFX. Great job! I don’t understand when my character is supposed to shoot. There is no indicator of a cooldown (or did I miss it?) and I couldn’t identify a pattern that triggers shooting. Also, when I lose, it tells me to do better next time. But, what is there to do better? The enemies run faster than me, and I have no control over my ability to shoot them. That’s confusing, because I really want to do better next time! Thank you for submitting it :-)

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Hehe, sorry for confusion :)

The shooting works in the following way: you need to face an enemy and it will shoot automatically at the distance of ~3 tiles. After that the will be a short reload. So the challenge is to position yourself properly to be able to shoot the target. From survival aspect, the enemies supposed to be slower, however, I had a bug in a released version pushing ppl for an extra effort.

I’ll try to think about how to improve visual feedback. Thanks for checking and for the feedback!

comments.author: Dann Sullivan
comments.text: Solid music and I really like the graphics style that you used. It’s an impressive accomplishment considering the restrictions.

api.name: Shameful Murder Desperadoes
api.description:
Shameful Murder Desperadoes is an action-packed game where you must face your fear of the number 13 by battling progressively stronger opponents. Level up by defeating weaker numbers and prepare for the ultimate fight against the feared Number 13.

Controls: WASD: Move around the map Space Bar: Roll in the direction you’re moving 1: Equip Sword 2: Equip Bow 3: Equip Shield Click: Use the selected item

Master your skills, improve your items, and conquer your fear!

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: I won! This is great! Very tight and polished experience. Nice soulslike vibes. My first run I went all sword, and got wrecked by the first boss. Second run I went all bow. Bow is kinda OP. Limiting ammo, and having random ammo drops around the map could have been interesting? It would give the player a reason to use the sword and roll mechanics, which are quite fun, but underpowered. Congrats on submitting, really great entry, well done!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Fun game, made it to the end, lost once playing around with different tactics but completed second go.

Nice graphics and controls work well. A little more movement from the basic enemies and some trees etc would help make it more fun!

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: Range > Melee. Tried first with the sword and lost badly. Bow is a little bit OP. Fun game. I wish the opponents did not wait to be attacked, it would be more exciting

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text:
This reminds me of Minecraft Dungeons with very similar weapons. I love that game and this game has similar vibes. As well as Souls-like and other games with medieval combat like Zelda.

The game centers around combat with enemies with the same abilities, but they shouldn’t work the same way. So I would implement telegraphing, which helps players see what attack is coming and reward them with reacting to it correctly.

For example, seeing the swing of a sword is about to be unleashed, the player can dodge, parry, or pull out the shield. And the decision can reward the player with stunning the enemy or give a predictable opening.

My game is similar in that my enemy tanks are the same as my own character but they don’t move or turn as fast as you. So their movements and attacks were predictable. My first implementation was hilarious because I was immediately swarmed and slaughtered by enemy tanks.

I hope this game leads to more interesting game design decisions.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Nice game with simpe graphics but with good mechanisms and fun to play! I recommend you to add the arrows and / or QZSD in the controls for people like me, who have not QWERTY keyboard :)

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Fun little game. It was far too easy in the first levels to just shoot everyone from afar, since i had infinite arrows. Even Number 13 was mostly done in my arrows. It wasn’t very practical to change between sword and shield, I would have liked to be able to sort of dual wield them. So I basically brute forced the game, until I got killed by Number 14. So 13 isn’t the strongest after all? The numbers didn’t see to indicate their strength.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
As other comments mentioned, sniping from the other end of the screen with a bow is the easiest strategy, and probably the safest.

When the opponents spawned with different AI, this added a lot to the action, very nice touch.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play the game: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, so when I try to move the browser search suddenly shows up. You should call .preventDefault() on all keyboard events to prevent this.
games: Lord Balsekil's Lairs of Bad Luck

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Nice little game. Would be cool, if the enemies would not be sitting ducks until they are hit. the player can even run through the, unharmed.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little battling game.

I like the graphics and animations. The “breathing” was a nice touch.

I started with the sword, which despite being good meant I needed to be close so the battles were quite “close”.

However once I switched to bow and arrow, things got a lot easier. Just placing myself on the map and clicking on the enemy target they didn’t stand a chance.

Even the larger enemies which came towards me were easily dispatched if I just walked around and kept clicking on them.

Was there infinite arrows? There wasn’t an indicator for this.

Perhaps once you’ve made the first battle intention, all characters should come for you rather than staying still so I could dispatch them easily one-by-one.

A fun game though.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: really nice and smooth and responsive gameplay! I wish the ai was more aggressive, and that I could use two things at the same. time. Also, I think we need a limited quiver of arrows that replenishes over time, to prevent bow spam and encourage hand to hand combat, which is where this game really shines. nice work.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Pretty fun! But I fail to see when the sword is useful. I found that I can basically just stand still and shoot everything with my bow. It’s only the “boss” enemies that really pose a problem and force me to move around.

api.name: 13all Room
api.description:
In this game you will be trying to escape a house in under 13 minutes or less. You will need to learn about everyone’s powers as you go through the rooms to grab room balls to open the final door.

– How to Play – The touch screen is all you need. Preferably play on a tablet or smartphone. Tap on the character icons to change personas on the block you control. Keep in mind that it’s important for you to try different personals because certain characters can see things that others can’t see. Also, there is 1 very well hidden room in the game… If you can find it, you’re so cool!

– Note to Players – This is only a submission for the mobile games. Even if you have a touch screen on your desktop computer, there may be a chance that something don’t function properly or the touch screen controls get stuck. If it happens, you can press the r button on your keyboard to reset the problem without losing game progress.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Nice, this was fun. I found the “You win” room with 2:30 remaining. I only found 156/169 coins though, so I must have missed the secret room. This was a satisfying experience, great job!

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I appreciate you playing my entry! Yes, I think the hidden room is a little TOO hidden and I’ll add a hint in a future update (not sure if you’ll need it unless you’re a completionist, but your hint is Riko’s path. One last clue is 13 never wins). This is my first rodeo and I failed to understand how web audio api worked (now studying for the future game jams).

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Seeing the progress of the balls you collect is nice. It took a while to understand the personas and how they differ, but I understood it after reading the description. I wasn’t able to escape before the time ran out unfortunately.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text:
I appreciate the feedback and you playing it fully! In all honesty, I should have put important info about the personas in game. (Adding that to my list of things to update).

The game ended up a little too blocky because my partner quit on me and pulled important assets out during the last 3 days before the deadline so the rooms got a bit too roomy for a 13 minute run to 1st timers. I’ll also work on that in a future update with better textures etc.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text:
Nice work!!!! I did NOT find the secret room but I know it’s there… right in front of me… somewhere….. =)

it took me a while to figure out how to move lol (I was tapping where I wanted to move like a silly mouse-user, even on my phone at first), I felt so silly when I noticed the grey gamepad area, I guess if those were grey arrows I would have noticed the obvious sooner. Once I could move, it took embarrassingly long until I realized you could move south/down!!!! hahaha so I tried in vain for a while in the first room laughing at how foolish I was, assuming north or nothing, floundering with the concept of multiple realities, but then it all clicked and I finally got it.

Overall, once I finally knew how to play, playing was fun! So all it needs is a little tutorial for the tired and lazy, that little bit of hand-holding to nudge us on our adventure! great work and thank you for joining js13k.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: I really appreciate the detailed feedback and the effort you put into playing the game! I need to keep in mind to make sure an optional tutorial is there for future players, as well as make it clear that the controller is actually a controller. Also I need to fill those rooms up with hints in the future.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Good job!

I agree it took me a while to understand how to move around :) I was lazily expecting to move around with my keyboard arrows. So maybe not the most adapted for desktop play.

Overall it looks quite a simple gathering game, but it has the nice touch of needing to handle multiple characters to solve everything.

Having a little 8-bit music playing in the background would be a great addition too!

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text:
I do apologize, but I never had this game in the Desktop category （though I probably should had put in full desktop support too, since some others thought the same).

I appreciate your feedback and you taking time out of your day to play the game! It really makes me happy to see the constructive feedback and I couldn’t agree more with your advice. I’ll admit that I had a skill issue when it comes to using web audio API to generate soumd but I’m working through a book to fix that problem soon. I look forward to reworking this game post gamejam with all the suggestions and putting a link to an update

comments.author: Tero Jäntti
comments.text:
Finished the game on my second try, with about a minute and a half time left! I too didn’t realize at first how to move the character, but once I got that, it felt so obvious! Yes, this one is on the mobile category, but in your future games I think that with relatively little effort you could add arrow keys and with that make desktop players happy :)

Moving between rooms works well and creates a sense of adventure, as you don’t know what’s behind each door. I especially like the use of colors and how different rooms have a different style. Using different characters wasn’t clear at first, but eventually I figured out to match characters with the colors of the room. It’s an interesting idea that could be expanded further.

Also, the start screen looks really cool!

As it is now, the rooms were rather big relative to what they contain, but you’ve probably got lots of ideas on how to expand the game further. So definitely keep going with games like this!

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text:
I really appreciate you playing the game even multiple times just to beat it. As the developer of this game, that makes me so happy!

I agree that I should had considered cross platform support from the beginning, and added options for keyboards and controllers.

I really am thankful that you understood what I was going for in this game. I really love the idea of having colorful puzzles, and I started this project as a team of 2, but when I lost my teammate, I ended up having to focus on making sure everything works, and as a result the finer details of the game, such as graphics, suffered. Though I’m very happy that I was able to submit a full working game.

The rooms are indeed a little roomy for this current version and in my opinion, I could had resized it, but at the same time, I want to make sure that each room is built with a meaning in future updates. I plan to add the right characters, assets etc in the game that should had been then in the first place while working in a tutorial.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game, lots to explore.

The idea of the game was good.

When I played on iOS holding down the virtual pad to move kept bring up menus or prompts to translate. Perhaps consider prevent default.

I didn’t find the hidden room, will have to play again.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text:
Thank you very much for playing the game!

Ah, that’s the blindspot that I missed with the prevent default. I really appreciate you letting me know this, since I currently have no access to iPhone devices. Well this also solves the desktop touch screen related problems too (I think).

I appreciate your and everyone’s attempt to find the hidden room. I assume you wouldn’t want to be spoiled but keep in mind this hint… Every persona has equal access to the hidden room.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: a game playable on phone but not utilizing keyboard is a tough question to answer why

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text: Good question. The best answer I can give is because I was confused about the categories of the contest. I believed that if I build a game for the mobile category, then it would be assumed that it wouldn’t have direct support for physical keyboards and controllers for desktop. (I now know that a lot of people end up playing this on PC anyway and encouraged desktop support). I’m working those things in for future patches.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
Before I provide feedback, I need to acknowledge 2 biases:

  • I played on a computer using a trackpad, so it was super painful to play (which made me grumpy)
  • I really don’t like the exploring phases in games

That said, this game has a lot of things implemented correctly. The controls are probably fine when playing on mobile, the physics (collection of items, collision with walls, etc) and gameplay in general were OK (having to switch between characters to see different things is a good idea). The graphics are unimpressive, and the lack of audio doesn’t help improve the atmosphere. A mini-map (at least of the places I visited) would have dramatically make the experience better. Also, I didn’t explore the entirety of the game, but I felt there wasn’t much to do apart from collecting stuff. Overall, I think there is a good game engine here, but it needs more to become a great game.

comments.author: kennyfully1988
comments.text:
Thank you for your feedback and the reasoning behind the feedback. This is tough feedback so I’ll carefully reply.

Keep in mind that I didn’t really build this game with desktop in mind (though I should had done so)

Even though you praised the controls on mobile, I’m going to assume that you probably didn’t test the controls on mobile.

I’m happy to see that the physics and core game mechanics were okay.

I agree that the graphics and absence of music isn’t ideal at all. Reasoning behind the graphics lacking is due to a pull-rug on game assets by a former partner. I didn’t have the right skills to make music via Web Audio API at the time.

I too thought about how useful a min-map would had been (post submission). I’m adding that to the game.

Do to the previous excuse I made about assets being pulled out of the project by a previous partner, I had to focus too much on making a playable version of the game.

Thanks once again for the constructive feedback. All helpful and most will be applied for my update.

api.description:
2048 / 13 is a puzzle game that would play pretty much like a 2048 … … if multiples of 13 were not there to thwart your attempts.

Each time you get 8 4 and 1 side by side, they fuse into a 13.

Play with keyboard, mouse or touch screen.

Music editor and player by Marcus Geelnard (included player under zlib/libpng licence) Background song is a cover of VectorDance by Cult.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Clever twist on 2048. I got a couple 52’s. I didn’t appreciate how much the 13’s would muck up the board, ha. Pretty polished experience. Great music. My biggest request would be to show the final score on the game over screen!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text: Thanks ! Initially the tile 13 was supposed to occupy 3 spaces where the original 8 4 and 1 were, but the playtests showed it was really impractical and pretty much stymied all attempts at future combos. Having it as a standard 1x1 tile is much more balanced. As for your request, check out the director’s cut, featuring current score and high score table :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text: A nice easy to play game. Music sounds really good. Controls are easy to master. Tricky to make the numbers merge on purpose, often they seemed to be a couple of turns out from where I wanted the blocks to fall. I’m not familiar with 2048. But great fun.

comments.author: Michelle Duke
comments.text:
Add a README file 😉

Great sound and music! Love the multiple ways to play - keyboard, mouse or touch screen.

Would be great to have a score counter, and a high score leader board. But other than that, great controls, fun game, loved how the board turns. One small thing you could add for the cherry on top, would be some sound effects; music is brilliant though. Nice work.

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text: README added to the repository. Thanks for the suggestions, the director’s cut now features realtime score and high scores table. As for sfx, they are notoriously difficult to add to puzzle games without being repetitive, I shall see how to do it properly.

comments.author: Asiel Díaz Benítez
comments.text:
the game would be much more re-playable if it displayed your current score and high-score, but not even the current score is known/visible

the text/font in the menu doesn’t look good but besides that the graphics and merge effects are nice

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, your wish has been granted, score and high score table made it to the director’s cut. The cursive font came from legacy code I reused it, I’ll try and find something more in line with the graphics style of the game.

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
Absolute genius.

Had a blast playing this.

The physics and the numbers coming from the top really makes you think.

The plot twist with the 13s’ really makes this game fun.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice game, really fits the theme with original gameplay inspired by 2048. Cool music too. the only improvement is about the rotation animation which is not really smooth, and a bit unconfortable after some rotations

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, I’ll see if I can make the game less resource intensive (which it is not supposed to be) and update the director’s cut accordingly. Could you please open an issue under GitHub and mention the hardware you use ? Thanks.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Very cool!

I got a 13 on my very first moves and got really confused on how to beat this game haha.

The music is incredible, the controls are intuitive, the game is fun. I don’t have much more to say!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Nice game with a very good music and very interesting rotation mechanisms. I had a lot of fun to play it!

comments.author: A Doucheboard Warrior
comments.text: It’s one of my favourite math games! No doubt that updating the UI layer will be potential game also for some kind of Apple Store game slot. I like to see how can be a new update game rolling the sides of the matrix

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: Oh! Got it!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: The rotation of the field is a genius addition to the classic gameplay! Nice music too

api.name: The Anti-Poker Protocol
api.description:
A bit of a different take on poker! Featuring CRT visuals, sprites/graphics generated in-game (no image textures at all!), mobile support, and some whacky NPCs which feature a stunning guaranteed (>1) amount of lines to bark at you.

Making a smart opponent which can play various hands proved too tricky in the end, so for my final entry I pivoted the game into a ‘play the highest card’ type of challenge. The opponents and game constraints should get a bit more difficult as you progress.

Mouse/Touch to play. Mobile Supported! When the game round starts, click the deck for an overload of information (game instructions) *some bugs for sure

Thankyou for testing & playing, I look forward to playing everyones games!

I didnt have time to add all the AVAX WEB3 features I had hoped in the end. But you will need to imagine - there was some NFT badges and progression until I tore the guts out of this trying to get it under 13k.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Oo, really like the look and feel of this. The pixelated graphics and CRT effect are a nice touch. The resolution may be a bit too low, because it was sometimes difficult to read the number on the card. The final score of my first playthrough was 659. This type of game makes you realize how much UI code is required for a card game. Well done, congrats on submitting.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, appreciate it Cody! Solid final score too. The best I have done so far is make it to Game 7. The UI was certainly a lot of work - and something I wanted to focus on this gamejam after my last gamejam attempt failed due to a lack of UI implementation/setup (I’m using no framework here, just raw JS). So focusing on developing my own custom UI class this gamejam paid off (all buttons/images/text are all managed by one UI object class). I agree the cards are still a bit too small. I feel like even if they just expand a bit bigger when you pick the cards up - that would help with readability :)

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
I was expecting Balatro =) With this cool effect and cards, very stylish.

I haven’t fully got the rules and played as normal poker, finished with 1600 points. Does the list of viable hand get updated and highlight what I already used? Haven’t seen 13th card yet =( Will try again soon. Great entry, love barks and visuals. Want to build card game myself now (with CRT filter).

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Thanks Alexey, yes very much Balatro inspired! (been playing plenty of it myself lately).

Unfortunately I didn’t have most of the poker hand detection code in the final version. A lot of it had to be cut, and a lot of it was only half working. So a pretty last minute pivot to just dealing with ‘High card’ only. Probably the biggest part of this game I feel is lacking, because I really wanted to at least have pairs and three of a kind and flushes/straights working in their simplest form (+ the opponent able to play those hands too)

The 13th card was a special card too, which also had to be mostly cut in the end. The high score needed to defeat an opponent now basically ‘activates’ the 13 card (but this card is not added to your deck as I originally planned). Originally I wanted it to be added to the deck once unlocked, and when the 13 was played in a viable hand it would defeat the current opponent & that’s how you would progress. So what is in the game currently is a very simplified version of this.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Looks good, but I did not get the rules.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
I like the visuals: nice graphics and animations making the game very stylish. Sounds as a feedback.

Not sure I got the rules right, but made it to the game 10 :) Feels like the highest card beats the opponent all the time.

Congratulations on submission!

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Correct! Just high cards are counted now. Its a simplified game from what I was aiming for, working currently on a post-jam version which will include the web3 stuff I was working on + more challenging poker hands.

Good work with game 10, I havent made it that far yet!

comments.author: WHITE6982
comments.text: The CRT effect looks cool, really Great Game!!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Score 182, nice UI and overall look and feel. All worked really well. Simple but fun game, I think I was playing correctly but sometimes was a little confused.

Shame you didnt get the music in but the sounds are good.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Loved the art style and animation of the game.

At first I thought the rules would count the pair of cards for highest value, but looks like it only cares which of the 2 cards is the highest? One round I played two cards and the opponent only played one card which matched my highest card in number and suite, which ended the game in a draw. If that’s the case I’m not sure what the point of playing 2 cards is.

The small text on the cards was difficult to read for some of them (such as the Q), especially with the CRT effect. Also not sure if it was intended, but I was dealt 2 Ace of Spades in a single game so handily won that round.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Thanks Steven, youre right it only counts a single high card right now. I was very close to having pairs, two pair, three/four of a kind and flushes added as well, but just ran out of time (and bytes!)

Working atm on a post-jam version which will have a bit more depth in the gameplay as I originally hoped to see (including a proper 52 card deck rather than purely random cards which leads to duplicates). If only that Ace of spades pair counted!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I love the look of this game, you’ve done a great job. Animation also worked well.

I tried the game on mobile and there were a few quirks.

It wanted to run in landscape mode, but when I rotated the X and Y axis didn’t rotate accordingly. Maybe I should disable rotation?

Also when rotated not everything fitted on the screen, some elements like buttons were so close to the edge that I couldn’t click them.

I’ll have to come back to this game and try on Desktop

Shame some features had to be dropped.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback Jasper!

Yeh mobile has some difficulties I couldnt entirely solve before the jam was over. The game forces itself into landscape mode on mobile, and I have found auto-rotate on a device will then mess up the X/Y axies. So yep youre right that atm your mobile rotation needs to be disabled. And some sized screens might not scale well, more testing & some rethinking required there.

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Super stylish and good looking! Good job!

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text: Love the shaders and all the menu design here. I found the BACK button hidden on the bottom-left corner. The number of the poker is hard to read. And I do not know the strategy at first. Seems being reactive to the opponent’s played card is the best strats,

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Nice graphics, but made numbers a bit unreadable sometimes! Nice CRT effect and rules explainations, animations of opponents and cards After 3 opponents the only available gameplay was “highest”, when do you introduce pair, suits and all other gameplay? “highest” is not the most fun gameplay in a card game

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: Thanks Jonathan, glad you liked the graphics! You are correct that the game is high-card only. I did have other hands partially implemented, but ran out of time and had to cut out a lot of code. I am working still on adding in other poker hand types though, for a post-jam version of the game - both to see the more complicated gameplay I originally imagined and to challenge myself in how it is implemented.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Great graphics and FX! The font on the cards was almost unreadable for me :(

The option for muting sounds effects doesn’t seem to work, and I wish there was a way to just disable the comments of the CPU player and just let me play. Other than that, nice game :)

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Nice graphics and animations! Congratulations!

I know how challenging can it be to implement a card game within the competition time frame :)

I couldn’t play on an Android Smartphone: the cursor would be placed slightly out of the real tap position and I wasn’t able to start the game, but it rendered ok, seems to have potential for mobile.

Feel free to take a look at my own game, would love your opinion!

https://js13kgames.com/2024/games/thirteen-truco

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Thanks Michel, appreciate the feedback. The mobile version does have its issues yes. I didnt put much time into testing mobile and it shows. The cursor issue is a tricky one, I tried a few times to fix it but had to go with an approximation, which is likely tuned to my mobile screen size and wont work for some other mobile screen sizes. So not very ideal there. But I am keen to get this template fixed up and working better for future gamejams, having mobile and PC support in the same build is what I was striving for, and its very close to working.

I just checked out your game too, and left you some feedback. Cheers!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
The old-monitor-look and the generated pixelart looks very cool.

BYO Music, haha! Love the humor ;-) I brought Scott Joplin’s “The Entertainer”, that matched well ;-)

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: Thanks Christoph, I’m glad you were able to BYO music haha A good choice too, perfect for a poker-like setting :D

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Clearly there was a lot of work put into this game. The sound effects are great and the vibe is overall well polished and cohesive.

However, I found the art style really hard on the eyes. I wish I could turn off the “CRT visuals”, they are honestly horrible. Add to that the very minimal font which is already really hard to read on its own, and that fact that the game area doesn’t expand to the full screen, and my eyes were pouring by the end of the game :(

I’m not sure I fully understood the gameplay, but basically i’m handed 2-4 cards over the course of the round, but I can only play 2, and then I need to be lucky that my opponent doesn’t have a higher card. While there’s a small amount of skill involved, the game is very lucky-oriented, which is honestly not my cup of tea.

I didn’t understand the connected to the theme, besides the highest card being 13.

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback João. The CRT effect certainly isnt for everyone. I had it in mind to add a CRT toggle in the options, which is mostly setup in the code already, but just ran out of time at the end. So more visual options is certainly something I could add to future games.

And yes, gameplay doesnt have as much depth in it as I wanted either, thats my main regret with the final entry! I was originally wanting to add all the common poker hand types, but in the end I had to pivot and only base it on high cards in order to have something to submit.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text: This was very compelling. The CRT shader somehow made me feel like I was in a smoky poker room. High stakes fun! Great game!

comments.author: Ewa Mazur
comments.text: The retro CRT visual effect is a fantastic touch, giving the game a nostalgic and immersive feel. The pixel art style is clean and well-executed, with a great use of limited color palettes to emphasize the retro aesthetic. The interface is simple but effective, making the game easy to navigate while maintaining a distinct retro-futuristic atmosphere. There is nothing to improve in your game’s graphic. The overall aesthetic fits perfectly with the theme of a retro card game, and the CRT effect adds an extra layer of charm. The attention to detail in maintaining the retro feel makes the game stand out. Great work on capturing that classic vibe while ensuring everything remains visually clear and polished!

comments.author: Alex Delderfield
comments.text: Thankyou Ewa, I’m glad you enjoyed the retro vibes so much. I really do like how the UI and graphics came together in the end! I am working still on a post-jam version, I really want to see a version with more depth and challenge in the gameplay, so getting those other poker hand types implemented currently :)

api.name: Robo 13th regiment attack
api.description: The robo 13th regiment is overruning you. Take them all down with your guns!!!!!!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
We need more high quality entries for the WebXR category. This was not that. Not even sure it was a game without a game over.

Edit: Perhaps I was a bit harsh after my first play session above. At least let me give some constructive feedback.

The point counting make it virtually impossible to die, combined with the robots not flying at you but over you, that’s why I thought this game had no game over. Graphics are ok, but look a bit too familiar adding to the game looking copied. The muzzle flash is nice but without at least something gun like looked a bit odd in thin air. The robots have an immersion breaking overlapping problem which you could solve using alphaTest.

Overall, this clearly showed you can make a VR game, but it looked like you didn’t spend too much time on it. I hope you participate next year to blow us away!

comments.author: Papyrus
comments.text: Thank you for your super kind words ❤️ The game is certainly incomplete. Check out my tweet about it https://x.com/CartesiusXYZ/status/1834584508475101267

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: The shifting texture on the ground is slightly nausea inducing (because the view registers motion while my body doesn’t). The vaporware sun is a nice touch.

comments.author: Papyrus
comments.text: I can imagine. Sorry about that, I wish I could take a month off for this amazing hackathon. Buttttt I wrote fun tweet about the creation of this broken game. https://x.com/CartesiusXYZ/status/1834584508475101267 Enjoy

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Simple fun game, I can see a lot of effort went into the aspects of the game you did complete so I hope you learned something through that process.

You have all the basics working and a great environment.

Lots of potential, come back next time and improve on where you left on! I commend anyone who trys the difficult categories!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: It looks like you have a great start of something. One thing I would suggest is allowing to play using the mouse. When developing a VR game it just makes it way easier if you can play through with the mouse and also allows people to check it out without needing to set up the VR. Looking forward to seeing what you make next year!

comments.author: Papyrus
comments.text:
Thank you for your awesome feedback. You had a lot of nice things to say for a very partial game sadly.

You can check out my attempt and story about it from this tweet https://x.com/CartesiusXYZ/status/1834584508475101267

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I was able to start the game on desktop by “shooting” with the mouse, but then not able to shoot once the game started.

Still, looks like a fun game

I’ll have to try again when I get a VR setup

comments.author: Akbar Sultanov
comments.text:
Theme: The game doesn’t clearly convey its main topic. The presence of robots doesn’t connect to the theme of fear associated with the number 13.

Gameplay: The mechanics feel very basic. The author could enhance the experience by incorporating projectiles or introducing more complex game mechanics. Additionally, the game appears to run indefinitely, continuously deducting from the player’s score, which may lead to frustration.

Audio: There is currently no audio. But the game could benefit from it.

Innovation: There doesn’t seem to be any innovative elements present.

Graphics: The visuals show promise, but they need more refinement and should better align with the main theme.

Controls: The controls require further improvement for a smoother gameplay experience.

comments.author: Papyrus
comments.text:
I totally agree with you and appreciate your feedback. I had major plans to do about 95% of what you are talking about. I wish I had more time to invested in it. Luckily with this hackathon you can always write, till next year!

I wrote all about it in my tweet https://x.com/CartesiusXYZ/status/1834584508475101267

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Theme: The only relationship to the theme is through the number of the regiment. It sounds a bit far in my opinion.

Gameplay: Pretty basic shooter game but really well executed. I just wish there would be an end.

Audio: Not Available.

Innovation: This is a nice implementation of a VR shooter.

Graphics: The grid is creating a bit of moiree pattern on the background which are a bit distracting, but the overall game looks well-fitting together.

Controls: The controls were not always as reactive as they could be.

comments.author: Kieran Farr
comments.text:
Graphics: The visuals make creative use of basic elements – a nice sun and grid shapes give off cyberpunk sunset vibes – but enemies are rudimentary sprites.

Gameplay: Gameplay is straightforward and has basic scoring but does not become challenging and lacks end conditions.

Innovation: The nice custom scene-scape adds a bit of uniqueness to this wave shooter clone

Controls: Controls are minimal but functional

Theme: Not a super close connection to the theme except for name

Audio: No audio

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: Fun game! I might have missed having more feedback from the gun—maybe some sort of ray, bullet, or visual effect to see that it’s hitting the enemy, and perhaps some sound as well to enhance that feedback when shooting or when hitting the enemy. But overall, the environment and gameplay are very well done. Great work!

api.name: 13 Cards Dungeon
api.description: 13 Card Dungeon is a micro card-based rogue-like game where the player uses 13 cards to build the dungeon and find the exit.

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text:
Unfortunately I could not get the game to load. I receive the following console errors:

Failed to load module script: Expected a JavaScript module script but the server responded with a MIME type of "image/svg+xml". Strict MIME type checking is enforced for module scripts per HTML spec.Understand this error
playerManager:1 Failed to load module script: Expected a JavaScript module script but the server responded with a MIME type of "text/html". Strict MIME type checking is enforced for module scripts per HTML spec.Understand this error
Card:1 Failed to load module script: Expected a JavaScript module script but the server responded with a MIME type of "text/html". Strict MIME type checking is enforced for module scripts per HTML spec.Understand this error
uiManager:1 Failed to load module script: Expected a JavaScript module script but the server responded with a MIME type of "text/html". Strict MIME type checking is enforced for module scripts per HTML spec.
```

Your cover art looks nice!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Looks good from the screenshot but fails to work because of “disallowed MIME type (“text/html”)”. bugger.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
As others have said, it unfortunately doesn’t work. When you import your Card model, you don’t include a file extension, so then the browser tries to reach https://play.js13kgames.com/13-cards-dungeon/src/models/cards/Card and since it doesn’t exist, responds with a 404 HTML page. If you used import Card from "../models/cards/Card.js" it’s possible it would have worked. This part would work, at least.

Cover art looks great and is full of promise for a fun game!

comments.author: Stanisław Małolepszy
comments.text:
I really wish I could play this, judging from the cover art, but the game doesn’t work for me. If you get a chance to fix the error, do let us know in the comments or on slack, and I’ll be happy to revisit my rating and this comment.

EDIT It looks like you submitted a zip with the src directory rather than the dist directory? I was able to build this game from source on GitHub, but I’ll withhold my rating until the build on js13kGames.com is fixed.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game started but the whole screen was grey.

I looked in the console and saw the errors relating to MIME type not allowed as others have reported.

The screenshot looks fun, and the graphics look good, just a shame I couldn’t play it

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
comments.text: Looked interesting and promising but doesn’t start :(

comments.author: Ryan Malm
comments.text: I was unable to get the game to load, same reasons as other feedback

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: Same bug as the others, but I installed the gme locally to test it. I love the graphics, gameplay is funny with ladders, it’s a labyrinth game. But I didn’t get it how ladders was managed sometime and missed one in level 4. Then game over and everything to restart :( A restart or level selection should have benn nice!

comments.author: Zark Attack
comments.text: dang, i really wanted to play this…sounds interesting.

comments.author: Richard Chung
comments.text:
Looked interesting and promising but doesn’t start :( I am looking forward to the post compo link then.

I am using Chrome to play and got a 404

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text:
Unfortunately, it does not work

(╥﹏╥)

comments.author: Benjamin Brown
comments.text: Nice little game. Feels like a clever sokobon variant. I wish I could restart the level I’m on instead of having to restart the whole game. Small nit, I wish the tiles that were not movable were marked clearly.
games: XIII Days To Save The Kingdom

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Thanks to Alkor fixing the game, this is finally playable. Judging from the thumbnail, I always wanted to play it!

The combination of cards to create a pathway and then move the player along the path is a great concept, and could be expanded by many gameplay-elements (for instance: treasure-chests on the way, that contain more path-cards, defeat- and loot-able enemies, cards for player stats/weapons/armor, maigc,…)

One thing that was not initially clear to me was, that the ladder-card is a one-way-ticket for up OR down and the player can not reach a ladder thats below the current non-ladder card. (This could be 3 card types: a two-way-ladder, a trapdoor to go down and a step-ladder to only go up)

I see much potential in this game, good job!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Now, when Alkor fixed the game I can play it and rate it!

It’s nice and finished cute puzzle game. Simple rules to start with but it’s getting harder and harder each level. Love the graphics and aesthetic.

It definitely misses the basic sound to make controls more responsive.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Interesting proposal, I feel it would had been more interesting if you could only place the cards next to the player, that way you will have to plan ahead before using them.

Also maps should be more complicated.

But I like it overall.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Ah, finally we’re able to play this and just in time! I really like the puzzles and the art style. This game needs music and sound effects!

I didn’t understand what the enemies did. It seems I was able to just always go past them and they just disappear. I only played a few levels. I wish there were more different mechanics introduced in each level to keep me engaged.

api.name: Escape 13th floor dungeon
api.description: Escape dungeon in 13 minutes

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Very intriguing premise. I got 3 gems, and unlocked floors 3, 5, 6, and 8. There were a few nonobvious steps for me, such as that the yellow gem only worked in the 2nd elevator. This could be a really fun game with a little more polish. Well done, congrats on submitting!

comments.author: Slava
comments.text: Hey! Thanks for reviewing the game and the feedback! ❤️ I agree that it requires more polish and a few minor bugs exist there.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A nice looking game and interesting concept.

I’m afraid I couldn’t work out how to use the lifts. I picked up a shape which seemed to open doors, then got a keypad up, but nothing I pressed seemed to do anything.

comments.author: Slava
comments.text: Hey! Thanks for the feedback and for reviewing the game! You can put shapes (gems) into the hole on the elevator floor dashboard to unlock more floors. One shape may work for one of three elevators. So do try putting shapes into hole in different elevators.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I got the red and the yellow gems, but, after that, I was not able to progress anymore :( In my opinion, it was fun to figure out the mechanisms of the game and I found the concept very interesting. I suggest you to start the game with a little tutorial in order to help the player.

comments.author: Slava
comments.text: Hey! Thanks for the feedback and for reviewing the game!! ❤️❤️ having the tutorial is an absolutely great idea - I will consider it in the next game. That should be somehow covered in the description, but unfortunately, the description can not be edited after the game is submitted.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Nice idea and intriguing gameplay. Unfortunately it appears the game has some bugs. I got stuck on Floor 5, the left Door Up was not working, all elevators were displaying 6 and were not opening, and my gems were stacking on top of each other, often blinking when trying to drag them, until they totally disappeared.

comments.author: Slava
comments.text:
Hey! Thanks for reviewing my game and for the feedback. There do bugs exist in the game. The inventory stacks all gems in one slot one upon another, as long they are with the same shape it seems they all disappeared. And the whole of the elevator where you put gems should work on each floor, but it works on the first one only. Elevators have configuration that they can stop on specific floors only. But you can pass the game, because I’ve done 5 time, but ofc i am biased :)

Nevertheless, thanks a lot!

comments.author: Siorki
comments.text:
An explanation at the beginning, or in the accompanying text, would have helped understand that the game is a point-n-click and that the squares on the bottom left are slots, I initially believed they were a minimap of the floor, showing all rooms.

Play once to understand where the keys are, then attempt the speedrun.

Unfortunately, the drag’n’drop is buggy, sometimes you lose the key in the middle and cannot retrieve it. Or you drop the red key, and upon picking the green key in the inventory, you get the red key again, unable to use the green one.. And the main interface should be more responsive, showing you have clicked, and react faster, this is essential for this type of game.

comments.author: Slava
comments.text:
Hey! Thanks for reviewing my game and for the feedback. There do bugs exist in the game. The inventory stacks all gems in one slot one upon another, as long they are with the same shape it seems they all disappeared. When all gems disappeared they can be found on the first floor. But yeah, those are some bugs breaking game experience. And yes I am agree that more descent exploration would really help understand how to play the game.

Thanks you so much for taking time to review and play!

comments.author: Emanuele Feronato
comments.text: This is an adventure/escape rooms, but it lacks a bit of information, it’s not really clear what players are supposed to do, first steps should be easier, or people might drop the game quickly.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I really didn’t know what to do or what to interact with, which feels a little frustrating as the time goes down. Well done creating an escape game though!

api.name: DR1V3N WILD
api.description:
An epic race across 10 stages! Can you get to the magic garden?

Your best distance and fastest time will be saved.

Recommended running in Chrome browser with Windows 10.

Controls

  • Arrows or Mouse: Drive
  • Space: Brake
  • F : Free Ride Mode
  • Esc: Title Screen

Features

  • 10 Bogus Stages
  • Custom WebGL Rendering
  • Procedural Art
  • AI Vehicles
  • Parallax Horizon
  • ZZFX sounds
  • Persistent Save Data

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Outstanding, reminded me of playing Jaguar XJ220 and Outrun on the Amiga. So much to take in from this game, the car is cool, great movement and even jumps! Managed to catch some air on one of the steeper hills.

Love how the scenery changes between stages, all of which are great. The rocks caught me out a few times, so did the grass on the road which was a nice touch.

Only thing missing was some dust off the tyres ;D

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks so much. I had planned to do particle effects like for tire smoke, collisions, and snow but had to cut to save space.

comments.author: Santi Herranz
comments.text: Stunning creativity, massive generation of on-screen elements, constant changes of scenery, natural driving movement and a thousand code tricks to pack an entire arcade game into 13KB. A huge gift to this competition. My sincere thanks!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thank you and you’re welcome. :)

comments.author: Andy Sommerville
comments.text: Holy cow! I can’t believe how much scenery you packed into this. Way cool.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks, making the scenery was my favorite part

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Great game! Love Outrun, love this. To me, the biggest omission is controller support. This is the type of game that really would be much better with a controller. That and a drift mechanic like the newer Outrun games and this is a 10/10 for me.

Still loved it though. Great work.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text:
thanks so much. I would have loved to include controller support in the js13k build but it was just too easy of a thing to cut to save space.

The version I posted on GitHub has some bonus features including gamepad and touch support. Playing with gamepad also my preferred way, check it out! https://killedbyapixel.github.io/Drive13K/

comments.author: Cody Ebberson
comments.text: Amazing work! That’s a ton of content! Driving gameplay feels pretty good. Stellar job

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thank, Cody, I put a lot of time researching other driving games and trying to get the physics right.

comments.author: Feenposhleen
comments.text: Really cool! The amount of stuff you’ve managed to squeeze into this is incredible. The car handling and controls is also pretty smooth, and the old school graphics are fun. Great job! Didn’t really see a connection to the theme of the competition though.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. I did have trouble coming up with a compelling game idea that used the theme. I thought of a few ways to use it in this game but they all seemed so gimmicky. So I included 13 a few times in the game, in the title, on the car and license plate. I think for js13k the theme is less of the point of the contest as it is for other game jams, with the size restriction being the main thing. I also didn’t manage to fit music. So next year my goal is theme and music.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Graphics is good (though horison is bland - arcade games usually have some cool city or something there). But gameplay is boring. Even if you constrict yourself to early arcade gaming formula, it could have a lot of things to make it more fun. Shooting, like in Chase HQ, plot, career, upgrades, etc.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thank you, i put a lot of work into the graphics. There actually is a city on the horizon in level 6. For this game I chose to focus on polishing a small set of simple mechanics rather then adding a whole lot of extra stuff. I’d like to use this game as a starting point to add a career mode in the future.

comments.author: Mezriss
comments.text: Each time you think that you’ve already seen it all there is a new, even cooler environment, incredible. Only minor issue is a game slowdown in tunnels.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks so much. I don’t think there would be slowdown in tunnels, it doesn’t draw them any different then anything else. the tunnels are just very large sprites. maybe an optical illusion?

comments.author: NoNiMad
comments.text: Really amazing graphics! Were you by chance inspired by Highway Pursuit? Reminded me a lot of it!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: i have never heard of Highway Pursuit, but it looks pretty cool. i was almost 100% inspired by OutRun, probably they were too.

comments.author: sdeseille
comments.text:
Wow, so impressed with the results of this game. A real variety of landscapes. The sensation of speed is intense. The game is very pleasant to play.

The best game I’ve ever played. Okay, I’ve played three games so far.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks so much, I packed this game with more content then even i had thought possible. i hope after you play a few more games it’s still one of your favotites. :)

comments.author: Raptor Dev
comments.text:
Fun little racing game, especially to be done in 13k bytes!

Until, mind you, the needless injection of politics into arguably an escapist medium.

Loved the js13k signs, then I saw VOTE! and I was like weird, but ok. Then I saw candidate signs littering the side of the track in a recreation of a retro racing game. I will stop and hold my tongue now.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks for your honest feedback. in the new build where I fixed some graphical issues I also managed to claw back enough space to swap in the OP games logo instead of the vote sign. the harris/walz signs was a hard decision for me to include but the fact is I have no money to donate and no time to volunteer but i believe the results election is very important to the world so this is my way of doing something. i don’t post much political stuff so I hope this is not too heavy handed.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
A brake? really? Did anyone even use it? heheh.

Very smooth production, so much color and variation in the graphics are really stunning.

I made it in 6:18.716 so far.
games: Badluck Butter Chicken Goes To Outer Space
Badluck Butter Chicken Flies The Unfriendly Skies

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. There is a patched build up to fix numerous graphical issues, but I also tweaked the gameplay a bit to make it more challenging. Let me know how that feels.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Very impressive! I agree with some of the other commenters that the changing scenery is really nice! That’s my favorite part of the game. Fun gameplay overall, too!

One small issue– once I entered “Free Ride Mode,” I couldn’t seem to exit it (i.e., the HUD stayed turned off forever). Maybe a bug? Or maybe intentional, since once you’re a Free Rider, I guess you can never really go back to your old lifestyle

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text:
thanks so much. I put so much work into the scenery.

When you enter free ride that forfeits your run. You can press escape to go back to the menu and start a new run in normal mode. It wouldn’t make sense to exit straight back into race mode because there is timer in free mode.

comments.author: Andrew Schreiber
comments.text: Smooth and delightful. Runs at your monitors FPS, so swap to 240fps for a real reflex challenge!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. I was able to patch the build to fix the frame rate bug so you should be good now, let me know if that fixes it for you!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
WOW! What a flashback to the good old times! (AMIGA!!! Lotus Turbo Challenge, Jaguar XJ-220, Outrun,…) I manged to finish all stages in 06:47.5 with one second left!

Good gamplay, exceptional graphics and good sfx.

The ever (smoothly) changing environment is really cool!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks so much, the environment is what I put the most work in, both the code and art side of it. I remember playing driving games like lotus turbo in dos, hopefully I captured some of that nostalgia.

comments.author: Jonas Olmstead
comments.text: One of the very best entries. Fun driving and the environments never seem to stop changing, which is just mind-blowing at 13k. Never found a use for the break. One tiny criticism, the obstacles in the road seem to shift position on curves as you get closer to them. Or maybe its just my bad driving.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. I patched the build to fix graphical issues and also tweaked things a bit to help with the issues you mentioned. Let me know if that feels better for you.

comments.author: Eoin McGrath
comments.text:
Not just technically really impressive, the gameplay and variety are also awesome!

Managed to get to stage 8. Those pesky rocks! ;)

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much, the jungle rocks are tough, I’ve lost many a run to them.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Brought me right back to good old Lotus :) Outstanding work, congratulations! Others have pointed out several great things about this game… but my favorite is by far the sound the car makes as it veers into the grass! The fast paced thunk thunk thunk sounds so lovely, and matches what is happening on screen exactly.

Great game!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. It was a lot of fun making the sound effects for this game, I wish I had a little more space to do a few more like screeching tires.

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: Very smooth and enjoyable! And a lot of content

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks so much, i tried to pack this with as much visual content as possible.

comments.author: Jonathan Vallet
comments.text: It remind me old games on DOS like “Lotus”, with many environments changing. Really nice graphics for the available place we have! A music in addition of sfx would have be great! An excellent game even if not fitting the yearly theme

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thank you, i was a big fan of dos gaming while growing up. I had almost forgot about that game. i tried so hard to include music that i coded up a radio station system but had to cut it for space!

comments.author: Lewis Nakao
comments.text: Amazing graphics! I really looked forward to seeing each and every level. Sometimes the traffic gets congested and it reminds me of driving on the H1 in Hawaii, lol. Thanks for making another enjoyable game!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks Lewis! The traffic gets backed up before the snow level especially. It’s just like real life!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: To me this is definitively one of the best games this year! It’s fun to play, the semi 3d rendering is just perfect, the details on the road are very nice. It reminds me when I was much younger and used to play “out run” :)

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thank you, ive always been a big driving game fan too, spent many hours playing outrun while researching this game!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Spectacular “false” 3D graphics with a lot of variety. Convincing gameplay and sound. Cute billboards.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks xem! It is actually real 3D, just most things are flat planes.

comments.author: IroncladDev
comments.text:
Amazing job.

Controls were super easy to use, Game is balanced very well, Gameplay is awesome.

Didn’t really match the theme too well, but holy cow this is amazing

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. Usually if I don’t have a theme idea, I just start making whatever I want and hope an idea will come to me for the theme. This year it didn’t, but I got 13 in there a few times at least. I planned for 13 stages but had to cut 3 to tighten it up.

comments.author: zancan
comments.text: Amazing Outrun inspired racing game, with procedural graphics and loads of surprises along the way. Loved it !

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much zancan! I was inspired by your art while making it and creating the algorithms for trees and plants. It will be fun to explore the art side of this more if I continue working on it without the size restriction.

comments.author: Adrien Guéret
comments.text: A very funy entry, I love the fact each stage has its own theme, it gives the feeling we travel very far far away :D Gameplay and gamefeel are vey good. Well done!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: thanks so much, a big part of my vision was to deliver the feeling of an epic journey in a few minutes time. planning each stage to make them feel unique was maybe the most fun and most difficult part!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A really well made game, looks and plays amazing.

Total outrun vibes.

The procgen and AI drivers are really impressive. I also like the way the landscape changes between different stages.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. The AI drivers was one of the hardest parts and still shipped with a bug where cars can get stuck inside other cars!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My result: stage 5, time 3:30.016

The only thing I’ve noticed is that for some reason sometimes when I don’t release the forward button, I still lose speed as if I had released it.

For improvements, I would like to not appear behind another car when driving into it, but push to the side, because if a truck hit me, I ended up behind it :).

Everything else - landscape, music, driving, cars, control, drift - very cool, congratulations!

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thank you! There are hills in the game that can cause you to gain or lose speed, maybe that is what happened? The vehicle collisions are based on the game outrun, where other cars are kind of like obstacles that just knock you back if you hit them.

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
Simply amazing! So many unique and beautiful stages which slowly become more challenging. Controlling the car felt easy and making mistakes every now and then was pretty forgiving, keeping it fun. I made it through the goal with 1 second left. :D

I wonder if a first person perspective could work.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. I will add a first person view to the enhanced version on github, that’s a great idea.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
A very polished racing game with IMPRESSIVE visuals. It’s hard to believe that such complex 3D graphics and details could be handled within 13kb.

The overall gameplay experience is fantastic, and perhaps adding some elements related to triskaidekaphobia would make the game truly flawless.

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much. My goal this time around was to use proc gen techniques and smart art direction to push the visuals way past what would be expected in that limit, while still delivering a competent and fun gameplay experience. The theme was very difficult this year so I slipped in a few nods to it but couldn’t come up with a compelling enough mechanic to include.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: very nice game and impressive how everything works and yet under 13kb, the ads are something else and it make things very remarkable, also loved the different biomes and change of view I’d love to make something similar one day

comments.author: Frank Force
comments.text: Thanks so much, I tried to push this visually one way past what would be expected in 13k. I plan to have the graphics part of the engine opensource for others to use in their next js13k game.

comments.author: Jerome Lecomte
